The BUFFERS output is the definitive indicator. If temp read and temp write are zero, the hash table fit in memory. If they are non-zero, the hash table spilled to disk. At HydroAlpes, the meters hash table is approximately 34 MB — far too large for the 4 MB default work_mem. Setting work_mem to 256 MB eliminates the spill entirely. The billing join went from 12 minutes (with spill) to 2 minutes (without spill) — a 6x improvement from a single configuration change. The remaining time is spent on the sequential scan of meter_readings, which requires the date index fix from lesson 1.
Tuning work_mem Safely

work_mem is allocated per-operation, not per-session. A query with 3 hash joins and 2 sorts allocates up to 5 × work_mem of memory. If work_mem is 256 MB and 20 concurrent sessions each run a query with 5 memory-consuming operators, the total memory demand is 20 × 5 × 256 MB = 25.6 GB. Setting work_mem too high risks out-of-memory errors under concurrent load. The safe approach: set a conservative global default (64 MB at HydroAlpes) and increase it per-session for specific queries that need more: SET LOCAL work_mem = '512MB' inside a transaction. This limits the higher allocation to one specific query without affecting the global setting.

blocks.title: Global work_mem changes affect the entire server
blocks.content: Changing work_mem globally (in postgresql.conf) affects every query on the server. A setting that works well for the billing pipeline may cause memory pressure when 50 analysts run concurrent ad-hoc queries. At HydroAlpes, the global work_mem is 64 MB (suitable for most queries), and the pipeline runs SET LOCAL work_mem = '256MB' at the start of each transaction that contains a large hash join. This targeted approach provides more memory where needed without risking server-wide memory exhaustion.

Merge join is the second major join algorithm in PostgreSQL. Unlike hash join (which builds a hash table in memory), merge join requires both inputs to be sorted on the join key, then performs a single linear pass through both sorted sequences, matching rows as it goes. The merge phase itself is extremely efficient — it touches each row exactly once. The cost is in the sorting: if the inputs are not already sorted, the database must sort them first, which can be expensive for large tables. The optimization opportunity is clear: if you can provide pre-sorted input (via an index or a prior sort), merge join avoids the sort cost entirely and becomes the fastest join algorithm for large-table-to-large-table joins.
When Merge Join Beats Hash Join

Merge join is faster than hash join in two scenarios. First, when both inputs are already sorted on the join key — either from indexes or from prior ORDER BY operations. The merge is a single linear pass, with no memory overhead for a hash table and no risk of disk spill. Second, when the hash table for the smaller input would exceed work_mem and spill to disk. A merge join with pre-sorted inputs avoids this entirely. The trade-off: if the inputs are not sorted and must be sorted explicitly, the sort cost can exceed the hash join cost, especially when one input is much smaller than the other (making the hash table small and unlikely to spill).
Index-Assisted Merge Join

-- If both tables have indexes on the join key, PostgreSQL can use
-- index scans to provide sorted input to a merge join — no explicit sort

-- Create indexes on join keys (if not already present)
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_meter_id ON silver.meter_readings (meter_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_meters_meter_id ON silver.meters (meter_id);

-- The optimizer may now choose a merge join with index-provided sort order
EXPLAIN
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, m.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

With indexes on meter_id in both tables, the optimizer can choose index scans that return rows in meter_id order, feeding them into a merge join without an explicit sort. The plan shows Merge Join with two Index Scan children — no Sort operators in between. This is the most efficient equi-join configuration for large tables: no hash table memory, no sort memory, just a single pass through index-ordered rows from both sides. At HydroAlpes, the meter_id indexes serve double duty: they support both join performance and primary key uniqueness enforcement.
The Sort Spill Problem

When the optimizer chooses a merge join but the inputs are not pre-sorted, it inserts Sort operators below the merge. Sorting 37.2 million rows requires substantial memory — allocated from work_mem, the same parameter that governs hash join memory. If the sort does not fit in work_mem, PostgreSQL performs an external merge sort: it sorts chunks that fit in memory, writes them to temporary files, then merges the sorted chunks. Like hash spill, sort spill is dramatically slower than in-memory sorting. The diagnostic is the same: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) reveals Sort Method: external merge Disk: NNN kB when a sort spills. The fix: increase work_mem, create an index to provide pre-sorted input, or let the optimizer choose a hash join instead.

blocks.title: Indexes serve multiple purposes
blocks.content: An index on the join key simultaneously serves three purposes: it speeds up joins (index-assisted merge join or nested loop with index lookup), it speeds up WHERE filters on the same column (index scan), and it enforces uniqueness if combined with a UNIQUE constraint. At HydroAlpes, every join column that appears in production queries has an index. The storage cost of the indexes is a small fraction of the table's total size, and the performance benefit for joins, filters, and sort avoidance is substantial.

HydroAlpes' data lives in two systems: the PostgreSQL warehouse (Silver and Gold analytical tables) and the Spark cluster (large-scale historical transformations and ML feature engineering). The analytics team needs to join warehouse data with Spark-processed results. The regulatory team needs to combine PostgreSQL billing data with externally provided price benchmarks stored in a separate database. When the data you need lives in different systems, you cannot simply write a SQL JOIN — each database only knows about its own tables. This lesson covers the three patterns for joining data across system boundaries.
Pattern 1: ETL Stage and Join

The most common and most reliable pattern: extract data from the remote system, load it into a staging table in the local database, then join locally. The extraction is a separate pipeline step (often orchestrated by Airflow) that runs before the join query. The staging table is a regular table that the optimizer can analyze, index, and join using all the strategies it knows. The trade-off: the data in the staging table is a snapshot — it may be stale by the time the join runs. At HydroAlpes, the Spark-processed ML features are exported to a Parquet file nightly, loaded into a PostgreSQL staging table at 05:00, and the billing query that joins them runs at 05:30. The 30-minute delay is acceptable for daily batch processing.

-- Stage-and-join pattern: the data is already in the local database
-- The staging table was loaded by a separate pipeline step

-- Step 1 (pipeline): COPY Spark results into PostgreSQL staging table
-- COPY staging.ml_features FROM '/data/exports/features_20250115.csv' CSV HEADER;

-- Step 2 (query): join locally with full optimizer support
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       ml.predicted_demand,
       mr.daily_kw - ml.predicted_demand AS forecast_error
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN staging.ml_features AS ml
    ON mr.meter_id = ml.meter_id AND mr.date = ml.prediction_date
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH';

The join is a standard local join — the optimizer has full statistics on both tables, can choose any join algorithm, and can use indexes on both sides. The staging table is temporary: it is truncated and reloaded before each pipeline run, ensuring the data is fresh for every execution. This pattern is simple, reliable, and performs well because the join operates entirely within one database engine with full optimizer support. The complexity is in the orchestration (ensuring the staging load completes before the join query runs), not in the SQL itself.
Pattern 2: Foreign Data Wrappers

PostgreSQL's Foreign Data Wrapper (FDW) extension allows you to define foreign tables that reference data in another PostgreSQL instance, a MySQL database, or even a flat file. Queries against foreign tables transparently fetch data from the remote source. The syntax is standard SQL — you JOIN a local table with a foreign table as if they were in the same database. The trade-off: the optimizer has limited information about the foreign table (no local statistics, no local indexes), and data transfer happens over the network during query execution, which can be slow for large foreign tables.

-- Define a foreign table that references a remote PostgreSQL database
-- (one-time setup by the DBA)
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS postgres_fdw;

CREATE SERVER remote_pricing
  FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER postgres_fdw
  OPTIONS (host 'pricing-db.hydroalpes.internal', dbname 'pricing', port '5432');

CREATE FOREIGN TABLE remote.benchmark_prices (
    market_zone      VARCHAR(10),
    price_timestamp  TIMESTAMP,
    benchmark_eur    NUMERIC(10,4)
) SERVER remote_pricing
  OPTIONS (schema_name 'public', table_name 'benchmark_prices');

-- Query the foreign table as if it were local
SELECT ep.market_zone,
       ep.price_eur_mwh,
       bp.benchmark_eur,
       ep.price_eur_mwh - bp.benchmark_eur AS spread
FROM   silver.energy_prices AS ep
  INNER JOIN remote.benchmark_prices AS bp
    ON ep.market_zone = bp.market_zone
   AND ep.price_timestamp = bp.price_timestamp
WHERE  ep.price_timestamp >= '2025-01-15'
  AND  ep.price_timestamp <  '2025-01-16';

The foreign table remote.benchmark_prices looks like a regular table in SQL. The optimizer generates a plan that fetches the matching rows from the remote server during execution. For small foreign tables or selective filters that push down to the remote server, this works well. For large foreign tables or non-pushable filters, the database may fetch the entire foreign table over the network, which is slow. At HydroAlpes, FDW is used for the benchmark prices (small table, highly selective filter on timestamp) but not for cross-referencing the ML features (large table, better served by the ETL stage pattern).
Pattern 3: Application-Level Join

When neither staging nor FDW is appropriate — for example, when the remote data is in a system that does not support SQL (a REST API, a document database, an S3-based data lake) — the join is performed in application code. The pipeline queries both sources independently, loads the results into memory (a Pandas DataFrame, a PySpark DataFrame, or a Python dictionary), and performs the join in code. This is the most flexible pattern but the least efficient: the join does not benefit from database optimization, and the memory required to hold both datasets limits the scale. At HydroAlpes, application-level joins are reserved for small, non-SQL sources like the weather API and the regulatory tariff schedule.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Choose the join pattern based on the remote data's size and update frequency. For large, daily-updated data: ETL stage and join (most reliable, best optimizer support). For small, infrequently updated reference data: FDW (simplest, no pipeline needed). For non-SQL sources: application-level join (most flexible, least efficient). At HydroAlpes, 80% of cross-system joins use the ETL stage pattern because it provides the best combination of reliability, performance, and debuggability.

This module covered join performance at production scale: diagnosing slow joins with plan analysis, detecting and resolving fan-out, choosing between semi-joins and anti-joins and inner joins, using LATERAL for correlated lookups, understanding cardinality estimation and its impact on plan quality, optimizing join ordering for multi-table queries, tuning hash join memory to prevent disk spill, leveraging merge join with indexes for large-table-to-large-table equi-joins, and joining data across system boundaries. This final lesson synthesizes those techniques into the decision tree and review checklist that HydroAlpes' data engineering team uses for every join in production.
The Join Decision Tree

rows: Do you need columns from the joined table?
Use INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN
Use EXISTS (semi-join) or NOT EXISTS (anti-join)
Is the join key unique in the dimension table?
Safe to join directly
Deduplicate first (ROW_NUMBER) or fix the source
Is the dimension table small (<1% of fact table)?
Expect hash join with small build side — verify no spill
Expect hash join or merge join — verify work_mem is sufficient
Does the query filter the fact table before joining?
Good — reduced intermediate volume
Add a CTE to filter first if statistics do not guide the optimizer
Are join cardinality estimates accurate (within 3x)?
Trust the optimizer's plan
Run ANALYZE; consider extended statistics for correlated columns
Does the plan show hash spill (temp read/write > 0)?
Increase work_mem or switch to merge join with indexes
Hash join is running efficiently in memory

blocks.content: The Join Review Checklist

Every pipeline join at HydroAlpes passes through a six-point review before deployment to production. The review is documented in the pull request description alongside the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, and it takes approximately 3 minutes per join. Each checkpoint targets a specific category of join problem that has caused a production incident in the past: fan-out from duplicate dimension keys, wrong algorithm choice from stale statistics, cardinality estimation errors that cascade through multi-table joins, hash table spill from insufficient work_mem, missing filter pushdown that inflates intermediate volumes, and dimension key duplication that silently multiplies rows. A join that fails any checkpoint goes back to the author for correction before the pull request is approved.

rows: Row count preservation
Compare COUNT(*) before and after join — ratio must be 1.0 ± 0.001
Join algorithm
EXPLAIN shows Hash Join or Merge Join for equi-joins; no Nested Loop on large tables
Cardinality accuracy
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: estimated rows within 3x of actual at every join node
No hash spill
BUFFERS: temp read=0 write=0 (or increase work_mem until no spill)
Filter before join
Selective WHERE conditions appear below the join in the plan, not above
Dimension key uniqueness
SELECT key, COUNT(*) GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 returns zero rows

blocks.content: The Escalation Path

When a join is slow despite passing the review checklist, HydroAlpes follows an escalation path. First level: update statistics (ANALYZE) and re-run — this resolves 60% of join performance issues. Second level: increase work_mem for the specific query and verify no hash spill — resolves another 20%. Third level: create or modify indexes on join keys to enable index-assisted merge join or nested loop — resolves 15%. Fourth level: restructure the query with CTEs to enforce explicit join ordering — resolves the remaining 5%. Each level is more invasive than the previous, so the team starts at level 1 and escalates only when the simpler fix does not resolve the problem.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 3, you will expand your analytical SQL toolkit beyond the junior-level window functions and GROUP BY patterns. Advanced frame specifications, GROUPING SETS for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting, and conditional aggregation with FILTER — these are the techniques that power HydroAlpes' executive dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts. Every advanced analytical query builds on the join optimization skills from this module, because the joins that feed the analytics must be efficient before the analytics can be fast.

chapters.title: Advanced Analytical SQL
chapters.description: Master advanced window function patterns, GROUPING SETS for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting, and conditional aggregation for complex reporting.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' regulatory affairs team needed a single report combining daily consumption by region, weekly consumption by region, monthly consumption by region, and a grand total — all in one result set. The junior approach required four separate queries with UNION ALL: one GROUP BY region, date; one GROUP BY region, DATE_TRUNC('week', date); one GROUP BY region, DATE_TRUNC('month', date); and one with no GROUP BY at all. The four queries scanned the 37.2-million-row January dataset four times, took 48 seconds in total, and produced a fragile pipeline where every schema change had to be replicated across four queries. The mid-level approach uses GROUPING SETS to produce the same result in a single scan, a single query, in 14 seconds. This module teaches the advanced analytical patterns that replace multi-query workarounds with single-query solutions.
The Mid-Level Analytical Toolkit

The junior track covered the five aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), GROUP BY, HAVING, and the core window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, running totals, moving averages). These tools handle the majority of analytical queries. But production reporting demands patterns that the basic toolkit cannot express cleanly: multi-level subtotals in a single pass, pivoted cross-tabs where months become columns, gap-aware moving averages that handle missing dates correctly, percentile-based outlier detection, and conditional aggregation that computes separate metrics for each customer type without multiple passes over the data. Each of these patterns has a junior-level workaround (multiple queries, application-level pivoting, CASE WHEN inside aggregates), and each has a mid-level solution that is faster, more readable, and more maintainable.
What This Module Covers

Lessons 2–3 extend window functions with advanced frame specifications (ROWS vs RANGE vs GROUPS, EXCLUDE clauses) and percentile functions (NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST, PERCENTILE_CONT). Lessons 4–5 introduce GROUPING SETS, ROLLUP, and CUBE for multi-level aggregation within a single query. Lessons 6–7 cover pivoting (rows to columns) and unpivoting (columns to rows) — the transformations that reshape data between analytical and reporting formats. Lesson 8 introduces the FILTER clause for conditional aggregation — the modern replacement for CASE WHEN inside aggregate functions. Lessons 9–10 combine these patterns into the production analytical queries that power HydroAlpes' dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts.

-- Preview: GROUPING SETS replaces four separate queries with one
-- Daily, weekly, monthly, and grand total — single scan, single query
SELECT mr.region,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)::DATE AS month,
       DATE_TRUNC('week', mr.date)::DATE  AS week,
       mr.date,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.date),                              -- daily by region
  (mr.region, DATE_TRUNC('week', mr.date)::DATE),    -- weekly by region
  (mr.region, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)::DATE),   -- monthly by region
  ()                                                  -- grand total
)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.date NULLS LAST;

This single query produces four aggregation levels simultaneously. The database scans the data once, computes all four grouping levels in a single pass, and returns the combined result. The NULL values in the non-grouped columns indicate which level each row represents — a concept you will learn in detail in lesson 4. The performance advantage over four separate queries is substantial: one scan instead of four, one sort instead of four, and one result set that a downstream dashboard can consume directly without application-level merging. This is the kind of consolidation that mid-level analytical SQL enables.

blocks.title: Single-scan patterns replace multi-query workarounds
blocks.content: Every technique in this module follows the same principle: replace multiple passes over the data with a single pass that computes multiple results simultaneously. GROUPING SETS replaces UNION ALL of multiple GROUP BY queries. FILTER replaces multiple CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregates. Pivoting replaces application-level row-to-column transformation. The database is almost always faster at these operations than application code, because it can optimize the single scan with indexes, parallelism, and memory management that application code cannot access.

The junior track covered ROWS BETWEEN for moving averages: ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW computes a 7-day moving average by counting physical rows. But physical rows and logical time intervals are not the same thing. If meter CH-BERN-00142 has no reading on January 8 (a gap in the data), the 7-row window includes January 1–7 and January 9 — spanning 8 calendar days, not 7. The RANGE frame mode solves this by operating on value ranges rather than row counts: RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '6 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW includes all rows within 6 calendar days of the current row, regardless of how many physical rows that spans. Understanding the difference between ROWS, RANGE, and GROUPS is essential for producing correct analytics on time-series data with gaps.
ROWS vs RANGE vs GROUPS

rows: ROWS
Physical row positions (1st, 2nd, 3rd row from current)
Ignores gaps — 7 rows might span 10 calendar days
Fixed-count windows: 'the previous 7 readings' regardless of dates
RANGE
Value ranges (all rows within a value distance from current row)
Respects gaps — only rows within the date range are included
Time-based windows: 'all readings within 7 calendar days'
GROUPS
Peer groups (all rows with the same ORDER BY value count as one group)
Peer-aware — tied values are treated as one unit
Peer-based windows: 'the previous 3 distinct dates' (PostgreSQL 11+)

blocks.content: RANGE for Gap-Aware Moving Averages

-- ROWS frame: 7-row window (may span more than 7 calendar days if gaps exist)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS rows_avg_7
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

-- RANGE frame: 7-calendar-day window (correct even with gaps)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '6 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS range_avg_7d
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

If January 8 has no reading, the ROWS frame on January 9 looks back 6 rows and includes January 1–7 and January 9 — an 8-day span with only 7 data points. The RANGE frame on January 9 looks back 6 calendar days and includes January 3–9 — a true 7-day window that correctly excludes dates outside the range. For continuous time-series without gaps (like HydroAlpes' daily readings for active meters), both produce identical results. For time-series with gaps (meters that occasionally fail to report, price calendars that skip weekends), RANGE produces the analytically correct result. At HydroAlpes, the energy price moving average uses RANGE because the price calendar has no entries on Swiss public holidays.
EXCLUDE Clauses

-- EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW: compute the average of neighbors, not including self
-- Useful for outlier detection: how does this reading compare to its neighbors?
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
         EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW
       ) AS neighbor_avg,
       mr.daily_kw - AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
         EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW
       ) AS deviation
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW removes the current row from the window computation. The neighbor_avg is the average of the 3 preceding and 3 following rows, excluding the current row itself. The deviation column shows how far the current reading deviates from its neighbors. This is a local outlier detection pattern: if the deviation is more than 3 standard deviations from the neighbor average, the reading is suspicious. PostgreSQL 11+ also supports EXCLUDE TIES (exclude rows with the same ORDER BY value as the current row) and EXCLUDE GROUP (exclude all rows in the current row's peer group). At HydroAlpes, the EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW pattern powers the anomaly detection queries in the data quality framework (Module 9).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use ROWS for fixed-count windows (the last 7 readings, the next 3 rows). Use RANGE for time-based windows (all readings within 7 calendar days, all prices within 1 hour). Use GROUPS for peer-based windows (the last 3 distinct dates, the next 2 distinct regions — rare but useful for categorical ordering). When in doubt, RANGE is the safer default for time-series because it handles gaps correctly. ROWS is appropriate only when you are certain the data has no gaps or when gaps are intentionally treated as non-existent.

HydroAlpes' pricing team needs to classify meters into consumption tiers: the top 25% by monthly consumption are 'high-usage' and pay a premium rate, the next 50% are 'standard,' and the bottom 25% are 'low-usage' and receive a discount. The junior approach uses subqueries: compute the 25th and 75th percentiles in one query, then use those values as filter thresholds in a CASE expression. The mid-level approach uses NTILE(4) to divide meters into quartiles in a single window function call, then maps quartiles to tier names. NTILE and the related percentile functions (PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST, PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC) provide built-in statistical classification that eliminates the multi-step workarounds.
NTILE — Dividing into Equal Buckets

-- Classify meters into consumption quartiles for tiered pricing
WITH monthly_totals AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS jan_total
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY mr.meter_id
),
quartiled AS (
  SELECT meter_id,
         jan_total,
         NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY jan_total DESC) AS quartile
  FROM   monthly_totals
)
SELECT quartile,
       CASE quartile
         WHEN 1 THEN 'High-usage (premium rate)'
         WHEN 2 THEN 'Above-average'
         WHEN 3 THEN 'Below-average'
         WHEN 4 THEN 'Low-usage (discount rate)'
       END AS tier_name,
       COUNT(*)           AS meter_count,
       MIN(jan_total)     AS min_kw,
       MAX(jan_total)     AS max_kw,
       AVG(jan_total)     AS avg_kw
FROM   quartiled
GROUP BY quartile
ORDER BY quartile;

NTILE(4) divides the 1.2 million meters into 4 approximately equal groups of 300,000 each, ordered by jan_total descending. Quartile 1 contains the highest consumers (above 182.40 kW/month), and quartile 4 contains the lowest. The outer query aggregates each quartile to show the tier boundaries, meter count, and average consumption. This single query replaces the three-step junior approach (compute percentiles, apply thresholds, classify) with a one-pass window function. The NTILE result is deterministic: ties at the boundary are assigned to the lower-numbered quartile.
PERCENT_RANK and CUME_DIST

PERCENT_RANK returns a value between 0 and 1 representing the row's relative position in the partition: 0 for the first row, 1 for the last, and proportional values in between. CUME_DIST returns the cumulative distribution: the fraction of rows that have a value less than or equal to the current row's value. Both are useful for statistical analysis. At HydroAlpes, PERCENT_RANK identifies where each meter falls in the consumption distribution: a PERCENT_RANK of 0.95 means the meter consumes more than 95% of all meters. CUME_DIST answers the inverse question: what percentage of meters consume less than or equal to a given value? These functions power the consumption distribution charts on HydroAlpes' executive dashboard.
PERCENTILE_CONT and PERCENTILE_DISC

-- Compute exact percentile values for the consumption distribution
-- PERCENTILE_CONT interpolates between values (continuous)
-- PERCENTILE_DISC returns an actual value from the dataset (discrete)
SELECT mr.region,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS median_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p75_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p95_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p99_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS mean_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

The percentile profile per region reveals information that the mean alone hides. BERN-NORTH has a median of 0.82 kW (half the meters consume less) but a mean of 1.54 kW — the distribution is right-skewed, pulled up by industrial meters. The p99 of 142.30 kW shows that 1% of meters consume more than 142 kW — these are the industrial facilities that dominate regional totals despite being a tiny fraction of the meter count. This profile shapes every analytical decision: billing tiers should be based on percentiles, not means, because the mean is distorted by outliers. At HydroAlpes, every Gold-layer consumption report includes the median alongside the mean per Lena's reporting standards.

blocks.title: Percentiles reveal what means hide
blocks.content: When the mean and median diverge significantly (mean 1.54, median 0.82), the distribution is skewed and the mean is misleading. Reporting only the mean tells stakeholders that 'the average meter consumes 1.54 kW/day,' but the reality is that half the meters consume less than 0.82. Always report percentiles alongside means for skewed distributions — which describes almost every real-world energy, financial, and usage dataset.

GROUP BY computes one aggregation level: one row per unique combination of the grouped columns. If you need multiple aggregation levels — totals by region, by customer type, by region-and-type, and a grand total — the junior approach requires four separate GROUP BY queries combined with UNION ALL. GROUPING SETS lets you specify all four levels in a single GROUP BY clause, and the database computes them in one scan. The result set contains rows from all specified levels, with NULL values in the columns that are not part of a particular level's grouping. GROUPING SETS is the foundation for multi-level reporting and is the basis for the ROLLUP and CUBE shortcuts you will learn in lesson 5.
The Syntax

-- Four aggregation levels in one query with GROUPING SETS
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)         AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.customer_type),  -- level 1: by region and type
  (mr.region),                    -- level 2: by region only
  (mr.customer_type),             -- level 3: by type only
  ()                              -- level 4: grand total
)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

region      | customer_type | total_kw        | readings
------------+---------------+-----------------+-----------
BERN-NORTH  | COMMERCIAL    |     491,225.65  |   155,000
BERN-NORTH  | INDUSTRIAL    |     620,945.25  |    46,500
BERN-NORTH  | RESIDENTIAL   |   1,149,774.70  | 1,271,000
BERN-NORTH  | (null)        |   2,261,945.60  | 1,472,500   ← region subtotal
BERN-SOUTH  | COMMERCIAL    |     478,118.40  |   151,200
...         | ...           |             ... |       ...
(null)      | COMMERCIAL    |   2,843,120.85  | 1,164,200   ← customer_type total
(null)      | INDUSTRIAL    |   3,582,237.10  |   348,200
(null)      | RESIDENTIAL   |   9,411,485.00  |10,287,600
(null)      | (null)        |  15,836,842.95  |11,800,000   ← grand total

The result contains four types of rows. Rows where both region and customer_type are non-NULL are the detail level (region-by-type). Rows where customer_type is NULL but region is non-NULL are region subtotals. Rows where region is NULL but customer_type is non-NULL are customer-type totals. The row where both are NULL is the grand total. The database computed all four levels in a single scan of the data — no UNION ALL, no four-pass aggregation. For HydroAlpes' 37.2 million January readings, this single-scan approach is roughly 3x faster than the four-query alternative.
The GROUPING() Function

-- GROUPING() distinguishes NULLs from grouping from real NULLs in the data
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       GROUPING(mr.region)        AS is_region_total,
       GROUPING(mr.customer_type) AS is_type_total,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.customer_type),
  (mr.region),
  ()
)
ORDER BY GROUPING(mr.region), mr.region NULLS LAST,
         GROUPING(mr.customer_type), mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

The GROUPING() function returns 1 if the column is aggregated at the current level (the NULL in the output represents 'all values') and 0 if the column is part of the current grouping. This distinguishes between a NULL that means 'this is a subtotal row' and a NULL that means 'the column's actual value is NULL.' At HydroAlpes, region never contains NULL data, so the distinction is academic. But in datasets where grouped columns can legitimately contain NULL values, GROUPING() is essential for correct interpretation. The ORDER BY clause uses GROUPING() to sort subtotal rows after detail rows within each region.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When building reporting queries with GROUPING SETS, include a level_indicator column using GROUPING() values: CASE WHEN GROUPING(region) = 0 AND GROUPING(customer_type) = 0 THEN 'detail' WHEN GROUPING(customer_type) = 1 THEN 'region_subtotal' ELSE 'grand_total' END. This makes the result self-documenting and allows downstream dashboards to filter or format each level differently — bold for subtotals, highlighted for grand totals — without parsing NULL patterns.

GROUPING SETS requires you to enumerate every aggregation level explicitly. For hierarchical data — where the levels follow a natural hierarchy (region → customer_type → grand total) — ROLLUP generates the hierarchical grouping sets automatically. For cross-tabulation data — where every combination of dimensions is needed — CUBE generates all possible grouping sets automatically. Both are shorthand for specific GROUPING SETS patterns: ROLLUP produces progressive subtotals from left to right, and CUBE produces every possible combination of the grouped columns.
ROLLUP — Hierarchical Subtotals

-- ROLLUP produces: (region, type), (region), ()
-- This is the hierarchical subtotal pattern: detail → region subtotal → grand total
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY ROLLUP (mr.region, mr.customer_type)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

ROLLUP (region, customer_type) is equivalent to GROUPING SETS ((region, customer_type), (region), ()). It produces three levels: the detail level (region + customer_type), the region subtotal (region only, customer_type NULL), and the grand total (both NULL). ROLLUP always produces N+1 grouping sets from N columns, progressing from left to right: the leftmost column is always included, and columns are dropped from right to left. This matches the natural hierarchy: you want subtotals by region (dropping customer_type) and a grand total (dropping everything), but you do not want subtotals by customer_type alone (dropping region) — that would be a cross-tab, not a hierarchy.
CUBE — All Combinations

-- CUBE produces: (region, type), (region), (type), ()
-- Every possible combination of the grouped columns
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY CUBE (mr.region, mr.customer_type)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

CUBE (region, customer_type) is equivalent to GROUPING SETS ((region, customer_type), (region), (customer_type), ()). It produces 2^N grouping sets from N columns — every possible subset. For two columns, that is 4 levels: detail, region-only, type-only, and grand total. CUBE is appropriate when every combination of dimensions is analytically meaningful — when you need both 'total for BERN-NORTH across all types' and 'total for COMMERCIAL across all regions.' At HydroAlpes, the regulatory report uses CUBE because the regulator requires subtotals in every dimension independently, not just hierarchically.
ROLLUP vs CUBE vs GROUPING SETS

rows.left: Produces N+1 grouping sets from N columns
rows.right: Produces 2^N grouping sets from N columns

rows.left: Subtotals follow a hierarchy: left columns always included
rows.right: Subtotals for every possible column combination

rows.left: ROLLUP(A, B, C) → (A,B,C), (A,B), (A), ()
Use for hierarchical dimensions (region → type → detail)
rows.right: Use for cross-tab reports where every dimension is independent

rows.left: Fewer output rows — simpler to consume
rows.right: More output rows — comprehensive but potentially overwhelming

blocks.content: GROUPING SETS is the general form — ROLLUP and CUBE are shortcuts for common patterns. You can always replace ROLLUP or CUBE with an explicit GROUPING SETS clause. The explicit form is necessary when you need a custom set of levels that does not match a pure hierarchy (ROLLUP) or a full cross-tab (CUBE). At HydroAlpes, the billing report uses ROLLUP (region hierarchy), the regulatory report uses CUBE (all dimension combinations), and the executive dashboard uses GROUPING SETS with a custom set that includes daily, weekly, and monthly levels but not quarterly — because the quarterly values come from a separate fiscal calendar.

blocks.title: Three columns with CUBE = 8 grouping sets
blocks.content: CUBE with 3 columns produces 8 grouping sets. With 4 columns, it produces 16. With 5, it produces 32. The output size grows exponentially with the number of columns. For production reports, prefer ROLLUP (linear growth) when a hierarchy is appropriate, and use CUBE or explicit GROUPING SETS only when you genuinely need every dimension combination. Unnecessary CUBE with many columns produces enormous result sets that downstream consumers cannot effectively use.

Pivoting transforms rows into columns: a result where each month appears as a separate row (January, February, March as three rows per region) becomes a result where each month is a separate column (jan_kw, feb_kw, mar_kw as three columns per region). This row-to-column transformation is one of the most requested reporting formats — spreadsheet users expect months as columns, not as rows — and it is also one of the most awkward to express in standard SQL. PostgreSQL provides two approaches: the conditional aggregation pattern (portable across all databases) and the crosstab function from the tablefunc extension (PostgreSQL-specific). Spark SQL provides a native PIVOT clause.
Conditional Aggregation Pivot

-- Pivot monthly consumption: one row per region, one column per month
-- Uses CASE WHEN inside SUM to route values to the correct column
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 1
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS jan_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 2
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS feb_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 3
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS mar_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-04-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

Each CASE WHEN expression routes values to the correct month column: when the month is January, the daily_kw is summed into jan_kw; when it is February, into feb_kw. Values from other months contribute NULL (the CASE has no ELSE), and SUM ignores NULLs, so each column accumulates only the values for its month. This pattern is portable — it works in every SQL database — but it requires hardcoding the column names. If the number of pivot values is unknown or changes frequently (e.g., pivoting by customer_type where new types might appear), the conditional approach requires query modification. For a fixed set of months, it is the standard approach.
Spark SQL PIVOT

-- Spark SQL native PIVOT syntax (not available in PostgreSQL)
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT region,
         EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date) AS mth,
         daily_kw
  FROM   silver_meter_readings
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-04-01'
)
PIVOT (
  SUM(daily_kw)
  FOR mth IN (1 AS jan_kw, 2 AS feb_kw, 3 AS mar_kw)
)
ORDER BY region;

Spark SQL's PIVOT clause is syntactically cleaner: you specify the aggregate function, the pivot column, and the values to pivot into columns. The result is identical to the conditional aggregation approach, but the intent is explicit rather than encoded in CASE WHEN expressions. The FOR ... IN clause lists the values that become columns — just as the conditional approach requires hardcoded months. Dynamic pivoting (where the pivot values come from the data) requires dynamic SQL generation in both PostgreSQL and Spark, which is covered in Module 5 (lesson 9). At HydroAlpes, the Spark pipeline uses PIVOT for reports that will be exported to Excel, and the PostgreSQL warehouse uses the conditional aggregation approach for the same reports served to dashboards.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Pivoting should be the last step in a query, applied to a small aggregated result set — not to millions of raw rows. First aggregate the data to the desired grain (GROUP BY region, month), then pivot the aggregated result. Pivoting raw data creates one column per pivot value per detail row, which is both slow and difficult to consume. At HydroAlpes, the pivot always operates on fewer than 1,000 rows (10 regions × 12 months × a few customer types) — the aggregation reduces millions of readings to a small summary before pivoting reshapes it.

Unpivoting is the reverse of pivoting: it transforms columns into rows. A table with columns jan_kw, feb_kw, mar_kw (wide format) becomes a table with columns month and kw (long format). Unpivoting is needed when data arrives from external sources in wide format — spreadsheets, reporting systems, and partner APIs that deliver one column per time period instead of one row per time period. HydroAlpes receives quarterly benchmark prices from the EPEX energy exchange in a CSV with columns region, q1_price, q2_price, q3_price, q4_price. The pipeline must unpivot these into rows (region, quarter, price) before they can be joined with the daily price data.
LATERAL with VALUES

-- Unpivot quarterly benchmark prices from wide to long format
-- LATERAL with VALUES creates rows from column values
SELECT bp.region,
       u.quarter,
       u.price_eur_mwh
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide AS bp
  CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    VALUES
      ('Q1', bp.q1_price),
      ('Q2', bp.q2_price),
      ('Q3', bp.q3_price),
      ('Q4', bp.q4_price)
  ) AS u(quarter, price_eur_mwh)
WHERE  u.price_eur_mwh IS NOT NULL  -- skip quarters with no data
ORDER BY bp.region, u.quarter;

region      | quarter | price_eur_mwh
------------+---------+--------------
BERN-NORTH  | Q1      |        48.20
BERN-NORTH  | Q2      |        42.10
BERN-NORTH  | Q3      |        38.80
BERN-NORTH  | Q4      |        52.40
BERN-SOUTH  | Q1      |        47.90
...

CROSS JOIN LATERAL with a VALUES clause creates multiple rows from each input row. For each region in the wide table, the LATERAL subquery produces four rows — one per quarter — by explicitly listing the (quarter_name, column_value) pairs. The WHERE clause filters out NULLs for quarters where benchmark data is not yet available. This pattern is the standard unpivoting approach in PostgreSQL. It is explicit, readable, and works for any number of columns. The trade-off is that the VALUES list must enumerate every column being unpivoted — if the wide table has 12 monthly columns, the VALUES list has 12 entries.
UNION ALL Alternative

-- Alternative: UNION ALL with one SELECT per source column
-- More verbose but works in every SQL dialect
SELECT region, 'Q1' AS quarter, q1_price AS price_eur_mwh
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q1_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q2', q2_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q2_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q3', q3_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q3_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q4', q4_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q4_price IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY region, quarter;

The UNION ALL approach produces the same result but scans the table once per column being unpivoted — four scans for four quarters. The LATERAL approach scans once and produces multiple rows per input row, making it more efficient for tables with many columns or many rows. At HydroAlpes, the LATERAL approach is the standard for unpivoting because the benchmark prices table has 10 regions × 4 quarters = 40 rows, making either approach fast, but the LATERAL version is more maintainable when new columns are added. In Spark SQL, the equivalent operation uses the stack() function or the UNPIVOT clause (Spark 3.4+), which provides cleaner syntax for the same transformation.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Unpivoting is an ingestion-time operation: convert wide-format external data to long format as early as possible in the pipeline (typically at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary). Once in long format, the data integrates naturally with GROUP BY, window functions, and joins — all of which expect one observation per row. Storing data in wide format and unpivoting at query time forces every consumer to handle the transformation, which is fragile and inconsistent. At HydroAlpes, the benchmark prices are unpivoted during Silver ingestion and stored in long format permanently.

The pivoting lesson used CASE WHEN inside SUM to route values to different columns. This pattern — conditional aggregation — is one of the most common in analytical SQL, but the CASE WHEN syntax is verbose and difficult to read when multiple conditions are involved. PostgreSQL (and the SQL:2003 standard) provides the FILTER clause as a cleaner alternative: SUM(daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') replaces SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' THEN daily_kw END). Both produce the same result, but FILTER is more readable, more maintainable, and often more efficient because the database can optimize the filter separately from the aggregation.
FILTER vs CASE WHEN

-- CASE WHEN approach (portable but verbose)
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS residential_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS commercial_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS industrial_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region;

-- FILTER approach (cleaner, PostgreSQL 9.4+)
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') AS residential_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL')  AS commercial_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL')  AS industrial_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region;

Both queries produce identical results. The FILTER version is more readable because each aggregate clearly states its condition as a subordinate clause rather than embedding it inside a CASE expression. The FILTER clause can be used with any aggregate function: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE condition), AVG(col) FILTER (WHERE condition), MIN, MAX, and even array_agg. At HydroAlpes, the team style guide mandates FILTER over CASE WHEN for conditional aggregation in all new PostgreSQL queries, reserving CASE WHEN for Spark SQL queries where FILTER is not yet supported.
Multi-Metric Reports in One Pass

-- Comprehensive regional report: multiple metrics across customer types
-- Single pass over the data, single GROUP BY, multiple FILTER conditions
SELECT mr.region,
       COUNT(*) AS total_readings,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE mr.daily_kw > 100) AS high_usage_readings,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS total_meters,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL') AS industrial_meters,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') AS residential_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS overall_avg,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL') AS industrial_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

Eight distinct metrics computed in a single GROUP BY: total readings, high-usage readings (over 100 kW), total distinct meters, industrial meters only, total consumption, residential consumption only, overall average, and industrial average. Without FILTER, this query would require eight CASE WHEN expressions — each adding 2–3 lines of verbose syntax. With FILTER, each metric is a concise, self-documenting expression. The database scans the data once, computes all eight metrics in a single aggregation pass, and produces the complete regional report. This single-pass efficiency is the reason FILTER-based conditional aggregation has become the preferred pattern for multi-metric dashboards.
Dialect Availability

FILTER is part of the SQL:2003 standard and is supported in PostgreSQL (9.4+), SQLite, and DuckDB. It is not yet supported in Spark SQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, or SQL Server — these databases require the CASE WHEN approach. At HydroAlpes, the PostgreSQL warehouse queries use FILTER, and the equivalent Spark SQL pipelines use CASE WHEN. The team maintains a translation guide that maps FILTER expressions to their CASE WHEN equivalents for cross-engine compatibility. When FILTER is not available, CASE WHEN inside aggregates is the universal fallback — verbose but functionally identical.

blocks.title: One scan, many metrics
blocks.content: The power of conditional aggregation — whether expressed with FILTER or CASE WHEN — is that it computes arbitrarily many conditional metrics in a single pass over the data. Without it, each conditional metric requires a separate query or a join with a filtered subquery. Ten metrics on a 37.2-million-row table as ten separate queries means scanning 372 million rows total. The same ten metrics with conditional aggregation scans 37.2 million rows once. The 10x reduction in I/O is the production reason to master this pattern.

The previous lessons taught individual analytical techniques: advanced frames, percentile functions, GROUPING SETS, pivoting, and FILTER. This lesson combines them into the production patterns that HydroAlpes' analytical queries use daily. Each pattern solves a specific reporting or analytical problem that appears repeatedly across energy data, financial data, and operational data. The patterns are composable — you can combine FILTER with GROUPING SETS, or window functions with pivoting, to create queries that would require dozens of lines of application code if implemented outside SQL.
Pattern 1: Cohort Analysis with NTILE

-- Classify meters into consumption deciles and analyze each cohort
WITH meter_totals AS (
  SELECT meter_id, region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS jan_total
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY meter_id, region
),
cohorted AS (
  SELECT *, NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY jan_total DESC) AS decile
  FROM   meter_totals
)
SELECT decile,
       COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
       SUM(jan_total) AS cohort_total_kw,
       ROUND(SUM(jan_total) / SUM(SUM(jan_total)) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_total,
       MIN(jan_total) AS min_kw,
       MAX(jan_total) AS max_kw
FROM   cohorted
GROUP BY decile
ORDER BY decile;

The top decile (120,000 meters, 10% of the fleet) accounts for 74.9% of total consumption. The bottom decile accounts for 0.3%. This Pareto analysis reveals the extreme concentration of energy usage in a small fraction of the meter fleet — a pattern that shapes billing strategy, grid capacity planning, and demand management programs. The query combines NTILE (lesson 3), a window function for percent-of-total (SUM OVER()), and GROUP BY to produce a complete cohort analysis in a single composable statement. At HydroAlpes, this cohort analysis runs monthly and feeds the capacity planning dashboard.
Pattern 2: Period-Over-Period Comparison with FILTER

-- Compare January 2025 vs January 2024 by region, single query
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
                                  AND mr.date <  '2025-02-01') AS jan_2025_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2024-01-01'
                                  AND mr.date <  '2024-02-01') AS jan_2024_kw,
       ROUND((
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01') /
         NULLIF(SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2024-01-01' AND mr.date < '2024-02-01'), 0)
         - 1) * 100, 1
       ) AS yoy_change_pct
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  (mr.date >= '2024-01-01' AND mr.date < '2024-02-01')
   OR  (mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01')
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

A single query computes January 2025 totals, January 2024 totals, and the year-over-year change percentage for each region. The FILTER clauses route readings from different years into separate columns, and the arithmetic computes the percentage change. NULLIF prevents division by zero for regions that had no data in January 2024 (the newly added sub-regions). This pattern replaces a self-join (joining the table with itself on region, with different date filters on each side) with a more efficient and readable conditional aggregation. At HydroAlpes, this year-over-year pattern appears in the executive dashboard, the regulatory filing, and the demand forecast input.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Combine GROUPING SETS with FILTER for the most powerful single-pass reports. GROUPING SETS provides multi-level subtotals (by region, by customer type, grand total). FILTER provides conditional metrics (residential vs industrial, current year vs prior year). Together, they produce a complete multi-dimensional, multi-metric report in a single scan — replacing what would otherwise be a matrix of separate queries or a complex application-level merge.

This module expanded your analytical SQL toolkit from the junior-level foundations to mid-level production patterns: advanced frame specifications for gap-aware moving averages, NTILE and percentile functions for statistical classification, GROUPING SETS with ROLLUP and CUBE for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting for format transformation, and FILTER for conditional aggregation. This final lesson synthesizes these techniques into the five core analytical queries that HydroAlpes' team maintains as reusable, version-controlled SQL files — the analytical query library that powers dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts.
The Five Core Queries

rows: Executive Summary
GROUPING SETS with FILTER for multi-level, multi-metric report
CEO dashboard, board reporting
Monthly
Regulatory Filing
CUBE with all dimension combinations, GROUPING() for level labels
Swiss ELCOM / Austrian E-Control
Quarterly
Capacity Forecast Input
RANGE-frame moving averages, PERCENTILE_CONT for demand profiles
Grid planning team
Weekly
Meter Classification
NTILE for tier assignment, period-over-period FILTER for trend
Billing and pricing team
Monthly
Anomaly Detection
EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW neighbor comparison, z-score with window functions
Data quality framework (Module 9)
Daily

blocks.content: Query Library Management

Each core query is a .sql file in HydroAlpes' Git repository, version-controlled alongside the pipeline code. The file includes a header comment block with the query's purpose, consumer, schedule, expected output schema, and the most recent performance baseline (execution time and row count). Changes to core queries go through the same pull request review as pipeline code changes: the reviewer checks the EXPLAIN output, verifies the GROUPING SETS and FILTER logic, and confirms that the output schema has not changed in a way that breaks downstream consumers. This process prevents the common failure mode where an analyst modifies a shared query and silently changes the metric definitions that dashboards depend on.

The query library is the bridge between the analytical techniques in this module and the production infrastructure in Modules 6–10. Module 6 (Testing) covers how to write assertion tests for each query's output. Module 7 (Deployment) covers how to promote query changes through environments. Module 8 (Performance) covers how to index and tune the tables these queries run against. Module 9 (Data Quality) covers the anomaly detection query in production depth. And Module 10 (Operations) covers the SLA governance that ensures these queries complete on time every day, every week, and every quarter.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 4, you will shift from querying data to modeling data. Star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, temporal tables, and the trade-offs between normalization and denormalization determine how the tables that your analytical queries read are designed. The analytical techniques from this module work best when the underlying data model is intentionally designed for the queries it serves — and Module 4 teaches you to design those models.

chapters.title: Data Modeling for Pipelines
chapters.description: Understand star and snowflake schemas, master slowly changing dimension patterns, design temporal tables, and choose the right modeling approach for different pipeline consumers.

blocks.content: At junior level, HydroAlpes had one team, two engineers, and four tables. Lena designed the schema, Markus wrote the queries, and every question could be answered with a straightforward join across a simple normalized model. At mid-level, HydroAlpes has three teams — data engineering, analytics, and ML — totaling 12 people, all querying the same data with different needs. The analytics team wants denormalized wide tables they can query without joins. The ML team wants feature tables with historical snapshots at arbitrary timestamps. The engineering team wants normalized tables with referential integrity for pipeline correctness. These conflicting requirements cannot be served by one table design. Data modeling is the practice of designing tables for their consumers — and at mid-level scale, different consumers need different models of the same underlying data.
The Modeling Problem

HydroAlpes' billing query joins meter_readings with meters, substations, and regions. The analytics team runs this four-table join 200 times per day across various dashboards. Each execution scans the meters, substations, and regions tables — tables that rarely change — and re-joins them with readings. The ML team needs to know what customer_type each meter had at the time of each reading, not just its current type — because some meters changed from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL mid-year, and the ML model must use the type that was effective when the reading was recorded. The engineering team needs to add a new sub-region without breaking any downstream query. Each requirement implies a different table design, and the modeling decisions made at the Silver and Gold layers determine whether those requirements can be met efficiently.
Layers and Models

The Bronze-Silver-Gold architecture from the junior track is a layering strategy, not a modeling strategy. Bronze stores raw ingested data. Silver stores validated, conformed data. Gold stores consumer-ready analytical data. But within each layer, many modeling choices remain: should Silver be fully normalized (one table per entity, no redundancy) or partially denormalized (common joins pre-computed)? Should Gold be a star schema (fact table surrounded by dimension tables) or a wide denormalized table (everything in one table)? Should dimensions be current-state only or historized with slowly changing dimension patterns? This module answers these questions with concrete patterns and the trade-offs that determine which pattern fits each situation.

blocks.title: HydroAlpes modeling layers and consumer patterns
blocks.content:
Bronze (raw)          Silver (validated)        Gold (consumer-ready)
┌────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────────────┐
│ CSV/JSON   │  ──▶  │ Normalized     │  ──▶  │ Star schema (analytics)  │
│ from SCADA │       │ tables with    │       │ Wide tables (ML)         │
│ and meters │       │ constraints    │       │ Summary tables (billing) │
└────────────┘       └────────────────┘       └──────────────────────────┘
                     │                         │
                     │ 3NF, referential         │ Star schema, SCD Type 2,
                     │ integrity, one            │ denormalized wide tables,
                     │ source of truth           │ temporal snapshots
                     │                         │
                     └── Engineering team ──────── Analytics + ML + Billing
blocks.caption: Each layer uses a different modeling approach. Silver is normalized for correctness. Gold is modeled per consumer: star schema for dashboards, wide tables for ML, and summary tables for billing. The modeling decisions at each layer determine query performance and consumer satisfaction.

blocks.title: Modeling is a design decision, not a technical constraint
blocks.content: There is no single correct data model. A star schema is optimal for dashboard queries but suboptimal for ML feature engineering. A normalized model is optimal for data integrity but suboptimal for analytical query performance. The mid-level skill is choosing the right model for each consumer and layer — and implementing the pipeline that transforms between them. This module teaches the patterns; you choose which to apply based on who consumes the data and what questions they ask.

sections.title: Fact Tables and Dimension Tables — Deep Dive

blocks.content: The junior track introduced fact tables (events, measurements) and dimension tables (descriptive context). At mid-level, the distinction needs precision because the fact table's properties determine how it can be aggregated. An additive fact can be summed across any dimension: daily_kw is additive — you can sum it by region, by customer type, by date, or by any combination, and the total is meaningful. A semi-additive fact can be summed across some dimensions but not others: reservoir_level_m can be summed across reservoirs (total system water) but not across dates (summing today's level with yesterday's level is meaningless). A non-additive fact cannot be summed at all: temperature_c is neither additive nor semi-additive — the 'sum of temperatures' is not a meaningful metric. Correctly classifying facts prevents aggregation errors that produce plausible but wrong numbers.
Additive Facts

-- Additive fact: daily_kw can be summed across any dimension
-- Regional total, monthly total, customer-type total — all meaningful
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw   -- valid: additive across region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region;

SELECT mr.date,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS daily_total  -- valid: additive across date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
GROUP BY mr.date
ORDER BY mr.date;

Both aggregations are valid because daily_kw is additive. The regional total represents total consumption across all meters in BERN-NORTH for January. The daily total represents total consumption across all BERN-NORTH meters on each day. You can sum daily_kw across any combination of dimensions, and the result has a clear business meaning. Most revenue, quantity, and consumption metrics are additive. At HydroAlpes, daily_kw, generation_kw (turbine output), and water_flow_m3s (when aggregated over time as volume) are all additive facts that can be summed freely.
Semi-Additive Facts

-- Semi-additive fact: reservoir_level_m can be summed across reservoirs
-- but NOT across dates
SELECT rl.date,
       SUM(rl.level_m)  AS system_water_level  -- VALID: sum across reservoirs
FROM   silver.reservoir_levels AS rl
WHERE  rl.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY rl.date;

-- WRONG: summing levels across dates produces nonsense
-- SELECT SUM(level_m) FROM reservoir_levels WHERE reservoir_id = 'RES-01'
-- This sums Monday's level + Tuesday's level + ... = meaningless number
-- Correct: AVG(level_m) or the level on a specific date

Reservoir levels are balances — point-in-time values, not cumulative flows. Summing the levels of all 18 reservoirs on January 15 gives the total system water level on that day — a meaningful metric for grid capacity. But summing a single reservoir's levels across 31 days gives an arbitrary number with no physical meaning. The correct across-date aggregation for a semi-additive fact is AVG (average level over the period) or the value at a specific point in time (end-of-month level). Account balances, inventory quantities, and population counts are all semi-additive: meaningful when summed across entities, meaningless when summed across time. At HydroAlpes, reservoir levels and meter account balances are marked as semi-additive in the data dictionary, and the Gold-layer views expose only valid aggregations.
The Grain Decision

A fact table's grain is the level of detail each row represents — one reading per meter per day, one telemetry point per turbine per minute, one price per market zone per hour. The grain determines which questions the table can answer and which aggregations are valid. HydroAlpes' meter_readings table has a daily grain (one row per meter per day). This means you can aggregate daily consumption into weekly, monthly, or annual totals. You cannot drill down into hourly consumption because the data does not exist at that granularity. Choosing the grain is the most consequential modeling decision: too coarse and the data cannot answer fine-grained questions; too fine and the table grows unmanageably large. At HydroAlpes, the daily grain for meter_readings was chosen because the billing system operates on daily totals, and sub-daily readings (available from smart meters) are aggregated into daily sums at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary.

blocks.title: Mixing grains in one fact table is a modeling error
blocks.content: If meter_readings contains both daily readings (one row per meter per day) and monthly summaries (one row per meter per month) in the same table, a SUM(daily_kw) query that does not filter by grain type will double-count: the monthly summary includes the same consumption that the daily rows represent. Each fact table must have exactly one grain. If you need both daily and monthly data, use separate tables (silver.meter_readings_daily and gold.meter_readings_monthly) with clear naming that communicates the grain.

A star schema places a fact table at the center, surrounded by dimension tables connected by foreign keys. Each dimension table is denormalized — it contains all descriptive attributes for one business entity in a single table, without sub-tables. The meters dimension contains meter_id, customer_type, region, substation_name, installation_date, and is_active — all in one table, even though a fully normalized model would separate regions and substations into their own tables. This denormalization eliminates the multi-table joins that slow down analytical queries: instead of joining readings with meters, then meters with substations, then substations with regions, the analyst joins readings with the denormalized meters dimension in a single join.
The HydroAlpes Gold Star Schema

-- Gold-layer star schema: fact table + denormalized dimension tables

-- Dimension: meters (denormalized — includes substation and region attributes)
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_meters (
    meter_key          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,   -- surrogate key
    meter_id           VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- natural key
    customer_type      VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    region             VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    region_name        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, -- from regions table
    country            VARCHAR(5)  NOT NULL,  -- from regions table
    substation_id      VARCHAR(20),
    substation_name    VARCHAR(100),           -- from substations table
    substation_capacity_mw INTEGER,            -- from substations table
    installation_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    is_active          BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    effective_from     DATE NOT NULL,
    effective_to       DATE                    -- NULL = current version
);

-- Fact: daily meter readings
CREATE TABLE gold.fact_meter_readings (
    reading_key        BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    meter_key          INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES gold.dim_meters(meter_key),
    date_key           DATE NOT NULL,
    daily_kw           DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
    daily_mwh          DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL  -- pre-computed unit conversion
);

-- Dimension: date (calendar dimension for time-based analysis)
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_date (
    date_key           DATE PRIMARY KEY,
    year               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    quarter            INTEGER NOT NULL,
    month              INTEGER NOT NULL,
    week_of_year       INTEGER NOT NULL,
    day_of_week        INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_weekend         BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    is_swiss_holiday   BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    is_austrian_holiday BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    fiscal_quarter     INTEGER NOT NULL
);

Three tables replace the junior-level six-table Silver model. The dim_meters dimension absorbs attributes from meters, substations, and regions into a single denormalized table with a surrogate key (meter_key). The fact_meter_readings table stores one row per reading, linking to dim_meters via the surrogate key. The dim_date table is a calendar dimension that lets analysts filter and group by fiscal quarter, weekend/holiday status, and other calendar attributes without computing them in every query. The analyst's query becomes: SELECT d.region_name, SUM(f.daily_mwh) FROM fact_meter_readings f JOIN dim_meters d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key JOIN dim_date dt ON f.date_key = dt.date_key WHERE dt.fiscal_quarter = 1 GROUP BY d.region_name — one join per dimension, no multi-hop joins.
Surrogate Keys

The dim_meters table uses a surrogate key (meter_key, an auto-incrementing integer) instead of the natural key (meter_id, a business identifier). Surrogate keys solve two problems. First, they are compact: a 4-byte integer is smaller than a 20-character VARCHAR, and the fact table stores billions of rows — each byte saved per row means gigabytes saved in total. Second, they support slowly changing dimensions (lessons 5–7): when a meter's customer_type changes, a new dim_meters row is created with a new meter_key but the same meter_id. The fact table links each reading to the correct dimension version via the surrogate key. The natural key (meter_id) appears in the dimension table for human readability but is not used for joins.
The Date Dimension

A date dimension is a pre-computed calendar table that stores one row per calendar date with attributes like year, quarter, month, day of week, and holiday flags. Analysts can filter and group by these attributes without calling EXTRACT() or DATE_TRUNC() in every query. At HydroAlpes, the date dimension includes both Swiss and Austrian holiday flags (because the two countries have different public holidays) and the fiscal quarter mapping (which does not align with calendar quarters for regulatory reporting). The date dimension is populated once for a 10-year range and never changes — it is the most stable table in the entire data model.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Populate the date dimension for 10 years in advance using generate_series: INSERT INTO gold.dim_date SELECT d::DATE, EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d), EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM d), ... FROM generate_series('2020-01-01'::DATE, '2030-12-31'::DATE, '1 day') AS d. This takes seconds and ensures the dimension is never missing a date. At HydroAlpes, the date dimension was populated once during initial deployment and has not been modified since — except to update holiday flags when the Swiss government announces new public holidays.

A star schema denormalizes dimensions: the meters dimension includes substation_name, region_name, and country — attributes that belong to related entities. A snowflake schema normalizes dimensions: the meters dimension contains only meter-specific attributes and a foreign key to a substations dimension, which in turn contains a foreign key to a regions dimension. The snowflake preserves normalization (no redundancy, single source of truth for each attribute) at the cost of additional joins. The star eliminates those joins at the cost of redundancy (substation_name is repeated in every meter row that belongs to the same substation). The choice between them depends on the consumer's query patterns and the team's tolerance for redundancy.
Snowflake Schema Structure

-- Snowflake schema: normalized dimensions with sub-dimensions
-- Same data as the star schema, but split into separate tables

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_regions (
    region_key    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    region_id     VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    region_name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    country       VARCHAR(5) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_substations (
    substation_key    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    substation_id     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    substation_name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    capacity_mw       INTEGER NOT NULL,
    region_key        INTEGER REFERENCES gold.dim_regions(region_key)
);

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_meters_snowflake (
    meter_key         SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    meter_id          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    customer_type     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    installation_date DATE NOT NULL,
    is_active         BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    substation_key    INTEGER REFERENCES gold.dim_substations(substation_key)
);

The snowflake schema separates regions, substations, and meters into distinct tables. To get a meter's region name, you join meters → substations → regions — two hops instead of zero in the star schema. The normalized structure means that if Zurich's region_name changes from 'Zurich Canton' to 'Canton of Zurich,' you update one row in dim_regions. In the star schema, you would update every meter row in BERN-NORTH — potentially hundreds of thousands of rows. The normalization advantage is real for attributes that change; the join cost is real for every query that needs those attributes.
Star vs Snowflake — The Decision

rows.left: Fewer joins: fact → dimension in one hop
rows.right: More joins: fact → dimension → sub-dimension

rows.left: Redundant attributes: substation_name repeated per meter
rows.right: No redundancy: each attribute stored once

rows.left: Faster queries: fewer joins = less CPU, memory, and I/O
rows.right: Slower queries: additional joins add latency

rows.left: Harder updates: attribute changes require updating many rows
rows.right: Easier updates: attribute changes update one row

rows.left: Simpler SQL for analysts: no multi-hop joins needed
rows.right: More complex SQL: analysts must know the join path

rows.left: Best for: read-heavy analytical workloads, dashboards
rows.right: Best for: write-heavy workloads, frequently changing attributes

blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the Gold layer uses a star schema because the primary consumers are analysts running read-heavy dashboard queries, and the dimension attributes change infrequently (substations are renamed once a decade, regions never). The Silver layer uses a snowflake-like normalized model because it is the engineering team's source of truth, and normalization prevents the update anomalies that could propagate incorrect data into Gold. This dual-model approach — normalized Silver for correctness, denormalized Gold for performance — is the standard architecture at HydroAlpes and the dominant pattern in modern data warehousing.

blocks.title: The normalization spectrum is a trade-off, not a judgment
blocks.content: Neither full normalization nor full denormalization is universally correct. Normalization optimizes for write correctness and storage efficiency. Denormalization optimizes for read performance and query simplicity. Production data platforms use both: normalized models in the transformation layer (Silver) and denormalized models in the consumption layer (Gold). The decision is driven by the consumer: if the consumer is a pipeline that writes frequently, normalize. If the consumer is a dashboard that reads frequently, denormalize.

Dimension attributes change over time. A meter's customer_type changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL when a building is converted to office space. A substation's name changes when the operator rebrands. A region's regulatory classification changes when a new energy law takes effect. These changes create a fundamental problem for the dimension table: should the old value be preserved (maintaining history) or overwritten (keeping only the current state)? The answer depends on whether historical queries need the old value. Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD) patterns classify these changes into types based on how the dimension table handles them. Type 1 — the simplest — overwrites the old value, losing history.
Type 1: Overwrite

-- Type 1 SCD: overwrite the old value with the new one
-- The substation's name changed from 'Alpine Grid B-4' to 'Alpine Hub B-4'
-- We simply UPDATE the dimension row — no history preserved

UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    substation_name = 'Alpine Hub B-4'
WHERE  substation_name = 'Alpine Grid B-4';

The UPDATE replaces 'Alpine Grid B-4' with 'Alpine Hub B-4' in every meter row that references the substation. After the update, every query — historical or current — returns the new name.
