If someone runs the January billing report six months later, it shows 'Alpine Hub B-4' even though the name was 'Alpine Grid B-4' in January. The history is lost. Type 1 is appropriate when the change is a correction (the old value was wrong and should not appear in any report) or when the attribute is cosmetic and history has no business value (name changes, formatting corrections, spelling fixes).
When Type 1 Is Appropriate

Type 1 is the right choice when historical accuracy of the changed attribute does not matter for any business process. At HydroAlpes, substation names are cosmetic labels — the billing system uses substation_id, not the name. Changing the name does not affect any calculation, any regulatory report, or any analytical metric. The name is for human readability only. Similarly, correcting a misspelled region_name from 'Inersbruck' to 'Innsbruck' is a Type 1 change — the old spelling was wrong and should be retroactively corrected everywhere. The decision criterion: would a historical report showing the old value mislead anyone? If yes, use Type 1 to fix it. If the old value was correct at the time, preserve it with Type 2 (next lesson).
Type 1 in the Pipeline

-- Pipeline pattern: Type 1 merge from staging to Gold dimension
-- Uses INSERT ON CONFLICT for upsert behavior
INSERT INTO gold.dim_meters (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name,
                             country, substation_id, substation_name,
                             substation_capacity_mw, installation_date, is_active)
SELECT s.meter_id, s.customer_type, s.region, s.region_name,
       s.country, s.substation_id, s.substation_name,
       s.substation_capacity_mw, s.installation_date, s.is_active
FROM   staging.meters_latest AS s
ON CONFLICT (meter_id) DO UPDATE
SET    substation_name         = EXCLUDED.substation_name,
       substation_capacity_mw  = EXCLUDED.substation_capacity_mw,
       region_name             = EXCLUDED.region_name,
       is_active               = EXCLUDED.is_active;

The INSERT ON CONFLICT pattern implements Type 1 as an idempotent pipeline operation. New meters are inserted. Existing meters (matched on meter_id) have their Type 1 attributes overwritten with the latest values from staging. Attributes not listed in the DO UPDATE SET clause (like customer_type and installation_date) are not modified — they are either immutable or handled by a different SCD type. At HydroAlpes, this pipeline runs daily at 05:15, after the staging table is populated from the meter management system. The entire dimension refresh takes approximately 4 seconds for 1.2 million meters.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Not all attributes in a dimension table need the same SCD type. A meter's substation_name might be Type 1 (overwrite — cosmetic) while its customer_type is Type 2 (historize — billing impact). Design the merge pipeline to apply the correct type per attribute: Type 1 attributes go in the ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE clause, and Type 2 attributes trigger a new row insertion (next lesson). Mixing SCD types in one table is the norm, not the exception.

In March 2025, meter CH-BERN-00142 was reclassified from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL because the building was converted to office space. The billing team needs to bill January and February readings at the residential rate (the rate effective when the readings were recorded) and March readings at the commercial rate. If the dimension uses Type 1 (overwrite), the customer_type is now COMMERCIAL everywhere — and January's billing retroactively applies the commercial rate, overcharging the customer. Type 2 SCD solves this by inserting a new dimension row with the new value and an effective date range, while keeping the old row with the old value. Each fact row links to the dimension version that was effective when the fact was recorded.
Type 2: Insert New Row

-- Type 2 SCD: two rows for the same meter, each with an effective period
-- The OLD row represents RESIDENTIAL (Jan 1 to Feb 28)
-- The NEW row represents COMMERCIAL (Mar 1 to NULL = current)

SELECT meter_key, meter_id, customer_type, effective_from, effective_to
FROM   gold.dim_meters
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
ORDER BY effective_from;

Two rows, two surrogate keys, one natural key. The first row (meter_key 14201) represents the meter as RESIDENTIAL from its installation date through February 28, 2025. The second row (meter_key 1214202) represents the same meter as COMMERCIAL from March 1, 2025 onward (effective_to = NULL means 'current'). The fact table links each reading to the correct dimension version: January readings link to meter_key 14201 (RESIDENTIAL), and March readings link to meter_key 1214202 (COMMERCIAL). The billing query automatically applies the correct rate because the join uses the surrogate key, which already encodes the temporal relationship.
The Point-in-Time Join

-- Join each reading with the dimension version effective at the reading date
-- This is the standard Type 2 query pattern
SELECT f.date_key,
       f.daily_kw,
       d.meter_id,
       d.customer_type   -- RESIDENTIAL for Jan/Feb, COMMERCIAL for Mar
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d
    ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  d.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
ORDER BY f.date_key;

If the fact table is loaded correctly — each reading linked to the dimension version effective at the reading date — the join automatically returns the correct customer_type per reading. January readings return RESIDENTIAL. March readings return COMMERCIAL. No date-range logic is needed in the query because the surrogate key encodes the temporal relationship. This is the power of Type 2 SCD: the complexity of temporal versioning is handled once (during fact loading) and the queries remain simple joins on the surrogate key. The alternative — storing the natural key in the fact table and joining with a date-range condition — is more complex, slower (range joins are less efficient than equality joins), and error-prone.
Implementing the Type 2 Pipeline

-- Type 2 pipeline: detect changes and insert new versions
-- Step 1: Close the old version (set effective_to)
UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    effective_to = '2025-02-28'
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  effective_to IS NULL;

-- Step 2: Insert the new version
INSERT INTO gold.dim_meters
    (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name, country,
     substation_id, substation_name, substation_capacity_mw,
     installation_date, is_active, effective_from, effective_to)
VALUES
    ('CH-BERN-00142', 'COMMERCIAL', 'BERN-NORTH', 'Bern North',
     'CH', 'SUB-BERN-04', 'Alpine Hub B-4', 120,
     '2018-11-02', TRUE, '2025-03-01', NULL);

The pipeline detects that CH-BERN-00142's customer_type changed from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL. It closes the old version by setting effective_to to the day before the change, then inserts a new version with effective_from set to the change date and effective_to set to NULL (current). In production, this logic is automated: the pipeline compares the staging table against the current dimension, identifies changed attributes, and processes each change through the close-old/insert-new pattern. At HydroAlpes, the Type 2 pipeline runs within the same transaction as the Type 1 updates, ensuring that the dimension is always in a consistent state — either fully updated or fully rolled back.

blocks.title: Type 2 dimensions grow over time
blocks.content: Every attribute change creates a new row. If 10% of meters change customer_type each year, the dim_meters table grows by 120,000 rows per year — a manageable increase. But if a rapidly changing attribute (like is_active, which toggles frequently) is tracked as Type 2, the dimension can grow dramatically. At HydroAlpes, only customer_type and region are Type 2 (business-critical, rarely changing). Attributes like is_active are Type 1 (overwrite) because they change frequently and historical status is tracked in a separate operational table, not in the dimension.

sections.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions — Type 3 and Hybrids

blocks.content: Type 1 preserves no history. Type 2 preserves complete history. Type 3 preserves limited history — specifically, the current value and the previous value — by adding a column for the prior value rather than inserting a new row. When meter CH-BERN-00142 changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL, a Type 3 implementation sets customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL' and previous_customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' in the same row. The dimension table does not grow (no new rows), but it retains enough history to answer 'what was the previous value?' without supporting arbitrary point-in-time queries.
Type 3: Add Column for Previous Value

-- Type 3 SCD: current and previous values in the same row
ALTER TABLE gold.dim_meters
  ADD COLUMN previous_customer_type VARCHAR(20),
  ADD COLUMN customer_type_changed_date DATE;

-- When a change occurs: shift current to previous, update current
UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    previous_customer_type     = customer_type,
       customer_type              = 'COMMERCIAL',
       customer_type_changed_date = '2025-03-01'
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142';

meter_id       | customer_type | previous_customer_type | changed_date
---------------+---------------+------------------------+-------------
CH-BERN-00142  | COMMERCIAL    | RESIDENTIAL            | 2025-03-01

One row, no new surrogate keys, no effective date ranges. The current type is COMMERCIAL, the previous type is RESIDENTIAL, and the change date records when the transition happened. This is simpler than Type 2 — no row versioning, no point-in-time joins — but it supports only one level of history. If the meter changes again (COMMERCIAL → INDUSTRIAL), the previous_customer_type becomes COMMERCIAL and the original RESIDENTIAL is lost. Type 3 is appropriate when the business needs to know only the immediately prior value, not the full history.
Hybrid Approaches

Production dimension tables rarely use a single SCD type for all attributes. HydroAlpes' dim_meters uses a hybrid approach: customer_type is Type 2 (full history required for billing accuracy), substation_name is Type 1 (cosmetic, no history needed), and region is Type 3 (the previous region is useful for migration analysis, but the full history of region changes is not). The pipeline processes each attribute according to its assigned type: Type 1 attributes are overwritten, Type 2 attributes trigger close-old/insert-new, and Type 3 attributes shift current to previous. This per-attribute typing is the standard in production data warehousing and is documented in HydroAlpes' data dictionary alongside each column definition.
SCD Type Selection Guide

rows: Type 1
Overwrite with new value
None — old value is lost
Corrections, cosmetic changes, attributes with no historical relevance
Type 2
Insert new row, close old row
Complete — every version preserved with effective dates
Attributes where historical accuracy affects business logic (billing, regulation)
Type 3
Add column for previous value
Limited — only current and one prior value
Attributes where only the most recent change matters (migration tracking)
Hybrid
Different types per attribute in the same table
Varies by attribute
Production reality — different attributes have different history requirements

blocks.title: Type 2 is the default for anything that affects money or regulation
blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the rule is simple: if an attribute's historical value affects a financial calculation (billing, pricing, revenue allocation) or a regulatory report (filed quarterly with the Swiss and Austrian energy regulators), it must be Type 2. Everything else is Type 1 unless a specific business case for limited history (Type 3) exists. This conservative default prevents the scenario where a retroactive analysis produces wrong results because historical dimension values were overwritten.

The Swiss energy regulator can request that HydroAlpes reproduce any billing report exactly as it would have appeared on the date it was originally generated. If the January billing report was generated on February 3 and the regulator asks for a copy on August 15, HydroAlpes must reproduce the report using the data and dimension values that existed on February 3 — not the current values. This means every dimension change since February 3 must be invisible, and every fact that arrived after February 3 must be excluded. SCD Type 2 handles the dimension side (effective dates), but the fact side also needs temporal tracking: when was each reading loaded into the warehouse? This is the bi-temporal modeling challenge: tracking both the valid time (when the reading was measured) and the transaction time (when the reading was loaded).
Valid Time vs Transaction Time

rows: Valid time
When the fact was true in the real world
The meter reading was measured on January 15
date (the reading date)
Transaction time
When the fact was recorded in the database
The reading was loaded into Silver on January 16 at 05:30
loaded_at (the ingestion timestamp)

blocks.content: Most analytical queries use valid time: 'total consumption for January 2025' filters on the reading date. Temporal queries use both: 'reproduce the January billing report as it would have appeared on February 3' filters readings to valid_time in January AND transaction_time before February 3. This excludes late-arriving readings that were loaded after February 3 — readings that were not available when the original report was generated. Bi-temporal modeling adds the loaded_at column to fact tables and the effective_from/effective_to columns to dimension tables, enabling arbitrary point-in-time reconstruction.
The Point-in-Time Query

-- Reproduce the January billing report as it would have appeared on Feb 3
-- Filter facts by valid time (reading date) AND transaction time (loaded_at)
-- Filter dimensions by effective date range containing the report date

SELECT d.region_name,
       d.customer_type,
       SUM(f.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d
    ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  f.date_key >= '2025-01-01'       -- valid time: January readings
  AND  f.date_key <  '2025-02-01'
  AND  f.loaded_at <= '2025-02-03'       -- transaction time: loaded by Feb 3
  AND  d.effective_from <= '2025-02-03'  -- dimension version effective on Feb 3
  AND  (d.effective_to IS NULL OR d.effective_to > '2025-02-03')
GROUP BY d.region_name, d.customer_type
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

The query applies three temporal filters. The valid time filter (date_key in January) selects the correct readings. The transaction time filter (loaded_at <= Feb 3) excludes readings that arrived after the report date. The dimension effective date filter selects the dimension version that was current on February 3 — before the March customer_type change. The result is the exact billing report that would have been generated on February 3, regardless of when the query is actually run. This temporal reconstruction capability is a regulatory requirement for HydroAlpes and is increasingly common in financial services, healthcare, and any domain where audit trail reproduction is mandated.
System-Versioned Temporal Tables (SQL:2011)

The SQL:2011 standard introduced system-versioned temporal tables: the database automatically maintains transaction-time history for every row. When a row is updated, the database keeps the old version with a system_time range column that records when the version was valid. Queries with FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF timestamp automatically return the version that was current at that timestamp. PostgreSQL does not yet natively support SQL:2011 system versioning, but it can be emulated using triggers or extensions (temporal_tables). At HydroAlpes, temporal behavior is implemented manually using loaded_at and effective_from/effective_to columns, which is more portable across databases and gives the engineering team explicit control over the temporal logic.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Add loaded_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP to every fact table, even if you do not currently need temporal queries. The column costs negligible storage (8 bytes per row), is populated automatically, and enables point-in-time reconstruction if a regulatory or audit requirement emerges later. At HydroAlpes, loaded_at is a mandatory column in every Silver and Gold fact table — a decision that paid off when the Swiss regulator requested report reproduction two years after the column was added.

The star schema in lesson 3 denormalized the dimension tables — collapsing substations and regions into the meters dimension. This lesson covers a more aggressive form of denormalization: collapsing dimension attributes directly into the fact table, eliminating the dimension join entirely. Instead of joining fact_meter_readings with dim_meters to get customer_type, the customer_type is stored directly in the fact table. The join is pre-computed at load time, and the analyst queries a single table. This is the wide table pattern — a fully denormalized fact table that contains all dimension attributes — and it is the format that the ML team at HydroAlpes needs for feature engineering.
The Wide Table

-- Wide table: all dimension attributes pre-joined into the fact table
-- No joins needed for any analytical or ML query
CREATE TABLE gold.readings_wide AS
SELECT f.date_key,
       f.daily_kw,
       f.daily_mwh,
       d.meter_id,
       d.customer_type,
       d.region,
       d.region_name,
       d.country,
       d.substation_id,
       d.substation_name,
       d.substation_capacity_mw,
       d.installation_date,
       dt.year,
       dt.quarter,
       dt.month,
       dt.is_weekend,
       dt.is_swiss_holiday
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_date AS dt ON f.date_key = dt.date_key;

The wide table contains every column from the fact and all dimensions in a single table. The ML team can query it directly without any joins: SELECT daily_kw, customer_type, region, month, is_weekend FROM gold.readings_wide WHERE region = 'BERN-NORTH' — ready for feature engineering. The trade-off is storage: the wide table repeats region_name, country, substation_name, and other dimension attributes for every reading row. For 37.2 million January readings, the substation_name 'Alpine Hub B-4' is stored 437,000 times (once per reading at that substation). The storage overhead is substantial but often acceptable for Gold-layer tables that serve specific consumers.
The Maintenance Cost

Denormalized tables must be rebuilt when dimension attributes change. If a substation is renamed (Type 1 change), the wide table must be rebuilt or updated to reflect the new name in every row that references that substation. If a meter's customer_type changes (Type 2), the wide table must be rebuilt to apply the correct type version to each reading. This rebuild cost is the primary disadvantage of the wide table: the more frequently dimensions change, the more frequently the wide table must be rebuilt. At HydroAlpes, the wide table is rebuilt nightly as part of the Gold refresh pipeline — a 15-minute CTAS operation that produces a fresh snapshot incorporating all dimension changes from the previous day.
When to Denormalize

Denormalize into a wide table when the consumer needs join-free access (ML feature engineering, export to external systems), when the dimension attributes change infrequently relative to the rebuild frequency, and when the storage overhead is acceptable. Do not denormalize when the dimension changes frequently and the rebuild cost exceeds the join savings, when multiple consumers need different subsets of attributes (each requiring its own wide table), or when storage constraints prohibit the redundancy. At HydroAlpes, the ML team's wide table is rebuilt nightly (acceptable rebuild cost, infrequent dimension changes). The real-time dashboard does not use a wide table because it needs sub-second query latency on current data, which the star schema provides via indexed dimension joins.

blocks.title: Different consumers, different models
blocks.content: HydroAlpes maintains three models of the same data in the Gold layer: a star schema for the analytics team's dashboards (fast joins, indexed dimensions), a wide table for the ML team's feature engineering (join-free, self-contained), and summary tables for the billing team's invoices (pre-aggregated by region and customer type). Each model optimizes for its consumer's access pattern. The pipeline that produces each model reads from the same Silver source and runs in the same nightly refresh window. The modeling cost is three Gold pipelines instead of one — but the consumer satisfaction is dramatically higher.

This module covered the data modeling patterns that determine how HydroAlpes' pipeline tables are designed: star schemas for analytical query performance, snowflake schemas for normalized correctness, SCD Types 1, 2, and 3 for handling dimension attribute changes, temporal tables for point-in-time reconstruction, and denormalized wide tables for ML and export consumers. This final lesson traces HydroAlpes' model evolution from the junior-level normalized Silver schema to the mid-level production architecture — showing how each modeling decision was driven by a specific consumer need or regulatory requirement.
The Evolution Timeline

rows: Junior (Month 1–6)
Normalized Silver schema with 6 tables, no Gold layer
Single team, simple queries, correctness over performance
Third normal form (3NF)
Analytics expansion (Month 7)
Gold star schema added with dim_meters, dim_date, fact_meter_readings
Analytics team joined — needed fast dashboard queries without multi-hop joins
Star schema with denormalized dimensions
Billing audit (Month 9)
SCD Type 2 added for customer_type and region in dim_meters
Regulator required historical billing accuracy — retroactive corrections unacceptable
Type 2 SCD with effective_from/effective_to
ML team joins (Month 11)
Wide table added to Gold: readings_wide with all dimension attributes pre-joined
ML team needed join-free feature engineering — star schema required too many joins
Full denormalization (wide table)
Regulatory audit (Month 14)
loaded_at column added to all fact tables; temporal query patterns established
Swiss regulator requested report reproduction capability — needed transaction time tracking
Bi-temporal modeling
Current state
Silver: normalized 3NF. Gold: star schema + wide table + summary tables
Three teams with distinct requirements served by purpose-built models
Hybrid multi-model architecture

blocks.content: The Decision Matrix for New Tables

When a new table is needed at HydroAlpes, the engineering team answers five questions to determine its modeling approach. Who is the primary consumer (engineering, analytics, ML, billing, regulatory)? How frequently do dimension attributes change (daily, monthly, rarely)? Does any business process require historical attribute values (billing at historical rates, regulatory reproduction)? How large is the table (millions of rows, billions of rows)? And how many dimensions does the primary query join? The answers map directly to modeling choices: engineering consumers get normalized tables, analytics consumers get star schemas, ML consumers get wide tables, and any attribute that affects money or regulation gets Type 2 SCD.

The model evolution also reveals a meta-principle: data models are not designed once and frozen. They evolve as teams grow, consumers change, and regulatory requirements emerge. The Silver layer — the engineering team's normalized source of truth — has been stable since month 3. The Gold layer has been restructured three times in 14 months as new consumers joined. This asymmetry is by design: the transformation layer (Silver) should be stable, and the consumption layer (Gold) should adapt to its consumers. The pipeline that transforms Silver to Gold is the bridge between stability and adaptability.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 5, you will learn the SQL patterns that implement these data models in production pipelines: MERGE for upsert operations that handle SCD Type 1 and Type 2 in a single statement, set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT) for data reconciliation across model versions, recursive CTEs for production hierarchy traversal, and advanced NULL handling for the edge cases that dimension processing creates. The modeling patterns from this module define what the pipeline should produce; Module 5 provides the SQL tools to produce it.

chapters.title: Advanced SQL Patterns
chapters.description: Master MERGE for upsert operations, set operations for combining result sets, production-depth recursive CTEs, and advanced NULL handling and conditional logic.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' Silver pipeline receives meter readings daily. Most readings are new rows (readings for today's date), but some are late arrivals — readings for previous dates that were delayed by network issues or meter firmware problems. The pipeline must insert new readings and update existing readings (if a corrected value arrives for a meter-date combination that already exists). At junior level, this required two statements: an INSERT for new rows and an UPDATE for existing rows, wrapped in a transaction. The MERGE statement (SQL:2003 standard) combines both operations into a single atomic statement: if the row exists, update it; if it does not, insert it. This upsert pattern is the foundation of idempotent pipeline design.
The MERGE Syntax (SQL Standard)

-- MERGE: insert new readings, update existing readings with corrected values
-- PostgreSQL 15+ supports the SQL standard MERGE statement
MERGE INTO silver.meter_readings AS target
USING staging.daily_readings AS source
  ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id
 AND target.date     = source.date
WHEN MATCHED AND source.daily_kw <> target.daily_kw THEN
  UPDATE SET daily_kw    = source.daily_kw,
             loaded_at   = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
  VALUES (source.meter_id, source.daily_kw, source.date,
          source.region, source.customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);

The MERGE statement has three parts. USING identifies the source data (the staging table with today's delivery). ON defines the matching condition (same meter and date). WHEN MATCHED defines what to do when a source row matches an existing target row — in this case, update the daily_kw if it changed and refresh the loaded_at timestamp. WHEN NOT MATCHED defines what to do when a source row has no match in the target — insert a new row. The additional condition AND source.daily_kw <> target.daily_kw prevents unnecessary updates when the value has not changed, which reduces write amplification and avoids triggering downstream change-detection pipelines on unchanged rows.
MERGE for SCD Type 2 Processing

-- MERGE for SCD Type 2: close old versions and insert new versions
-- Handles Type 1 (overwrite) and Type 2 (new version) in one statement
MERGE INTO gold.dim_meters AS target
USING staging.meters_latest AS source
  ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id
 AND target.effective_to IS NULL  -- match only the current version
WHEN MATCHED AND target.customer_type <> source.customer_type THEN
  -- Type 2 attribute changed: close the current version
  UPDATE SET effective_to = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
WHEN MATCHED AND target.substation_name <> source.substation_name THEN
  -- Type 1 attribute changed: overwrite in place
  UPDATE SET substation_name = source.substation_name
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  -- New meter: insert first version
  INSERT (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name, country,
          substation_id, substation_name, substation_capacity_mw,
          installation_date, is_active, effective_from, effective_to)
  VALUES (source.meter_id, source.customer_type, source.region,
          source.region_name, source.country, source.substation_id,
          source.substation_name, source.substation_capacity_mw,
          source.installation_date, source.is_active,
          CURRENT_DATE, NULL);
-- Note: the new Type 2 version must be inserted in a separate step
-- because MERGE cannot both UPDATE and INSERT for the same source row

This MERGE handles three cases in one statement: new meters are inserted, Type 1 changes (substation_name) are overwritten, and Type 2 changes (customer_type) close the current version. The new Type 2 version — a new row with the updated customer_type and effective_from = CURRENT_DATE — must be inserted separately because SQL MERGE processes each source row against at most one WHEN clause. At HydroAlpes, the SCD pipeline runs the MERGE first (closing old versions and handling Type 1 changes), then runs a follow-up INSERT for new Type 2 versions detected by comparing source against target where effective_to was just set. This two-step approach is the standard pattern for MERGE-based SCD processing.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: MERGE is idempotent when the ON condition uses a natural key: running the same staging data through the MERGE twice produces the same result because the second run matches existing rows and either updates them to the same values or skips them (if the AND condition prevents unnecessary updates). This idempotency is critical for pipeline reliability — if the pipeline fails after MERGE and reruns, no duplicate rows are created. At HydroAlpes, every Silver-layer load uses MERGE instead of INSERT to guarantee idempotent reprocessing.

Before PostgreSQL 15 added the SQL standard MERGE, PostgreSQL provided its own upsert mechanism: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT. Unlike MERGE (which requires a USING source table), ON CONFLICT works with any INSERT statement — including INSERT ... VALUES and INSERT ... SELECT. The ON CONFLICT clause specifies what to do when the inserted row violates a unique constraint: DO UPDATE overwrites the conflicting row, and DO NOTHING silently skips it. ON CONFLICT is more concise than MERGE for simple upserts and remains the preferred pattern at HydroAlpes for single-table idempotent inserts where MERGE's multi-case logic is unnecessary.
DO UPDATE — The Upsert

-- Upsert: insert new readings or update existing ones
-- ON CONFLICT targets the (meter_id, date) primary key
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
FROM   staging.daily_readings
ON CONFLICT (meter_id, date) DO UPDATE
SET    daily_kw  = EXCLUDED.daily_kw,
       loaded_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
WHERE  silver.meter_readings.daily_kw <> EXCLUDED.daily_kw;

The EXCLUDED pseudo-table references the row that would have been inserted — the source data. When a conflict occurs on the (meter_id, date) primary key, the DO UPDATE SET clause overwrites daily_kw with the new value from EXCLUDED and refreshes loaded_at. The WHERE clause prevents unnecessary updates when the value has not changed — identical to the MERGE pattern from lesson 1. The ON CONFLICT target can be any unique constraint or unique index, not just the primary key. At HydroAlpes, ON CONFLICT on the composite primary key is the standard pattern for idempotent Silver-layer inserts.
DO NOTHING — Idempotent Insert

-- Idempotent insert: insert only if the row does not already exist
-- Silently skip rows that conflict with the primary key
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
FROM   staging.daily_readings
ON CONFLICT (meter_id, date) DO NOTHING;

DO NOTHING skips any row that conflicts with an existing row — no update, no error, no side effects. This pattern is appropriate when the first arrival is authoritative and corrections are not expected. At HydroAlpes, Bronze-to-Silver loading uses DO NOTHING because the Bronze data is append-only and the first version of each reading is considered correct. If a corrected reading arrives later, the correction pipeline uses DO UPDATE to overwrite the value. The separation between initial load (DO NOTHING) and correction processing (DO UPDATE) makes the pipeline intent explicit and debuggable.
ON CONFLICT vs MERGE

rows.left: Works with VALUES and SELECT — any INSERT
rows.right: Requires a USING source table or subquery

rows.left: Single-case logic: one action per conflict
rows.right: Multi-case logic: different actions for MATCHED vs NOT MATCHED

rows.left: Conflict target is a constraint or index
rows.right: Match condition is an arbitrary ON clause

rows.left: PostgreSQL-specific syntax (also supported in SQLite)
rows.right: SQL standard (PostgreSQL 15+, Spark SQL 3.x, Snowflake, BigQuery)

rows.left: More concise for simple upserts
rows.right: More flexible for SCD processing and conditional logic

blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the team uses ON CONFLICT for straightforward upserts (Silver-layer fact loading, Bronze-to-Silver deduplication) and MERGE for complex SCD processing (Gold-layer dimension updates with Type 1 and Type 2 handling). The choice is driven by the complexity of the conditional logic: if the upsert is 'insert or update one set of columns,' ON CONFLICT is cleaner and more concise. If the upsert involves multiple conditions with different actions per condition, MERGE is more expressive and self-documenting. Both patterns produce idempotent pipelines, which is the primary requirement for production reliability.

blocks.title: ON CONFLICT requires a unique constraint
blocks.content: ON CONFLICT targets a specific unique constraint or index. If the target constraint does not exist, the ON CONFLICT clause has no effect — duplicates are inserted silently. At HydroAlpes, every table that uses ON CONFLICT has an explicit PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraint on the conflict columns. The pipeline's CI tests (Module 6) verify that the constraint exists before the pipeline runs, preventing the scenario where a dropped constraint silently converts an upsert into a duplicate-creating insert.

Set operations combine the results of two or more SELECT queries into a single result set. UNION combines all rows from both queries, removing duplicates. UNION ALL combines all rows including duplicates. INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in both queries. EXCEPT returns rows that appear in the first query but not in the second. These operations are the SQL equivalent of set algebra — union, intersection, and difference — and they solve a specific category of problems in data engineering: combining results from multiple sources, reconciling data across pipeline layers, and detecting discrepancies between datasets.
UNION ALL for Combining Sources

-- Combine readings from Swiss and Austrian source systems
-- Both systems deliver in the same schema but arrive via different feeds
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, 'CH' AS source_country
FROM   staging.swiss_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
UNION ALL
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, 'AT' AS source_country
FROM   staging.austrian_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

UNION ALL is the correct choice for combining feeds from different sources because each source may legitimately contain the same meter_id (if a meter is registered in both systems) — UNION would incorrectly deduplicate those rows. UNION ALL preserves all rows from both queries without deduplication, making it the safe default for combining data sources. At HydroAlpes, the Bronze ingestion layer uses UNION ALL to combine the Swiss and Austrian feeds into a single staging table before Silver-layer validation identifies and resolves any cross-system duplicates.
EXCEPT for Reconciliation

-- Find readings that exist in Silver but not in Gold (dropped during transformation)
SELECT meter_id, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
EXCEPT
SELECT d.meter_id, f.date_key
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  f.date_key = '2025-01-15';

-- Result: rows in Silver but not in Gold — readings lost during transformation

EXCEPT returns rows from the first query that do not appear in the second query — the set difference. This is the most powerful reconciliation tool in SQL: it directly answers 'what is in Silver but not in Gold?' If the result is empty, every Silver reading has a corresponding Gold fact. If rows appear, those readings were lost during the Silver-to-Gold transformation — typically because a dimension join excluded them (unregistered meters with no dimension row). At HydroAlpes, an EXCEPT-based reconciliation runs after every Gold refresh. A non-empty result triggers an alert and prevents the Gold data from being served to dashboards until the discrepancy is resolved.
INTERSECT for Common Elements

INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in both queries — the overlap between two datasets. At HydroAlpes, INTERSECT identifies meters that exist in both the Swiss and Austrian registration systems (cross-border meters near the national boundary). These meters require special handling in the billing pipeline because they are subject to two countries' energy regulations. INTERSECT is used less frequently than UNION ALL and EXCEPT in data engineering, but it is the cleanest way to express 'find the common elements' without a JOIN or an IN subquery.
Performance: UNION vs UNION ALL

UNION (without ALL) deduplicates the combined result by sorting or hashing the rows and removing duplicates — an operation that costs O(N log N) for sorting or O(N) for hashing, where N is the total row count. UNION ALL does no deduplication — it simply concatenates the results in O(N) time with no additional memory. For large datasets, the difference is significant: UNION on 37.2 million rows requires a sort or hash pass over all 37.2 million rows; UNION ALL streams them directly. At HydroAlpes, the rule is: use UNION ALL unless you specifically need deduplication, and if you need deduplication, verify that the duplicate detection is correct (same row in both queries, not just same meter_id with different daily_kw values).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: EXCEPT is the simplest and most readable reconciliation tool. Use it to compare pipeline layers after every transformation: SELECT key_columns FROM source EXCEPT SELECT key_columns FROM target. If the result is empty, the transformation preserved all source rows. If rows appear, those rows were lost. At HydroAlpes, every pipeline step has an EXCEPT-based reconciliation check that runs as an automated assertion (Module 6). This catches data loss within minutes of the pipeline run, not days later when a human notices missing data.

The junior track introduced recursive CTEs as a tool for traversing hierarchies with a simple three-level example (region → substation → meter). At mid-level, recursive CTEs become a production tool for solving real graph-traversal problems: tracing the path from a failed meter through the grid to the affected substation and reservoir, computing the transitive closure of dependencies between pipeline tables, and generating date sequences for gap-filling. This lesson covers the production patterns, the performance safeguards, and the cycle-detection techniques that make recursive CTEs safe for production use.
Grid Topology Traversal

-- Traverse the grid topology from a failed substation upstream to the reservoir
-- The grid_connections table: from_node, to_node, connection_type

WITH RECURSIVE upstream_path AS (
  -- Anchor: start at the failed substation
  SELECT node_id, parent_node_id, node_type, 1 AS depth,
         ARRAY[node_id] AS path
  FROM   silver.grid_topology
  WHERE  node_id = 'SUB-BERN-04'

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: walk upstream to the parent node
  SELECT gt.node_id, gt.parent_node_id, gt.node_type,
         up.depth + 1,
         up.path || gt.node_id
  FROM   upstream_path AS up
    INNER JOIN silver.grid_topology AS gt
      ON up.parent_node_id = gt.node_id
  WHERE  up.depth < 20                     -- safety: max depth
    AND  gt.node_id <> ALL(up.path)        -- safety: cycle detection
)
SELECT depth, node_id, node_type, path
FROM   upstream_path
ORDER BY depth;

The recursive CTE traces the grid from substation SUB-BERN-04 upstream through the grid section, the dam, and finally to the reservoir. The path column (a PostgreSQL ARRAY) accumulates the node IDs at each step, providing the complete traversal path. Two safety mechanisms prevent runaway recursion: WHERE depth < 20 limits the maximum traversal depth, and gt.node_id <> ALL(up.path) detects cycles by checking whether the next node already appears in the current path. Without these safeguards, a cycle in the grid topology (node A references node B, which references node A) would cause infinite recursion.
Date Sequence Generation

-- Generate a date sequence without generate_series (portable across databases)
-- Useful in Spark SQL and other engines that lack generate_series
WITH RECURSIVE date_spine AS (
  SELECT DATE '2025-01-01' AS date
  UNION ALL
  SELECT date + INTERVAL '1 day'
  FROM   date_spine
  WHERE  date < DATE '2025-01-31'
)
SELECT date FROM date_spine ORDER BY date;

This recursive CTE generates every date in January 2025 by starting with January 1 and adding one day per recursion step. The termination condition (date < January 31) ensures exactly 31 iterations. This pattern is the portable alternative to PostgreSQL's generate_series function — it works in every database that supports recursive CTEs, including Spark SQL, BigQuery, and SQL Server. At HydroAlpes, the date spine is used for gap detection (CROSS JOIN the spine with regions, then LEFT JOIN with actual readings to find missing date-region combinations) in Spark SQL pipelines where generate_series is not available.
Performance and Safety

Recursive CTEs can be expensive because each recursion step produces a new set of rows that feeds the next step. A hierarchy with 1,000 nodes and an average branching factor of 10 produces 10,000 rows at depth 2, 100,000 at depth 3, and 1 million at depth 4. Without a depth limit, the recursion can produce billions of rows and exhaust memory. Production-safe recursive CTEs always include a depth limit (WHERE depth < N) and, for graph traversal, a cycle-detection mechanism (tracking visited nodes in an array or a WITH clause). PostgreSQL 14+ also supports the CYCLE clause as a built-in cycle detection mechanism: WITH RECURSIVE ... CYCLE node_id SET is_cycle USING path — which is cleaner than manual array-based detection.

blocks.title: Always include a depth limit
blocks.content: A recursive CTE without a depth limit runs until it terminates naturally (no more rows to produce) or hits the database's recursion limit (work_mem exhaustion, statement timeout). Natural termination depends on the data — and data can have unexpected cycles or deeper hierarchies than anticipated. The depth limit is a safety net that prevents runaway recursion regardless of data anomalies. At HydroAlpes, every recursive CTE includes WHERE depth < 50 even when the expected maximum depth is 5, because the limit costs nothing on normal data and prevents catastrophe on anomalous data.

The junior track used simple CASE expressions for value classification: CASE WHEN daily_kw > 100 THEN 'high' ELSE 'normal' END. At mid-level, CASE expressions become a general-purpose conditional logic tool that replaces procedural IF/ELSE blocks in pipeline SQL. Multi-condition CASE with priority ordering, nested CASE for decision trees, CASE inside aggregation for conditional metrics, and CASE for data type conversion and error handling are patterns that appear in virtually every production pipeline. This lesson covers the advanced CASE patterns that HydroAlpes' team uses daily.
Multi-Condition CASE with Priority

-- Tiered pricing: conditions evaluated in order, first match wins
-- Higher tiers are checked first to ensure correct classification
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       CASE
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 1000 THEN 'Tier 4: Industrial peak'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 100  THEN 'Tier 3: Commercial high'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 10   THEN 'Tier 2: Standard'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 0    THEN 'Tier 1: Low-usage discount'
         ELSE 'Invalid: negative consumption'
       END AS pricing_tier,
       CASE
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 1000 THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.082
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 100  THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.094
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 10   THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.112
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 0    THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.098
         ELSE 0
       END AS estimated_charge_eur
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  10;

CASE evaluates conditions in order and returns the value for the first TRUE condition. The ordering matters: if the >= 10 condition were listed before >= 100, a reading of 500 kW would match >= 10 first and be classified as Tier 2 instead of the correct Tier 3. By listing conditions from most restrictive (>= 1000) to least restrictive (>= 0), each reading falls into the correct tier. The ELSE clause catches unexpected values (negative readings that should have been filtered by the Silver quality gate) and classifies them explicitly rather than defaulting to NULL. At HydroAlpes, the billing pipeline uses this tiered CASE for provisional pricing before the final rate schedule is applied.
CASE for Safe Division

-- Safe division: prevent division by zero with CASE or NULLIF
-- Pattern 1: CASE — explicit, readable
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
       CASE
         WHEN COUNT(*) = 0 THEN NULL
         ELSE SUM(daily_kw) / COUNT(*)
       END AS safe_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;

-- Pattern 2: NULLIF — concise
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS safe_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;

Division by zero produces an error in PostgreSQL and NULL in some other databases. Both CASE and NULLIF prevent the error by returning NULL when the denominator is zero. NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) returns NULL if the count is zero (because NULLIF returns NULL when its two arguments are equal), and NULL / anything = NULL — no error. The CASE version is more verbose but more readable for engineers who are not familiar with NULLIF. At HydroAlpes, the team prefers NULLIF for simple zero-prevention and CASE for complex conditional divisions where the zero-prevention is part of a larger conditional expression.
CASE Inside UPDATE for Conditional Logic

-- Conditional UPDATE: set quality_flag based on multiple business rules
UPDATE silver.meter_readings
SET    quality_flag = CASE
         WHEN daily_kw < 0                          THEN 'NEGATIVE'
         WHEN daily_kw > 5000                        THEN 'IMPLAUSIBLE'
         WHEN daily_kw = 0 AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL' THEN 'SUSPICIOUS_ZERO'
         WHEN daily_kw IS NULL                       THEN 'MISSING'
         ELSE 'VALID'
       END
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

CASE inside UPDATE applies different classification logic to each row based on its values — the SQL equivalent of an IF/ELIF/ELSE block in Python. The quality_flag column receives a different value depending on which condition the row matches. This pattern is the foundation of data quality tagging: every reading is classified into a quality category in a single pass, and downstream consumers can filter to quality_flag = 'VALID' to exclude problematic readings. At HydroAlpes, quality tagging runs as the first step of the Silver pipeline, before any joins or aggregations, so that quality flags are available for every downstream operation.

blocks.title: CASE is SQL's general-purpose conditional
blocks.content: Unlike Python, SQL has no IF statement outside of procedural extensions (PL/pgSQL). CASE is the only conditional construct available in standard SQL, which means it handles every conditional need: value classification, safe arithmetic, error handling, conditional aggregation, and data quality tagging. Mastering CASE at mid-level depth means recognizing it as a universal tool, not a simple value mapper.

NULL values propagate silently through SQL expressions. NULL + 10 = NULL. NULL = NULL is not TRUE — it is NULL (unknown). NULL in a GROUP BY column creates a NULL group. NULL in an aggregate is ignored by SUM and AVG but counted by COUNT(*). These behaviors were introduced in the junior track, but at mid-level, NULL handling becomes a production concern: a single unhandled NULL in a billing calculation can produce a NULL total_kw that silently drops a region from the billing report. This lesson covers the production NULL-handling toolkit: COALESCE, NULLIF, IS DISTINCT FROM, and the GREATEST/LEAST functions with NULL semantics.
COALESCE — Cascading Defaults

-- COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument
-- Cascading fallback: try the primary source, then the fallback, then a default
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       COALESCE(m.customer_type, s.default_customer_type, 'UNKNOWN') AS customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

COALESCE with three arguments implements a cascading fallback: first try the meter's customer_type from the meters table, then try the substation's default_customer_type, and finally fall back to 'UNKNOWN' if both are NULL. This prevents NULL customer_type values from propagating into the billing pipeline, where they would cause the GROUP BY to create a NULL group that downstream reports cannot interpret. At HydroAlpes, COALESCE appears in every LEFT JOIN enrichment query as a defensive pattern: even if the dimension lookup fails (LEFT JOIN produces NULL), the fact row always has a non-NULL classification.
IS DISTINCT FROM — NULL-Safe Comparison

-- Standard equality: NULL = NULL → NULL (unknown) — never TRUE
SELECT * FROM silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id = NULL;              -- Returns ZERO rows (wrong!)

-- IS DISTINCT FROM: treats NULL as a comparable value
-- NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL → TRUE (NULLs are 'the same')
SELECT * FROM silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL;  -- Correct: returns NULL rows

-- Detect changes including NULL → value and value → NULL transitions
SELECT s.meter_id,
       t.customer_type AS current_type,
       s.customer_type AS new_type
FROM   staging.meters_latest AS s
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS t
    ON s.meter_id = t.meter_id AND t.effective_to IS NULL
WHERE  s.customer_type IS DISTINCT FROM t.customer_type;

IS DISTINCT FROM is the NULL-safe version of <>. Regular <> returns NULL when either side is NULL — which means NULL-to-value and value-to-NULL changes are invisible to standard comparison operators. IS DISTINCT FROM treats NULL as a comparable value: NULL IS DISTINCT FROM 'COMMERCIAL' is TRUE, and NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL is TRUE. At HydroAlpes, the SCD change-detection pipeline uses IS DISTINCT FROM to detect all attribute changes, including transitions to and from NULL. Without it, a meter whose customer_type changes from 'RESIDENTIAL' to NULL (a data quality issue) would not trigger a Type 2 version — the change would be silently ignored.
GREATEST and LEAST with NULLs

GREATEST(a, b, c) returns the largest value. LEAST(a, b, c) returns the smallest. In PostgreSQL, if any argument is NULL, the function returns NULL — unlike many other databases where NULLs are ignored. This means GREATEST(10, NULL, 5) returns NULL in PostgreSQL, not 10. The workaround: wrap each argument in COALESCE to provide a default. GREATEST(COALESCE(a, 0), COALESCE(b, 0), COALESCE(c, 0)) guarantees a non-NULL result. At HydroAlpes, this pattern appears in the reservoir level monitoring pipeline, where GREATEST is used to find the maximum level across multiple sensors per reservoir — and a malfunctioning sensor reporting NULL should not cause the entire computation to return NULL.

blocks.title: NULL is the silent data corruptor
blocks.content: A NULL in a SUM is silently ignored — SUM(10, NULL, 5) = 15, not NULL. A NULL in arithmetic propagates — 10 + NULL = NULL. A NULL in a comparison returns UNKNOWN — NULL = NULL is not TRUE. A NULL in GROUP BY creates a NULL group. Each behavior is individually documented but collectively creates a minefield for pipeline engineers. The mid-level discipline is: handle NULLs explicitly at every pipeline boundary with COALESCE for defaults, IS DISTINCT FROM for change detection, and CASE for conditional classification. Never assume a column is non-NULL unless a NOT NULL constraint enforces it.

HydroAlpes' meter_id follows a convention: CH-BERN-00142 encodes the country (CH), the region (BERN), and the sequence number (00142). At junior level, this convention was just a naming pattern. At mid-level, parsing these identifiers is a pipeline operation: extracting the country code for routing, the region code for validation against the regions table, and the sequence number for gap detection. String functions — split_part, substring, regexp_matches, and string_agg — are the tools that parse, validate, and reconstruct identifier strings in production pipelines. This lesson covers the production string patterns that appear in every data engineering codebase.
Parsing Identifiers

-- Parse meter_id components using split_part (delimiter-based extraction)
SELECT meter_id,
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 1) AS country_code,   -- CH
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 2) AS region_code,     -- BERN
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 3) AS sequence_num     -- 00142
FROM   silver.meters
LIMIT  5;

-- Validate: find meters with malformed IDs (wrong number of parts)
SELECT meter_id
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  array_length(string_to_array(meter_id, '-'), 1) <> 3;

split_part extracts the Nth part of a delimited string. split_part(meter_id, '-', 1) returns everything before the first hyphen (the country code). The validation query uses string_to_array to split the entire identifier and checks whether the result has exactly 3 parts. Meters with malformed IDs (missing hyphens, extra parts, empty segments) are flagged for data quality review. At HydroAlpes, this validation runs as part of the Bronze-to-Silver quality gate — malformed IDs are routed to the quarantine table instead of blocking the pipeline.
Regular Expressions

-- Validate meter_id format with a regex pattern
-- Expected: 2 uppercase letters, hyphen, 2-20 uppercase letters, hyphen, 5 digits
SELECT meter_id,
       meter_id ~ '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$' AS is_valid_format
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  meter_id !~ '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$'
LIMIT  10;

PostgreSQL's ~ operator matches a string against a regular expression. The pattern ^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$ validates the meter_id format: two uppercase letters, a hyphen, 2–20 uppercase letters, a hyphen, and exactly 5 digits. The WHERE clause filters to non-matching IDs, surfacing format violations. Regular expressions are the most powerful validation tool for string-formatted data, but they are also the most opaque — a complex regex is difficult to read and debug. At HydroAlpes, regex patterns are stored as named constants in the pipeline configuration (METER_ID_PATTERN = '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$') so they are documented, version-controlled, and reusable across queries.
Aggregating Strings

-- string_agg: concatenate values from multiple rows into a single string
-- Useful for generating comma-separated lists in reports
SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
       string_agg(DISTINCT customer_type, ', ' ORDER BY customer_type) AS types_present
FROM   silver.meters
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

region      | meter_count | types_present
------------+-------------+---------------------------------------
BERN-NORTH  |     135,000 | COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, RESIDENTIAL
BERN-SOUTH  |     128,000 | COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, RESIDENTIAL
INNSBRUCK-E |      98,000 | COMMERCIAL, RESIDENTIAL
...

string_agg concatenates values from multiple rows into a single delimited string. The DISTINCT keyword deduplicates before concatenating, and ORDER BY controls the concatenation order. This pattern is useful for generating human-readable summaries (comma-separated lists of customer types per region) and for debugging (concatenating all error messages from a quarantine table into a single diagnostic string per meter). The Spark SQL equivalent is collect_list for arrays and concat_ws for string joining. At HydroAlpes, string_agg appears in the data quality dashboard queries where each region's profile is summarized in a single row.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When parsing structured identifiers (meter_id, substation_id, file paths), prefer split_part over regex for simple delimited extraction — it is faster, more readable, and easier to maintain. Use regex only when the parsing requires pattern matching (variable-length components, optional parts, format validation). At HydroAlpes, split_part handles 95% of identifier parsing; regex is reserved for validation and for parsing identifiers from external systems with inconsistent formats.

HydroAlpes operates across two time zones (CET for Swiss operations, CET for Austrian operations — currently the same, but historically different during daylight saving transitions). Meter readings use DATE (no time component). Turbine telemetry uses TIMESTAMPTZ (with timezone awareness). Energy prices use TIMESTAMP at hourly granularity in UTC. The billing period alignment uses fiscal months that start on the 15th of each calendar month (a Swiss regulatory requirement). Every one of these temporal variations requires specific date/time handling patterns, and getting them wrong produces billing errors that take months to detect and correct. This lesson covers the production date patterns that handle real-world temporal complexity.
DATE_TRUNC for Period Alignment

-- Align dates to billing periods (fiscal month starts on the 15th)
-- A reading on January 20 belongs to the billing period starting January 15
-- A reading on January 10 belongs to the billing period starting December 15
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       CASE
         WHEN EXTRACT(DAY FROM mr.date) >= 15
           THEN DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) + INTERVAL '14 days'
         ELSE DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) - INTERVAL '17 days'
       END AS billing_period_start
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-31'
ORDER BY mr.date;

Standard DATE_TRUNC('month', date) aligns to the 1st of the month. HydroAlpes' billing period starts on the 15th, requiring custom alignment logic. The CASE expression checks whether the reading falls on or after the 15th (belongs to the current month's billing period) or before the 15th (belongs to the previous month's billing period). This custom alignment pattern is common in financial and regulatory contexts where fiscal periods do not align with calendar boundaries. The key principle: compute the period_start as a derived column early in the pipeline (in the Silver-to-Gold transformation), so downstream queries can GROUP BY billing_period_start without recomputing the alignment logic.
Timezone-Safe Timestamp Conversion

-- Convert turbine telemetry timestamps from UTC to local time
-- TIMESTAMPTZ handles the conversion automatically based on session timezone
SET timezone = 'Europe/Zurich';

SELECT tt.turbine_id,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'UTC'     AS utc_time,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/Zurich' AS zurich_time,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/Vienna' AS vienna_time
FROM   silver.turbine_telemetry AS tt
WHERE  tt.ts >= '2025-01-15T00:00:00Z'
  AND  tt.ts <  '2025-01-16T00:00:00Z'
LIMIT  5;

AT TIME ZONE converts a TIMESTAMPTZ value to a specific timezone's local time. For Swiss turbines, Zurich time is CET (UTC+1) in winter and CEST (UTC+2) in summer. The database handles daylight saving transitions automatically — the engineer does not need to know the DST rules. At HydroAlpes, all timestamps are stored as TIMESTAMPTZ in UTC and converted to local time only at the presentation layer (in Gold-layer views and dashboard queries). This UTC-storage, local-display pattern prevents the class of bugs where timestamps are ambiguous during daylight saving transitions (the hour from 02:00 to 03:00 occurs twice during the autumn clock change).
Interval Arithmetic

-- Common date arithmetic patterns
SELECT CURRENT_DATE                                  AS today,
       CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'               AS yesterday,
       CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'              AS one_week_ago,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)             AS first_of_month,
       (DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
        + INTERVAL '1 month' - INTERVAL '1 day')::DATE AS last_of_month,
       CURRENT_DATE - (CURRENT_DATE - DATE '2025-01-01') AS days_since_jan1;

Interval arithmetic in PostgreSQL is type-safe: DATE + INTERVAL produces a TIMESTAMP, which can be cast back to DATE with ::DATE. The first-of-month and last-of-month patterns are the most commonly used in pipeline date logic — they define the boundaries of monthly aggregation periods, billing cycles, and retention windows. At HydroAlpes, these patterns are encapsulated in a SQL function library (a set of CREATE FUNCTION statements) that the pipeline calls rather than recomputing inline: SELECT hydroalpes.last_day_of_month('2025-01-15') returns '2025-01-31'. This encapsulation prevents subtle bugs (like forgetting the -1 day adjustment for last-of-month) and makes the date logic version-controlled and testable.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Store all timestamps as TIMESTAMPTZ in UTC. Convert to local time only at the presentation layer. Use DATE (not TIMESTAMP) for columns that represent calendar dates without time components (reading dates, billing dates, installation dates). Encapsulate complex date arithmetic in SQL functions so the logic is defined once, tested once, and reused everywhere. At HydroAlpes, the date function library contains 12 functions (first_of_month, last_of_month, billing_period_start, fiscal_quarter, etc.) that the entire pipeline depends on.

Every SQL query in this course so far has been static: the table names, column names, and conditions are fixed at write time. But some pipeline operations require queries that are constructed at runtime: generating partition-specific DDL for each new month, building PIVOT column lists from dynamic data, or constructing WHERE clauses based on pipeline configuration. Dynamic SQL — generating and executing SQL strings at runtime — is the tool for these operations. It is also one of the most dangerous tools in SQL: a dynamically constructed query that includes unsanitized user input is vulnerable to SQL injection, and a dynamically constructed query that is syntactically wrong fails at runtime, not at development time.
Parameterized Queries

-- Parameterized query in PL/pgSQL: safe against SQL injection
-- The pipeline passes target_date as a parameter, not as string concatenation
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION hydroalpes.refresh_gold_billing(
    target_date DATE
) RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
  DELETE FROM gold.regional_consumption
  WHERE  month = DATE_TRUNC('month', target_date);

  INSERT INTO gold.regional_consumption
      (region, customer_type, month, total_kw, meter_count)
  SELECT mr.region,
         m.customer_type,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) AS month,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw),
         COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id)
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
    INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  WHERE  mr.date >= target_date
    AND  mr.date <  target_date + INTERVAL '1 month'
  GROUP BY mr.region, m.customer_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

The function accepts target_date as a parameter and uses it in the WHERE clause. The parameter is bound by the database — it is never concatenated into the SQL string, so SQL injection is impossible. This is the safe approach to dynamic behavior: parameterize the values that change between runs, but keep the query structure static. At HydroAlpes, every pipeline query that varies by date, region, or batch ID uses parameterized functions rather than string-constructed SQL. The Airflow pipeline calls SELECT hydroalpes.refresh_gold_billing('2025-01-01') each day, passing the target date as a parameter.
Dynamic DDL for Partition Management

EXECUTE runs a dynamically constructed SQL string. The format function with %I (identifier, safely quoted) and %L (literal, safely quoted) prevents SQL injection even though the query is constructed at runtime. The function creates a new partition table named meter_readings_2025_02 for the February date range. This dynamic DDL is necessary because partition names include the month and year — values that change on each invocation. At HydroAlpes, this function is called by the Airflow DAG on the first of each month, creating the partition for the current month before the daily ingestion pipeline runs.
When Dynamic SQL Is Necessary

Dynamic SQL is necessary when table names, column names, or DDL structure vary at runtime — situations that parameterized queries cannot handle because SQL parameters only substitute values, not identifiers or keywords. At HydroAlpes, dynamic SQL appears in three contexts: partition management (creating monthly partitions with date-based names), dynamic pivoting (generating CASE WHEN columns from a list of values queried from a metadata table), and schema migration (generating ALTER TABLE statements from a configuration file). For everything else — date-filtered queries, region-filtered queries, batch-specific processing — parameterized static SQL is preferred because it is type-checked at function creation time and safe by construction.

blocks.title: Never concatenate unsanitized input into SQL strings
blocks.content: The SQL injection vulnerability is straightforward: if you construct a query like 'SELECT * FROM meters WHERE region = ''' || user_input || '''' and user_input contains '; DROP TABLE meters; --, the constructed string executes a destructive command. Always use format(%I, %L) in PL/pgSQL or parameterized queries in application code. At HydroAlpes, the CI linter flags any PL/pgSQL function that uses || for string concatenation inside EXECUTE — format() is the only permitted pattern for dynamic SQL construction.

This module covered the advanced SQL patterns that implement the data models from Module 4 in production pipelines: MERGE and INSERT ON CONFLICT for upsert operations, set operations for data combination and reconciliation, recursive CTEs for graph traversal and sequence generation, advanced CASE for conditional pipeline logic, NULL-safe comparison and cascading defaults, string functions for identifier parsing and validation, date patterns for period alignment and timezone handling, and dynamic SQL for partition management and schema evolution. This final lesson organizes these patterns into a catalog with selection guidance — a reference that HydroAlpes' team consults when choosing the right pattern for a new pipeline operation.
The Pattern Catalog

rows: Idempotent fact loading
INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE
MERGE (for multi-case logic)
ON CONFLICT for simple upserts; MERGE when different actions needed per match type
SCD Type 2 processing
MERGE + follow-up INSERT
Separate UPDATE + INSERT in transaction
MERGE for combined Type 1 + Type 2; separate statements when logic is complex
Cross-layer reconciliation
EXCEPT on key columns
LEFT JOIN + IS NULL (anti-join)
EXCEPT for simple existence check; anti-join when you need columns from both sides
Source combination
UNION ALL
INSERT from multiple sources
UNION ALL for in-query combination; separate INSERTs for staged loading
Hierarchy traversal
Recursive CTE with depth limit
Multiple self-joins (fixed depth only)
Recursive CTE for variable depth; self-joins for known fixed depth (simpler, faster)
NULL-safe comparison
IS DISTINCT FROM
COALESCE to a sentinel value, then compare
IS DISTINCT FROM for change detection; COALESCE for providing defaults
Identifier parsing
split_part (delimiter-based)
regexp_matches (pattern-based)
split_part for simple delimited IDs; regex for complex or variable formats
Period alignment
DATE_TRUNC + CASE for custom periods
Encapsulated function
Inline CASE for one-off queries; function for reusable period logic

blocks.content: The Architecture Phase Complete

Modules 4 and 5 together form the architecture phase. Module 4 taught what to build: star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, temporal tables, and denormalized wide tables. Module 5 taught how to build it: MERGE for upsert loading, EXCEPT for reconciliation, recursive CTEs for topology traversal, CASE and COALESCE for defensive pipeline logic, and parameterized functions for reusable pipeline operations. The data models from Module 4 and the SQL patterns from Module 5 combine to produce the complete Silver-to-Gold transformation pipeline that serves HydroAlpes' three teams with purpose-built analytical datasets.

The next phase — engineering discipline (Modules 6–7) — shifts from building pipelines to hardening them. Module 6 covers testing: how do you verify that the MERGE loaded the correct data, that the SCD processing created the right versions, and that the reconciliation EXCEPT returned zero rows? Module 7 covers deployment: how do you promote these pipeline changes through development, staging, and production environments safely, with migration scripts, version tracking, and rollback strategies? The architecture is designed. The patterns are implemented. Now the engineering practices that make them production-reliable are the focus.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 6, you will learn to test every pattern from this module systematically. The MERGE should produce the correct row counts — an assertion query verifies it. The SCD Type 2 pipeline should create exactly one new version per changed attribute — a regression test compares before-and-after snapshots. The EXCEPT reconciliation should return zero rows — but what if it returns rows? Module 6 teaches the testing framework that catches pipeline bugs before they reach production, turning ad-hoc verification into automated, repeatable, and comprehensive quality assurance.

chapters.title: Testing SQL Pipelines
chapters.description: Master systematic testing of SQL transformations with assertion queries, data contract tests, schema validation, statistical regression tests, and CI integration.

blocks.content: In April 2025, Markus refactored the billing CTE chain to improve readability. He renamed the intermediate CTE from filtered_readings to january_readings and adjusted the WHERE clause. The refactored query produced the same row count as the original. Code review approved the change. The billing pipeline ran for two weeks before the analytics team noticed that total consumption was 3% lower than the prior month's trend predicted. Investigation revealed that Markus had accidentally changed the date filter from mr.date < '2025-02-01' to mr.date <= '2025-01-31' — which excluded readings with a date of January 31 stored as a TIMESTAMP ('2025-01-31 00:00:01' is after midnight and does not match <= '2025-01-31'). The row count was the same because the timestamps all had time 00:00:00 in the test environment. Production data had sub-second timestamps from late-arriving corrections.
The Cost of Undetected SQL Bugs

The billing undercount affected two invoice cycles and required manual correction with 10 sub-regions worth of customers. The root cause — a boundary condition in a date filter — would have been caught by a simple assertion test: 'the sum of daily_kw for January must match the sum from the previous run within 0.1%.' The test would have failed on the first run after the refactor, flagging the discrepancy before the pipeline output reached any consumer. The two-week detection delay cost HydroAlpes approximately 40 hours of investigation and correction work. The assertion test would have taken 5 minutes to write and would have caught the bug in 5 seconds.
What SQL Testing Covers

SQL testing is not the same as application testing. There are no unit tests for individual functions (SQL queries are not decomposed into callable units in the same way as Python functions). Instead, SQL testing focuses on the contract between the query and its consumers: does the output have the expected schema, the expected row count, the expected value ranges, and the expected relationships with other tables? This module covers five categories of SQL tests. Assertion queries verify data properties (no negative values, no duplicates, no NULLs in required columns). Schema contract tests verify table structure (columns exist, types match, constraints are present). Statistical regression tests verify that aggregated metrics fall within expected ranges. Referential integrity tests verify cross-table relationships. And CTE-level tests verify that individual transformation steps produce correct intermediate results.

The testing approach is defensive: every test is a SELECT query that returns zero rows when the data is correct and returns violating rows when it is not. This design makes tests composable (combine with UNION ALL), automatable (run in CI, check exit code), and debuggable (the violating rows themselves tell you what went wrong). By the end of this module, you will have a complete testing framework that catches data quality issues, schema drift, and transformation regressions before they reach production consumers.

blocks.title: SQL tests are SQL queries
blocks.content: Every test in this module is a standard SELECT statement — the same SQL you have been writing since the junior track. There is no test framework to install, no special syntax to learn, and no external tooling required. A test is a query that returns zero rows on success. This simplicity is the reason SQL testing scales: any engineer who can write a WHERE clause can write a test, and any pipeline that can run a query can run a test.

An assertion query is a SELECT that returns zero rows when the data meets a condition and returns the violating rows when it does not. The pattern is: SELECT rows WHERE violation_condition. If the result is empty, the assertion passes. If the result has rows, each row is a specific violation that tells you exactly which record failed and why. Assertion queries are the simplest and most common form of SQL test, and they directly encode the business rules that the data must satisfy. At HydroAlpes, every Silver table has at least five assertion queries that run after every pipeline load, and the pipeline is blocked from promoting data to Gold if any assertion fails.
The Five Standard Assertions

-- Assertion 1: No NULL values in required columns
SELECT meter_id, date, 'NULL daily_kw' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw IS NULL
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 2: No negative consumption values
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Negative daily_kw' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw < 0
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 3: No duplicate primary keys
SELECT meter_id, date, COUNT(*) AS copies, 'Duplicate key' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
GROUP BY meter_id, date
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

-- Assertion 4: All regions are valid (referential integrity)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.region, 'Unknown region' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r ON mr.region = r.region_id
WHERE  r.region_id IS NULL
  AND  mr.date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 5: Value range check
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Implausible value' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw > 5000
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

Each assertion follows the same pattern: SELECT the identifying columns plus a violation description WHERE the violation condition is true. The violation column makes each assertion self-documenting — when the pipeline logs the results, the violation type is immediately visible without checking which assertion produced the row. The date filter restricts each assertion to yesterday's data (the most recent pipeline load), keeping the assertions fast even on large tables. At HydroAlpes, the five assertions above run in under 2 seconds total against 1.2 million rows — negligible cost compared to the hours of debugging they prevent.
Combining Assertions

-- Combine all assertions into one query for pipeline integration
-- Zero rows = all assertions pass; any rows = at least one assertion failed
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw::TEXT AS value, violation
FROM (
  SELECT meter_id, date, NULL::DOUBLE PRECISION AS daily_kw, 'NULL daily_kw' AS violation
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw IS NULL AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Negative daily_kw'
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw < 0 AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Implausible value'
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw > 5000 AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
) AS violations
ORDER BY violation, meter_id;

UNION ALL combines multiple assertion queries into a single result set. The pipeline executes this one combined query and checks whether the result is empty. If empty, all assertions passed and the pipeline proceeds to Gold. If rows exist, the pipeline logs the violations, routes the failing data to the quarantine table, and alerts the team. This combined-assertion pattern is the foundation of HydroAlpes' automated data quality gate — a single query that encodes all business rules and produces a machine-readable violation report when any rule is broken.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Store assertion queries in version-controlled .sql files alongside the pipeline code. Each file contains one assertion, named descriptively: assert_no_null_daily_kw.sql, assert_no_duplicates.sql. The pipeline runner executes each file and checks the row count. This separation makes assertions reviewable, versionable, and independently modifiable — changing one assertion does not risk breaking others. At HydroAlpes, the assertion file directory is the single source of truth for what the pipeline guarantees about its output.

Assertion queries verify the data in a table. Schema contract tests verify the table itself: do the expected columns exist, are they the correct types, are the constraints in place, and does the table match the documented specification? Schema drift — where a table's actual structure diverges from its documented specification — is a subtle and common problem. An ALTER TABLE that adds a column during a hotfix, a migration that changes a column type for compatibility, or a dropped constraint that was 'temporary' but never restored — all produce schema drift that assertion queries do not detect because the data in the changed columns might still be valid. Schema contract tests catch the structural changes themselves.
