Z-score and period-over-period for every fact table
Post-pipeline quality DAG task
Quarantine captures all rejected rows
No data is silently dropped — bad rows go to quarantine with reasons
Pipeline code review verifies quarantine routing
Thresholds are calibrated from historical data
Set at 99th percentile of normal variation, reviewed quarterly
Threshold values stored in configuration, not hardcoded

blocks.content: The Weekly Quality Review

Every Monday, HydroAlpes' engineering team reviews the week's quality metrics in a 15-minute standup. The review covers: tables with any failed checks (investigation status and resolution), the quarantine table's growth rate (trending up or down), SLA compliance percentage for timeliness (target: 99.5% of pipeline runs complete before deadline), and any new quality rules added in response to incidents from the previous week. The review is intentionally brief — the dashboard provides the data, and the team focuses on action items rather than analysis. Action items feed into the sprint: a recurring completeness failure becomes a pipeline fix ticket, a growing quarantine becomes a source-system investigation ticket.

The quality framework is the bridge between Modules 6–8 and Module 10. Module 6's tests catch bugs before deployment. Module 7's deployment process prevents ad-hoc schema changes. Module 8's performance tuning keeps queries fast and resource-efficient. Module 9's quality framework monitors the data flowing through the optimized, tested, safely-deployed pipeline, ensuring that the results are not just fast but correct. Module 10 synthesizes all four operational capabilities into the practices that keep HydroAlpes' platform reliable: SLA management, incident diagnosis, capacity planning, and the team workflows that coordinate 12 engineers across 3 teams serving dashboards, billing, and ML consumers.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 10, you will learn to operate the complete platform: SLA definition and enforcement, incident diagnosis workflows, performance regression detection, capacity planning for growing data, backup and recovery, operational runbooks, and the team communication practices that keep a multi-team data platform running reliably. The quality framework from this module provides the observability that Module 10's operational practices depend on — you cannot manage SLAs without freshness metrics, and you cannot diagnose incidents without the quality trend data that the dq_metrics table provides.

chapters.title: Production SQL Operations
chapters.description: Operate a production SQL platform with SLA management, incident diagnosis, performance regression detection, capacity planning, and operational runbooks.

sections.title: From Writing SQL to Operating SQL

blocks.content: When HydroAlpes had one team and two engineers, operating the SQL platform meant Lena checking the pipeline manually every morning. If it succeeded, the day proceeded. If it failed, she fixed it. At mid-level — three teams, 12 engineers, an 06:00 UTC SLA, and consumers who depend on the data for billing, dashboards, and ML feature engineering — manual operation is impossible. The pipeline runs while the team sleeps. Failures must be detected automatically, diagnosed quickly, and resolved before consumers notice. Performance degradation must be caught before it breaches SLAs. Capacity limits must be anticipated before they cause outages. The shift from writing SQL to operating SQL is the shift from building a pipeline to ensuring it runs reliably, every day, at scale.
What Operations Covers

This module covers the operational practices that keep the platform running. SLA definition and monitoring: what does the platform promise to its consumers, and how do you verify the promise is kept? Performance regression detection: how do you catch a query that is gradually slowing down before it breaches an SLA? Incident diagnosis: when something breaks at 04:00, what is the systematic workflow for finding and fixing the problem? Lock contention and deadlocks: what happens when two pipelines try to write to the same table simultaneously? Capacity planning: how do you predict when the current infrastructure will be insufficient? Backup and recovery: how do you protect against data loss? Operational runbooks: how do you document procedures so that any engineer can handle any incident? And team workflows: how do 12 engineers across 3 teams coordinate without stepping on each other?

These are not SQL syntax topics — they are engineering practices that use SQL as a tool. The SLA monitoring query is a SQL query against the dq_metrics table. The performance regression detector is a SQL query against the performance_log table. The deadlock investigation uses pg_locks and pg_stat_activity. Capacity planning uses table size extrapolation queries. Every operational practice in this module is implemented with the SQL skills you have built across Modules 1–9. The new element is not the SQL — it is the operational framework that surrounds it.

blocks.title: Operations is the culmination of every module
blocks.content: Module 1 taught plan reading — used for incident diagnosis. Module 2 taught join optimization — used when a slow join is the root cause. Module 6 taught testing — the automated guard that catches problems before they reach production. Module 7 taught deployment — the controlled process that prevents ad-hoc changes. Module 8 taught tuning — the toolkit for fixing performance problems. Module 9 taught quality monitoring — the observability that detects problems in the data. This module connects them all into an operational system.

An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a measurable promise to a consumer. HydroAlpes' SLAs define what the data platform delivers, when, and how deviations are handled. Without SLAs, 'the pipeline is slow' is a subjective complaint. With SLAs, 'the Gold billing table was not refreshed by 06:00 UTC on 3 days this month' is a measurable breach that triggers an investigation. SLAs convert vague expectations into concrete, monitorable commitments. They also create accountability: the engineering team owns the SLA, and breaches are tracked, reviewed, and resolved — not dismissed as 'it'll be fixed later.'
HydroAlpes' SLA Definitions

rows: Billing team
Gold billing summary refreshed by 06:00 UTC daily
MAX(loaded_at) on gold.regional_consumption < 06:00
99.5% of days (≤ 1.5 breaches per month)
Analytics dashboards
Silver data current within 2 hours of source delivery
Freshness metric from Module 9 dq_metrics table
99% of hourly checks pass
ML team
Wide table refreshed by 07:00 UTC daily
MAX(loaded_at) on gold.readings_wide < 07:00
98% of days
Data quality
All 6-dimension quality checks pass for Silver fact tables
Pass rate from dq_metrics
95% daily pass rate across all dimensions

blocks.content: SLA Monitoring Query

-- SLA compliance: what percentage of days met the billing SLA this month?
WITH daily_completion AS (
  SELECT date_trunc('day', loaded_at) AS load_day,
         MAX(loaded_at) AS completion_time,
         MAX(loaded_at)::TIME < TIME '06:00' AS met_sla
  FROM   gold.regional_consumption
  WHERE  loaded_at >= date_trunc('month', CURRENT_DATE)
  GROUP BY date_trunc('day', loaded_at)
)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS days_measured,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE met_sla) AS days_met,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE NOT met_sla) AS days_breached,
       ROUND(
         COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE met_sla)::NUMERIC / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) * 100, 1
       ) AS compliance_pct
FROM   daily_completion;

The query computes monthly SLA compliance for the billing refresh: 27 of 28 days met the 06:00 deadline (96.4%). The target is 99.5% (roughly 1.5 breaches per month), so the single breach this month is within tolerance. If the compliance drops below 99.5% — 2 or more breaches — the engineering team investigates the root causes and adds them to the sprint backlog. At HydroAlpes, SLA compliance is computed daily and displayed on the team's operational dashboard alongside the quality metrics from Module 9. The SLA query runs as a scheduled Airflow task that writes to a monitoring.sla_compliance table, enabling month-over-month trend analysis.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Set SLA targets that are achievable but not trivial. A 100% target means every single breach triggers an incident — which creates alert fatigue and discourages ambitious infrastructure changes. A 99.5% target allows 1–2 breaches per month for maintenance, upgrades, and rare external failures. At HydroAlpes, the billing SLA is 99.5%, the dashboard freshness SLA is 99%, and the ML wide table SLA is 98% — reflecting each consumer's tolerance for delayed data. The billing team needs data every morning without exception. The ML team can retrain with yesterday's features if today's are delayed.

A query that runs in 8 seconds today may run in 12 seconds next week, 18 seconds the week after, and 45 seconds a month later — at which point someone notices that the pipeline is 'suddenly slow.' The slowdown was not sudden. It was gradual, caused by growing data volume, accumulating table bloat, or stale statistics drifting further from reality. Performance regression detection catches these gradual degradations by tracking query execution times over time and alerting when a query's performance deviates significantly from its historical baseline. The alert fires when the query goes from 8 seconds to 12 — not when it reaches 45.
The Performance Log Table

-- Performance log: record execution time for each pipeline query
CREATE TABLE monitoring.performance_log (
    log_id           BIGSERIAL    PRIMARY KEY,
    query_name       VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'billing_gold_refresh'
    executed_at      TIMESTAMPTZ  NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    execution_ms     INTEGER      NOT NULL,
    rows_affected    BIGINT,
    plan_hash        VARCHAR(64),  -- MD5 of EXPLAIN output to detect plan changes
    notes            TEXT
);

-- The pipeline logs execution time after each query
-- Example (in the pipeline script):
-- start_time = clock_timestamp();
-- EXECUTE the billing query;
-- INSERT INTO monitoring.performance_log (query_name, execution_ms, rows_affected)
-- VALUES ('billing_gold_refresh',
--         EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM clock_timestamp() - start_time) * 1000,
--         row_count);
Regression Detection Query

-- Detect queries whose recent performance deviates > 50% from baseline
-- Baseline: average execution time over the previous 4 weeks
-- Recent: average execution time over the last 7 days
WITH baseline AS (
  SELECT query_name,
         AVG(execution_ms) AS baseline_ms,
         STDDEV(execution_ms) AS baseline_stddev
  FROM   monitoring.performance_log
  WHERE  executed_at >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '35 days'
    AND  executed_at <  CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7 days'
  GROUP BY query_name
),
recent AS (
  SELECT query_name,
         AVG(execution_ms) AS recent_ms
  FROM   monitoring.performance_log
  WHERE  executed_at >= CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '7 days'
  GROUP BY query_name
)
SELECT b.query_name,
       ROUND(b.baseline_ms) AS baseline_ms,
       ROUND(r.recent_ms)   AS recent_ms,
       ROUND((r.recent_ms - b.baseline_ms) / NULLIF(b.baseline_ms, 0) * 100, 1)
         AS pct_change,
       CASE
         WHEN r.recent_ms > b.baseline_ms * 1.5 THEN 'REGRESSION'
         WHEN r.recent_ms > b.baseline_ms * 1.2 THEN 'WARNING'
         ELSE 'STABLE'
       END AS status
FROM   baseline b
  INNER JOIN recent r ON b.query_name = r.query_name
WHERE  r.recent_ms > b.baseline_ms * 1.2
ORDER BY pct_change DESC;

query_name                | baseline_ms | recent_ms | pct_change | status
--------------------------+-------------+-----------+------------+-----------
capacity_forecast_refresh |        4200 |      7140 |       70.0 | REGRESSION
billing_gold_refresh      |        8100 |      9720 |       20.0 | WARNING
ml_wide_table_refresh     |       15400 |     15800 |        2.6 | (filtered)

The regression detection query compares each pipeline query's recent average execution time (last 7 days) against its baseline (previous 4 weeks). A query that increased by more than 50% is flagged as REGRESSION — requiring immediate investigation. A query that increased by 20–50% is flagged as WARNING — requiring attention in the next sprint. The capacity_forecast_refresh query degraded 70% from its baseline, indicating a problem: perhaps table growth pushed the query past an index scan tipping point, or stale statistics caused a plan change. The plan_hash column in performance_log helps: if the hash changed between the baseline and recent periods, the optimizer changed the plan — and comparing the two plans reveals what changed.

blocks.title: Catching regressions early is cheaper than fixing incidents
blocks.content: A 20% regression detected this week becomes a 15-minute investigation and a targeted fix (run ANALYZE, add an index, adjust work_mem). The same regression left undetected for 3 months becomes a 70% degradation, an SLA breach, and a multi-hour incident investigation. The performance log and regression detection query are the early warning system. At HydroAlpes, the regression query runs weekly and produces an email report to the engineering team. Regressions caught at the WARNING stage have never progressed to an SLA breach.

At 04:12 UTC, the on-call engineer receives a PagerDuty alert: 'billing_gold_refresh failed with error: canceling statement due to statement timeout.' The pipeline did not complete. The Gold billing table has yesterday's data, not today's. The 06:00 SLA is 108 minutes away. The engineer must diagnose the failure, determine the root cause, apply a fix, and verify the pipeline runs successfully — all before 06:00. This is incident diagnosis: a systematic, time-pressured workflow for identifying and resolving production failures. This lesson covers the five-step diagnosis workflow and the five most common root causes at HydroAlpes.
The Five-Step Workflow

Step 1 (Identify): read the error message and determine which query failed. The Airflow task log shows the exact SQL statement and the PostgreSQL error. Step 2 (Reproduce): run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the failed query in a read-only session to see the execution plan without modifying data. Step 3 (Diagnose): read the plan using the Module 1 diagnostic checklist — check for sequential scans, hash spill, bad row estimates, and lock waits. Step 4 (Fix): apply the targeted fix based on the diagnosis — run ANALYZE, increase work_mem, kill a blocking session, or restart a stalled vacuum. Step 5 (Verify): rerun the pipeline and confirm it completes within the SLA window. If time permits, add the root cause and fix to the incident log for the post-incident review.
The Five Most Common Root Causes

rows: Stale statistics
Statement timeout; plan shows bad row estimates
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: estimated rows << actual rows
Run ANALYZE on the affected table
Missing index
Statement timeout; plan shows Seq Scan with high filter rejection
Seq Scan with Rows Removed >> rows returned
Create the missing index; run ANALYZE
Lock contention
Statement timeout; query waiting for lock
pg_stat_activity shows waiting; pg_locks shows blocker
Identify and terminate the blocking session
Hash spill to disk
Statement timeout; plan shows hash join with temp blocks
BUFFERS: temp read/write > 0 on hash join
Increase work_mem for the session
Data volume growth
Gradual slowdown; plan unchanged but data larger
Table size increased; same plan takes longer on more data
Partition the table, add indexes, or optimize the query

blocks.content:
-- Quick diagnosis: check for blocking sessions and lock waits
SELECT blocked.pid     AS blocked_pid,
       blocked.query   AS blocked_query,
       blocking.pid    AS blocking_pid,
       blocking.query  AS blocking_query,
       blocking.state  AS blocking_state
FROM   pg_stat_activity AS blocked
  JOIN pg_locks AS bl ON blocked.pid = bl.pid AND NOT bl.granted
  JOIN pg_locks AS gl ON bl.locktype = gl.locktype
                     AND bl.relation = gl.relation
                     AND bl.pid <> gl.pid AND gl.granted
  JOIN pg_stat_activity AS blocking ON gl.pid = blocking.pid
WHERE  blocked.wait_event_type = 'Lock';

This query identifies lock contention: which session is blocked, which session is blocking it, and what query each is running. If the billing pipeline is blocked by a long-running analytics query that holds a lock on the meter_readings table, the fix is either to terminate the analytics query (pg_terminate_backend(blocking_pid)) or to wait for it to complete if it is close to finishing. At HydroAlpes, lock contention between the pipeline and analytics queries is the third most common incident cause — resolved by scheduling the pipeline during a window when analytics queries are not running, and by setting statement_timeout on analytics connections to prevent long-running queries from holding locks indefinitely.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Keep a pre-written diagnosis script that runs the most common diagnostic queries in sequence: check pg_stat_activity for active queries, check pg_locks for contention, check pg_stat_user_tables for stale statistics, and check pg_stat_user_indexes for unused indexes. At HydroAlpes, the on-call engineer runs SELECT * FROM hydroalpes.diagnose_incident() — a stored function that produces a consolidated diagnostic report in under 2 seconds. The function does not fix anything — it provides the information the engineer needs to make the fix decision.

When two sessions write to the same table simultaneously, they acquire locks to prevent data corruption. Session A's INSERT acquires a RowExclusiveLock on meter_readings. Session B's ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN requires an AccessExclusiveLock on the same table — which blocks until Session A's lock is released. If Session A's INSERT takes 3 minutes, Session B waits 3 minutes. If Session B is the billing pipeline and Session A is a maintenance script that the DBA forgot was running, the billing pipeline is delayed by the maintenance script's runtime. Lock contention is the operational scenario where two legitimate operations interfere with each other through the locking mechanism.
Lock Types and Compatibility

PostgreSQL uses multiple lock types with different levels of exclusivity. RowShareLock (from SELECT FOR UPDATE) is compatible with other RowShareLocks — multiple sessions can select for update simultaneously. RowExclusiveLock (from INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) is compatible with other RowExclusiveLocks — multiple sessions can write simultaneously (they lock individual rows, not the table). AccessExclusiveLock (from ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE, TRUNCATE) is incompatible with every other lock — it blocks all concurrent access. The operational implication: INSERTs do not block each other (safe to run concurrent pipelines), but ALTER TABLE blocks everything (must be scheduled during a quiet window).
Deadlocks

A deadlock occurs when two sessions each hold a lock that the other needs. Session A locks table X and tries to lock table Y. Session B locks table Y and tries to lock table X. Neither can proceed because each is waiting for the other. PostgreSQL detects deadlocks automatically (within deadlock_timeout, default 1 second) and terminates one of the sessions with the error 'deadlock detected.' The terminated session's transaction is rolled back; the surviving session proceeds. At HydroAlpes, a deadlock occurred when the billing pipeline and the quality monitoring pipeline both tried to update dq_metrics and regional_consumption in opposite order. The fix: ensure both pipelines update tables in the same order (alphabetical by table name), eliminating the circular dependency.

-- Monitor for lock contention in real time
SELECT pid, usename, state, wait_event_type, wait_event,
       query_start, NOW() - query_start AS duration,
       LEFT(query, 80) AS query_preview
FROM   pg_stat_activity
WHERE  state = 'active'
   OR  wait_event_type = 'Lock'
ORDER BY query_start;

The monitoring query shows all active queries and all queries waiting for locks. The wait_event_type = 'Lock' filter identifies sessions that are blocked. The duration column shows how long each query has been running or waiting. At HydroAlpes, this query runs continuously on the operational dashboard (refreshed every 10 seconds), providing real-time visibility into lock contention. A query waiting for a lock for more than 60 seconds triggers a Slack notification to #data-platform. A query waiting for more than 300 seconds triggers a PagerDuty alert because it is likely to cause an SLA breach.
Prevention Strategies

Lock contention is best prevented, not diagnosed after the fact. HydroAlpes uses three prevention strategies. First, schedule DDL operations (ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX) during the 03:00–04:00 maintenance window when no pipeline or analytics queries are running. Second, use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY instead of CREATE INDEX — the concurrent variant acquires a weaker lock that does not block reads or writes, though it takes longer to build. Third, ensure all pipelines that modify multiple tables do so in a consistent order (alphabetical) to prevent deadlocks. These practices have reduced lock-related incidents from weekly (in the first month of multi-team operations) to quarterly.

blocks.title: ALTER TABLE blocks everything
blocks.content: ALTER TABLE acquires an AccessExclusiveLock that blocks all concurrent reads, writes, and other DDL operations on the affected table. On a table that is read continuously by dashboards and written to by the pipeline, an ALTER TABLE during business hours blocks all consumers until it completes. At HydroAlpes, the deployment standards from Module 7 require that ALTER TABLE migrations run during the 03:00–04:00 window. The one exception: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN, which in PostgreSQL 11+ acquires only a brief AccessExclusiveLock for the catalog update and does not rewrite the table.

HydroAlpes' meter_readings table grows by 1.2 million rows per day — 37.2 million per month, 438 million per year. At this rate, the table will reach 1 billion rows in approximately 18 months. Queries that run in 8 seconds on 130 million rows may take 50 seconds on 1 billion rows if the execution plan does not change — but the plan will likely change, because the optimizer recalculates cost estimates as the table grows, potentially switching from an index scan to a sequential scan when the selectivity crosses a threshold. Capacity planning anticipates these inflection points and triggers preemptive action (partitioning, index optimization, hardware scaling) before they cause performance problems.
Growth Projection

-- Project table size growth based on recent insertion rate
WITH monthly_growth AS (
  SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', loaded_at) AS month,
         COUNT(*) AS rows_added,
         pg_size_pretty(SUM(pg_column_size(mr.*))::BIGINT) AS data_added
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  loaded_at >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '6 months'
  GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', loaded_at)
  ORDER BY month
)
SELECT month,
       rows_added,
       data_added,
       SUM(rows_added) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS cumulative_rows
FROM   monthly_growth;

The growth is linear: approximately 37 million rows and 890 MB per month. At this rate, the table adds 10.7 GB per year in data alone (plus indexes, which typically add 60–80% overhead). The projection enables three planning decisions. First, storage: will the current disk have space for 2 years of growth? (Current: 3.2 TB table + 2 TB indexes; projected 2-year addition: 21 GB data + 17 GB indexes = manageable.) Second, performance: when will the table cross thresholds that affect query plans? (The 100M-row partitioning threshold was crossed at month 3; monthly partitions are already in place.) Third, maintenance: will VACUUM and ANALYZE complete within their maintenance windows as the table grows? (Current VACUUM: 22 minutes; projected at 1B rows: approximately 3 hours — may need partitioned VACUUM.)
Infrastructure Scaling Triggers

rows: Table exceeds 100M rows
pg_class.reltuples > 100,000,000
Partition by month (already done for meter_readings)
Disk usage exceeds 70%
df output or cloud storage alert
Expand storage or archive old partitions to cold storage
VACUUM exceeds maintenance window
pg_stat_user_tables.last_autovacuum duration > 2 hours
Tune autovacuum parameters or run VACUUM per partition
Query performance baseline degraded 50%+
performance_log regression detection
Investigate plan changes; add/optimize indexes; review work_mem
Connection pool saturation
PgBouncer pool exhaustion alerts
Increase pool size or optimize connection usage patterns

blocks.title: Capacity planning is projection, not reaction
blocks.content: Reacting to capacity limits (disk full, queries timing out, connections exhausted) produces incidents. Projecting capacity limits 3–6 months in advance produces planned maintenance windows and orderly upgrades. At HydroAlpes, the quarterly performance review (Module 8, lesson 10) includes a capacity projection for every monitored table, every index, and every materialized view. The projection identifies which resources will exceed their thresholds in the next quarter, and the infrastructure team pre-provisions the upgrades.

All the performance tuning, quality monitoring, and deployment discipline in the world cannot protect against hardware failure, accidental data deletion, or ransomware. Backup and recovery is the last line of defense: when everything else fails, the ability to restore the database to a known-good state is the difference between a 30-minute recovery and a permanent data loss. This lesson covers the three backup strategies that HydroAlpes uses, the recovery procedures for each, and the testing practice that verifies backups actually work before a disaster occurs.
Three Backup Strategies

rows: Logical backup
pg_dump / pg_restore
Hours (depends on database size)
Schema migration testing, cross-version upgrades, selective table restore
Physical backup
pg_basebackup
Minutes to hours (file copy + WAL replay)
Full database restore after hardware failure
Point-in-time recovery
WAL archiving + pg_basebackup
Minutes to hours (base backup + WAL replay to target time)
Recovering from accidental data deletion or corruption

blocks.content: Logical backups (pg_dump) produce SQL scripts that recreate the schema and data. They are portable across PostgreSQL versions and allow selective restore (restore one table, not the entire database). But they are slow for large databases: dumping 3.2 TB of meter_readings takes hours. Physical backups (pg_basebackup) copy the database files directly, which is faster but produces a backup that can only be restored to the same PostgreSQL major version. Point-in-time recovery (PITR) combines physical backups with continuous WAL archiving, enabling restoration to any timestamp — not just the backup time. At HydroAlpes, all three strategies are used: logical backups for monthly archival, physical backups nightly, and continuous WAL archiving for PITR capability.
The Recovery Test

A backup that has never been tested is not a backup — it is a hope. HydroAlpes runs a quarterly recovery test: restore the latest physical backup to a test server, replay WAL to a target timestamp, run the schema contract tests from Module 6 to verify the schema is intact, and run a subset of assertion queries to verify data integrity. The test takes approximately 2 hours and is documented in a runbook. The test has caught two issues: once, WAL archiving had silently stopped due to a full archive disk, and the backup was 3 days old instead of the expected minutes. Once, a physical backup was corrupted by a disk error during the copy. Both issues would have been catastrophic during a real disaster but were caught and fixed during the quarterly test.

blocks.title: Untested backups are unreliable
blocks.content: The backup system can fail silently: WAL archiving stops, backup scripts fail without alerting, storage fills up and truncates the backup. The only way to know the backup works is to restore it. At HydroAlpes, the quarterly recovery test is a non-negotiable operational practice. The test result (pass/fail, recovery time, data integrity check results) is recorded in the operational log and reviewed by Lena. A failed recovery test is treated as a P1 incident — because it means the platform has no disaster recovery capability until the backup system is fixed.

An operational runbook is a documented procedure for handling a specific operational scenario: a pipeline failure, a lock contention incident, a backup restoration, or a schema migration. The runbook specifies the trigger (what event initiates the procedure), the diagnosis steps (what to check and in what order), the resolution steps (what to do to fix the problem), and the verification steps (how to confirm the fix worked). Runbooks ensure that any engineer on the team — not just the expert who built the pipeline — can handle any incident. They are the operational equivalent of the code documentation from Module 7: they make institutional knowledge explicit, version-controlled, and accessible.
HydroAlpes' Core Runbooks

rows: Stale Silver Data
Freshness alert: data > 2 hours old
Check source system status → check Airflow task logs → check staging table → restart pipeline if source is available
Failed Gold Refresh
Pipeline failure alert
Read error message → run EXPLAIN ANALYZE → diagnose per 5-cause table → apply fix → rerun → verify SLA
Lock Contention
Query waiting > 60 seconds
Identify blocker via pg_stat_activity → assess blocker's importance → terminate or wait → rerun blocked query
Quality Alert (Critical)
dq_metrics failure on completeness < 80%
Block Gold refresh → check source completeness → check pipeline logs → resolve or escalate → unblock Gold
Database Recovery
Hardware failure or data corruption detected
Assess damage scope → choose recovery strategy → restore from backup → verify with schema + data tests → resume operations

blocks.content: Runbook Maintenance

Runbooks are living documents that must be updated after every incident. If the on-call engineer follows the 'Failed Gold Refresh' runbook and discovers a root cause not covered by the five-cause table (for example, a disk-full condition on the temp tablespace), the runbook is updated to include the new cause, its diagnostic signal, and its fix. This update is part of the post-incident review (lesson 9) — not a separate task that gets deprioritized. At HydroAlpes, runbook updates are tracked as action items in the post-incident review document, with a designated owner and a due date. The runbook repository is version-controlled alongside the pipeline code, so changes are reviewed and approved through the standard PR process.

The quality of a runbook is measured by whether a new team member can follow it without asking for help. If the runbook says 'check the pipeline logs' but does not specify where the logs are or how to access them, a new engineer is stuck. At HydroAlpes, every new engineer follows each runbook during their onboarding week (in a sandbox environment), and any step that requires clarification is updated immediately. This onboarding-driven improvement cycle ensures that runbooks remain clear and current — because the people who find ambiguities are the same people who need the runbooks most.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: A runbook should be executable by any engineer on the team within the SLA window. If the billing Gold refresh fails at 04:12 and the SLA is 06:00, the on-call engineer has 108 minutes. The runbook must be completable within that window — including diagnosis, fix, verification, and documentation. If a runbook typically requires 90 minutes, the team has 18 minutes of margin. If it requires 120 minutes, the team must either shorten the runbook (automate diagnosis steps) or extend the SLA window. At HydroAlpes, every runbook includes an estimated completion time, and runbooks that exceed 60 minutes are flagged for automation.

Technical practices — testing, deployment, monitoring, runbooks — are necessary but not sufficient for reliable operations. The human coordination layer determines whether those practices actually work under pressure: who gets paged at 04:00, who has authority to terminate a blocking query, who communicates the incident to stakeholders, and who leads the post-incident review. At HydroAlpes, the three-team structure (engineering, analytics, ML) creates coordination challenges that a single-team operation does not face: the analytics team's dashboard query may be blocking the engineering team's pipeline, and resolving the contention requires cross-team communication and decision-making.
The On-Call Rotation

HydroAlpes' on-call rotation cycles through the engineering team's 6 engineers on a weekly basis. The on-call engineer is responsible for responding to all production alerts within 15 minutes during the 03:00–09:00 pipeline window, and within 60 minutes during business hours. The on-call engineer has authority to terminate blocking queries, restart pipelines, and invoke runbooks without approval. Escalation to Lena (the engineering lead) is required for decisions that affect other teams: terminating an analytics query, blocking the Gold refresh, or invoking disaster recovery. The rotation schedule is published 4 weeks in advance, and swaps are coordinated through a shared calendar.
Incident Communication

When an incident occurs, the on-call engineer follows a communication protocol with three stages. Stage 1 (Acknowledge): within 15 minutes of the alert, post in #data-platform-incidents on Slack: 'Investigating: [description].' This tells the team that someone is on it. Stage 2 (Update): every 30 minutes during an active incident, post an update: 'Root cause identified as [X], applying fix [Y], estimated resolution: [time].' This keeps stakeholders informed without requiring them to ask. Stage 3 (Resolve): when the incident is resolved, post the resolution: 'Resolved: [description]. Root cause: [X]. Fix: [Y]. SLA impact: [met/breached by N minutes].' The resolution post becomes the seed for the post-incident review.
Post-Incident Reviews

Every incident that breaches an SLA or affects a consumer receives a post-incident review within 5 business days. The review is blameless: it focuses on what happened, why, and how to prevent recurrence — not on who made a mistake. The review document has four sections. Timeline: a minute-by-minute account of the incident from alert to resolution. Root cause: the technical cause (stale statistics, lock contention, source system failure) and the process cause (missing automation, insufficient monitoring, unclear runbook). Action items: specific improvements with owners and due dates. Lessons learned: insights that the team should internalize. At HydroAlpes, the post-incident review is the primary mechanism for platform improvement — every incident makes the platform stronger by adding a test, updating a runbook, or automating a manual step.

blocks.title: Blameless culture enables honest analysis
blocks.content: If the post-incident review assigns blame, engineers hide their mistakes and the review produces sanitized narratives that miss the real lessons. If the review is blameless — focused on systems and processes, not on individuals — engineers share what actually happened, including the wrong turns and the lucky breaks. At HydroAlpes, Lena opens every post-incident review with: 'We are here to make the system better, not to find fault. What happened, and what can we change so it does not happen again?'

Ten modules. One hundred lessons. Fifty-three hours. You started this mid-level track able to write correct analytical SQL — joins, CTEs, window functions, aggregations. You end it able to operate a production SQL platform: reading execution plans to diagnose performance problems, designing indexes and partitions for scale, modeling data for multiple consumers with slowly changing dimensions and temporal tables, implementing idempotent pipelines with MERGE and upsert patterns, testing transformations systematically with assertion queries and regression tests, deploying safely with versioned migrations and CI/CD pipelines, tuning performance with targeted diagnostics, monitoring quality with automated frameworks, and operating reliably with SLAs, incident workflows, and runbooks.
The HydroAlpes Platform

HydroAlpes' data platform has evolved across this course. The billing query that opened Module 1 — 12 minutes, no index, stale statistics — now runs in 3 seconds with a composite index, fresh statistics from pipeline-integrated ANALYZE, and partition pruning on a monthly-partitioned meter_readings table. The fan-out incident from Module 2 — doubled billing totals from duplicate dimension keys — is prevented by automated row-count preservation tests that run after every join. The SCD Type 2 dimension processing from Module 4 is implemented with MERGE and tested with golden-dataset regression tests. The quality framework from Module 9 monitors six dimensions across every Silver table, with dashboards that Lena checks every Monday and alerts that wake the on-call engineer at 04:00 when the billing pipeline fails.

The platform serves three teams. The analytics team queries the star schema Gold layer through views that abstract the join complexity. The ML team reads the denormalized wide table for feature engineering. The billing team consumes the materialized view that is refreshed nightly with concurrent refresh. Each consumer has an SLA, each SLA is monitored, and each breach triggers the incident workflow that produces a post-incident review and a platform improvement. The platform is not a collection of queries — it is an operational system with testing, deployment, monitoring, and incident management practices that keep it reliable as data grows, teams expand, and requirements evolve.
What Comes Next

The SQL Senior-Level Track picks up where this course ends. You know how to operate a production SQL platform for one company with three teams. The senior track teaches you to operate at enterprise scale: multi-tenant platforms serving dozens of teams, schema registries and data contracts across organizational boundaries, advanced optimizer internals for extreme-scale query tuning, and the governance frameworks that ensure data quality, security, and compliance across a large organization. The shift from mid-level to senior is the shift from operating one platform to designing the infrastructure that enables many platforms to coexist safely and efficiently.

blocks.title: You are ready for production
blocks.content: You can read a query plan and diagnose why a query is slow. You can design indexes, partitions, and materialized views that make it fast. You can model data for three different consumer teams with star schemas, SCDs, and wide tables. You can write idempotent pipelines with MERGE and test them with assertion queries and regression suites. You can deploy safely with versioned migrations and CI/CD. You can monitor quality with automated frameworks and respond to incidents with documented runbooks. These are not classroom skills — they are the practices that mid-level data engineers use every day to keep production platforms running. The foundation is built. The platform is yours to operate.

modules.title: Query Plans and the Optimizer
modules.description: Read and interpret query execution plans, understand optimizer decisions, and use plan analysis to verify that the database executes queries the way you intend.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' billing aggregation query had run reliably for six months. Every morning at 05:30, the pipeline computed regional consumption totals from the Silver meter_readings table, joined with the meters dimension for customer type, and wrote the results to the Gold billing summary. The query took 4 seconds. Then HydroAlpes expanded from 4 regions to 10 sub-regions and the meter count grew from 350,000 to 1.2 million. Nobody changed the query. The logic was identical. But the next morning, the billing pipeline took 47 seconds — and the morning after, after a full month of data had accumulated in the new partitioning scheme, it took 3 minutes and 40 seconds. The query was correct. The data was correct. The execution plan had changed, and nobody noticed because nobody looked.
The Mid-Level Shift

In the junior-level track, you learned to trust the database: write SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, and let the optimizer choose the fastest execution strategy. That trust was appropriate when you were learning the language on tables with thousands or hundreds of thousands of rows. At mid-level scale — 1.2 million meters, 1.3 billion historical rows, 3.2 TB of Silver data — the optimizer's decisions have outsized consequences. A sequential scan on a 50-million-row table reads every row from disk. An index scan on the same table reads only the matching rows. The difference is not 10% — it is 100x. A hash join that fits in memory completes in seconds. The same join that spills to disk because work_mem is too small takes minutes. These are not code bugs. They are plan inefficiencies that are invisible unless you read the plan.

The execution plan is the contract between your SQL and the database engine. Your query describes what data you want. The plan describes how the engine will retrieve it: which tables to scan, which indexes to use, which join algorithms to apply, which sort strategies to employ, and in what order. When the plan matches your intent — index scan for selective filters, hash join for equi-joins, partition pruning for date-ranged queries — the query is efficient. When it does not, the query is wasteful, and no amount of SQL rewriting will help unless you understand why the optimizer made the choice it made. Reading plans is the diagnostic skill that underpins every performance optimization in this course.
The Incident Diagnosis

-- The billing query that slowed from 4 seconds to 3 minutes 40 seconds
-- The SQL is correct. The plan is the problem.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT mr.region,
       m.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS active_meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, m.customer_type
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

QUERY PLAN
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Sort  (cost=892341.20..892341.30 rows=30 width=52)
       (actual time=219847.112..219847.118 rows=30 loops=1)
   Sort Key: (sum(mr.daily_kw)) DESC
   ->  HashAggregate  (cost=892338.10..892340.60 rows=30 width=52)
             (actual time=219846.892..219846.988 rows=30 loops=1)
         ->  Hash Join  (cost=12840.00..855838.10 rows=36500000 width=28)
                   (actual time=412.204..198432.761 rows=37200000 loops=1)
               Hash Cond: (mr.meter_id = m.meter_id)
               ->  Seq Scan on meter_readings mr  (cost=0.00..684210.00 rows=37200000 width=36)
                         (actual time=0.028..98712.341 rows=37200000 loops=1)
                     Filter: ((date >= '2025-01-01') AND (date < '2025-02-01'))
                     Rows Removed by Filter: 92800000
               ->  Hash  (cost=10200.00..10200.00 rows=1200000 width=28)
                         (actual time=398.112..398.112 rows=1200000 loops=1)
                     ->  Seq Scan on meters m  (cost=0.00..10200.00 rows=1200000 width=28)
 Planning Time: 2.841 ms
 Execution Time: 219848.204 ms

The plan reveals two problems. First, the Seq Scan on meter_readings reads all 130 million rows (37.2M January + 92.8M filtered out) instead of using an index on the date column — the 92.8 million rows removed by the filter were read from disk for nothing. Second, the Hash Join builds a hash table from the entire 1.2-million-row meters table, which is correct but would be faster with an index-based nested loop if the readings side were reduced by an index scan first. The execution time is 219 seconds — 3 minutes and 40 seconds. An index scan on date would reduce the readings scan from 130 million rows to 37.2 million, and the total execution from 219 seconds to under 10 seconds. The fix is not a query rewrite. It is an index: CREATE INDEX idx_readings_date ON silver.meter_readings (date).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: HydroAlpes' new rule after the incident: every query that runs on a schedule must have its EXPLAIN ANALYZE output reviewed before deployment. The review checks for sequential scans on large tables, unexpected join strategies, and estimated-versus-actual row count discrepancies. This 2-minute review prevents the kind of slow degradation that went unnoticed for three weeks.

EXPLAIN is the statement that reveals the optimizer's plan without executing the query. In PostgreSQL, EXPLAIN SELECT ... prints the planned execution tree: which operations the database will perform, in what order, and the optimizer's cost and row estimates for each step. The plan reads bottom-to-top and inside-out: the deepest indented operation runs first (typically a table scan), and each parent operation processes its children's output. Learning to read EXPLAIN output fluently is the foundational skill of this module — every subsequent lesson builds on the ability to look at a plan and understand what the database intends to do.
The Operator Vocabulary

rows: Seq Scan
Reads every row in the table from disk
Efficient for large result sets; problematic when filter is selective and an index exists
Index Scan
Uses an index to find matching rows, then fetches from table
Fast for selective filters; check that the index matches the filter columns
Bitmap Index Scan + Bitmap Heap Scan
Uses index to build a bitmap of matching pages, then fetches pages
Good for medium selectivity; combines multiple index lookups
Index Only Scan
Reads entirely from the index without touching the table
Fastest scan type; requires a covering index and up-to-date visibility map
Hash Join
Builds a hash table from the smaller input, probes with the larger
Fast for equi-joins; watch for hash table spill when work_mem is too small
Merge Join
Merges two sorted inputs in a single pass
Efficient when both inputs are already sorted (from indexes or prior sorts)
Nested Loop
For each row in the outer input, scans the inner input
Fast when outer is very small; catastrophic when both are large
HashAggregate
Groups rows using a hash table and applies aggregate functions
Fast; watch for memory pressure with high-cardinality group keys
Sort
Sorts rows by specified columns
Expensive on large datasets; check if an index could provide pre-sorted input

blocks.content: A Plan Walkthrough

-- A simple aggregation query
EXPLAIN
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 Sort  (cost=18510.04..18510.05 rows=4 width=40)
   Sort Key: (sum(daily_kw)) DESC
   ->  HashAggregate  (cost=18509.90..18509.98 rows=4 width=40)
         Group Key: region
         ->  Index Scan using idx_readings_date on meter_readings mr
                   (cost=0.56..16259.90 rows=1200000 width=38)
               Index Cond: (date = '2025-01-15'::date)

Reading bottom-to-top: the Index Scan on meter_readings uses the idx_readings_date index to find rows where date = '2025-01-15', estimating 1.2 million rows (one day of readings for 1.2M meters). The HashAggregate groups by region and computes SUM(daily_kw), estimating 4 result rows (the 4 original regions — the plan was generated before the sub-region expansion). The Sort orders the 4 rows by total_kw descending. Each operator shows a cost estimate in the format (startup_cost..total_cost rows=estimated_rows width=bytes_per_row). The total cost of the final Sort (18510.05) is what the optimizer uses to compare this plan against alternatives.
Reading Indentation

Indentation represents parent-child relationships. The Index Scan is a child of the HashAggregate — it produces the rows that the aggregate consumes. The HashAggregate is a child of the Sort — it produces the grouped rows that the sort orders. The arrow (-->) in the text output indicates 'feeds into.' Data flows from bottom to top: the scan produces rows, the aggregate groups them, the sort orders them. When plans have multiple children — as in joins, where the left and right inputs are both children of the join operator — the indentation branches. Reading these branches correctly is essential for understanding join plans, which you will learn in lesson 5.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When reading a plan, start at the bottom (the scan) and work up. At each operator, ask: is this the right strategy? For scans: is it using an index for a selective filter? For joins: is the algorithm appropriate for the table sizes? For aggregates: is the group key cardinality reasonable? For sorts: could an index provide the order? These four questions catch the majority of plan inefficiencies.

EXPLAIN shows what the optimizer plans to do. EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually runs the query and shows both the plan and the real execution metrics: actual time, actual row counts, and actual loop counts alongside the estimated values. The comparison between estimated and actual is the most powerful diagnostic tool in query optimization. When the optimizer estimates 1,000 rows but the query produces 1.2 million, every cost calculation based on that estimate is wrong — and the plan built on those calculations is likely suboptimal. Stale or missing statistics are the most common cause, and EXPLAIN ANALYZE is how you detect them.
The Syntax

-- EXPLAIN ANALYZE: runs the query and shows actual metrics
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT mr.region,
       COUNT(*) AS reading_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
GROUP BY mr.region;

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 HashAggregate  (cost=17891.50..17891.60 rows=10 width=18)
               (actual time=4821.432..4821.448 rows=10 loops=1)
   Group Key: region
   Buffers: shared hit=1204 read=48210
   ->  Seq Scan on meter_readings mr  (cost=0.00..17016.50 rows=350000 width=10)
                 (actual time=0.031..4412.892 rows=912000 loops=1)
         Filter: ((date = '2025-01-15') AND (customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'))
         Rows Removed by Filter: 36288000
         Buffers: shared hit=1204 read=48210
 Planning Time: 0.412 ms
 Execution Time: 4821.891 ms

Two critical discrepancies. First, the Seq Scan estimated 350,000 rows but actually produced 912,000 — the statistics underestimated the number of residential meters on January 15 by 2.6x. The optimizer built a plan expecting a small result set, but the actual data was nearly three times larger. Second, 36.3 million rows were read and discarded by the filter. The Seq Scan read every row in the table (37.2 million) to find 912,000 matching ones — a 2.4% selectivity that an index scan would handle far more efficiently. The BUFFERS output shows 48,210 pages read from disk, confirming heavy I/O. An index on (date, customer_type) would reduce this to approximately 1,200 pages.
Estimated vs Actual — The Three Signals

rows: Actual rows >> estimated rows
The optimizer underestimated data volume and may have chosen a plan for small data
Stale statistics, correlated columns, or data loaded since last ANALYZE
Run ANALYZE on the table; consider extended statistics for correlated columns
Actual rows << estimated rows
The optimizer overestimated and may have chosen an overly conservative plan
Statistics skewed by outliers or deleted rows not yet vacuumed
Run VACUUM ANALYZE; check if dead tuples are inflating statistics
Actual time >> estimated cost (proportionally)
The execution was slower than the cost model predicted
Disk I/O contention, lock waits, hash spill to disk, or parallelism not utilized
Check Buffers output for disk reads; check work_mem for hash spill

blocks.content: The BUFFERS Option

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) adds buffer statistics to the output: shared hit (pages found in the buffer cache, no disk I/O needed) and shared read (pages read from disk). High read counts relative to hit counts indicate that the query is reading cold data — data not recently accessed and not cached in memory. For the billing query above, shared read=48210 and shared hit=1204 means 97.6% of pages were read from disk. This cold-read pattern is expected for the first query after a server restart or for rarely accessed data. For a query that runs every morning, high read counts suggest the shared_buffers configuration is too small to retain the working set between runs.

blocks.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs the query
blocks.content: Unlike EXPLAIN (which only plans), EXPLAIN ANALYZE actually executes the query. For SELECT queries, this is safe — it reads data but does not modify anything. For INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE, EXPLAIN ANALYZE will modify data. To safely analyze DML plans, wrap them in a transaction and roll back: BEGIN; EXPLAIN ANALYZE DELETE FROM ...; ROLLBACK;. This gives you the actual execution metrics without committing the changes.

Every query plan starts with a scan — reading data from a table. The scan strategy determines how much data the database reads and how fast it finds the rows you need. PostgreSQL has four scan strategies, each optimal for different selectivity ranges. Understanding when the optimizer chooses each strategy, and verifying that the choice is correct, is the single most impactful skill for query performance. A wrong scan strategy on a 130-million-row table is the difference between a 2-second query and a 3-minute query. This lesson covers all four strategies and the conditions that trigger each one.
Sequential Scan

A sequential scan reads every row in the table from the first page to the last, evaluating the filter condition on each row. It is the simplest and most predictable strategy: total I/O equals the table's total size on disk, regardless of how selective the filter is. Sequential scans are efficient when most rows match the filter (low selectivity) because the I/O is sequential (fast on spinning disks and SSDs alike). They are inefficient when few rows match (high selectivity) because the database reads gigabytes of data to find megabytes of results. The optimizer chooses a sequential scan when there is no useful index, when the filter is not selective enough to justify random I/O, or when the table is small enough that reading it entirely is fast.
Index Scan

An index scan uses a B-tree (or other index type) to find the rows matching the filter, then fetches each matching row from the table by its physical location. It reads only the matching rows plus the index pages, which is dramatically less I/O than a sequential scan when the filter is selective. For HydroAlpes' meter_readings table with 130 million rows, filtering to one day (1.2 million rows, about 0.9% selectivity) with an index scan reads roughly 1% of the table instead of 100%. The trade-off: each row fetch requires a random I/O (jumping to a different page on disk), which is slower per-page than sequential reads. The optimizer chooses an index scan when selectivity is high enough that the reduced row count outweighs the random I/O cost.
Bitmap Index Scan

A bitmap scan is a two-phase strategy that bridges sequential and index scans. Phase 1 (Bitmap Index Scan) reads the index and builds a bitmap of table pages that contain matching rows. Phase 2 (Bitmap Heap Scan) reads those pages sequentially, skipping pages with no matches. The sequential page reads avoid the random I/O penalty of a regular index scan, while the bitmap avoids reading pages with no matches (as a sequential scan would). The optimizer chooses a bitmap scan at medium selectivity — too selective for a sequential scan, but not selective enough for efficient random-access index scanning. Bitmap scans can also combine multiple indexes: a bitmap on the date index AND a bitmap on the region index intersected to find rows matching both conditions.
Index Only Scan

An index only scan reads all needed data from the index itself without accessing the table at all. This is the fastest scan type because it eliminates table I/O entirely. It requires a covering index — an index that contains all columns the query needs, not just the filter columns. If the query selects meter_id and daily_kw and filters on date, an index on (date) INCLUDE (meter_id, daily_kw) is a covering index that enables an index only scan. The additional requirement: the table's visibility map must be up-to-date (maintained by VACUUM), so PostgreSQL can confirm that all index entries point to visible (committed) rows without checking the table.
The Selectivity Spectrum

blocks.title: Scan strategy selection based on filter selectivity
blocks.content:
Filter selectivity (% of rows matching):

0%        1%         5%        20%        50%       100%
│─────────│──────────│─────────│──────────│─────────│
│         │          │         │          │         │
│  Index  │  Bitmap  │ Bitmap  │  Seq Scan preferred │
│  Scan   │  Index   │ or Seq  │  (sequential I/O    │
│  (fast  │  Scan    │  Scan   │   beats random I/O) │
│  random │  (sorted │ (cross- │                     │
│  access)│  pages)  │  over)  │                     │
│         │          │         │                     │

Exact thresholds depend on table size, page distribution, and I/O costs.
blocks.caption: The optimizer switches strategies as selectivity changes. Highly selective queries use index scans. Medium selectivity uses bitmap scans. Low selectivity uses sequential scans. The crossover points depend on the cost model parameters and the table's physical characteristics.

blocks.title: The optimizer can be wrong
blocks.content: The optimizer's scan choice is based on statistics: estimated row counts, estimated page counts, and configured cost parameters. If the statistics are stale (the table grew since the last ANALYZE), the optimizer underestimates the row count and may choose a sequential scan where an index scan would be faster. If random_page_cost is set too high for SSD storage, the optimizer overpenalizes index scans and defaults to sequential scans more often than it should. When EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows a sequential scan with 97% of rows filtered out, the first investigation step is: are the statistics current, and do the cost parameters match the hardware?

Joins are the most expensive operations in analytical queries because they potentially process millions of rows from two or more tables. PostgreSQL implements three join algorithms, each with different performance characteristics: nested loop (fast when one side is tiny), hash join (fast for equi-joins on medium-to-large tables), and merge join (fast when both sides are pre-sorted). The optimizer's choice between these algorithms has more impact on query performance than any other single decision in the plan. Understanding how to read join operators in the plan, verify the optimizer's choice, and intervene when it chooses wrong is the core skill for join optimization.
Nested Loop Join

A nested loop join iterates through every row in the outer (driving) table and, for each row, looks up matching rows in the inner table. If the inner table has an index on the join column, the lookup is fast — one index probe per outer row. If there is no index, the inner table must be scanned entirely for each outer row, which is catastrophically slow for large tables. The optimizer chooses nested loop when the outer table is very small (a few hundred rows) and the inner table has an index on the join key. At HydroAlpes, nested loop appears when joining a small subquery result (e.g., the top 10 meters by consumption) with the large meter_readings table using an index on meter_id.
Hash Join

A hash join builds a hash table from the smaller input (the build side), then probes the hash table with each row from the larger input (the probe side). The hash table is built in memory using work_mem. If the build side fits in work_mem, the join is a single pass and very fast. If it does not fit, PostgreSQL spills hash batches to disk and performs a multi-batch join — significantly slower. The optimizer chooses hash join for equi-joins (join conditions using =) when neither side is small enough for nested loop and neither is pre-sorted for a merge join. Hash join is the most common algorithm in HydroAlpes' analytical queries.

-- This equi-join typically uses a hash join strategy
EXPLAIN
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, m.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m
    ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hash Join  (cost=38400.00..56891.50 rows=1200000 width=44)
   Hash Cond: (mr.meter_id = m.meter_id)
   ->  Index Scan using idx_readings_date on meter_readings mr
             (cost=0.56..16259.90 rows=1200000 width=28)
         Index Cond: (date = '2025-01-15'::date)
   ->  Hash  (cost=10200.00..10200.00 rows=1200000 width=28)
         ->  Seq Scan on meters m  (cost=0.00..10200.00 rows=1200000 width=28)

The plan shows a Hash Join with two children. The lower child (Seq Scan on meters) is the build side — it reads all 1.2 million meters into a hash table. The upper child (Index Scan on meter_readings) is the probe side — it uses the date index to find January 15 readings and probes the hash table to find each meter's customer_type. The Hash Cond confirms the join condition is an equality on meter_id. This is an efficient plan: the build side is the smaller table (meters, 1.2M rows) and the probe side is filtered first (readings for one day, also 1.2M rows). The hash table size is 1.2M × 28 bytes ≈ 34 MB, which fits comfortably in a reasonable work_mem setting.
Merge Join

A merge join requires both inputs to be sorted on the join key, then performs a single linear pass through both sorted lists, matching rows as it goes. If the inputs are already sorted (from indexes or prior operations), the merge is very efficient — it avoids the memory overhead of a hash table and the random I/O of nested loop. If the inputs are not sorted, the database must sort them first, which adds cost. The optimizer chooses merge join when both inputs are pre-sorted (common after ORDER BY or when indexes provide the sort order) or when the hash table would be too large for available memory. At HydroAlpes, merge join appears in large-table-to-large-table joins where both sides have indexes on the join key.
When the Optimizer Chooses Wrong

The optimizer's join choice is based on estimated row counts and cost parameters. When estimates are wrong, the choice can be wrong. A hash join where the build side is estimated at 10,000 rows but actually contains 5 million rows will spill to disk and run 10x slower than expected. A nested loop chosen because the optimizer estimated 100 outer rows but the actual count is 50,000 will perform 50,000 index probes instead of 100. The diagnostic is always the same: compare actual rows against estimated rows in EXPLAIN ANALYZE. If the ratio exceeds 3:1, the statistics need updating (ANALYZE), or the query needs restructuring to give the optimizer better information.

blocks.title: Do not force join strategies without diagnosis
blocks.content: PostgreSQL supports join hints through enable_hashjoin, enable_mergejoin, and enable_nestloop GUC parameters. Setting enable_hashjoin = off forces the optimizer to use a different strategy. But disabling strategies without understanding why the optimizer chose the current one masks the problem instead of fixing it. The correct workflow is: read EXPLAIN ANALYZE, identify the root cause (stale statistics, wrong cost parameters, missing index), fix the root cause, and verify the plan improves. Forcing strategies is a last resort, not a first step.

Every cost number in an EXPLAIN plan — (cost=0.56..16259.90) — is a dimensionless estimate produced by the optimizer's cost model. The cost model assigns a configurable weight to each type of operation: reading a sequential page, reading a random page, processing a tuple, evaluating an expression. The optimizer computes the total cost of each possible plan by summing these weighted operations, then chooses the plan with the lowest total cost. Understanding the cost model explains why the optimizer makes the choices it does — and when those choices are wrong because the cost parameters do not match the hardware.
The Core Cost Parameters

rows: seq_page_cost
Cost of reading one page sequentially from disk
Baseline — all other costs are relative to this
random_page_cost
Cost of reading one page via random access (index lookup)
Higher value favors sequential scans over index scans
cpu_tuple_cost
Cost of processing one row through a filter or projection
Affects the relative cost of CPU-bound vs I/O-bound operations
cpu_index_tuple_cost
Cost of processing one index entry during an index scan
Lower than tuple cost because index entries are simpler
effective_cache_size
4 GB
Estimate of memory available for caching (OS + PostgreSQL)
Larger value makes the optimizer more willing to use index scans

blocks.content: Why Defaults Are Often Wrong

The default random_page_cost of 4.0 assumes that a random page read is 4x more expensive than a sequential read — which was accurate for spinning hard drives where the disk head must physically move. On modern SSDs, random reads are only about 1.1–1.5x slower than sequential reads. With the default random_page_cost on SSD storage, the optimizer overpenalizes index scans by 3x, causing it to prefer sequential scans even when an index scan would be faster. HydroAlpes runs entirely on SSD storage and sets random_page_cost = 1.1, which aligns the cost model with the hardware and lets the optimizer choose index scans when they are genuinely faster.

After setting random_page_cost = 1.1, the same query may switch from a Seq Scan to an Index Scan because the optimizer no longer overpenalizes random I/O. This is not a hack — it is correcting the cost model to match the actual hardware characteristics. The SET command applies to the current session only; to make the change persistent, the DBA sets it in postgresql.conf. At HydroAlpes, the infrastructure team configures cost parameters as part of server provisioning, ensuring that every PostgreSQL instance's cost model matches its storage type.
How Cost Estimates Are Computed

The optimizer computes a plan's total cost by combining I/O cost and CPU cost. For a sequential scan: I/O cost = number_of_pages × seq_page_cost, CPU cost = number_of_rows × cpu_tuple_cost. For an index scan: I/O cost = number_of_index_pages × random_page_cost + number_of_heap_pages × random_page_cost, CPU cost = number_of_rows × (cpu_index_tuple_cost + cpu_tuple_cost). The plan with the lower total cost wins. The cost number in EXPLAIN is the total of these components, expressed in seq_page_cost units. A cost of 16,259.90 means 'roughly equivalent to sequentially reading 16,260 pages' — not 16,260 milliseconds or 16,260 anything else. Cost is a relative unit for comparison, not an absolute measurement of time.

blocks.title: Cost is for comparison, not for timing
blocks.content: A plan with cost 16,000 is not necessarily faster than a plan with cost 20,000 in wall-clock time. The cost model uses simplified assumptions: uniform page sizes, independent tuple processing, no caching effects. Real execution depends on cache hit rates, concurrent load, and I/O scheduling. Use cost to compare alternative plans for the same query, not to predict absolute execution time. EXPLAIN ANALYZE gives you actual time; EXPLAIN gives you relative cost.

The optimizer's plan quality depends entirely on the accuracy of its row count estimates, and those estimates depend on table statistics. Statistics are metadata about each column's value distribution: the number of distinct values, the most common values and their frequencies, the histogram of value distribution, and the correlation between physical row order and logical value order. PostgreSQL stores these statistics in the pg_statistic system catalog and updates them via the ANALYZE command. When statistics are accurate, the optimizer makes good plans. When they are stale or missing, the optimizer makes plans based on wrong assumptions — and the resulting performance can be orders of magnitude worse than necessary.
What ANALYZE Collects

When you run ANALYZE silver.meter_readings, PostgreSQL samples rows from the table (by default, 30,000 rows per column) and computes three types of statistics. The n_distinct estimate records how many unique values the column contains — 1.2 million for meter_id, 10 for region, 3 for customer_type. The most common values (MCV) list records the most frequent values and their frequencies — for region, the MCVs might show BERN-NORTH at 15%, ZURICH-EAST at 14%, etc. The histogram records the distribution of values not covered by the MCV list, divided into equal-frequency buckets. Together, these statistics let the optimizer estimate how many rows will match any given filter condition.

-- Run ANALYZE to update statistics for a specific table
ANALYZE silver.meter_readings;

-- Check when statistics were last updated
SELECT relname, last_analyze, last_autoanalyze, n_live_tup
FROM   pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
ORDER BY relname;

The output shows that meter_readings was last auto-analyzed at 05:45 this morning and last manually analyzed at 06:10 (HydroAlpes runs ANALYZE after the daily Silver load completes). The meters table was auto-analyzed five days ago — which is fine because it changes slowly. Auto-analyze triggers when a configurable fraction of the table has been modified since the last analysis. For HydroAlpes' meter_readings (130M rows), the default trigger threshold of 10% means auto-analyze fires after 13 million new rows — roughly 11 days of data. For a daily pipeline, this is too infrequent. HydroAlpes' pipeline explicitly runs ANALYZE after every daily load to ensure statistics are always current.
The Stale Statistics Incident

HydroAlpes loaded 3 months of backfill data — 108 million rows — into meter_readings in a single batch. The load completed at 04:00. The billing query ran at 05:30. Auto-analyze had not yet triggered (it runs on a timer, not immediately after loads). The statistics still reflected the pre-backfill state: 22 million rows instead of 130 million. The optimizer estimated the January filter would produce 170,000 rows (based on the old statistics). The actual count was 1.2 million. The optimizer chose a nested loop join that was optimal for 170,000 rows but catastrophic for 1.2 million. The query ran for 22 minutes before Markus killed it. The fix: ANALYZE silver.meter_readings before running the billing query. The subsequent run completed in 6 seconds.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: HydroAlpes' pipeline rule: run ANALYZE on every table that received a bulk load before any downstream query runs against it. The ANALYZE is part of the pipeline, not an afterthought. For meter_readings (130M rows), ANALYZE takes approximately 15 seconds — a negligible cost that prevents minutes or hours of wasted query time from stale statistics. This single practice prevents more performance incidents than any other optimization technique.

After five lessons of plan reading mechanics, you can now identify individual operators and understand what they do. This lesson shifts from mechanics to pattern recognition: the plan shapes that signal specific performance problems. Each anti-pattern has a recognizable signature in the EXPLAIN output, a root cause, and a targeted fix. Recognizing these patterns by sight — without needing to think through the cost model each time — is the plan-reading fluency that experienced engineers develop. HydroAlpes' plan review checklist is organized around these anti-patterns because they account for over 90% of query performance problems.
Anti-Pattern 1: Seq Scan with High Filter Rejection

Signature: Seq Scan with Rows Removed by Filter that is 10x or more higher than the rows returned. The query reads the entire table to find a small fraction of rows. Root cause: no index on the filter column, or stale statistics that make the optimizer underestimate selectivity. Fix: create an index on the filter column, or run ANALYZE to update statistics so the optimizer correctly estimates that an index scan is cheaper. At HydroAlpes, this anti-pattern appeared when the date index was accidentally dropped during a migration and the billing query fell back to a full table scan — 130 million rows read, 37.2 million kept.
Anti-Pattern 2: Nested Loop on Large Tables

Signature: Nested Loop join where the outer side has thousands or millions of rows and the inner side does not use an index. Each outer row triggers a full scan of the inner table. Root cause: the optimizer underestimated the outer row count (stale statistics) and chose nested loop thinking the outer side was tiny. Fix: update statistics with ANALYZE. If the outer side is genuinely large, the optimizer should choose a hash join or merge join. At HydroAlpes, this appeared in the backfill incident — the optimizer estimated 170,000 outer rows (small enough for nested loop) but the actual count was 1.2 million.
Anti-Pattern 3: Hash Join Spill to Disk

Signature: Hash Join with actual time far exceeding cost estimate, and BUFFERS showing temp reads/writes (indicating disk spill). The hash table exceeded work_mem and spilled to temporary files on disk. Root cause: work_mem too small for the build side of the hash join, or the build side larger than estimated. Fix: increase work_mem for the session or globally. If the build side is genuinely too large (millions of rows with wide columns), consider whether a merge join on pre-sorted data would avoid the memory pressure entirely. At HydroAlpes, the default work_mem of 4 MB was sufficient for junior-scale queries but insufficient for mid-level scale. Increasing to 256 MB eliminated hash spill for all production queries.
Anti-Pattern 4: Sort on Unsorted Large Data

Signature: Sort operator consuming a large amount of time and memory, often with disk spill. The database is sorting millions of rows for an ORDER BY that the query needs. Root cause: no index that provides the required sort order. Fix: create an index on the ORDER BY columns so the database can read data in sorted order without an explicit sort. Alternatively, if the sort is required by a merge join, ensure both join inputs have suitable indexes. At HydroAlpes, the billing query's ORDER BY total_kw DESC requires sorting the aggregated result — only 30 rows, so the sort is trivial. But a query that orders 1.2 million rows by daily_kw DESC benefits enormously from an index on daily_kw.
The Diagnostic Checklist

rows: Seq Scan on a large table with selective filter
Rows Removed by Filter >> rows returned
Create an index on the filter column; run ANALYZE
Nested Loop with large outer table
Loops count in thousands or millions on the inner scan
Run ANALYZE; if problem persists, check for missing index on inner join key
Actual rows >> estimated rows
Ratio exceeds 3:1 at any plan node
Run ANALYZE; consider extended statistics for correlated columns
Hash with temp read/write
BUFFERS shows temp blocks
Increase work_mem for the query or globally
Sort with high memory or disk usage
Sort Method: external merge (disk spill)
Increase work_mem; consider an index to provide pre-sorted input

blocks.title: Anti-patterns have root causes, not workarounds
blocks.content: Each anti-pattern points to a specific root cause: missing index, stale statistics, wrong cost parameters, or insufficient work_mem. The fix addresses the root cause — it does not rewrite the query to work around the bad plan. If the optimizer is choosing a sequential scan because of stale statistics, the fix is ANALYZE, not adding a query hint. If a hash join spills because work_mem is too small, the fix is increasing work_mem, not rewriting the join as a subquery. Fix the cause, not the symptom.

HydroAlpes' production pipeline runs on two engines: PostgreSQL for the warehouse (analytical queries, Gold-layer serving, and dashboard consumption) and Spark SQL for large-scale transformations (Silver processing, historical reprocessing, and cross-system joins). The SQL skills you have built in this module transfer directly between them — the diagnostic questions are the same — but the execution plan format and optimizer behavior differ in ways that affect how you diagnose performance problems. This lesson extends your plan-reading skills to Spark SQL, covering the four-phase Catalyst plan, the Spark-specific operators, and the key differences from PostgreSQL.
Spark SQL's EXPLAIN

-- Spark SQL EXPLAIN (from a notebook or spark.sql() call)
EXPLAIN EXTENDED
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver_meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=false
+- Sort [total_kw#42 DESC], true, 0
   +- Exchange rangepartitioning(total_kw#42 DESC, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS
      +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#2], functions=[sum(daily_kw#1)])
         +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#2, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS
            +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#2], functions=[partial_sum(daily_kw#1)])
               +- FileScan parquet [region#2,daily_kw#1]
                    PartitionFilters: [date#3 = 2025-01-15]
                    PushedFilters: []
                    ReadSchema: struct<region:string,daily_kw:double>

Spark's physical plan reads bottom-to-top like PostgreSQL's, but with different operators. FileScan parquet replaces Seq Scan/Index Scan — Spark reads Parquet files, not database pages. PartitionFilters shows that date = '2025-01-15' is applied at the file system level (partition pruning), equivalent to PostgreSQL's partition pruning. Exchange hashpartitioning is a shuffle — data redistributed across executors by the hash of the region column. The two-phase HashAggregate (partial then final) is the same concept as PostgreSQL's partial aggregation: compute partial sums per partition before shuffling, then combine after. AdaptiveSparkPlan indicates that AQE (Adaptive Query Execution) may modify the plan at runtime.
Key Differences from PostgreSQL

rows.left: Index Scan uses B-tree indexes to find rows
rows.right: No row-level indexes; uses partition pruning and Parquet column/row group skipping

rows.left: Hash Join, Merge Join, Nested Loop
rows.right: BroadcastHashJoin, SortMergeJoin, ShuffledHashJoin

rows.left: Cost model uses page-level I/O estimates
rows.right: Cost model uses data size and cluster resource estimates

rows.left: ANALYZE updates statistics in the catalog
rows.right: ANALYZE TABLE updates Hive metastore statistics for CBO

rows.left: No shuffles — single server processes everything
rows.right: Exchange operators represent shuffles — data movement between executors

rows.left: EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs the query and shows actual times
