rows.right: No equivalent; use Spark UI's SQL tab for actual execution metrics

blocks.content: The most important conceptual difference: PostgreSQL can use row-level indexes to jump directly to matching rows. Spark has no row-level indexes — it relies on partition pruning (skipping entire directories), Parquet predicate pushdown (skipping row groups within files), and column pruning (reading only needed columns). This means that the same filter that uses a B-tree index in PostgreSQL relies on directory structure and file metadata in Spark. The optimization techniques are different, but the diagnostic approach is the same: read the plan, verify that filters are pushed as close to the data as possible, and check that unnecessary data is not being read.

blocks.title: One skill, two engines
blocks.content: The plan-reading skill transfers: identify the scan strategy, verify filter pushdown, check the join algorithm, compare estimated versus actual row counts. The specific operators differ between PostgreSQL and Spark, but the diagnostic questions are identical. An engineer who can read a PostgreSQL plan can learn to read a Spark plan in an afternoon. The analytical reasoning — why is this scan reading too much data, why did the optimizer choose this join strategy — is engine-independent.

This module taught you to read execution plans, understand the optimizer's cost model, diagnose stale statistics, recognize anti-patterns, and extend your skills to Spark SQL. The remaining question is: when do you actually use these skills? The answer is not 'when something is slow' — by then, the bad plan has already cost time and resources. The answer is 'before every deployment.' A plan review habit — checking the execution plan of every query before it reaches production — prevents performance problems instead of reacting to them. This lesson establishes the review practice that HydroAlpes uses for every pipeline query.
The Five-Question Review

HydroAlpes' plan review is structured around five questions, each targeting the most common category of plan inefficiency. The review takes 2–3 minutes per query and is embedded in the code review process — the EXPLAIN output is included in every pull request that adds or modifies a pipeline query. A query passes review when all five questions have satisfactory answers. A query that fails any question is sent back to the author for investigation and correction before the pull request is approved for merging.

rows: Are filters pushed to the scan?
Check for PartitionFilters (Spark) or Index Cond (PostgreSQL) on the scan operator
Selective filters appear on the scan, not on a separate Filter operator above it
Are unused columns pruned?
Check ReadSchema (Spark) or the column list in Index Scan (PostgreSQL)
Only the columns needed for output and join conditions are read
Is the join strategy appropriate?
Check for BroadcastHashJoin/Hash Join on small-large joins, SortMergeJoin/Merge Join on large-large joins
Small dimension tables are broadcast or hash-built; large fact tables are not fully scanned for nested loops
Are row estimates reasonable?
Compare estimated rows to known data characteristics (e.g., 1.2M rows per day)
Estimates within 3x of expected values; no 100-row estimates for million-row tables
Are there unexpected sorts or shuffles?
Count Sort/Exchange operators; verify each one is necessary
No redundant shuffles; sorts only for ORDER BY or merge join inputs

blocks.content: Integrating with Code Review

At HydroAlpes, the plan review is embedded in the pull request workflow. The author runs EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a staging dataset) and pastes the output into the PR description under a 'Plan Review' section. The reviewer reads the plan against the five-question checklist and comments on any concerns. If the plan shows a sequential scan on a table that should have an index, the reviewer requests an index creation migration in the same PR. If the estimated rows are wildly wrong, the reviewer requests ANALYZE and a re-run of the plan. This takes 2–3 minutes per query and has caught four performance regressions in HydroAlpes' first month of adoption.
When to Re-Review

A plan review at deployment time validates the plan against the current data state. But plans change over time: data grows, statistics drift, and the optimizer may choose different strategies as the table evolves from 50 million rows to 130 million. HydroAlpes re-reviews plans in three situations. First, after a significant data volume change (a backfill, a schema change, or a threshold crossing like growing past 100 million rows). Second, after a PostgreSQL version upgrade (optimizer improvements can change plan choices). Third, when a query's execution time increases by more than 50% compared to its historical baseline — detected by the performance monitoring framework in Module 8.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 2, you will apply your plan-reading skills to the most expensive operation in analytical SQL: joins. You will learn to diagnose fan-out (when joins silently multiply rows), detect and resolve skew, choose between semi-joins and anti-joins, and use lateral joins for correlated lookups. Every diagnostic in Module 2 starts with the plan — because the plan is where join performance problems become visible.

modules.title: Advanced Join Patterns
modules.description: Master the performance characteristics of joins at production scale, diagnose and resolve fan-out and skew problems, and learn semi-join, anti-join, and lateral join patterns.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' Gold-layer billing pipeline joins meter_readings with meters, substations, and regions — four tables chained by foreign keys. At junior-level scale (350,000 meters, 4 regions), the pipeline completed in 8 seconds. After the expansion to 1.2 million meters and 10 sub-regions, the same query — identical SQL, identical join logic — takes 12 minutes. The 4x data increase produced a 90x slowdown. This disproportionate degradation is the defining characteristic of join performance problems: joins do not scale linearly with data volume because the optimizer's strategy choices have exponential consequences at scale. A hash join that fits in memory at 350K rows spills to disk at 1.2M rows. A merge join that was efficient with sorted input becomes expensive when the sort itself takes minutes. This module teaches you to diagnose these problems using the plan-reading skills from Module 1 and to resolve them with targeted join optimizations.
Why Joins Are the Bottleneck

Scans, filters, and aggregations operate on one table at a time. Their cost grows linearly with data size: twice the data means roughly twice the work. Joins operate on the Cartesian product of two tables: every row in the left table is potentially compared with every row in the right table. The optimizer avoids the full Cartesian product by using join keys and algorithms (hash join, merge join, nested loop), but the underlying combinatorial complexity means that join performance is far more sensitive to data size, key distribution, and algorithm choice than any single-table operation. A 10x increase in table size can produce a 100x increase in join cost if the algorithm changes from in-memory hash to disk-spilling sort-merge.
The Diagnostic Approach

Module 1 established the practice of reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE output before diagnosing a performance problem. For joins, the diagnostic focuses on three specific plan elements. First, the join algorithm: is it Hash Join, Merge Join, or Nested Loop — and is that the right choice for the table sizes involved? Second, the row estimates: does the optimizer's estimated join output match the actual output, or did it underestimate (causing downstream operators to receive far more data than they planned for)? Third, the resource usage: did the hash table spill to disk (visible in BUFFERS as temp read/write blocks), or did a sort consume excessive memory? Every join performance lesson in this module starts with these three diagnostic checks.

-- The billing pipeline join that degraded from 8 seconds to 12 minutes
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT mr.region,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name AS substation_name,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, m.customer_type, s.name
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Sort  (cost=1284510.30..1284510.50 rows=85 width=60)
      (actual time=718432.210..718432.228 rows=85 loops=1)
   ->  HashAggregate  (cost=1284502.10..1284508.10 rows=85 width=60)
              (actual time=718431.890..718431.998 rows=85 loops=1)
         ->  Hash Join  (cost=12940.00..1247002.10 rows=37200000 width=48)
                   (actual time=892.110..694218.440 rows=37200000 loops=1)
               Hash Cond: (m.substation_id = s.substation_id)
               ->  Hash Join  (cost=12840.00..1209502.10 rows=37200000 width=44)
                         (actual time=842.304..612841.220 rows=37200000 loops=1)
                     Hash Cond: (mr.meter_id = m.meter_id)
                     Buffers: shared hit=2104 read=148210, temp read=98400 write=98400
                     ->  Seq Scan on meter_readings mr
                               (actual time=0.048..198432.110 rows=37200000 loops=1)
                           Filter: ((date >= '2025-01-01') AND (date < '2025-02-01'))
                           Rows Removed by Filter: 92800000
                     ->  Hash  (actual time=840.112..840.112 rows=1200000 loops=1)
                           Buffers: temp read=12400 write=12400
                           ->  Seq Scan on meters m
               ->  Hash  (actual time=0.821..0.821 rows=85 loops=1)
                     ->  Seq Scan on substations s
 Planning Time: 4.210 ms
 Execution Time: 718433.881 ms

The plan reveals three problems working together to produce the 12-minute execution. First, the Seq Scan on meter_readings reads 130 million rows to produce 37.2 million — no index on date. Second, the inner Hash Join builds a hash table from the 1.2 million meters rows, and the BUFFERS line shows temp read=12400 write=12400 — the hash table spilled to disk because work_mem is too small. Third, 37.2 million rows flow through two join operators and a hash aggregate. The fixes: create an index on meter_readings(date) to reduce the scan to 37.2M rows directly, increase work_mem to fit the meters hash table in memory, and verify that the substations join (85 rows) could use a nested loop instead of a hash join. Each fix addresses a specific plan element identified by the diagnostic.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When a multi-table join is slow, diagnose from the inside out. Start with the innermost scan (the first data source), check for index usage and filter pushdown. Then check each join layer: algorithm choice, row estimate accuracy, and resource consumption. The bottleneck is almost always in one specific layer, not spread evenly across the entire plan. Fixing that one layer typically resolves 80% of the performance problem.

The billing team reported that January's total consumption was 15.8 million kW — double the expected 7.9 million. The pipeline had not changed. The data source had not changed. Markus ran SELECT COUNT(*) FROM silver.meter_readings WHERE date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01' and got 37.2 million rows — exactly the expected 1.2 million meters × 31 days. Then he ran the same count on the joined result: 74.4 million rows. The join had doubled the row count. The cause: the meters dimension table had been reloaded from a new source system, and 600,000 meters had two rows each — one from the old system and one from the new, with slightly different installation_date values but the same meter_id. The INNER JOIN matched each reading with both meter rows, silently doubling 600,000 meters' readings. This is a fan-out: a join that produces more rows than the left input because the right side has duplicate keys.
What Fan-Out Is

A fan-out occurs when a join produces more rows than the driving table because the joined table has multiple rows per key. In a one-to-many relationship (one meter has many readings), the fact table is on the 'many' side and the dimension is on the 'one' side — the join preserves the row count. A fan-out reverses this: the dimension has duplicates, so each fact row matches multiple dimension rows and the result set expands. Fan-outs are insidious because the query runs without errors, produces plausible-looking results, and the extra rows are only visible through row count comparison or aggregate discrepancies. At HydroAlpes, the doubled billing totals were not caught until a human noticed the numbers were too high — three days after the pipeline ran.
Detecting Fan-Out

-- Step 1: Count the driving table
SELECT COUNT(*) AS reading_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01';
-- Result: 37,200,000

-- Step 2: Count the joined result
SELECT COUNT(*) AS joined_count
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01';
-- Result: 74,400,000  ← DOUBLE! Fan-out detected.

-- Step 3: Find the duplicate keys in the dimension table
SELECT meter_id, COUNT(*) AS copies
FROM   silver.meters
GROUP BY meter_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY copies DESC
LIMIT  10;

The diagnostic is a three-step pattern. Step 1: count the driving table (the fact table or the table whose row count you expect to be preserved). Step 2: count the joined result. If the joined count exceeds the driving count, a fan-out is occurring. Step 3: find the duplicate keys in the joined table that are causing the multiplication. At HydroAlpes, this three-step check is now part of every pipeline's post-join validation. The pipeline computes COUNT(*) before and after the join and raises an alert if the ratio exceeds 1.001 — allowing for a tiny tolerance due to late-arriving dimension records.
Resolving Fan-Out

The fix depends on the root cause. If the dimension table has accidental duplicates (as in HydroAlpes' case), deduplicate the dimension before joining. Use the ROW_NUMBER pattern from the junior track (Module 8, lesson 5): assign a rank per key and keep only rank 1. If the dimension legitimately has multiple rows per key — a slowly changing dimension with Type 2 history (which you will learn in Module 4) — the join condition must include the effective date range to match each fact row with the correct dimension version. The wrong fix is to add DISTINCT to the joined result: this masks the fan-out by collapsing duplicated rows but may discard legitimate duplicates and silently changes the aggregation semantics.

-- Fix: deduplicate the dimension before joining
WITH deduped_meters AS (
  SELECT meter_id, customer_type, region, substation_id,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY meter_id
           ORDER BY installation_date DESC  -- keep the most recent version
         ) AS rn
  FROM   silver.meters
)
SELECT mr.region,
       dm.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN deduped_meters AS dm
    ON mr.meter_id = dm.meter_id AND dm.rn = 1
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region, dm.customer_type
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

The CTE deduplicates the meters table using ROW_NUMBER, keeping only the most recent version per meter_id. The join condition includes dm.rn = 1 to ensure only one dimension row matches each reading. The result: 37.2 million rows — exactly matching the driving table count, with no fan-out. The billing total returns to the correct 7.9 million kW. This deduplication-before-join pattern is the standard fix for accidental fan-out and should be the first approach whenever a dimension table's key uniqueness cannot be guaranteed by a primary key constraint.

blocks.title: Fan-out is silent and expensive
blocks.content: Fan-out produces no errors, no warnings, and plausible-looking results. The doubled rows flow through every downstream aggregation, doubling totals, averages, and counts. The only reliable detection is comparing the pre-join and post-join row counts. At HydroAlpes, this check is mandatory for every pipeline join and runs as an automated assertion (Module 6, lesson 2) after every pipeline execution. The three-day delay in detecting the billing doubling cost HydroAlpes one incorrect invoice run and a manual correction cycle.

The junior track introduced EXISTS for testing row existence and NOT EXISTS for finding missing rows. At mid-level, these patterns become optimization strategies with specific plan signatures and performance characteristics. A semi-join (EXISTS) returns rows from the left table that have at least one match in the right table — but unlike INNER JOIN, it never multiplies rows because it stops after finding the first match. An anti-join (NOT EXISTS) returns rows from the left table that have no match in the right table — the complement of the semi-join. Understanding when the optimizer converts your SQL into semi-join or anti-join operators, and when it fails to, is the difference between a query that scans 1.2 million rows and one that scans 130 million.
Semi-Join in Practice

-- Semi-join: find meters that have at least one high reading in January
-- EXISTS stops after finding the first match — no fan-out possible
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT m.meter_id, m.customer_type, m.region
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  EXISTS (
         SELECT 1
         FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
         WHERE  mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
           AND  mr.daily_kw > 100
           AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
           AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
       );

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hash Semi Join  (cost=680210.50..692451.30 rows=42000 width=44)
               (actual time=8421.204..9102.881 rows=38420 loops=1)
   Hash Cond: (m.meter_id = mr.meter_id)
   ->  Seq Scan on meters m  (cost=0.00..10200.00 rows=1200000 width=44)
   ->  Hash  (cost=680100.50..680100.50 rows=42000 width=20)
         ->  Index Scan using idx_readings_date on meter_readings mr
                   (cost=0.56..680100.50 rows=42000 width=20)
               Index Cond: ((date >= '2025-01-01') AND (date < '2025-02-01'))
               Filter: (daily_kw > 100)

The plan shows a Hash Semi Join — not a Hash Join. The Semi Join operator guarantees that each left row (from meters) appears at most once in the output, even if multiple readings match the EXISTS condition. This is the critical difference from INNER JOIN: the Semi Join does not multiply rows. The optimizer automatically converted the EXISTS subquery into a semi-join because it recognized the pattern. The result: 38,420 meters have at least one reading above 100 kW in January. Each meter appears exactly once, regardless of how many high readings it has. An equivalent INNER JOIN would produce one row per high reading, potentially millions of rows that would then need deduplication.
Anti-Join in Practice

-- Anti-join: find meters with NO readings at all in January (inactive meters)
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT m.meter_id, m.customer_type, m.region
FROM   silver.meters AS m
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
         SELECT 1
         FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
         WHERE  mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
           AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
           AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
       );

QUERY PLAN
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hash Anti Join  (cost=680210.50..692451.30 rows=1158000 width=44)
               (actual time=8512.441..9841.220 rows=0 loops=1)
   Hash Cond: (m.meter_id = mr.meter_id)
   ->  Seq Scan on meters m
   ->  Hash
         ->  Index Scan using idx_readings_date on meter_readings mr

The plan shows a Hash Anti Join — the complement of the Semi Join. It returns meters from the left table that have no matching readings in the right table. In this case, the result is zero rows — every meter reported at least once in January. If the result contained rows, those would be the inactive meters needing investigation. The Anti Join is the optimizer's implementation of NOT EXISTS, and it is always NULL-safe — unlike NOT IN, which silently returns empty results when the subquery contains NULLs. This is why HydroAlpes' style guide mandates NOT EXISTS over NOT IN for all anti-join patterns.
Semi-Join vs INNER JOIN vs IN

rows: EXISTS (semi-join)
Hash Semi Join or Nested Loop Semi Join
Never — stops at first match
Safe — checks existence, not values
Filtering to rows that have a related record
INNER JOIN
Hash Join, Merge Join, or Nested Loop
Yes — one output row per match
Safe (NULLs never match on =)
Enriching rows with columns from the joined table
IN (subquery)
Hash Semi Join (often converted by optimizer)
Never — equivalent to EXISTS after optimization
NOT IN is NOT safe — fails silently with NULLs
Simple value-list filtering
NOT EXISTS (anti-join)
Hash Anti Join
Never — returns unmatched rows only
Safe — checks existence, not values
Finding rows with no related record

blocks.title: Choose the pattern that matches the intent
blocks.content: If you need columns from the joined table, use INNER JOIN (or LEFT JOIN). If you only need to know whether a related row exists, use EXISTS — it is semantically clearer, never causes fan-out, and the optimizer can execute it as an efficient Semi Join. If you need to find rows without a related record, use NOT EXISTS — it is NULL-safe and the optimizer executes it as an Anti Join. Choosing the wrong pattern (INNER JOIN when you mean EXISTS) produces correct results at junior scale but causes fan-out or unnecessary work at mid-level scale.

Standard joins have a limitation: the right side of the join cannot reference columns from the left side. The right table is fixed — it is the same for every left row. A LATERAL join removes this restriction: the right side is a subquery that can reference columns from the current left row, producing a different result set for each left row. This is a correlated join — conceptually similar to the correlated subqueries from the junior track (Module 7, lesson 6), but expressed as a join rather than a WHERE clause subquery. LATERAL joins solve problems that would otherwise require correlated subqueries or complex window function workarounds, particularly the 'top-N per group' pattern when N varies by group or when additional columns from the matched rows are needed.
The Syntax

-- For each substation, find the 3 highest-consuming meters on January 15
-- LATERAL allows the right subquery to reference s.substation_id from the left
SELECT s.substation_id,
       s.name AS substation_name,
       top.meter_id,
       top.daily_kw
FROM   silver.substations AS s
  CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw
    FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
    WHERE  mr.substation_id = s.substation_id   -- references left side
      AND  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
    ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
    LIMIT 3
  ) AS top
ORDER BY s.substation_id, top.daily_kw DESC;

substation_id | substation_name           | meter_id       | daily_kw
--------------+---------------------------+----------------+---------
SUB-BERN-01   | Alpine Grid Station B-1   | CH-BERN-58331  |   943.50
SUB-BERN-01   | Alpine Grid Station B-1   | CH-BERN-52017  |   712.80
SUB-BERN-01   | Alpine Grid Station B-1   | CH-BERN-48892  |   687.40
SUB-BERN-04   | Alpine Grid Station B-4   | CH-BERN-65004  | 1,871.20
SUB-BERN-04   | Alpine Grid Station B-4   | CH-BERN-41120  |   554.20
SUB-BERN-04   | Alpine Grid Station B-4   | CH-BERN-39001  |   498.30
...

For each row in the substations table (85 rows), the LATERAL subquery finds the top 3 consuming meters at that substation on January 15. The subquery references s.substation_id from the outer table — this is the LATERAL capability. Without LATERAL, you would need a CTE with ROW_NUMBER partitioned by substation_id, or a correlated subquery. The LATERAL version is often more readable and can be more efficient because the database can use an index on (substation_id, date, daily_kw DESC) to satisfy each subquery's ORDER BY and LIMIT without scanning the entire readings table.
LATERAL vs ROW_NUMBER for Top-N Per Group

The junior track's top-N pattern uses ROW_NUMBER in a CTE: assign ranks, then filter WHERE rn <= N. This works but computes ranks for every row in every group, even groups you do not care about. LATERAL can be more efficient when the outer table is small (85 substations) because the database executes a targeted index lookup per outer row rather than a full window function pass over all 37.2 million readings. The trade-off reverses when the outer table is large: LATERAL with 1.2 million outer rows means 1.2 million subquery executions, which is typically slower than a single ROW_NUMBER pass. The decision framework: use LATERAL when the outer table is small and indexed lookups are fast; use ROW_NUMBER when the outer table is large or when you need ranks for all groups.
LATERAL for Correlated Lookups

-- For each meter reading, find the most recent energy price
-- LATERAL allows a correlated lookup by reading date
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.date,
       price.price_eur_mwh,
       price.price_timestamp
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    SELECT ep.price_eur_mwh, ep.price_timestamp
    FROM   silver.energy_prices AS ep
    WHERE  ep.market_zone = 'CH'
      AND  ep.price_timestamp <= mr.date + INTERVAL '1 day'  -- references left side
    ORDER BY ep.price_timestamp DESC
    LIMIT 1
  ) AS price
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
LIMIT  5;

This LATERAL join finds the most recent energy price for each meter reading by looking up the price table with a correlated condition on the reading's date. The subquery references mr.date from the left side to filter prices up to and including the reading day, then returns the most recent one. This temporal lookup pattern — 'find the most recent version of a related record as of each row's timestamp' — is one of the most common LATERAL use cases in data engineering. The alternative (a range join with a window function to pick the closest match) is significantly more complex and harder to read.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: LATERAL joins require an index on the subquery's filter and sort columns to be efficient. For the top-3-per-substation query, an index on meter_readings(substation_id, date, daily_kw DESC) makes each subquery a fast index scan. Without the index, each subquery triggers a sequential scan of the entire readings table — 85 substations × full table scan = catastrophic. At HydroAlpes, every LATERAL join is accompanied by an index creation in the same migration script.

The optimizer's join strategy choice depends on its estimate of how many rows the join will produce — the join cardinality. A hash join sized for 100,000 output rows works well. The same hash join confronted with 10 million actual output rows spills to disk and runs 50x slower. The accuracy of the join cardinality estimate determines whether the optimizer's downstream decisions (memory allocation, sort strategy, aggregate approach) are appropriate. When join cardinality estimates are wrong — and they frequently are, especially in multi-table joins — every downstream operator inherits the bad estimate, and the cumulative effect on performance is multiplicative.
How the Optimizer Estimates Join Cardinality

For an equi-join on column A from table T1 and column B from table T2, the optimizer estimates the output row count as: rows(T1) × rows(T2) / max(n_distinct(A), n_distinct(B)). This formula assumes uniform distribution: every distinct value of A appears equally often, and every distinct value of B appears equally often. If T1 has 37.2 million rows with 1.2 million distinct meter_ids, and T2 has 1.2 million rows with 1.2 million distinct meter_ids, the estimate is 37.2M × 1.2M / 1.2M = 37.2 million — which is correct if each meter_id appears exactly once in T2. If meter_ids are not uniformly distributed (some meters have far more readings than others), the estimate can be significantly wrong.
When Estimates Go Wrong

Three situations produce bad join cardinality estimates. First, correlated columns: the optimizer estimates a join on (region, customer_type) by multiplying the selectivities independently, but in reality, BERN-NORTH has no INDUSTRIAL customers — the correlation means fewer rows than the independent estimate predicts. Second, stale statistics: the optimizer uses n_distinct values from the last ANALYZE, but the table has grown since then and the distinct count has changed. Third, multi-table join cascading: a bad estimate on the first join propagates through subsequent joins, getting worse at each step. If the first join estimates 100,000 rows but produces 500,000, the second join's memory allocation is based on 100,000 and may be insufficient for 500,000.

-- Detect cardinality estimation errors in a multi-table join
-- Compare estimated rows vs actual rows at each join operator
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT mr.region, m.customer_type, s.name,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
GROUP BY mr.region, m.customer_type, s.name;

In the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, check the (rows=estimated) vs (actual rows=N) at each join operator. If the first join estimates 120,000 rows but produces 180,000, the error is 1.5x — manageable. If it estimates 12,000 but produces 180,000, the error is 15x — the optimizer's downstream decisions are based on a fundamentally wrong assumption. The fix depends on the cause: run ANALYZE to update statistics, create extended statistics for correlated columns (CREATE STATISTICS ... ON region, customer_type FROM silver.meter_readings), or restructure the query to reduce the number of joins where cascading estimates can accumulate.
Extended Statistics for Correlated Columns

-- Create extended statistics for correlated columns
-- This tells the optimizer that region and customer_type are not independent
CREATE STATISTICS stats_readings_region_type (dependencies)
  ON region, customer_type
  FROM silver.meter_readings;

-- Update the extended statistics
ANALYZE silver.meter_readings;

-- Now the optimizer accounts for the correlation when estimating
-- join cardinality with filters on both columns

Extended statistics (PostgreSQL 10+) let you tell the optimizer about column correlations. The dependencies type records functional dependencies between columns — if region determines which customer_types are available, the optimizer uses this to produce better estimates. The ndistinct type records the actual number of distinct value combinations, rather than the product of individual distinct counts. At HydroAlpes, extended statistics on (region, customer_type) and (substation_id, region) improved join cardinality estimates by 3–5x for the billing pipeline, reducing the frequency of hash spill events from daily to never.

blocks.title: Cardinality is the root of most plan problems
blocks.content: Almost every bad plan trace back to a bad cardinality estimate somewhere in the tree. A wrong row count estimate causes the optimizer to choose the wrong join algorithm, allocate the wrong amount of memory, and misjudge the cost of downstream operations. When diagnosing a slow query, the first check should always be: are the estimated row counts at each plan node within 3x of the actual counts? If not, fix the statistics before trying any other optimization.

When a query joins three or more tables, the database must decide the order in which to process the joins. A three-table join (A JOIN B JOIN C) can be executed as (A JOIN B) JOIN C, A JOIN (B JOIN C), or (A JOIN C) JOIN B — three possible orderings, each with different performance characteristics. For a five-table join, there are 120 possible orderings. The optimizer evaluates the cost of each ordering and picks the cheapest, but with limited time and imperfect statistics, it does not always pick the best one. Understanding join ordering — and knowing when to intervene — is essential for optimizing the multi-table joins that produce HydroAlpes' Gold-layer analytical datasets.
The Principle: Reduce Early

The most important optimization principle for multi-table joins is: reduce the data volume as early as possible. Joining the most selective table first — the one that eliminates the most rows — means subsequent joins process fewer rows. If meter_readings has 37.2 million rows for January and substations has 85 rows, joining meter_readings with substations first (via meters) does not reduce the row count because every reading has a substation. But filtering meter_readings to BERN-NORTH first (approximately 3.7 million rows) and then joining with substations processes 10x fewer rows through the join chain. The optimizer applies this principle automatically when statistics are accurate. When they are not, the optimizer may join in the wrong order.
The HydroAlpes Reordering

-- Original query: optimizer joined in written order
-- meter_readings → meters → substations → regions
-- Total join volume: 37.2M × 1.2M × 85 × 10 (simplified)

-- Rewritten with explicit ordering hint via CTE:
-- First filter, then join small tables, then join large tables
WITH filtered_readings AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
    AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
    AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
),
enriched AS (
  SELECT fr.meter_id, fr.daily_kw, fr.date, fr.region,
         m.customer_type, m.substation_id
  FROM   filtered_readings AS fr
    INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON fr.meter_id = m.meter_id
)
SELECT e.region,
       e.customer_type,
       s.name AS substation_name,
       SUM(e.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   enriched AS e
  INNER JOIN silver.substations AS s ON e.substation_id = s.substation_id
GROUP BY e.region, e.customer_type, s.name
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

The CTE-based approach explicitly materializes the filter-first step: reduce 130 million rows to 3.7 million in the first CTE, join with 1.2 million meters in the second CTE (producing 3.7 million enriched rows), then join with 85 substations in the final query. The intermediate row counts are dramatically smaller than the original plan where 37.2 million rows flowed through every join layer. At HydroAlpes, rewriting the billing query with explicit CTE ordering reduced execution time from 12 minutes to 48 seconds — a 15x improvement from the same logical query expressed in a way that guides the optimizer toward the efficient ordering.
When the Optimizer Gets Ordering Right

The PostgreSQL optimizer is generally excellent at join ordering when statistics are accurate. It uses a dynamic programming algorithm that evaluates all possible orderings for up to join_collapse_limit tables (default: 8) and a greedy algorithm for larger join counts. With accurate statistics, the optimizer considers the selectivity of each join condition, the size of each intermediate result, and the available indexes, then picks the ordering with the lowest total cost. Manual reordering through CTEs is unnecessary when statistics are current and the query involves fewer than 8 tables. The need for manual ordering arises when statistics are stale, when correlated columns produce bad estimates, or when the query exceeds join_collapse_limit.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Before manually reordering joins, try ANALYZE first. If the optimizer's ordering is wrong because statistics are stale, updating statistics is the correct and maintainable fix. Manual reordering via CTEs should be reserved for cases where accurate statistics still produce a suboptimal ordering — typically multi-table joins with correlated filter conditions that the optimizer cannot model. At HydroAlpes, fewer than 5% of queries require manual ordering after statistics are current.

Hash join is the workhorse algorithm for equi-joins in analytical queries. It builds a hash table from the smaller input (the build side), then probes the hash table with each row from the larger input (the probe side). When the hash table fits in memory, hash join is the fastest algorithm for large equi-joins. When it does not fit, PostgreSQL spills hash batches to temporary files on disk, and performance degrades dramatically — often by 10x or more. This lesson covers the hash join mechanics, the memory boundary that triggers spill, and the tuning parameters that keep hash joins in memory.
The Two Phases

Phase 1 (Build): the database reads every row from the build side, computes a hash of the join key, and inserts the row into a hash table in memory. The build side is always the smaller input — the optimizer chooses which side to build based on estimated row counts. Phase 2 (Probe): the database reads each row from the probe side, computes the same hash of the join key, and looks up matching rows in the hash table. A hash table lookup is O(1) — constant time regardless of the table size — so the probe phase is fast. The total cost is dominated by the build phase (one full read of the smaller table) plus the probe phase (one full read of the larger table). For HydroAlpes' billing join, the build side is meters (1.2 million rows, approximately 34 MB) and the probe side is meter_readings (37.2 million rows for January).
The work_mem Boundary

The hash table is built in memory allocated from the work_mem parameter. If the hash table fits within work_mem, the join completes in a single pass: build the table, probe it, done. If the hash table exceeds work_mem, PostgreSQL splits the build into batches, writes excess batches to temporary files on disk, then processes each batch separately — reading it back from disk during the probe phase. The disk I/O for writing and reading batches is orders of magnitude slower than in-memory operations. The threshold is sharp: a hash join just under work_mem runs in seconds; the same join just over work_mem runs in minutes.

-- Check current work_mem
SHOW work_mem;
-- Default: 4MB — far too small for mid-level scale

-- Increase work_mem for the current session
SET work_mem = '256MB';

-- Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and check for disk spill
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, m.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

-- Look for: Buffers: temp read=0 write=0 (no spill — good)
--       vs: Buffers: temp read=12400 write=12400 (spill — bad)

The BUFFERS output is the definitive indicator. If temp read and temp write are zero, the hash table fit in memory. If they are non-zero, the hash table spilled to disk. At HydroAlpes, the meters hash table is approximately 34 MB — far too large for the 4 MB default work_mem. Setting work_mem to 256 MB eliminates the spill entirely. The billing join went from 12 minutes (with spill) to 2 minutes (without spill) — a 6x improvement from a single configuration change. The remaining time is spent on the sequential scan of meter_readings, which requires the date index fix from lesson 1.
Tuning work_mem Safely

work_mem is allocated per-operation, not per-session. A query with 3 hash joins and 2 sorts allocates up to 5 × work_mem of memory. If work_mem is 256 MB and 20 concurrent sessions each run a query with 5 memory-consuming operators, the total memory demand is 20 × 5 × 256 MB = 25.6 GB. Setting work_mem too high risks out-of-memory errors under concurrent load. The safe approach: set a conservative global default (64 MB at HydroAlpes) and increase it per-session for specific queries that need more: SET LOCAL work_mem = '512MB' inside a transaction. This limits the higher allocation to one specific query without affecting the global setting.

blocks.title: Global work_mem changes affect the entire server
blocks.content: Changing work_mem globally (in postgresql.conf) affects every query on the server. A setting that works well for the billing pipeline may cause memory pressure when 50 analysts run concurrent ad-hoc queries. At HydroAlpes, the global work_mem is 64 MB (suitable for most queries), and the pipeline runs SET LOCAL work_mem = '256MB' at the start of each transaction that contains a large hash join. This targeted approach provides more memory where needed without risking server-wide memory exhaustion.

Merge join is the second major join algorithm in PostgreSQL. Unlike hash join (which builds a hash table in memory), merge join requires both inputs to be sorted on the join key, then performs a single linear pass through both sorted sequences, matching rows as it goes. The merge phase itself is extremely efficient — it touches each row exactly once. The cost is in the sorting: if the inputs are not already sorted, the database must sort them first, which can be expensive for large tables. The optimization opportunity is clear: if you can provide pre-sorted input (via an index or a prior sort), merge join avoids the sort cost entirely and becomes the fastest join algorithm for large-table-to-large-table joins.
When Merge Join Beats Hash Join

Merge join is faster than hash join in two scenarios. First, when both inputs are already sorted on the join key — either from indexes or from prior ORDER BY operations. The merge is a single linear pass, with no memory overhead for a hash table and no risk of disk spill. Second, when the hash table for the smaller input would exceed work_mem and spill to disk. A merge join with pre-sorted inputs avoids this entirely. The trade-off: if the inputs are not sorted and must be sorted explicitly, the sort cost can exceed the hash join cost, especially when one input is much smaller than the other (making the hash table small and unlikely to spill).
Index-Assisted Merge Join

-- If both tables have indexes on the join key, PostgreSQL can use
-- index scans to provide sorted input to a merge join — no explicit sort

-- Create indexes on join keys (if not already present)
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_meter_id ON silver.meter_readings (meter_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_meters_meter_id ON silver.meters (meter_id);

-- The optimizer may now choose a merge join with index-provided sort order
EXPLAIN
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, m.customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

With indexes on meter_id in both tables, the optimizer can choose index scans that return rows in meter_id order, feeding them into a merge join without an explicit sort. The plan shows Merge Join with two Index Scan children — no Sort operators in between. This is the most efficient equi-join configuration for large tables: no hash table memory, no sort memory, just a single pass through index-ordered rows from both sides. At HydroAlpes, the meter_id indexes serve double duty: they support both join performance and primary key uniqueness enforcement.
The Sort Spill Problem

When the optimizer chooses a merge join but the inputs are not pre-sorted, it inserts Sort operators below the merge. Sorting 37.2 million rows requires substantial memory — allocated from work_mem, the same parameter that governs hash join memory. If the sort does not fit in work_mem, PostgreSQL performs an external merge sort: it sorts chunks that fit in memory, writes them to temporary files, then merges the sorted chunks. Like hash spill, sort spill is dramatically slower than in-memory sorting. The diagnostic is the same: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) reveals Sort Method: external merge Disk: NNN kB when a sort spills. The fix: increase work_mem, create an index to provide pre-sorted input, or let the optimizer choose a hash join instead.

blocks.title: Indexes serve multiple purposes
blocks.content: An index on the join key simultaneously serves three purposes: it speeds up joins (index-assisted merge join or nested loop with index lookup), it speeds up WHERE filters on the same column (index scan), and it enforces uniqueness if combined with a UNIQUE constraint. At HydroAlpes, every join column that appears in production queries has an index. The storage cost of the indexes is a small fraction of the table's total size, and the performance benefit for joins, filters, and sort avoidance is substantial.

HydroAlpes' data lives in two systems: the PostgreSQL warehouse (Silver and Gold analytical tables) and the Spark cluster (large-scale historical transformations and ML feature engineering). The analytics team needs to join warehouse data with Spark-processed results. The regulatory team needs to combine PostgreSQL billing data with externally provided price benchmarks stored in a separate database. When the data you need lives in different systems, you cannot simply write a SQL JOIN — each database only knows about its own tables. This lesson covers the three patterns for joining data across system boundaries.
Pattern 1: ETL Stage and Join

The most common and most reliable pattern: extract data from the remote system, load it into a staging table in the local database, then join locally. The extraction is a separate pipeline step (often orchestrated by Airflow) that runs before the join query. The staging table is a regular table that the optimizer can analyze, index, and join using all the strategies it knows. The trade-off: the data in the staging table is a snapshot — it may be stale by the time the join runs. At HydroAlpes, the Spark-processed ML features are exported to a Parquet file nightly, loaded into a PostgreSQL staging table at 05:00, and the billing query that joins them runs at 05:30. The 30-minute delay is acceptable for daily batch processing.

-- Stage-and-join pattern: the data is already in the local database
-- The staging table was loaded by a separate pipeline step

-- Step 1 (pipeline): COPY Spark results into PostgreSQL staging table
-- COPY staging.ml_features FROM '/data/exports/features_20250115.csv' CSV HEADER;

-- Step 2 (query): join locally with full optimizer support
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       ml.predicted_demand,
       mr.daily_kw - ml.predicted_demand AS forecast_error
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN staging.ml_features AS ml
    ON mr.meter_id = ml.meter_id AND mr.date = ml.prediction_date
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH';

The join is a standard local join — the optimizer has full statistics on both tables, can choose any join algorithm, and can use indexes on both sides. The staging table is temporary: it is truncated and reloaded before each pipeline run, ensuring the data is fresh for every execution. This pattern is simple, reliable, and performs well because the join operates entirely within one database engine with full optimizer support. The complexity is in the orchestration (ensuring the staging load completes before the join query runs), not in the SQL itself.
Pattern 2: Foreign Data Wrappers

PostgreSQL's Foreign Data Wrapper (FDW) extension allows you to define foreign tables that reference data in another PostgreSQL instance, a MySQL database, or even a flat file. Queries against foreign tables transparently fetch data from the remote source. The syntax is standard SQL — you JOIN a local table with a foreign table as if they were in the same database. The trade-off: the optimizer has limited information about the foreign table (no local statistics, no local indexes), and data transfer happens over the network during query execution, which can be slow for large foreign tables.

-- Define a foreign table that references a remote PostgreSQL database
-- (one-time setup by the DBA)
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS postgres_fdw;

CREATE SERVER remote_pricing
  FOREIGN DATA WRAPPER postgres_fdw
  OPTIONS (host 'pricing-db.hydroalpes.internal', dbname 'pricing', port '5432');

CREATE FOREIGN TABLE remote.benchmark_prices (
    market_zone      VARCHAR(10),
    price_timestamp  TIMESTAMP,
    benchmark_eur    NUMERIC(10,4)
) SERVER remote_pricing
  OPTIONS (schema_name 'public', table_name 'benchmark_prices');

-- Query the foreign table as if it were local
SELECT ep.market_zone,
       ep.price_eur_mwh,
       bp.benchmark_eur,
       ep.price_eur_mwh - bp.benchmark_eur AS spread
FROM   silver.energy_prices AS ep
  INNER JOIN remote.benchmark_prices AS bp
    ON ep.market_zone = bp.market_zone
   AND ep.price_timestamp = bp.price_timestamp
WHERE  ep.price_timestamp >= '2025-01-15'
  AND  ep.price_timestamp <  '2025-01-16';

The foreign table remote.benchmark_prices looks like a regular table in SQL. The optimizer generates a plan that fetches the matching rows from the remote server during execution. For small foreign tables or selective filters that push down to the remote server, this works well. For large foreign tables or non-pushable filters, the database may fetch the entire foreign table over the network, which is slow. At HydroAlpes, FDW is used for the benchmark prices (small table, highly selective filter on timestamp) but not for cross-referencing the ML features (large table, better served by the ETL stage pattern).
Pattern 3: Application-Level Join

When neither staging nor FDW is appropriate — for example, when the remote data is in a system that does not support SQL (a REST API, a document database, an S3-based data lake) — the join is performed in application code. The pipeline queries both sources independently, loads the results into memory (a Pandas DataFrame, a PySpark DataFrame, or a Python dictionary), and performs the join in code. This is the most flexible pattern but the least efficient: the join does not benefit from database optimization, and the memory required to hold both datasets limits the scale. At HydroAlpes, application-level joins are reserved for small, non-SQL sources like the weather API and the regulatory tariff schedule.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Choose the join pattern based on the remote data's size and update frequency. For large, daily-updated data: ETL stage and join (most reliable, best optimizer support). For small, infrequently updated reference data: FDW (simplest, no pipeline needed). For non-SQL sources: application-level join (most flexible, least efficient). At HydroAlpes, 80% of cross-system joins use the ETL stage pattern because it provides the best combination of reliability, performance, and debuggability.

This module covered join performance at production scale: diagnosing slow joins with plan analysis, detecting and resolving fan-out, choosing between semi-joins and anti-joins and inner joins, using LATERAL for correlated lookups, understanding cardinality estimation and its impact on plan quality, optimizing join ordering for multi-table queries, tuning hash join memory to prevent disk spill, leveraging merge join with indexes for large-table-to-large-table equi-joins, and joining data across system boundaries. This final lesson synthesizes those techniques into the decision tree and review checklist that HydroAlpes' data engineering team uses for every join in production.
The Join Decision Tree

rows: Do you need columns from the joined table?
Use INNER JOIN or LEFT JOIN
Use EXISTS (semi-join) or NOT EXISTS (anti-join)
Is the join key unique in the dimension table?
Safe to join directly
Deduplicate first (ROW_NUMBER) or fix the source
Is the dimension table small (<1% of fact table)?
Expect hash join with small build side — verify no spill
Expect hash join or merge join — verify work_mem is sufficient
Does the query filter the fact table before joining?
Good — reduced intermediate volume
Add a CTE to filter first if statistics do not guide the optimizer
Are join cardinality estimates accurate (within 3x)?
Trust the optimizer's plan
Run ANALYZE; consider extended statistics for correlated columns
Does the plan show hash spill (temp read/write > 0)?
Increase work_mem or switch to merge join with indexes
Hash join is running efficiently in memory

blocks.content: The Join Review Checklist

Every pipeline join at HydroAlpes passes through a six-point review before deployment to production. The review is documented in the pull request description alongside the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output, and it takes approximately 3 minutes per join. Each checkpoint targets a specific category of join problem that has caused a production incident in the past: fan-out from duplicate dimension keys, wrong algorithm choice from stale statistics, cardinality estimation errors that cascade through multi-table joins, hash table spill from insufficient work_mem, missing filter pushdown that inflates intermediate volumes, and dimension key duplication that silently multiplies rows. A join that fails any checkpoint goes back to the author for correction before the pull request is approved.

rows: Row count preservation
Compare COUNT(*) before and after join — ratio must be 1.0 ± 0.001
Join algorithm
EXPLAIN shows Hash Join or Merge Join for equi-joins; no Nested Loop on large tables
Cardinality accuracy
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: estimated rows within 3x of actual at every join node
No hash spill
BUFFERS: temp read=0 write=0 (or increase work_mem until no spill)
Filter before join
Selective WHERE conditions appear below the join in the plan, not above
Dimension key uniqueness
SELECT key, COUNT(*) GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 returns zero rows

blocks.content: The Escalation Path

When a join is slow despite passing the review checklist, HydroAlpes follows an escalation path. First level: update statistics (ANALYZE) and re-run — this resolves 60% of join performance issues. Second level: increase work_mem for the specific query and verify no hash spill — resolves another 20%. Third level: create or modify indexes on join keys to enable index-assisted merge join or nested loop — resolves 15%. Fourth level: restructure the query with CTEs to enforce explicit join ordering — resolves the remaining 5%. Each level is more invasive than the previous, so the team starts at level 1 and escalates only when the simpler fix does not resolve the problem.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 3, you will expand your analytical SQL toolkit beyond the junior-level window functions and GROUP BY patterns. Advanced frame specifications, GROUPING SETS for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting, and conditional aggregation with FILTER — these are the techniques that power HydroAlpes' executive dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts. Every advanced analytical query builds on the join optimization skills from this module, because the joins that feed the analytics must be efficient before the analytics can be fast.

modules.title: Advanced Analytical SQL
modules.description: Master advanced window function patterns, GROUPING SETS for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting, and conditional aggregation for complex reporting.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' regulatory affairs team needed a single report combining daily consumption by region, weekly consumption by region, monthly consumption by region, and a grand total — all in one result set. The junior approach required four separate queries with UNION ALL: one GROUP BY region, date; one GROUP BY region, DATE_TRUNC('week', date); one GROUP BY region, DATE_TRUNC('month', date); and one with no GROUP BY at all. The four queries scanned the 37.2-million-row January dataset four times, took 48 seconds in total, and produced a fragile pipeline where every schema change had to be replicated across four queries. The mid-level approach uses GROUPING SETS to produce the same result in a single scan, a single query, in 14 seconds. This module teaches the advanced analytical patterns that replace multi-query workarounds with single-query solutions.
The Mid-Level Analytical Toolkit

The junior track covered the five aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), GROUP BY, HAVING, and the core window functions (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD, running totals, moving averages). These tools handle the majority of analytical queries. But production reporting demands patterns that the basic toolkit cannot express cleanly: multi-level subtotals in a single pass, pivoted cross-tabs where months become columns, gap-aware moving averages that handle missing dates correctly, percentile-based outlier detection, and conditional aggregation that computes separate metrics for each customer type without multiple passes over the data. Each of these patterns has a junior-level workaround (multiple queries, application-level pivoting, CASE WHEN inside aggregates), and each has a mid-level solution that is faster, more readable, and more maintainable.
What This Module Covers

Lessons 2–3 extend window functions with advanced frame specifications (ROWS vs RANGE vs GROUPS, EXCLUDE clauses) and percentile functions (NTILE, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST, PERCENTILE_CONT). Lessons 4–5 introduce GROUPING SETS, ROLLUP, and CUBE for multi-level aggregation within a single query. Lessons 6–7 cover pivoting (rows to columns) and unpivoting (columns to rows) — the transformations that reshape data between analytical and reporting formats. Lesson 8 introduces the FILTER clause for conditional aggregation — the modern replacement for CASE WHEN inside aggregate functions. Lessons 9–10 combine these patterns into the production analytical queries that power HydroAlpes' dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts.

-- Preview: GROUPING SETS replaces four separate queries with one
-- Daily, weekly, monthly, and grand total — single scan, single query
SELECT mr.region,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)::DATE AS month,
       DATE_TRUNC('week', mr.date)::DATE  AS week,
       mr.date,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.date),                              -- daily by region
  (mr.region, DATE_TRUNC('week', mr.date)::DATE),    -- weekly by region
  (mr.region, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)::DATE),   -- monthly by region
  ()                                                  -- grand total
)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.date NULLS LAST;

This single query produces four aggregation levels simultaneously. The database scans the data once, computes all four grouping levels in a single pass, and returns the combined result. The NULL values in the non-grouped columns indicate which level each row represents — a concept you will learn in detail in lesson 4. The performance advantage over four separate queries is substantial: one scan instead of four, one sort instead of four, and one result set that a downstream dashboard can consume directly without application-level merging. This is the kind of consolidation that mid-level analytical SQL enables.

blocks.title: Single-scan patterns replace multi-query workarounds
blocks.content: Every technique in this module follows the same principle: replace multiple passes over the data with a single pass that computes multiple results simultaneously. GROUPING SETS replaces UNION ALL of multiple GROUP BY queries. FILTER replaces multiple CASE WHEN expressions inside aggregates. Pivoting replaces application-level row-to-column transformation. The database is almost always faster at these operations than application code, because it can optimize the single scan with indexes, parallelism, and memory management that application code cannot access.

The junior track covered ROWS BETWEEN for moving averages: ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW computes a 7-day moving average by counting physical rows. But physical rows and logical time intervals are not the same thing. If meter CH-BERN-00142 has no reading on January 8 (a gap in the data), the 7-row window includes January 1–7 and January 9 — spanning 8 calendar days, not 7. The RANGE frame mode solves this by operating on value ranges rather than row counts: RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '6 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW includes all rows within 6 calendar days of the current row, regardless of how many physical rows that spans. Understanding the difference between ROWS, RANGE, and GROUPS is essential for producing correct analytics on time-series data with gaps.
ROWS vs RANGE vs GROUPS

rows: ROWS
Physical row positions (1st, 2nd, 3rd row from current)
Ignores gaps — 7 rows might span 10 calendar days
Fixed-count windows: 'the previous 7 readings' regardless of dates
RANGE
Value ranges (all rows within a value distance from current row)
Respects gaps — only rows within the date range are included
Time-based windows: 'all readings within 7 calendar days'
GROUPS
Peer groups (all rows with the same ORDER BY value count as one group)
Peer-aware — tied values are treated as one unit
Peer-based windows: 'the previous 3 distinct dates' (PostgreSQL 11+)

blocks.content: RANGE for Gap-Aware Moving Averages

-- ROWS frame: 7-row window (may span more than 7 calendar days if gaps exist)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS rows_avg_7
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

-- RANGE frame: 7-calendar-day window (correct even with gaps)
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '6 days' PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
       ) AS range_avg_7d
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

If January 8 has no reading, the ROWS frame on January 9 looks back 6 rows and includes January 1–7 and January 9 — an 8-day span with only 7 data points. The RANGE frame on January 9 looks back 6 calendar days and includes January 3–9 — a true 7-day window that correctly excludes dates outside the range. For continuous time-series without gaps (like HydroAlpes' daily readings for active meters), both produce identical results. For time-series with gaps (meters that occasionally fail to report, price calendars that skip weekends), RANGE produces the analytically correct result. At HydroAlpes, the energy price moving average uses RANGE because the price calendar has no entries on Swiss public holidays.
EXCLUDE Clauses

-- EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW: compute the average of neighbors, not including self
-- Useful for outlier detection: how does this reading compare to its neighbors?
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
         EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW
       ) AS neighbor_avg,
       mr.daily_kw - AVG(mr.daily_kw) OVER (
         ORDER BY mr.date
         ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND 3 FOLLOWING
         EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW
       ) AS deviation
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-14'
ORDER BY mr.date;

EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW removes the current row from the window computation. The neighbor_avg is the average of the 3 preceding and 3 following rows, excluding the current row itself. The deviation column shows how far the current reading deviates from its neighbors. This is a local outlier detection pattern: if the deviation is more than 3 standard deviations from the neighbor average, the reading is suspicious. PostgreSQL 11+ also supports EXCLUDE TIES (exclude rows with the same ORDER BY value as the current row) and EXCLUDE GROUP (exclude all rows in the current row's peer group). At HydroAlpes, the EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW pattern powers the anomaly detection queries in the data quality framework (Module 9).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use ROWS for fixed-count windows (the last 7 readings, the next 3 rows). Use RANGE for time-based windows (all readings within 7 calendar days, all prices within 1 hour). Use GROUPS for peer-based windows (the last 3 distinct dates, the next 2 distinct regions — rare but useful for categorical ordering). When in doubt, RANGE is the safer default for time-series because it handles gaps correctly. ROWS is appropriate only when you are certain the data has no gaps or when gaps are intentionally treated as non-existent.

HydroAlpes' pricing team needs to classify meters into consumption tiers: the top 25% by monthly consumption are 'high-usage' and pay a premium rate, the next 50% are 'standard,' and the bottom 25% are 'low-usage' and receive a discount. The junior approach uses subqueries: compute the 25th and 75th percentiles in one query, then use those values as filter thresholds in a CASE expression. The mid-level approach uses NTILE(4) to divide meters into quartiles in a single window function call, then maps quartiles to tier names. NTILE and the related percentile functions (PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST, PERCENTILE_CONT, PERCENTILE_DISC) provide built-in statistical classification that eliminates the multi-step workarounds.
NTILE — Dividing into Equal Buckets

-- Classify meters into consumption quartiles for tiered pricing
WITH monthly_totals AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS jan_total
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY mr.meter_id
),
quartiled AS (
  SELECT meter_id,
         jan_total,
         NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY jan_total DESC) AS quartile
  FROM   monthly_totals
)
SELECT quartile,
       CASE quartile
         WHEN 1 THEN 'High-usage (premium rate)'
         WHEN 2 THEN 'Above-average'
         WHEN 3 THEN 'Below-average'
         WHEN 4 THEN 'Low-usage (discount rate)'
       END AS tier_name,
       COUNT(*)           AS meter_count,
       MIN(jan_total)     AS min_kw,
       MAX(jan_total)     AS max_kw,
       AVG(jan_total)     AS avg_kw
FROM   quartiled
GROUP BY quartile
ORDER BY quartile;

NTILE(4) divides the 1.2 million meters into 4 approximately equal groups of 300,000 each, ordered by jan_total descending. Quartile 1 contains the highest consumers (above 182.40 kW/month), and quartile 4 contains the lowest. The outer query aggregates each quartile to show the tier boundaries, meter count, and average consumption. This single query replaces the three-step junior approach (compute percentiles, apply thresholds, classify) with a one-pass window function. The NTILE result is deterministic: ties at the boundary are assigned to the lower-numbered quartile.
PERCENT_RANK and CUME_DIST

PERCENT_RANK returns a value between 0 and 1 representing the row's relative position in the partition: 0 for the first row, 1 for the last, and proportional values in between. CUME_DIST returns the cumulative distribution: the fraction of rows that have a value less than or equal to the current row's value. Both are useful for statistical analysis. At HydroAlpes, PERCENT_RANK identifies where each meter falls in the consumption distribution: a PERCENT_RANK of 0.95 means the meter consumes more than 95% of all meters. CUME_DIST answers the inverse question: what percentage of meters consume less than or equal to a given value? These functions power the consumption distribution charts on HydroAlpes' executive dashboard.
PERCENTILE_CONT and PERCENTILE_DISC

-- Compute exact percentile values for the consumption distribution
-- PERCENTILE_CONT interpolates between values (continuous)
-- PERCENTILE_DISC returns an actual value from the dataset (discrete)
SELECT mr.region,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.50) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS median_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.75) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p75_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.95) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p95_kw,
       PERCENTILE_CONT(0.99) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY mr.daily_kw) AS p99_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS mean_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

The percentile profile per region reveals information that the mean alone hides. BERN-NORTH has a median of 0.82 kW (half the meters consume less) but a mean of 1.54 kW — the distribution is right-skewed, pulled up by industrial meters. The p99 of 142.30 kW shows that 1% of meters consume more than 142 kW — these are the industrial facilities that dominate regional totals despite being a tiny fraction of the meter count. This profile shapes every analytical decision: billing tiers should be based on percentiles, not means, because the mean is distorted by outliers. At HydroAlpes, every Gold-layer consumption report includes the median alongside the mean per Lena's reporting standards.

blocks.title: Percentiles reveal what means hide
blocks.content: When the mean and median diverge significantly (mean 1.54, median 0.82), the distribution is skewed and the mean is misleading. Reporting only the mean tells stakeholders that 'the average meter consumes 1.54 kW/day,' but the reality is that half the meters consume less than 0.82. Always report percentiles alongside means for skewed distributions — which describes almost every real-world energy, financial, and usage dataset.

GROUP BY computes one aggregation level: one row per unique combination of the grouped columns. If you need multiple aggregation levels — totals by region, by customer type, by region-and-type, and a grand total — the junior approach requires four separate GROUP BY queries combined with UNION ALL. GROUPING SETS lets you specify all four levels in a single GROUP BY clause, and the database computes them in one scan. The result set contains rows from all specified levels, with NULL values in the columns that are not part of a particular level's grouping. GROUPING SETS is the foundation for multi-level reporting and is the basis for the ROLLUP and CUBE shortcuts you will learn in lesson 5.
The Syntax

-- Four aggregation levels in one query with GROUPING SETS
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)         AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.customer_type),  -- level 1: by region and type
  (mr.region),                    -- level 2: by region only
  (mr.customer_type),             -- level 3: by type only
  ()                              -- level 4: grand total
)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

region      | customer_type | total_kw        | readings
------------+---------------+-----------------+-----------
BERN-NORTH  | COMMERCIAL    |     491,225.65  |   155,000
BERN-NORTH  | INDUSTRIAL    |     620,945.25  |    46,500
BERN-NORTH  | RESIDENTIAL   |   1,149,774.70  | 1,271,000
BERN-NORTH  | (null)        |   2,261,945.60  | 1,472,500   ← region subtotal
BERN-SOUTH  | COMMERCIAL    |     478,118.40  |   151,200
...         | ...           |             ... |       ...
(null)      | COMMERCIAL    |   2,843,120.85  | 1,164,200   ← customer_type total
(null)      | INDUSTRIAL    |   3,582,237.10  |   348,200
(null)      | RESIDENTIAL   |   9,411,485.00  |10,287,600
(null)      | (null)        |  15,836,842.95  |11,800,000   ← grand total

The result contains four types of rows. Rows where both region and customer_type are non-NULL are the detail level (region-by-type). Rows where customer_type is NULL but region is non-NULL are region subtotals. Rows where region is NULL but customer_type is non-NULL are customer-type totals. The row where both are NULL is the grand total. The database computed all four levels in a single scan of the data — no UNION ALL, no four-pass aggregation. For HydroAlpes' 37.2 million January readings, this single-scan approach is roughly 3x faster than the four-query alternative.
The GROUPING() Function

-- GROUPING() distinguishes NULLs from grouping from real NULLs in the data
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       GROUPING(mr.region)        AS is_region_total,
       GROUPING(mr.customer_type) AS is_type_total,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
  (mr.region, mr.customer_type),
  (mr.region),
  ()
)
ORDER BY GROUPING(mr.region), mr.region NULLS LAST,
         GROUPING(mr.customer_type), mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

The GROUPING() function returns 1 if the column is aggregated at the current level (the NULL in the output represents 'all values') and 0 if the column is part of the current grouping. This distinguishes between a NULL that means 'this is a subtotal row' and a NULL that means 'the column's actual value is NULL.' At HydroAlpes, region never contains NULL data, so the distinction is academic. But in datasets where grouped columns can legitimately contain NULL values, GROUPING() is essential for correct interpretation. The ORDER BY clause uses GROUPING() to sort subtotal rows after detail rows within each region.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When building reporting queries with GROUPING SETS, include a level_indicator column using GROUPING() values: CASE WHEN GROUPING(region) = 0 AND GROUPING(customer_type) = 0 THEN 'detail' WHEN GROUPING(customer_type) = 1 THEN 'region_subtotal' ELSE 'grand_total' END. This makes the result self-documenting and allows downstream dashboards to filter or format each level differently — bold for subtotals, highlighted for grand totals — without parsing NULL patterns.

GROUPING SETS requires you to enumerate every aggregation level explicitly. For hierarchical data — where the levels follow a natural hierarchy (region → customer_type → grand total) — ROLLUP generates the hierarchical grouping sets automatically. For cross-tabulation data — where every combination of dimensions is needed — CUBE generates all possible grouping sets automatically. Both are shorthand for specific GROUPING SETS patterns: ROLLUP produces progressive subtotals from left to right, and CUBE produces every possible combination of the grouped columns.
ROLLUP — Hierarchical Subtotals

-- ROLLUP produces: (region, type), (region), ()
-- This is the hierarchical subtotal pattern: detail → region subtotal → grand total
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY ROLLUP (mr.region, mr.customer_type)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

ROLLUP (region, customer_type) is equivalent to GROUPING SETS ((region, customer_type), (region), ()). It produces three levels: the detail level (region + customer_type), the region subtotal (region only, customer_type NULL), and the grand total (both NULL). ROLLUP always produces N+1 grouping sets from N columns, progressing from left to right: the leftmost column is always included, and columns are dropped from right to left. This matches the natural hierarchy: you want subtotals by region (dropping customer_type) and a grand total (dropping everything), but you do not want subtotals by customer_type alone (dropping region) — that would be a cross-tab, not a hierarchy.
CUBE — All Combinations

-- CUBE produces: (region, type), (region), (type), ()
-- Every possible combination of the grouped columns
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)   AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*)           AS readings
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY CUBE (mr.region, mr.customer_type)
ORDER BY mr.region NULLS LAST, mr.customer_type NULLS LAST;

CUBE (region, customer_type) is equivalent to GROUPING SETS ((region, customer_type), (region), (customer_type), ()). It produces 2^N grouping sets from N columns — every possible subset. For two columns, that is 4 levels: detail, region-only, type-only, and grand total. CUBE is appropriate when every combination of dimensions is analytically meaningful — when you need both 'total for BERN-NORTH across all types' and 'total for COMMERCIAL across all regions.' At HydroAlpes, the regulatory report uses CUBE because the regulator requires subtotals in every dimension independently, not just hierarchically.
ROLLUP vs CUBE vs GROUPING SETS

rows.left: Produces N+1 grouping sets from N columns
rows.right: Produces 2^N grouping sets from N columns

rows.left: Subtotals follow a hierarchy: left columns always included
rows.right: Subtotals for every possible column combination

rows.left: ROLLUP(A, B, C) → (A,B,C), (A,B), (A), ()
Use for hierarchical dimensions (region → type → detail)
rows.right: Use for cross-tab reports where every dimension is independent

rows.left: Fewer output rows — simpler to consume
rows.right: More output rows — comprehensive but potentially overwhelming

blocks.content: GROUPING SETS is the general form — ROLLUP and CUBE are shortcuts for common patterns. You can always replace ROLLUP or CUBE with an explicit GROUPING SETS clause. The explicit form is necessary when you need a custom set of levels that does not match a pure hierarchy (ROLLUP) or a full cross-tab (CUBE). At HydroAlpes, the billing report uses ROLLUP (region hierarchy), the regulatory report uses CUBE (all dimension combinations), and the executive dashboard uses GROUPING SETS with a custom set that includes daily, weekly, and monthly levels but not quarterly — because the quarterly values come from a separate fiscal calendar.

blocks.title: Three columns with CUBE = 8 grouping sets
blocks.content: CUBE with 3 columns produces 8 grouping sets. With 4 columns, it produces 16. With 5, it produces 32. The output size grows exponentially with the number of columns. For production reports, prefer ROLLUP (linear growth) when a hierarchy is appropriate, and use CUBE or explicit GROUPING SETS only when you genuinely need every dimension combination. Unnecessary CUBE with many columns produces enormous result sets that downstream consumers cannot effectively use.

Pivoting transforms rows into columns: a result where each month appears as a separate row (January, February, March as three rows per region) becomes a result where each month is a separate column (jan_kw, feb_kw, mar_kw as three columns per region). This row-to-column transformation is one of the most requested reporting formats — spreadsheet users expect months as columns, not as rows — and it is also one of the most awkward to express in standard SQL. PostgreSQL provides two approaches: the conditional aggregation pattern (portable across all databases) and the crosstab function from the tablefunc extension (PostgreSQL-specific). Spark SQL provides a native PIVOT clause.
Conditional Aggregation Pivot

-- Pivot monthly consumption: one row per region, one column per month
-- Uses CASE WHEN inside SUM to route values to the correct column
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 1
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS jan_kw,
