       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 2
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS feb_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN EXTRACT(MONTH FROM mr.date) = 3
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS mar_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-04-01'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

Each CASE WHEN expression routes values to the correct month column: when the month is January, the daily_kw is summed into jan_kw; when it is February, into feb_kw. Values from other months contribute NULL (the CASE has no ELSE), and SUM ignores NULLs, so each column accumulates only the values for its month. This pattern is portable — it works in every SQL database — but it requires hardcoding the column names. If the number of pivot values is unknown or changes frequently (e.g., pivoting by customer_type where new types might appear), the conditional approach requires query modification. For a fixed set of months, it is the standard approach.
Spark SQL PIVOT

-- Spark SQL native PIVOT syntax (not available in PostgreSQL)
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT region,
         EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date) AS mth,
         daily_kw
  FROM   silver_meter_readings
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-04-01'
)
PIVOT (
  SUM(daily_kw)
  FOR mth IN (1 AS jan_kw, 2 AS feb_kw, 3 AS mar_kw)
)
ORDER BY region;

Spark SQL's PIVOT clause is syntactically cleaner: you specify the aggregate function, the pivot column, and the values to pivot into columns. The result is identical to the conditional aggregation approach, but the intent is explicit rather than encoded in CASE WHEN expressions. The FOR ... IN clause lists the values that become columns — just as the conditional approach requires hardcoded months. Dynamic pivoting (where the pivot values come from the data) requires dynamic SQL generation in both PostgreSQL and Spark, which is covered in Module 5 (lesson 9). At HydroAlpes, the Spark pipeline uses PIVOT for reports that will be exported to Excel, and the PostgreSQL warehouse uses the conditional aggregation approach for the same reports served to dashboards.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Pivoting should be the last step in a query, applied to a small aggregated result set — not to millions of raw rows. First aggregate the data to the desired grain (GROUP BY region, month), then pivot the aggregated result. Pivoting raw data creates one column per pivot value per detail row, which is both slow and difficult to consume. At HydroAlpes, the pivot always operates on fewer than 1,000 rows (10 regions × 12 months × a few customer types) — the aggregation reduces millions of readings to a small summary before pivoting reshapes it.

Unpivoting is the reverse of pivoting: it transforms columns into rows. A table with columns jan_kw, feb_kw, mar_kw (wide format) becomes a table with columns month and kw (long format). Unpivoting is needed when data arrives from external sources in wide format — spreadsheets, reporting systems, and partner APIs that deliver one column per time period instead of one row per time period. HydroAlpes receives quarterly benchmark prices from the EPEX energy exchange in a CSV with columns region, q1_price, q2_price, q3_price, q4_price. The pipeline must unpivot these into rows (region, quarter, price) before they can be joined with the daily price data.
LATERAL with VALUES

-- Unpivot quarterly benchmark prices from wide to long format
-- LATERAL with VALUES creates rows from column values
SELECT bp.region,
       u.quarter,
       u.price_eur_mwh
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide AS bp
  CROSS JOIN LATERAL (
    VALUES
      ('Q1', bp.q1_price),
      ('Q2', bp.q2_price),
      ('Q3', bp.q3_price),
      ('Q4', bp.q4_price)
  ) AS u(quarter, price_eur_mwh)
WHERE  u.price_eur_mwh IS NOT NULL  -- skip quarters with no data
ORDER BY bp.region, u.quarter;

region      | quarter | price_eur_mwh
------------+---------+--------------
BERN-NORTH  | Q1      |        48.20
BERN-NORTH  | Q2      |        42.10
BERN-NORTH  | Q3      |        38.80
BERN-NORTH  | Q4      |        52.40
BERN-SOUTH  | Q1      |        47.90
...

CROSS JOIN LATERAL with a VALUES clause creates multiple rows from each input row. For each region in the wide table, the LATERAL subquery produces four rows — one per quarter — by explicitly listing the (quarter_name, column_value) pairs. The WHERE clause filters out NULLs for quarters where benchmark data is not yet available. This pattern is the standard unpivoting approach in PostgreSQL. It is explicit, readable, and works for any number of columns. The trade-off is that the VALUES list must enumerate every column being unpivoted — if the wide table has 12 monthly columns, the VALUES list has 12 entries.
UNION ALL Alternative

-- Alternative: UNION ALL with one SELECT per source column
-- More verbose but works in every SQL dialect
SELECT region, 'Q1' AS quarter, q1_price AS price_eur_mwh
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q1_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q2', q2_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q2_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q3', q3_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q3_price IS NOT NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT region, 'Q4', q4_price
FROM   staging.benchmark_prices_wide WHERE q4_price IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY region, quarter;

The UNION ALL approach produces the same result but scans the table once per column being unpivoted — four scans for four quarters. The LATERAL approach scans once and produces multiple rows per input row, making it more efficient for tables with many columns or many rows. At HydroAlpes, the LATERAL approach is the standard for unpivoting because the benchmark prices table has 10 regions × 4 quarters = 40 rows, making either approach fast, but the LATERAL version is more maintainable when new columns are added. In Spark SQL, the equivalent operation uses the stack() function or the UNPIVOT clause (Spark 3.4+), which provides cleaner syntax for the same transformation.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Unpivoting is an ingestion-time operation: convert wide-format external data to long format as early as possible in the pipeline (typically at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary). Once in long format, the data integrates naturally with GROUP BY, window functions, and joins — all of which expect one observation per row. Storing data in wide format and unpivoting at query time forces every consumer to handle the transformation, which is fragile and inconsistent. At HydroAlpes, the benchmark prices are unpivoted during Silver ingestion and stored in long format permanently.

The pivoting lesson used CASE WHEN inside SUM to route values to different columns. This pattern — conditional aggregation — is one of the most common in analytical SQL, but the CASE WHEN syntax is verbose and difficult to read when multiple conditions are involved. PostgreSQL (and the SQL:2003 standard) provides the FILTER clause as a cleaner alternative: SUM(daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') replaces SUM(CASE WHEN customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' THEN daily_kw END). Both produce the same result, but FILTER is more readable, more maintainable, and often more efficient because the database can optimize the filter separately from the aggregation.
FILTER vs CASE WHEN

-- CASE WHEN approach (portable but verbose)
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS residential_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS commercial_kw,
       SUM(CASE WHEN mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL'
                THEN mr.daily_kw END) AS industrial_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region;

-- FILTER approach (cleaner, PostgreSQL 9.4+)
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') AS residential_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL')  AS commercial_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL')  AS industrial_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region;

Both queries produce identical results. The FILTER version is more readable because each aggregate clearly states its condition as a subordinate clause rather than embedding it inside a CASE expression. The FILTER clause can be used with any aggregate function: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE condition), AVG(col) FILTER (WHERE condition), MIN, MAX, and even array_agg. At HydroAlpes, the team style guide mandates FILTER over CASE WHEN for conditional aggregation in all new PostgreSQL queries, reserving CASE WHEN for Spark SQL queries where FILTER is not yet supported.
Multi-Metric Reports in One Pass

-- Comprehensive regional report: multiple metrics across customer types
-- Single pass over the data, single GROUP BY, multiple FILTER conditions
SELECT mr.region,
       COUNT(*) AS total_readings,
       COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE mr.daily_kw > 100) AS high_usage_readings,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) AS total_meters,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL') AS industrial_meters,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL') AS residential_kw,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) AS overall_avg,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL') AS industrial_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

Eight distinct metrics computed in a single GROUP BY: total readings, high-usage readings (over 100 kW), total distinct meters, industrial meters only, total consumption, residential consumption only, overall average, and industrial average. Without FILTER, this query would require eight CASE WHEN expressions — each adding 2–3 lines of verbose syntax. With FILTER, each metric is a concise, self-documenting expression. The database scans the data once, computes all eight metrics in a single aggregation pass, and produces the complete regional report. This single-pass efficiency is the reason FILTER-based conditional aggregation has become the preferred pattern for multi-metric dashboards.
Dialect Availability

FILTER is part of the SQL:2003 standard and is supported in PostgreSQL (9.4+), SQLite, and DuckDB. It is not yet supported in Spark SQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift, or SQL Server — these databases require the CASE WHEN approach. At HydroAlpes, the PostgreSQL warehouse queries use FILTER, and the equivalent Spark SQL pipelines use CASE WHEN. The team maintains a translation guide that maps FILTER expressions to their CASE WHEN equivalents for cross-engine compatibility. When FILTER is not available, CASE WHEN inside aggregates is the universal fallback — verbose but functionally identical.

blocks.title: One scan, many metrics
blocks.content: The power of conditional aggregation — whether expressed with FILTER or CASE WHEN — is that it computes arbitrarily many conditional metrics in a single pass over the data. Without it, each conditional metric requires a separate query or a join with a filtered subquery. Ten metrics on a 37.2-million-row table as ten separate queries means scanning 372 million rows total. The same ten metrics with conditional aggregation scans 37.2 million rows once. The 10x reduction in I/O is the production reason to master this pattern.

The previous lessons taught individual analytical techniques: advanced frames, percentile functions, GROUPING SETS, pivoting, and FILTER. This lesson combines them into the production patterns that HydroAlpes' analytical queries use daily. Each pattern solves a specific reporting or analytical problem that appears repeatedly across energy data, financial data, and operational data. The patterns are composable — you can combine FILTER with GROUPING SETS, or window functions with pivoting, to create queries that would require dozens of lines of application code if implemented outside SQL.
Pattern 1: Cohort Analysis with NTILE

-- Classify meters into consumption deciles and analyze each cohort
WITH meter_totals AS (
  SELECT meter_id, region,
         SUM(daily_kw) AS jan_total
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
  GROUP BY meter_id, region
),
cohorted AS (
  SELECT *, NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY jan_total DESC) AS decile
  FROM   meter_totals
)
SELECT decile,
       COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
       SUM(jan_total) AS cohort_total_kw,
       ROUND(SUM(jan_total) / SUM(SUM(jan_total)) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_total,
       MIN(jan_total) AS min_kw,
       MAX(jan_total) AS max_kw
FROM   cohorted
GROUP BY decile
ORDER BY decile;

The top decile (120,000 meters, 10% of the fleet) accounts for 74.9% of total consumption. The bottom decile accounts for 0.3%. This Pareto analysis reveals the extreme concentration of energy usage in a small fraction of the meter fleet — a pattern that shapes billing strategy, grid capacity planning, and demand management programs. The query combines NTILE (lesson 3), a window function for percent-of-total (SUM OVER()), and GROUP BY to produce a complete cohort analysis in a single composable statement. At HydroAlpes, this cohort analysis runs monthly and feeds the capacity planning dashboard.
Pattern 2: Period-Over-Period Comparison with FILTER

-- Compare January 2025 vs January 2024 by region, single query
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
                                  AND mr.date <  '2025-02-01') AS jan_2025_kw,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2024-01-01'
                                  AND mr.date <  '2024-02-01') AS jan_2024_kw,
       ROUND((
         SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01') /
         NULLIF(SUM(mr.daily_kw) FILTER (WHERE mr.date >= '2024-01-01' AND mr.date < '2024-02-01'), 0)
         - 1) * 100, 1
       ) AS yoy_change_pct
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  (mr.date >= '2024-01-01' AND mr.date < '2024-02-01')
   OR  (mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01')
GROUP BY mr.region
ORDER BY mr.region;

A single query computes January 2025 totals, January 2024 totals, and the year-over-year change percentage for each region. The FILTER clauses route readings from different years into separate columns, and the arithmetic computes the percentage change. NULLIF prevents division by zero for regions that had no data in January 2024 (the newly added sub-regions). This pattern replaces a self-join (joining the table with itself on region, with different date filters on each side) with a more efficient and readable conditional aggregation. At HydroAlpes, this year-over-year pattern appears in the executive dashboard, the regulatory filing, and the demand forecast input.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Combine GROUPING SETS with FILTER for the most powerful single-pass reports. GROUPING SETS provides multi-level subtotals (by region, by customer type, grand total). FILTER provides conditional metrics (residential vs industrial, current year vs prior year). Together, they produce a complete multi-dimensional, multi-metric report in a single scan — replacing what would otherwise be a matrix of separate queries or a complex application-level merge.

This module expanded your analytical SQL toolkit from the junior-level foundations to mid-level production patterns: advanced frame specifications for gap-aware moving averages, NTILE and percentile functions for statistical classification, GROUPING SETS with ROLLUP and CUBE for multi-level aggregation, pivoting and unpivoting for format transformation, and FILTER for conditional aggregation. This final lesson synthesizes these techniques into the five core analytical queries that HydroAlpes' team maintains as reusable, version-controlled SQL files — the analytical query library that powers dashboards, regulatory reports, and capacity forecasts.
The Five Core Queries

rows: Executive Summary
GROUPING SETS with FILTER for multi-level, multi-metric report
CEO dashboard, board reporting
Monthly
Regulatory Filing
CUBE with all dimension combinations, GROUPING() for level labels
Swiss ELCOM / Austrian E-Control
Quarterly
Capacity Forecast Input
RANGE-frame moving averages, PERCENTILE_CONT for demand profiles
Grid planning team
Weekly
Meter Classification
NTILE for tier assignment, period-over-period FILTER for trend
Billing and pricing team
Monthly
Anomaly Detection
EXCLUDE CURRENT ROW neighbor comparison, z-score with window functions
Data quality framework (Module 9)
Daily

blocks.content: Query Library Management

Each core query is a .sql file in HydroAlpes' Git repository, version-controlled alongside the pipeline code. The file includes a header comment block with the query's purpose, consumer, schedule, expected output schema, and the most recent performance baseline (execution time and row count). Changes to core queries go through the same pull request review as pipeline code changes: the reviewer checks the EXPLAIN output, verifies the GROUPING SETS and FILTER logic, and confirms that the output schema has not changed in a way that breaks downstream consumers. This process prevents the common failure mode where an analyst modifies a shared query and silently changes the metric definitions that dashboards depend on.

The query library is the bridge between the analytical techniques in this module and the production infrastructure in Modules 6–10. Module 6 (Testing) covers how to write assertion tests for each query's output. Module 7 (Deployment) covers how to promote query changes through environments. Module 8 (Performance) covers how to index and tune the tables these queries run against. Module 9 (Data Quality) covers the anomaly detection query in production depth. And Module 10 (Operations) covers the SLA governance that ensures these queries complete on time every day, every week, and every quarter.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 4, you will shift from querying data to modeling data. Star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, temporal tables, and the trade-offs between normalization and denormalization determine how the tables that your analytical queries read are designed. The analytical techniques from this module work best when the underlying data model is intentionally designed for the queries it serves — and Module 4 teaches you to design those models.

modules.title: Data Modeling for Pipelines
modules.description: Understand star and snowflake schemas, master slowly changing dimension patterns, design temporal tables, and choose the right modeling approach for different pipeline consumers.

blocks.content: At junior level, HydroAlpes had one team, two engineers, and four tables. Lena designed the schema, Markus wrote the queries, and every question could be answered with a straightforward join across a simple normalized model. At mid-level, HydroAlpes has three teams — data engineering, analytics, and ML — totaling 12 people, all querying the same data with different needs. The analytics team wants denormalized wide tables they can query without joins. The ML team wants feature tables with historical snapshots at arbitrary timestamps. The engineering team wants normalized tables with referential integrity for pipeline correctness. These conflicting requirements cannot be served by one table design. Data modeling is the practice of designing tables for their consumers — and at mid-level scale, different consumers need different models of the same underlying data.
The Modeling Problem

HydroAlpes' billing query joins meter_readings with meters, substations, and regions. The analytics team runs this four-table join 200 times per day across various dashboards. Each execution scans the meters, substations, and regions tables — tables that rarely change — and re-joins them with readings. The ML team needs to know what customer_type each meter had at the time of each reading, not just its current type — because some meters changed from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL mid-year, and the ML model must use the type that was effective when the reading was recorded. The engineering team needs to add a new sub-region without breaking any downstream query. Each requirement implies a different table design, and the modeling decisions made at the Silver and Gold layers determine whether those requirements can be met efficiently.
Layers and Models

The Bronze-Silver-Gold architecture from the junior track is a layering strategy, not a modeling strategy. Bronze stores raw ingested data. Silver stores validated, conformed data. Gold stores consumer-ready analytical data. But within each layer, many modeling choices remain: should Silver be fully normalized (one table per entity, no redundancy) or partially denormalized (common joins pre-computed)? Should Gold be a star schema (fact table surrounded by dimension tables) or a wide denormalized table (everything in one table)? Should dimensions be current-state only or historized with slowly changing dimension patterns? This module answers these questions with concrete patterns and the trade-offs that determine which pattern fits each situation.

blocks.title: HydroAlpes modeling layers and consumer patterns
blocks.content:
Bronze (raw)          Silver (validated)        Gold (consumer-ready)
┌────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────────────┐
│ CSV/JSON   │  ──▶  │ Normalized     │  ──▶  │ Star schema (analytics)  │
│ from SCADA │       │ tables with    │       │ Wide tables (ML)         │
│ and meters │       │ constraints    │       │ Summary tables (billing) │
└────────────┘       └────────────────┘       └──────────────────────────┘
                     │                         │
                     │ 3NF, referential         │ Star schema, SCD Type 2,
                     │ integrity, one            │ denormalized wide tables,
                     │ source of truth           │ temporal snapshots
                     │                         │
                     └── Engineering team ──────── Analytics + ML + Billing
blocks.caption: Each layer uses a different modeling approach. Silver is normalized for correctness. Gold is modeled per consumer: star schema for dashboards, wide tables for ML, and summary tables for billing. The modeling decisions at each layer determine query performance and consumer satisfaction.

blocks.title: Modeling is a design decision, not a technical constraint
blocks.content: There is no single correct data model. A star schema is optimal for dashboard queries but suboptimal for ML feature engineering. A normalized model is optimal for data integrity but suboptimal for analytical query performance. The mid-level skill is choosing the right model for each consumer and layer — and implementing the pipeline that transforms between them. This module teaches the patterns; you choose which to apply based on who consumes the data and what questions they ask.

sections.title: Fact Tables and Dimension Tables — Deep Dive

blocks.content: The junior track introduced fact tables (events, measurements) and dimension tables (descriptive context). At mid-level, the distinction needs precision because the fact table's properties determine how it can be aggregated. An additive fact can be summed across any dimension: daily_kw is additive — you can sum it by region, by customer type, by date, or by any combination, and the total is meaningful. A semi-additive fact can be summed across some dimensions but not others: reservoir_level_m can be summed across reservoirs (total system water) but not across dates (summing today's level with yesterday's level is meaningless). A non-additive fact cannot be summed at all: temperature_c is neither additive nor semi-additive — the 'sum of temperatures' is not a meaningful metric. Correctly classifying facts prevents aggregation errors that produce plausible but wrong numbers.
Additive Facts

-- Additive fact: daily_kw can be summed across any dimension
-- Regional total, monthly total, customer-type total — all meaningful
SELECT mr.region,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw   -- valid: additive across region
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY mr.region;

SELECT mr.date,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS daily_total  -- valid: additive across date
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
GROUP BY mr.date
ORDER BY mr.date;

Both aggregations are valid because daily_kw is additive. The regional total represents total consumption across all meters in BERN-NORTH for January. The daily total represents total consumption across all BERN-NORTH meters on each day. You can sum daily_kw across any combination of dimensions, and the result has a clear business meaning. Most revenue, quantity, and consumption metrics are additive. At HydroAlpes, daily_kw, generation_kw (turbine output), and water_flow_m3s (when aggregated over time as volume) are all additive facts that can be summed freely.
Semi-Additive Facts

-- Semi-additive fact: reservoir_level_m can be summed across reservoirs
-- but NOT across dates
SELECT rl.date,
       SUM(rl.level_m)  AS system_water_level  -- VALID: sum across reservoirs
FROM   silver.reservoir_levels AS rl
WHERE  rl.date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY rl.date;

-- WRONG: summing levels across dates produces nonsense
-- SELECT SUM(level_m) FROM reservoir_levels WHERE reservoir_id = 'RES-01'
-- This sums Monday's level + Tuesday's level + ... = meaningless number
-- Correct: AVG(level_m) or the level on a specific date

Reservoir levels are balances — point-in-time values, not cumulative flows. Summing the levels of all 18 reservoirs on January 15 gives the total system water level on that day — a meaningful metric for grid capacity. But summing a single reservoir's levels across 31 days gives an arbitrary number with no physical meaning. The correct across-date aggregation for a semi-additive fact is AVG (average level over the period) or the value at a specific point in time (end-of-month level). Account balances, inventory quantities, and population counts are all semi-additive: meaningful when summed across entities, meaningless when summed across time. At HydroAlpes, reservoir levels and meter account balances are marked as semi-additive in the data dictionary, and the Gold-layer views expose only valid aggregations.
The Grain Decision

A fact table's grain is the level of detail each row represents — one reading per meter per day, one telemetry point per turbine per minute, one price per market zone per hour. The grain determines which questions the table can answer and which aggregations are valid. HydroAlpes' meter_readings table has a daily grain (one row per meter per day). This means you can aggregate daily consumption into weekly, monthly, or annual totals. You cannot drill down into hourly consumption because the data does not exist at that granularity. Choosing the grain is the most consequential modeling decision: too coarse and the data cannot answer fine-grained questions; too fine and the table grows unmanageably large. At HydroAlpes, the daily grain for meter_readings was chosen because the billing system operates on daily totals, and sub-daily readings (available from smart meters) are aggregated into daily sums at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary.

blocks.title: Mixing grains in one fact table is a modeling error
blocks.content: If meter_readings contains both daily readings (one row per meter per day) and monthly summaries (one row per meter per month) in the same table, a SUM(daily_kw) query that does not filter by grain type will double-count: the monthly summary includes the same consumption that the daily rows represent. Each fact table must have exactly one grain. If you need both daily and monthly data, use separate tables (silver.meter_readings_daily and gold.meter_readings_monthly) with clear naming that communicates the grain.

A star schema places a fact table at the center, surrounded by dimension tables connected by foreign keys. Each dimension table is denormalized — it contains all descriptive attributes for one business entity in a single table, without sub-tables. The meters dimension contains meter_id, customer_type, region, substation_name, installation_date, and is_active — all in one table, even though a fully normalized model would separate regions and substations into their own tables. This denormalization eliminates the multi-table joins that slow down analytical queries: instead of joining readings with meters, then meters with substations, then substations with regions, the analyst joins readings with the denormalized meters dimension in a single join.
The HydroAlpes Gold Star Schema

-- Gold-layer star schema: fact table + denormalized dimension tables

-- Dimension: meters (denormalized — includes substation and region attributes)
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_meters (
    meter_key          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,   -- surrogate key
    meter_id           VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, -- natural key
    customer_type      VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    region             VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    region_name        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, -- from regions table
    country            VARCHAR(5)  NOT NULL,  -- from regions table
    substation_id      VARCHAR(20),
    substation_name    VARCHAR(100),           -- from substations table
    substation_capacity_mw INTEGER,            -- from substations table
    installation_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    is_active          BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    effective_from     DATE NOT NULL,
    effective_to       DATE                    -- NULL = current version
);

-- Fact: daily meter readings
CREATE TABLE gold.fact_meter_readings (
    reading_key        BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    meter_key          INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES gold.dim_meters(meter_key),
    date_key           DATE NOT NULL,
    daily_kw           DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL,
    daily_mwh          DOUBLE PRECISION NOT NULL  -- pre-computed unit conversion
);

-- Dimension: date (calendar dimension for time-based analysis)
CREATE TABLE gold.dim_date (
    date_key           DATE PRIMARY KEY,
    year               INTEGER NOT NULL,
    quarter            INTEGER NOT NULL,
    month              INTEGER NOT NULL,
    week_of_year       INTEGER NOT NULL,
    day_of_week        INTEGER NOT NULL,
    is_weekend         BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    is_swiss_holiday   BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    is_austrian_holiday BOOLEAN NOT NULL,
    fiscal_quarter     INTEGER NOT NULL
);

Three tables replace the junior-level six-table Silver model. The dim_meters dimension absorbs attributes from meters, substations, and regions into a single denormalized table with a surrogate key (meter_key). The fact_meter_readings table stores one row per reading, linking to dim_meters via the surrogate key. The dim_date table is a calendar dimension that lets analysts filter and group by fiscal quarter, weekend/holiday status, and other calendar attributes without computing them in every query. The analyst's query becomes: SELECT d.region_name, SUM(f.daily_mwh) FROM fact_meter_readings f JOIN dim_meters d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key JOIN dim_date dt ON f.date_key = dt.date_key WHERE dt.fiscal_quarter = 1 GROUP BY d.region_name — one join per dimension, no multi-hop joins.
Surrogate Keys

The dim_meters table uses a surrogate key (meter_key, an auto-incrementing integer) instead of the natural key (meter_id, a business identifier). Surrogate keys solve two problems. First, they are compact: a 4-byte integer is smaller than a 20-character VARCHAR, and the fact table stores billions of rows — each byte saved per row means gigabytes saved in total. Second, they support slowly changing dimensions (lessons 5–7): when a meter's customer_type changes, a new dim_meters row is created with a new meter_key but the same meter_id. The fact table links each reading to the correct dimension version via the surrogate key. The natural key (meter_id) appears in the dimension table for human readability but is not used for joins.
The Date Dimension

A date dimension is a pre-computed calendar table that stores one row per calendar date with attributes like year, quarter, month, day of week, and holiday flags. Analysts can filter and group by these attributes without calling EXTRACT() or DATE_TRUNC() in every query. At HydroAlpes, the date dimension includes both Swiss and Austrian holiday flags (because the two countries have different public holidays) and the fiscal quarter mapping (which does not align with calendar quarters for regulatory reporting). The date dimension is populated once for a 10-year range and never changes — it is the most stable table in the entire data model.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Populate the date dimension for 10 years in advance using generate_series: INSERT INTO gold.dim_date SELECT d::DATE, EXTRACT(YEAR FROM d), EXTRACT(QUARTER FROM d), ... FROM generate_series('2020-01-01'::DATE, '2030-12-31'::DATE, '1 day') AS d. This takes seconds and ensures the dimension is never missing a date. At HydroAlpes, the date dimension was populated once during initial deployment and has not been modified since — except to update holiday flags when the Swiss government announces new public holidays.

A star schema denormalizes dimensions: the meters dimension includes substation_name, region_name, and country — attributes that belong to related entities. A snowflake schema normalizes dimensions: the meters dimension contains only meter-specific attributes and a foreign key to a substations dimension, which in turn contains a foreign key to a regions dimension. The snowflake preserves normalization (no redundancy, single source of truth for each attribute) at the cost of additional joins. The star eliminates those joins at the cost of redundancy (substation_name is repeated in every meter row that belongs to the same substation). The choice between them depends on the consumer's query patterns and the team's tolerance for redundancy.
Snowflake Schema Structure

-- Snowflake schema: normalized dimensions with sub-dimensions
-- Same data as the star schema, but split into separate tables

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_regions (
    region_key    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    region_id     VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    region_name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    country       VARCHAR(5) NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_substations (
    substation_key    SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    substation_id     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    substation_name   VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    capacity_mw       INTEGER NOT NULL,
    region_key        INTEGER REFERENCES gold.dim_regions(region_key)
);

CREATE TABLE gold.dim_meters_snowflake (
    meter_key         SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    meter_id          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    customer_type     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    installation_date DATE NOT NULL,
    is_active         BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    substation_key    INTEGER REFERENCES gold.dim_substations(substation_key)
);

The snowflake schema separates regions, substations, and meters into distinct tables. To get a meter's region name, you join meters → substations → regions — two hops instead of zero in the star schema. The normalized structure means that if Zurich's region_name changes from 'Zurich Canton' to 'Canton of Zurich,' you update one row in dim_regions. In the star schema, you would update every meter row in BERN-NORTH — potentially hundreds of thousands of rows. The normalization advantage is real for attributes that change; the join cost is real for every query that needs those attributes.
Star vs Snowflake — The Decision

rows.left: Fewer joins: fact → dimension in one hop
rows.right: More joins: fact → dimension → sub-dimension

rows.left: Redundant attributes: substation_name repeated per meter
rows.right: No redundancy: each attribute stored once

rows.left: Faster queries: fewer joins = less CPU, memory, and I/O
rows.right: Slower queries: additional joins add latency

rows.left: Harder updates: attribute changes require updating many rows
rows.right: Easier updates: attribute changes update one row

rows.left: Simpler SQL for analysts: no multi-hop joins needed
rows.right: More complex SQL: analysts must know the join path

rows.left: Best for: read-heavy analytical workloads, dashboards
rows.right: Best for: write-heavy workloads, frequently changing attributes

blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the Gold layer uses a star schema because the primary consumers are analysts running read-heavy dashboard queries, and the dimension attributes change infrequently (substations are renamed once a decade, regions never). The Silver layer uses a snowflake-like normalized model because it is the engineering team's source of truth, and normalization prevents the update anomalies that could propagate incorrect data into Gold. This dual-model approach — normalized Silver for correctness, denormalized Gold for performance — is the standard architecture at HydroAlpes and the dominant pattern in modern data warehousing.

blocks.title: The normalization spectrum is a trade-off, not a judgment
blocks.content: Neither full normalization nor full denormalization is universally correct. Normalization optimizes for write correctness and storage efficiency. Denormalization optimizes for read performance and query simplicity. Production data platforms use both: normalized models in the transformation layer (Silver) and denormalized models in the consumption layer (Gold). The decision is driven by the consumer: if the consumer is a pipeline that writes frequently, normalize. If the consumer is a dashboard that reads frequently, denormalize.

Dimension attributes change over time. A meter's customer_type changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL when a building is converted to office space. A substation's name changes when the operator rebrands. A region's regulatory classification changes when a new energy law takes effect. These changes create a fundamental problem for the dimension table: should the old value be preserved (maintaining history) or overwritten (keeping only the current state)? The answer depends on whether historical queries need the old value. Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD) patterns classify these changes into types based on how the dimension table handles them. Type 1 — the simplest — overwrites the old value, losing history.
Type 1: Overwrite

-- Type 1 SCD: overwrite the old value with the new one
-- The substation's name changed from 'Alpine Grid B-4' to 'Alpine Hub B-4'
-- We simply UPDATE the dimension row — no history preserved

UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    substation_name = 'Alpine Hub B-4'
WHERE  substation_name = 'Alpine Grid B-4';

The UPDATE replaces 'Alpine Grid B-4' with 'Alpine Hub B-4' in every meter row that references the substation. After the update, every query — historical or current — returns the new name. If someone runs the January billing report six months later, it shows 'Alpine Hub B-4' even though the name was 'Alpine Grid B-4' in January. The history is lost. Type 1 is appropriate when the change is a correction (the old value was wrong and should not appear in any report) or when the attribute is cosmetic and history has no business value (name changes, formatting corrections, spelling fixes).
When Type 1 Is Appropriate

Type 1 is the right choice when historical accuracy of the changed attribute does not matter for any business process. At HydroAlpes, substation names are cosmetic labels — the billing system uses substation_id, not the name. Changing the name does not affect any calculation, any regulatory report, or any analytical metric. The name is for human readability only. Similarly, correcting a misspelled region_name from 'Inersbruck' to 'Innsbruck' is a Type 1 change — the old spelling was wrong and should be retroactively corrected everywhere. The decision criterion: would a historical report showing the old value mislead anyone? If yes, use Type 1 to fix it. If the old value was correct at the time, preserve it with Type 2 (next lesson).
Type 1 in the Pipeline

-- Pipeline pattern: Type 1 merge from staging to Gold dimension
-- Uses INSERT ON CONFLICT for upsert behavior
INSERT INTO gold.dim_meters (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name,
                             country, substation_id, substation_name,
                             substation_capacity_mw, installation_date, is_active)
SELECT s.meter_id, s.customer_type, s.region, s.region_name,
       s.country, s.substation_id, s.substation_name,
       s.substation_capacity_mw, s.installation_date, s.is_active
FROM   staging.meters_latest AS s
ON CONFLICT (meter_id) DO UPDATE
SET    substation_name         = EXCLUDED.substation_name,
       substation_capacity_mw  = EXCLUDED.substation_capacity_mw,
       region_name             = EXCLUDED.region_name,
       is_active               = EXCLUDED.is_active;

The INSERT ON CONFLICT pattern implements Type 1 as an idempotent pipeline operation. New meters are inserted. Existing meters (matched on meter_id) have their Type 1 attributes overwritten with the latest values from staging. Attributes not listed in the DO UPDATE SET clause (like customer_type and installation_date) are not modified — they are either immutable or handled by a different SCD type. At HydroAlpes, this pipeline runs daily at 05:15, after the staging table is populated from the meter management system. The entire dimension refresh takes approximately 4 seconds for 1.2 million meters.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Not all attributes in a dimension table need the same SCD type. A meter's substation_name might be Type 1 (overwrite — cosmetic) while its customer_type is Type 2 (historize — billing impact). Design the merge pipeline to apply the correct type per attribute: Type 1 attributes go in the ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE clause, and Type 2 attributes trigger a new row insertion (next lesson). Mixing SCD types in one table is the norm, not the exception.

In March 2025, meter CH-BERN-00142 was reclassified from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL because the building was converted to office space. The billing team needs to bill January and February readings at the residential rate (the rate effective when the readings were recorded) and March readings at the commercial rate. If the dimension uses Type 1 (overwrite), the customer_type is now COMMERCIAL everywhere — and January's billing retroactively applies the commercial rate, overcharging the customer. Type 2 SCD solves this by inserting a new dimension row with the new value and an effective date range, while keeping the old row with the old value. Each fact row links to the dimension version that was effective when the fact was recorded.
Type 2: Insert New Row

-- Type 2 SCD: two rows for the same meter, each with an effective period
-- The OLD row represents RESIDENTIAL (Jan 1 to Feb 28)
-- The NEW row represents COMMERCIAL (Mar 1 to NULL = current)

SELECT meter_key, meter_id, customer_type, effective_from, effective_to
FROM   gold.dim_meters
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
ORDER BY effective_from;

Two rows, two surrogate keys, one natural key. The first row (meter_key 14201) represents the meter as RESIDENTIAL from its installation date through February 28, 2025. The second row (meter_key 1214202) represents the same meter as COMMERCIAL from March 1, 2025 onward (effective_to = NULL means 'current'). The fact table links each reading to the correct dimension version: January readings link to meter_key 14201 (RESIDENTIAL), and March readings link to meter_key 1214202 (COMMERCIAL). The billing query automatically applies the correct rate because the join uses the surrogate key, which already encodes the temporal relationship.
The Point-in-Time Join

-- Join each reading with the dimension version effective at the reading date
-- This is the standard Type 2 query pattern
SELECT f.date_key,
       f.daily_kw,
       d.meter_id,
       d.customer_type   -- RESIDENTIAL for Jan/Feb, COMMERCIAL for Mar
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d
    ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  d.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
ORDER BY f.date_key;

If the fact table is loaded correctly — each reading linked to the dimension version effective at the reading date — the join automatically returns the correct customer_type per reading. January readings return RESIDENTIAL. March readings return COMMERCIAL. No date-range logic is needed in the query because the surrogate key encodes the temporal relationship. This is the power of Type 2 SCD: the complexity of temporal versioning is handled once (during fact loading) and the queries remain simple joins on the surrogate key. The alternative — storing the natural key in the fact table and joining with a date-range condition — is more complex, slower (range joins are less efficient than equality joins), and error-prone.
Implementing the Type 2 Pipeline

-- Type 2 pipeline: detect changes and insert new versions
-- Step 1: Close the old version (set effective_to)
UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    effective_to = '2025-02-28'
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  effective_to IS NULL;

-- Step 2: Insert the new version
INSERT INTO gold.dim_meters
    (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name, country,
     substation_id, substation_name, substation_capacity_mw,
     installation_date, is_active, effective_from, effective_to)
VALUES
    ('CH-BERN-00142', 'COMMERCIAL', 'BERN-NORTH', 'Bern North',
     'CH', 'SUB-BERN-04', 'Alpine Hub B-4', 120,
     '2018-11-02', TRUE, '2025-03-01', NULL);

The pipeline detects that CH-BERN-00142's customer_type changed from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL. It closes the old version by setting effective_to to the day before the change, then inserts a new version with effective_from set to the change date and effective_to set to NULL (current). In production, this logic is automated: the pipeline compares the staging table against the current dimension, identifies changed attributes, and processes each change through the close-old/insert-new pattern. At HydroAlpes, the Type 2 pipeline runs within the same transaction as the Type 1 updates, ensuring that the dimension is always in a consistent state — either fully updated or fully rolled back.

blocks.title: Type 2 dimensions grow over time
blocks.content: Every attribute change creates a new row. If 10% of meters change customer_type each year, the dim_meters table grows by 120,000 rows per year — a manageable increase. But if a rapidly changing attribute (like is_active, which toggles frequently) is tracked as Type 2, the dimension can grow dramatically. At HydroAlpes, only customer_type and region are Type 2 (business-critical, rarely changing). Attributes like is_active are Type 1 (overwrite) because they change frequently and historical status is tracked in a separate operational table, not in the dimension.

sections.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions — Type 3 and Hybrids

blocks.content: Type 1 preserves no history. Type 2 preserves complete history. Type 3 preserves limited history — specifically, the current value and the previous value — by adding a column for the prior value rather than inserting a new row. When meter CH-BERN-00142 changes from RESIDENTIAL to COMMERCIAL, a Type 3 implementation sets customer_type = 'COMMERCIAL' and previous_customer_type = 'RESIDENTIAL' in the same row. The dimension table does not grow (no new rows), but it retains enough history to answer 'what was the previous value?' without supporting arbitrary point-in-time queries.
Type 3: Add Column for Previous Value

-- Type 3 SCD: current and previous values in the same row
ALTER TABLE gold.dim_meters
  ADD COLUMN previous_customer_type VARCHAR(20),
  ADD COLUMN customer_type_changed_date DATE;

-- When a change occurs: shift current to previous, update current
UPDATE gold.dim_meters
SET    previous_customer_type     = customer_type,
       customer_type              = 'COMMERCIAL',
       customer_type_changed_date = '2025-03-01'
WHERE  meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142';

meter_id       | customer_type | previous_customer_type | changed_date
---------------+---------------+------------------------+-------------
CH-BERN-00142  | COMMERCIAL    | RESIDENTIAL            | 2025-03-01

One row, no new surrogate keys, no effective date ranges. The current type is COMMERCIAL, the previous type is RESIDENTIAL, and the change date records when the transition happened. This is simpler than Type 2 — no row versioning, no point-in-time joins — but it supports only one level of history. If the meter changes again (COMMERCIAL → INDUSTRIAL), the previous_customer_type becomes COMMERCIAL and the original RESIDENTIAL is lost. Type 3 is appropriate when the business needs to know only the immediately prior value, not the full history.
Hybrid Approaches

Production dimension tables rarely use a single SCD type for all attributes. HydroAlpes' dim_meters uses a hybrid approach: customer_type is Type 2 (full history required for billing accuracy), substation_name is Type 1 (cosmetic, no history needed), and region is Type 3 (the previous region is useful for migration analysis, but the full history of region changes is not). The pipeline processes each attribute according to its assigned type: Type 1 attributes are overwritten, Type 2 attributes trigger close-old/insert-new, and Type 3 attributes shift current to previous. This per-attribute typing is the standard in production data warehousing and is documented in HydroAlpes' data dictionary alongside each column definition.
SCD Type Selection Guide

rows: Type 1
Overwrite with new value
None — old value is lost
Corrections, cosmetic changes, attributes with no historical relevance
Type 2
Insert new row, close old row
Complete — every version preserved with effective dates
Attributes where historical accuracy affects business logic (billing, regulation)
Type 3
Add column for previous value
Limited — only current and one prior value
Attributes where only the most recent change matters (migration tracking)
Hybrid
Different types per attribute in the same table
Varies by attribute
Production reality — different attributes have different history requirements

blocks.title: Type 2 is the default for anything that affects money or regulation
blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the rule is simple: if an attribute's historical value affects a financial calculation (billing, pricing, revenue allocation) or a regulatory report (filed quarterly with the Swiss and Austrian energy regulators), it must be Type 2. Everything else is Type 1 unless a specific business case for limited history (Type 3) exists. This conservative default prevents the scenario where a retroactive analysis produces wrong results because historical dimension values were overwritten.

The Swiss energy regulator can request that HydroAlpes reproduce any billing report exactly as it would have appeared on the date it was originally generated. If the January billing report was generated on February 3 and the regulator asks for a copy on August 15, HydroAlpes must reproduce the report using the data and dimension values that existed on February 3 — not the current values. This means every dimension change since February 3 must be invisible, and every fact that arrived after February 3 must be excluded. SCD Type 2 handles the dimension side (effective dates), but the fact side also needs temporal tracking: when was each reading loaded into the warehouse? This is the bi-temporal modeling challenge: tracking both the valid time (when the reading was measured) and the transaction time (when the reading was loaded).
Valid Time vs Transaction Time

rows: Valid time
When the fact was true in the real world
The meter reading was measured on January 15
date (the reading date)
Transaction time
When the fact was recorded in the database
The reading was loaded into Silver on January 16 at 05:30
loaded_at (the ingestion timestamp)

blocks.content: Most analytical queries use valid time: 'total consumption for January 2025' filters on the reading date. Temporal queries use both: 'reproduce the January billing report as it would have appeared on February 3' filters readings to valid_time in January AND transaction_time before February 3. This excludes late-arriving readings that were loaded after February 3 — readings that were not available when the original report was generated. Bi-temporal modeling adds the loaded_at column to fact tables and the effective_from/effective_to columns to dimension tables, enabling arbitrary point-in-time reconstruction.
The Point-in-Time Query

-- Reproduce the January billing report as it would have appeared on Feb 3
-- Filter facts by valid time (reading date) AND transaction time (loaded_at)
-- Filter dimensions by effective date range containing the report date

SELECT d.region_name,
       d.customer_type,
       SUM(f.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d
    ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  f.date_key >= '2025-01-01'       -- valid time: January readings
  AND  f.date_key <  '2025-02-01'
  AND  f.loaded_at <= '2025-02-03'       -- transaction time: loaded by Feb 3
  AND  d.effective_from <= '2025-02-03'  -- dimension version effective on Feb 3
  AND  (d.effective_to IS NULL OR d.effective_to > '2025-02-03')
GROUP BY d.region_name, d.customer_type
ORDER BY total_kw DESC;

The query applies three temporal filters. The valid time filter (date_key in January) selects the correct readings. The transaction time filter (loaded_at <= Feb 3) excludes readings that arrived after the report date. The dimension effective date filter selects the dimension version that was current on February 3 — before the March customer_type change. The result is the exact billing report that would have been generated on February 3, regardless of when the query is actually run. This temporal reconstruction capability is a regulatory requirement for HydroAlpes and is increasingly common in financial services, healthcare, and any domain where audit trail reproduction is mandated.
System-Versioned Temporal Tables (SQL:2011)

The SQL:2011 standard introduced system-versioned temporal tables: the database automatically maintains transaction-time history for every row. When a row is updated, the database keeps the old version with a system_time range column that records when the version was valid. Queries with FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF timestamp automatically return the version that was current at that timestamp. PostgreSQL does not yet natively support SQL:2011 system versioning, but it can be emulated using triggers or extensions (temporal_tables). At HydroAlpes, temporal behavior is implemented manually using loaded_at and effective_from/effective_to columns, which is more portable across databases and gives the engineering team explicit control over the temporal logic.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Add loaded_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP to every fact table, even if you do not currently need temporal queries. The column costs negligible storage (8 bytes per row), is populated automatically, and enables point-in-time reconstruction if a regulatory or audit requirement emerges later. At HydroAlpes, loaded_at is a mandatory column in every Silver and Gold fact table — a decision that paid off when the Swiss regulator requested report reproduction two years after the column was added.

The star schema in lesson 3 denormalized the dimension tables — collapsing substations and regions into the meters dimension. This lesson covers a more aggressive form of denormalization: collapsing dimension attributes directly into the fact table, eliminating the dimension join entirely. Instead of joining fact_meter_readings with dim_meters to get customer_type, the customer_type is stored directly in the fact table. The join is pre-computed at load time, and the analyst queries a single table. This is the wide table pattern — a fully denormalized fact table that contains all dimension attributes — and it is the format that the ML team at HydroAlpes needs for feature engineering.
The Wide Table

-- Wide table: all dimension attributes pre-joined into the fact table
-- No joins needed for any analytical or ML query
CREATE TABLE gold.readings_wide AS
SELECT f.date_key,
       f.daily_kw,
       f.daily_mwh,
       d.meter_id,
       d.customer_type,
       d.region,
       d.region_name,
       d.country,
       d.substation_id,
       d.substation_name,
       d.substation_capacity_mw,
       d.installation_date,
       dt.year,
       dt.quarter,
       dt.month,
       dt.is_weekend,
       dt.is_swiss_holiday
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_date AS dt ON f.date_key = dt.date_key;

The wide table contains every column from the fact and all dimensions in a single table. The ML team can query it directly without any joins: SELECT daily_kw, customer_type, region, month, is_weekend FROM gold.readings_wide WHERE region = 'BERN-NORTH' — ready for feature engineering. The trade-off is storage: the wide table repeats region_name, country, substation_name, and other dimension attributes for every reading row. For 37.2 million January readings, the substation_name 'Alpine Hub B-4' is stored 437,000 times (once per reading at that substation). The storage overhead is substantial but often acceptable for Gold-layer tables that serve specific consumers.
The Maintenance Cost

Denormalized tables must be rebuilt when dimension attributes change. If a substation is renamed (Type 1 change), the wide table must be rebuilt or updated to reflect the new name in every row that references that substation. If a meter's customer_type changes (Type 2), the wide table must be rebuilt to apply the correct type version to each reading. This rebuild cost is the primary disadvantage of the wide table: the more frequently dimensions change, the more frequently the wide table must be rebuilt. At HydroAlpes, the wide table is rebuilt nightly as part of the Gold refresh pipeline — a 15-minute CTAS operation that produces a fresh snapshot incorporating all dimension changes from the previous day.
When to Denormalize

Denormalize into a wide table when the consumer needs join-free access (ML feature engineering, export to external systems), when the dimension attributes change infrequently relative to the rebuild frequency, and when the storage overhead is acceptable. Do not denormalize when the dimension changes frequently and the rebuild cost exceeds the join savings, when multiple consumers need different subsets of attributes (each requiring its own wide table), or when storage constraints prohibit the redundancy. At HydroAlpes, the ML team's wide table is rebuilt nightly (acceptable rebuild cost, infrequent dimension changes). The real-time dashboard does not use a wide table because it needs sub-second query latency on current data, which the star schema provides via indexed dimension joins.

blocks.title: Different consumers, different models
blocks.content: HydroAlpes maintains three models of the same data in the Gold layer: a star schema for the analytics team's dashboards (fast joins, indexed dimensions), a wide table for the ML team's feature engineering (join-free, self-contained), and summary tables for the billing team's invoices (pre-aggregated by region and customer type). Each model optimizes for its consumer's access pattern. The pipeline that produces each model reads from the same Silver source and runs in the same nightly refresh window. The modeling cost is three Gold pipelines instead of one — but the consumer satisfaction is dramatically higher.

This module covered the data modeling patterns that determine how HydroAlpes' pipeline tables are designed: star schemas for analytical query performance, snowflake schemas for normalized correctness, SCD Types 1, 2, and 3 for handling dimension attribute changes, temporal tables for point-in-time reconstruction, and denormalized wide tables for ML and export consumers. This final lesson traces HydroAlpes' model evolution from the junior-level normalized Silver schema to the mid-level production architecture — showing how each modeling decision was driven by a specific consumer need or regulatory requirement.
The Evolution Timeline

rows: Junior (Month 1–6)
Normalized Silver schema with 6 tables, no Gold layer
Single team, simple queries, correctness over performance
Third normal form (3NF)
Analytics expansion (Month 7)
Gold star schema added with dim_meters, dim_date, fact_meter_readings
Analytics team joined — needed fast dashboard queries without multi-hop joins
Star schema with denormalized dimensions
Billing audit (Month 9)
SCD Type 2 added for customer_type and region in dim_meters
Regulator required historical billing accuracy — retroactive corrections unacceptable
Type 2 SCD with effective_from/effective_to
ML team joins (Month 11)
Wide table added to Gold: readings_wide with all dimension attributes pre-joined
ML team needed join-free feature engineering — star schema required too many joins
Full denormalization (wide table)
Regulatory audit (Month 14)
loaded_at column added to all fact tables; temporal query patterns established
Swiss regulator requested report reproduction capability — needed transaction time tracking
Bi-temporal modeling
Current state
Silver: normalized 3NF. Gold: star schema + wide table + summary tables
Three teams with distinct requirements served by purpose-built models
Hybrid multi-model architecture

blocks.content: The Decision Matrix for New Tables

When a new table is needed at HydroAlpes, the engineering team answers five questions to determine its modeling approach. Who is the primary consumer (engineering, analytics, ML, billing, regulatory)? How frequently do dimension attributes change (daily, monthly, rarely)? Does any business process require historical attribute values (billing at historical rates, regulatory reproduction)? How large is the table (millions of rows, billions of rows)? And how many dimensions does the primary query join? The answers map directly to modeling choices: engineering consumers get normalized tables, analytics consumers get star schemas, ML consumers get wide tables, and any attribute that affects money or regulation gets Type 2 SCD.

The model evolution also reveals a meta-principle: data models are not designed once and frozen. They evolve as teams grow, consumers change, and regulatory requirements emerge. The Silver layer — the engineering team's normalized source of truth — has been stable since month 3. The Gold layer has been restructured three times in 14 months as new consumers joined. This asymmetry is by design: the transformation layer (Silver) should be stable, and the consumption layer (Gold) should adapt to its consumers. The pipeline that transforms Silver to Gold is the bridge between stability and adaptability.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 5, you will learn the SQL patterns that implement these data models in production pipelines: MERGE for upsert operations that handle SCD Type 1 and Type 2 in a single statement, set operations (UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT) for data reconciliation across model versions, recursive CTEs for production hierarchy traversal, and advanced NULL handling for the edge cases that dimension processing creates. The modeling patterns from this module define what the pipeline should produce; Module 5 provides the SQL tools to produce it.

modules.title: Advanced SQL Patterns
modules.description: Master MERGE for upsert operations, set operations for combining result sets, production-depth recursive CTEs, and advanced NULL handling and conditional logic.

blocks.content: HydroAlpes' Silver pipeline receives meter readings daily. Most readings are new rows (readings for today's date), but some are late arrivals — readings for previous dates that were delayed by network issues or meter firmware problems. The pipeline must insert new readings and update existing readings (if a corrected value arrives for a meter-date combination that already exists). At junior level, this required two statements: an INSERT for new rows and an UPDATE for existing rows, wrapped in a transaction. The MERGE statement (SQL:2003 standard) combines both operations into a single atomic statement: if the row exists, update it; if it does not, insert it. This upsert pattern is the foundation of idempotent pipeline design.
The MERGE Syntax (SQL Standard)

-- MERGE: insert new readings, update existing readings with corrected values
-- PostgreSQL 15+ supports the SQL standard MERGE statement
MERGE INTO silver.meter_readings AS target
USING staging.daily_readings AS source
  ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id
 AND target.date     = source.date
WHEN MATCHED AND source.daily_kw <> target.daily_kw THEN
  UPDATE SET daily_kw    = source.daily_kw,
             loaded_at   = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  INSERT (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
  VALUES (source.meter_id, source.daily_kw, source.date,
          source.region, source.customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);

The MERGE statement has three parts. USING identifies the source data (the staging table with today's delivery). ON defines the matching condition (same meter and date). WHEN MATCHED defines what to do when a source row matches an existing target row — in this case, update the daily_kw if it changed and refresh the loaded_at timestamp. WHEN NOT MATCHED defines what to do when a source row has no match in the target — insert a new row. The additional condition AND source.daily_kw <> target.daily_kw prevents unnecessary updates when the value has not changed, which reduces write amplification and avoids triggering downstream change-detection pipelines on unchanged rows.
MERGE for SCD Type 2 Processing

-- MERGE for SCD Type 2: close old versions and insert new versions
-- Handles Type 1 (overwrite) and Type 2 (new version) in one statement
MERGE INTO gold.dim_meters AS target
USING staging.meters_latest AS source
  ON target.meter_id = source.meter_id
 AND target.effective_to IS NULL  -- match only the current version
WHEN MATCHED AND target.customer_type <> source.customer_type THEN
  -- Type 2 attribute changed: close the current version
  UPDATE SET effective_to = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
WHEN MATCHED AND target.substation_name <> source.substation_name THEN
  -- Type 1 attribute changed: overwrite in place
  UPDATE SET substation_name = source.substation_name
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
  -- New meter: insert first version
  INSERT (meter_id, customer_type, region, region_name, country,
          substation_id, substation_name, substation_capacity_mw,
          installation_date, is_active, effective_from, effective_to)
  VALUES (source.meter_id, source.customer_type, source.region,
          source.region_name, source.country, source.substation_id,
          source.substation_name, source.substation_capacity_mw,
          source.installation_date, source.is_active,
          CURRENT_DATE, NULL);
-- Note: the new Type 2 version must be inserted in a separate step
-- because MERGE cannot both UPDATE and INSERT for the same source row

This MERGE handles three cases in one statement: new meters are inserted, Type 1 changes (substation_name) are overwritten, and Type 2 changes (customer_type) close the current version. The new Type 2 version — a new row with the updated customer_type and effective_from = CURRENT_DATE — must be inserted separately because SQL MERGE processes each source row against at most one WHEN clause. At HydroAlpes, the SCD pipeline runs the MERGE first (closing old versions and handling Type 1 changes), then runs a follow-up INSERT for new Type 2 versions detected by comparing source against target where effective_to was just set. This two-step approach is the standard pattern for MERGE-based SCD processing.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: MERGE is idempotent when the ON condition uses a natural key: running the same staging data through the MERGE twice produces the same result because the second run matches existing rows and either updates them to the same values or skips them (if the AND condition prevents unnecessary updates). This idempotency is critical for pipeline reliability — if the pipeline fails after MERGE and reruns, no duplicate rows are created. At HydroAlpes, every Silver-layer load uses MERGE instead of INSERT to guarantee idempotent reprocessing.

Before PostgreSQL 15 added the SQL standard MERGE, PostgreSQL provided its own upsert mechanism: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT. Unlike MERGE (which requires a USING source table), ON CONFLICT works with any INSERT statement — including INSERT ... VALUES and INSERT ... SELECT. The ON CONFLICT clause specifies what to do when the inserted row violates a unique constraint: DO UPDATE overwrites the conflicting row, and DO NOTHING silently skips it. ON CONFLICT is more concise than MERGE for simple upserts and remains the preferred pattern at HydroAlpes for single-table idempotent inserts where MERGE's multi-case logic is unnecessary.
DO UPDATE — The Upsert

-- Upsert: insert new readings or update existing ones
-- ON CONFLICT targets the (meter_id, date) primary key
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
FROM   staging.daily_readings
ON CONFLICT (meter_id, date) DO UPDATE
SET    daily_kw  = EXCLUDED.daily_kw,
       loaded_at = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
WHERE  silver.meter_readings.daily_kw <> EXCLUDED.daily_kw;

The EXCLUDED pseudo-table references the row that would have been inserted — the source data. When a conflict occurs on the (meter_id, date) primary key, the DO UPDATE SET clause overwrites daily_kw with the new value from EXCLUDED and refreshes loaded_at. The WHERE clause prevents unnecessary updates when the value has not changed — identical to the MERGE pattern from lesson 1. The ON CONFLICT target can be any unique constraint or unique index, not just the primary key. At HydroAlpes, ON CONFLICT on the composite primary key is the standard pattern for idempotent Silver-layer inserts.
DO NOTHING — Idempotent Insert

-- Idempotent insert: insert only if the row does not already exist
-- Silently skip rows that conflict with the primary key
INSERT INTO silver.meter_readings
    (meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, loaded_at)
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
FROM   staging.daily_readings
ON CONFLICT (meter_id, date) DO NOTHING;

DO NOTHING skips any row that conflicts with an existing row — no update, no error, no side effects. This pattern is appropriate when the first arrival is authoritative and corrections are not expected. At HydroAlpes, Bronze-to-Silver loading uses DO NOTHING because the Bronze data is append-only and the first version of each reading is considered correct. If a corrected reading arrives later, the correction pipeline uses DO UPDATE to overwrite the value. The separation between initial load (DO NOTHING) and correction processing (DO UPDATE) makes the pipeline intent explicit and debuggable.
ON CONFLICT vs MERGE

rows.left: Works with VALUES and SELECT — any INSERT
rows.right: Requires a USING source table or subquery

rows.left: Single-case logic: one action per conflict
rows.right: Multi-case logic: different actions for MATCHED vs NOT MATCHED

rows.left: Conflict target is a constraint or index
rows.right: Match condition is an arbitrary ON clause

rows.left: PostgreSQL-specific syntax (also supported in SQLite)
rows.right: SQL standard (PostgreSQL 15+, Spark SQL 3.x, Snowflake, BigQuery)

rows.left: More concise for simple upserts
rows.right: More flexible for SCD processing and conditional logic

blocks.content: At HydroAlpes, the team uses ON CONFLICT for straightforward upserts (Silver-layer fact loading, Bronze-to-Silver deduplication) and MERGE for complex SCD processing (Gold-layer dimension updates with Type 1 and Type 2 handling). The choice is driven by the complexity of the conditional logic: if the upsert is 'insert or update one set of columns,' ON CONFLICT is cleaner and more concise. If the upsert involves multiple conditions with different actions per condition, MERGE is more expressive and self-documenting. Both patterns produce idempotent pipelines, which is the primary requirement for production reliability.

blocks.title: ON CONFLICT requires a unique constraint
blocks.content: ON CONFLICT targets a specific unique constraint or index. If the target constraint does not exist, the ON CONFLICT clause has no effect — duplicates are inserted silently. At HydroAlpes, every table that uses ON CONFLICT has an explicit PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraint on the conflict columns. The pipeline's CI tests (Module 6) verify that the constraint exists before the pipeline runs, preventing the scenario where a dropped constraint silently converts an upsert into a duplicate-creating insert.

Set operations combine the results of two or more SELECT queries into a single result set. UNION combines all rows from both queries, removing duplicates. UNION ALL combines all rows including duplicates. INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in both queries. EXCEPT returns rows that appear in the first query but not in the second. These operations are the SQL equivalent of set algebra — union, intersection, and difference — and they solve a specific category of problems in data engineering: combining results from multiple sources, reconciling data across pipeline layers, and detecting discrepancies between datasets.
UNION ALL for Combining Sources

-- Combine readings from Swiss and Austrian source systems
-- Both systems deliver in the same schema but arrive via different feeds
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, 'CH' AS source_country
FROM   staging.swiss_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
UNION ALL
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, 'AT' AS source_country
FROM   staging.austrian_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

UNION ALL is the correct choice for combining feeds from different sources because each source may legitimately contain the same meter_id (if a meter is registered in both systems) — UNION would incorrectly deduplicate those rows. UNION ALL preserves all rows from both queries without deduplication, making it the safe default for combining data sources. At HydroAlpes, the Bronze ingestion layer uses UNION ALL to combine the Swiss and Austrian feeds into a single staging table before Silver-layer validation identifies and resolves any cross-system duplicates.
EXCEPT for Reconciliation

-- Find readings that exist in Silver but not in Gold (dropped during transformation)
SELECT meter_id, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
EXCEPT
SELECT d.meter_id, f.date_key
FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
WHERE  f.date_key = '2025-01-15';

-- Result: rows in Silver but not in Gold — readings lost during transformation

EXCEPT returns rows from the first query that do not appear in the second query — the set difference. This is the most powerful reconciliation tool in SQL: it directly answers 'what is in Silver but not in Gold?' If the result is empty, every Silver reading has a corresponding Gold fact. If rows appear, those readings were lost during the Silver-to-Gold transformation — typically because a dimension join excluded them (unregistered meters with no dimension row). At HydroAlpes, an EXCEPT-based reconciliation runs after every Gold refresh. A non-empty result triggers an alert and prevents the Gold data from being served to dashboards until the discrepancy is resolved.
INTERSECT for Common Elements

INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in both queries — the overlap between two datasets. At HydroAlpes, INTERSECT identifies meters that exist in both the Swiss and Austrian registration systems (cross-border meters near the national boundary). These meters require special handling in the billing pipeline because they are subject to two countries' energy regulations. INTERSECT is used less frequently than UNION ALL and EXCEPT in data engineering, but it is the cleanest way to express 'find the common elements' without a JOIN or an IN subquery.
Performance: UNION vs UNION ALL

UNION (without ALL) deduplicates the combined result by sorting or hashing the rows and removing duplicates — an operation that costs O(N log N) for sorting or O(N) for hashing, where N is the total row count. UNION ALL does no deduplication — it simply concatenates the results in O(N) time with no additional memory. For large datasets, the difference is significant: UNION on 37.2 million rows requires a sort or hash pass over all 37.2 million rows; UNION ALL streams them directly. At HydroAlpes, the rule is: use UNION ALL unless you specifically need deduplication, and if you need deduplication, verify that the duplicate detection is correct (same row in both queries, not just same meter_id with different daily_kw values).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: EXCEPT is the simplest and most readable reconciliation tool. Use it to compare pipeline layers after every transformation: SELECT key_columns FROM source EXCEPT SELECT key_columns FROM target. If the result is empty, the transformation preserved all source rows. If rows appear, those rows were lost. At HydroAlpes, every pipeline step has an EXCEPT-based reconciliation check that runs as an automated assertion (Module 6). This catches data loss within minutes of the pipeline run, not days later when a human notices missing data.

The junior track introduced recursive CTEs as a tool for traversing hierarchies with a simple three-level example (region → substation → meter). At mid-level, recursive CTEs become a production tool for solving real graph-traversal problems: tracing the path from a failed meter through the grid to the affected substation and reservoir, computing the transitive closure of dependencies between pipeline tables, and generating date sequences for gap-filling. This lesson covers the production patterns, the performance safeguards, and the cycle-detection techniques that make recursive CTEs safe for production use.
Grid Topology Traversal

-- Traverse the grid topology from a failed substation upstream to the reservoir
-- The grid_connections table: from_node, to_node, connection_type

WITH RECURSIVE upstream_path AS (
  -- Anchor: start at the failed substation
  SELECT node_id, parent_node_id, node_type, 1 AS depth,
         ARRAY[node_id] AS path
  FROM   silver.grid_topology
  WHERE  node_id = 'SUB-BERN-04'

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive: walk upstream to the parent node
  SELECT gt.node_id, gt.parent_node_id, gt.node_type,
         up.depth + 1,
         up.path || gt.node_id
  FROM   upstream_path AS up
    INNER JOIN silver.grid_topology AS gt
      ON up.parent_node_id = gt.node_id
  WHERE  up.depth < 20                     -- safety: max depth
    AND  gt.node_id <> ALL(up.path)        -- safety: cycle detection
)
SELECT depth, node_id, node_type, path
FROM   upstream_path
ORDER BY depth;

The recursive CTE traces the grid from substation SUB-BERN-04 upstream through the grid section, the dam, and finally to the reservoir.
