The path column (a PostgreSQL ARRAY) accumulates the node IDs at each step, providing the complete traversal path. Two safety mechanisms prevent runaway recursion: WHERE depth < 20 limits the maximum traversal depth, and gt.node_id <> ALL(up.path) detects cycles by checking whether the next node already appears in the current path. Without these safeguards, a cycle in the grid topology (node A references node B, which references node A) would cause infinite recursion.
Date Sequence Generation

-- Generate a date sequence without generate_series (portable across databases)
-- Useful in Spark SQL and other engines that lack generate_series
WITH RECURSIVE date_spine AS (
  SELECT DATE '2025-01-01' AS date
  UNION ALL
  SELECT date + INTERVAL '1 day'
  FROM   date_spine
  WHERE  date < DATE '2025-01-31'
)
SELECT date FROM date_spine ORDER BY date;

This recursive CTE generates every date in January 2025 by starting with January 1 and adding one day per recursion step. The termination condition (date < January 31) ensures exactly 31 iterations. This pattern is the portable alternative to PostgreSQL's generate_series function — it works in every database that supports recursive CTEs, including Spark SQL, BigQuery, and SQL Server. At HydroAlpes, the date spine is used for gap detection (CROSS JOIN the spine with regions, then LEFT JOIN with actual readings to find missing date-region combinations) in Spark SQL pipelines where generate_series is not available.
Performance and Safety

Recursive CTEs can be expensive because each recursion step produces a new set of rows that feeds the next step. A hierarchy with 1,000 nodes and an average branching factor of 10 produces 10,000 rows at depth 2, 100,000 at depth 3, and 1 million at depth 4. Without a depth limit, the recursion can produce billions of rows and exhaust memory. Production-safe recursive CTEs always include a depth limit (WHERE depth < N) and, for graph traversal, a cycle-detection mechanism (tracking visited nodes in an array or a WITH clause). PostgreSQL 14+ also supports the CYCLE clause as a built-in cycle detection mechanism: WITH RECURSIVE ... CYCLE node_id SET is_cycle USING path — which is cleaner than manual array-based detection.

blocks.title: Always include a depth limit
blocks.content: A recursive CTE without a depth limit runs until it terminates naturally (no more rows to produce) or hits the database's recursion limit (work_mem exhaustion, statement timeout). Natural termination depends on the data — and data can have unexpected cycles or deeper hierarchies than anticipated. The depth limit is a safety net that prevents runaway recursion regardless of data anomalies. At HydroAlpes, every recursive CTE includes WHERE depth < 50 even when the expected maximum depth is 5, because the limit costs nothing on normal data and prevents catastrophe on anomalous data.

The junior track used simple CASE expressions for value classification: CASE WHEN daily_kw > 100 THEN 'high' ELSE 'normal' END. At mid-level, CASE expressions become a general-purpose conditional logic tool that replaces procedural IF/ELSE blocks in pipeline SQL. Multi-condition CASE with priority ordering, nested CASE for decision trees, CASE inside aggregation for conditional metrics, and CASE for data type conversion and error handling are patterns that appear in virtually every production pipeline. This lesson covers the advanced CASE patterns that HydroAlpes' team uses daily.
Multi-Condition CASE with Priority

-- Tiered pricing: conditions evaluated in order, first match wins
-- Higher tiers are checked first to ensure correct classification
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       CASE
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 1000 THEN 'Tier 4: Industrial peak'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 100  THEN 'Tier 3: Commercial high'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 10   THEN 'Tier 2: Standard'
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 0    THEN 'Tier 1: Low-usage discount'
         ELSE 'Invalid: negative consumption'
       END AS pricing_tier,
       CASE
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 1000 THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.082
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 100  THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.094
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 10   THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.112
         WHEN mr.daily_kw >= 0    THEN mr.daily_kw * 0.098
         ELSE 0
       END AS estimated_charge_eur
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
ORDER BY mr.daily_kw DESC
LIMIT  10;

CASE evaluates conditions in order and returns the value for the first TRUE condition. The ordering matters: if the >= 10 condition were listed before >= 100, a reading of 500 kW would match >= 10 first and be classified as Tier 2 instead of the correct Tier 3. By listing conditions from most restrictive (>= 1000) to least restrictive (>= 0), each reading falls into the correct tier. The ELSE clause catches unexpected values (negative readings that should have been filtered by the Silver quality gate) and classifies them explicitly rather than defaulting to NULL. At HydroAlpes, the billing pipeline uses this tiered CASE for provisional pricing before the final rate schedule is applied.
CASE for Safe Division

-- Safe division: prevent division by zero with CASE or NULLIF
-- Pattern 1: CASE — explicit, readable
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw,
       COUNT(*) AS reading_count,
       CASE
         WHEN COUNT(*) = 0 THEN NULL
         ELSE SUM(daily_kw) / COUNT(*)
       END AS safe_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;

-- Pattern 2: NULLIF — concise
SELECT region,
       SUM(daily_kw) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS safe_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;

Division by zero produces an error in PostgreSQL and NULL in some other databases. Both CASE and NULLIF prevent the error by returning NULL when the denominator is zero. NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) returns NULL if the count is zero (because NULLIF returns NULL when its two arguments are equal), and NULL / anything = NULL — no error. The CASE version is more verbose but more readable for engineers who are not familiar with NULLIF. At HydroAlpes, the team prefers NULLIF for simple zero-prevention and CASE for complex conditional divisions where the zero-prevention is part of a larger conditional expression.
CASE Inside UPDATE for Conditional Logic

-- Conditional UPDATE: set quality_flag based on multiple business rules
UPDATE silver.meter_readings
SET    quality_flag = CASE
         WHEN daily_kw < 0                          THEN 'NEGATIVE'
         WHEN daily_kw > 5000                        THEN 'IMPLAUSIBLE'
         WHEN daily_kw = 0 AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL' THEN 'SUSPICIOUS_ZERO'
         WHEN daily_kw IS NULL                       THEN 'MISSING'
         ELSE 'VALID'
       END
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

CASE inside UPDATE applies different classification logic to each row based on its values — the SQL equivalent of an IF/ELIF/ELSE block in Python. The quality_flag column receives a different value depending on which condition the row matches. This pattern is the foundation of data quality tagging: every reading is classified into a quality category in a single pass, and downstream consumers can filter to quality_flag = 'VALID' to exclude problematic readings. At HydroAlpes, quality tagging runs as the first step of the Silver pipeline, before any joins or aggregations, so that quality flags are available for every downstream operation.

blocks.title: CASE is SQL's general-purpose conditional
blocks.content: Unlike Python, SQL has no IF statement outside of procedural extensions (PL/pgSQL). CASE is the only conditional construct available in standard SQL, which means it handles every conditional need: value classification, safe arithmetic, error handling, conditional aggregation, and data quality tagging. Mastering CASE at mid-level depth means recognizing it as a universal tool, not a simple value mapper.

NULL values propagate silently through SQL expressions. NULL + 10 = NULL. NULL = NULL is not TRUE — it is NULL (unknown). NULL in a GROUP BY column creates a NULL group. NULL in an aggregate is ignored by SUM and AVG but counted by COUNT(*). These behaviors were introduced in the junior track, but at mid-level, NULL handling becomes a production concern: a single unhandled NULL in a billing calculation can produce a NULL total_kw that silently drops a region from the billing report. This lesson covers the production NULL-handling toolkit: COALESCE, NULLIF, IS DISTINCT FROM, and the GREATEST/LEAST functions with NULL semantics.
COALESCE — Cascading Defaults

-- COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument
-- Cascading fallback: try the primary source, then the fallback, then a default
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       COALESCE(m.customer_type, s.default_customer_type, 'UNKNOWN') AS customer_type
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15';

COALESCE with three arguments implements a cascading fallback: first try the meter's customer_type from the meters table, then try the substation's default_customer_type, and finally fall back to 'UNKNOWN' if both are NULL. This prevents NULL customer_type values from propagating into the billing pipeline, where they would cause the GROUP BY to create a NULL group that downstream reports cannot interpret. At HydroAlpes, COALESCE appears in every LEFT JOIN enrichment query as a defensive pattern: even if the dimension lookup fails (LEFT JOIN produces NULL), the fact row always has a non-NULL classification.
IS DISTINCT FROM — NULL-Safe Comparison

-- Standard equality: NULL = NULL → NULL (unknown) — never TRUE
SELECT * FROM silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id = NULL;              -- Returns ZERO rows (wrong!)

-- IS DISTINCT FROM: treats NULL as a comparable value
-- NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL → TRUE (NULLs are 'the same')
SELECT * FROM silver.meters
WHERE  substation_id IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL;  -- Correct: returns NULL rows

-- Detect changes including NULL → value and value → NULL transitions
SELECT s.meter_id,
       t.customer_type AS current_type,
       s.customer_type AS new_type
FROM   staging.meters_latest AS s
  INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS t
    ON s.meter_id = t.meter_id AND t.effective_to IS NULL
WHERE  s.customer_type IS DISTINCT FROM t.customer_type;

IS DISTINCT FROM is the NULL-safe version of <>. Regular <> returns NULL when either side is NULL — which means NULL-to-value and value-to-NULL changes are invisible to standard comparison operators. IS DISTINCT FROM treats NULL as a comparable value: NULL IS DISTINCT FROM 'COMMERCIAL' is TRUE, and NULL IS NOT DISTINCT FROM NULL is TRUE. At HydroAlpes, the SCD change-detection pipeline uses IS DISTINCT FROM to detect all attribute changes, including transitions to and from NULL. Without it, a meter whose customer_type changes from 'RESIDENTIAL' to NULL (a data quality issue) would not trigger a Type 2 version — the change would be silently ignored.
GREATEST and LEAST with NULLs

GREATEST(a, b, c) returns the largest value. LEAST(a, b, c) returns the smallest. In PostgreSQL, if any argument is NULL, the function returns NULL — unlike many other databases where NULLs are ignored. This means GREATEST(10, NULL, 5) returns NULL in PostgreSQL, not 10. The workaround: wrap each argument in COALESCE to provide a default. GREATEST(COALESCE(a, 0), COALESCE(b, 0), COALESCE(c, 0)) guarantees a non-NULL result. At HydroAlpes, this pattern appears in the reservoir level monitoring pipeline, where GREATEST is used to find the maximum level across multiple sensors per reservoir — and a malfunctioning sensor reporting NULL should not cause the entire computation to return NULL.

blocks.title: NULL is the silent data corruptor
blocks.content: A NULL in a SUM is silently ignored — SUM(10, NULL, 5) = 15, not NULL. A NULL in arithmetic propagates — 10 + NULL = NULL. A NULL in a comparison returns UNKNOWN — NULL = NULL is not TRUE. A NULL in GROUP BY creates a NULL group. Each behavior is individually documented but collectively creates a minefield for pipeline engineers. The mid-level discipline is: handle NULLs explicitly at every pipeline boundary with COALESCE for defaults, IS DISTINCT FROM for change detection, and CASE for conditional classification. Never assume a column is non-NULL unless a NOT NULL constraint enforces it.

HydroAlpes' meter_id follows a convention: CH-BERN-00142 encodes the country (CH), the region (BERN), and the sequence number (00142). At junior level, this convention was just a naming pattern. At mid-level, parsing these identifiers is a pipeline operation: extracting the country code for routing, the region code for validation against the regions table, and the sequence number for gap detection. String functions — split_part, substring, regexp_matches, and string_agg — are the tools that parse, validate, and reconstruct identifier strings in production pipelines. This lesson covers the production string patterns that appear in every data engineering codebase.
Parsing Identifiers

-- Parse meter_id components using split_part (delimiter-based extraction)
SELECT meter_id,
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 1) AS country_code,   -- CH
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 2) AS region_code,     -- BERN
       split_part(meter_id, '-', 3) AS sequence_num     -- 00142
FROM   silver.meters
LIMIT  5;

-- Validate: find meters with malformed IDs (wrong number of parts)
SELECT meter_id
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  array_length(string_to_array(meter_id, '-'), 1) <> 3;

split_part extracts the Nth part of a delimited string. split_part(meter_id, '-', 1) returns everything before the first hyphen (the country code). The validation query uses string_to_array to split the entire identifier and checks whether the result has exactly 3 parts. Meters with malformed IDs (missing hyphens, extra parts, empty segments) are flagged for data quality review. At HydroAlpes, this validation runs as part of the Bronze-to-Silver quality gate — malformed IDs are routed to the quarantine table instead of blocking the pipeline.
Regular Expressions

-- Validate meter_id format with a regex pattern
-- Expected: 2 uppercase letters, hyphen, 2-20 uppercase letters, hyphen, 5 digits
SELECT meter_id,
       meter_id ~ '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$' AS is_valid_format
FROM   silver.meters
WHERE  meter_id !~ '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$'
LIMIT  10;

PostgreSQL's ~ operator matches a string against a regular expression. The pattern ^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$ validates the meter_id format: two uppercase letters, a hyphen, 2–20 uppercase letters, a hyphen, and exactly 5 digits. The WHERE clause filters to non-matching IDs, surfacing format violations. Regular expressions are the most powerful validation tool for string-formatted data, but they are also the most opaque — a complex regex is difficult to read and debug. At HydroAlpes, regex patterns are stored as named constants in the pipeline configuration (METER_ID_PATTERN = '^[A-Z]{2}-[A-Z]{2,20}-[0-9]{5}$') so they are documented, version-controlled, and reusable across queries.
Aggregating Strings

-- string_agg: concatenate values from multiple rows into a single string
-- Useful for generating comma-separated lists in reports
SELECT region,
       COUNT(*) AS meter_count,
       string_agg(DISTINCT customer_type, ', ' ORDER BY customer_type) AS types_present
FROM   silver.meters
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY region;

region      | meter_count | types_present
------------+-------------+---------------------------------------
BERN-NORTH  |     135,000 | COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, RESIDENTIAL
BERN-SOUTH  |     128,000 | COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, RESIDENTIAL
INNSBRUCK-E |      98,000 | COMMERCIAL, RESIDENTIAL
...

string_agg concatenates values from multiple rows into a single delimited string. The DISTINCT keyword deduplicates before concatenating, and ORDER BY controls the concatenation order. This pattern is useful for generating human-readable summaries (comma-separated lists of customer types per region) and for debugging (concatenating all error messages from a quarantine table into a single diagnostic string per meter). The Spark SQL equivalent is collect_list for arrays and concat_ws for string joining. At HydroAlpes, string_agg appears in the data quality dashboard queries where each region's profile is summarized in a single row.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When parsing structured identifiers (meter_id, substation_id, file paths), prefer split_part over regex for simple delimited extraction — it is faster, more readable, and easier to maintain. Use regex only when the parsing requires pattern matching (variable-length components, optional parts, format validation). At HydroAlpes, split_part handles 95% of identifier parsing; regex is reserved for validation and for parsing identifiers from external systems with inconsistent formats.

HydroAlpes operates across two time zones (CET for Swiss operations, CET for Austrian operations — currently the same, but historically different during daylight saving transitions). Meter readings use DATE (no time component). Turbine telemetry uses TIMESTAMPTZ (with timezone awareness). Energy prices use TIMESTAMP at hourly granularity in UTC. The billing period alignment uses fiscal months that start on the 15th of each calendar month (a Swiss regulatory requirement). Every one of these temporal variations requires specific date/time handling patterns, and getting them wrong produces billing errors that take months to detect and correct. This lesson covers the production date patterns that handle real-world temporal complexity.
DATE_TRUNC for Period Alignment

-- Align dates to billing periods (fiscal month starts on the 15th)
-- A reading on January 20 belongs to the billing period starting January 15
-- A reading on January 10 belongs to the billing period starting December 15
SELECT mr.date,
       mr.daily_kw,
       CASE
         WHEN EXTRACT(DAY FROM mr.date) >= 15
           THEN DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) + INTERVAL '14 days'
         ELSE DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) - INTERVAL '17 days'
       END AS billing_period_start
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.meter_id = 'CH-BERN-00142'
  AND  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date <= '2025-01-31'
ORDER BY mr.date;

Standard DATE_TRUNC('month', date) aligns to the 1st of the month. HydroAlpes' billing period starts on the 15th, requiring custom alignment logic. The CASE expression checks whether the reading falls on or after the 15th (belongs to the current month's billing period) or before the 15th (belongs to the previous month's billing period). This custom alignment pattern is common in financial and regulatory contexts where fiscal periods do not align with calendar boundaries. The key principle: compute the period_start as a derived column early in the pipeline (in the Silver-to-Gold transformation), so downstream queries can GROUP BY billing_period_start without recomputing the alignment logic.
Timezone-Safe Timestamp Conversion

-- Convert turbine telemetry timestamps from UTC to local time
-- TIMESTAMPTZ handles the conversion automatically based on session timezone
SET timezone = 'Europe/Zurich';

SELECT tt.turbine_id,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'UTC'     AS utc_time,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/Zurich' AS zurich_time,
       tt.ts AT TIME ZONE 'Europe/Vienna' AS vienna_time
FROM   silver.turbine_telemetry AS tt
WHERE  tt.ts >= '2025-01-15T00:00:00Z'
  AND  tt.ts <  '2025-01-16T00:00:00Z'
LIMIT  5;

AT TIME ZONE converts a TIMESTAMPTZ value to a specific timezone's local time. For Swiss turbines, Zurich time is CET (UTC+1) in winter and CEST (UTC+2) in summer. The database handles daylight saving transitions automatically — the engineer does not need to know the DST rules. At HydroAlpes, all timestamps are stored as TIMESTAMPTZ in UTC and converted to local time only at the presentation layer (in Gold-layer views and dashboard queries). This UTC-storage, local-display pattern prevents the class of bugs where timestamps are ambiguous during daylight saving transitions (the hour from 02:00 to 03:00 occurs twice during the autumn clock change).
Interval Arithmetic

-- Common date arithmetic patterns
SELECT CURRENT_DATE                                  AS today,
       CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'               AS yesterday,
       CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'              AS one_week_ago,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)             AS first_of_month,
       (DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
        + INTERVAL '1 month' - INTERVAL '1 day')::DATE AS last_of_month,
       CURRENT_DATE - (CURRENT_DATE - DATE '2025-01-01') AS days_since_jan1;

Interval arithmetic in PostgreSQL is type-safe: DATE + INTERVAL produces a TIMESTAMP, which can be cast back to DATE with ::DATE. The first-of-month and last-of-month patterns are the most commonly used in pipeline date logic — they define the boundaries of monthly aggregation periods, billing cycles, and retention windows. At HydroAlpes, these patterns are encapsulated in a SQL function library (a set of CREATE FUNCTION statements) that the pipeline calls rather than recomputing inline: SELECT hydroalpes.last_day_of_month('2025-01-15') returns '2025-01-31'. This encapsulation prevents subtle bugs (like forgetting the -1 day adjustment for last-of-month) and makes the date logic version-controlled and testable.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Store all timestamps as TIMESTAMPTZ in UTC. Convert to local time only at the presentation layer. Use DATE (not TIMESTAMP) for columns that represent calendar dates without time components (reading dates, billing dates, installation dates). Encapsulate complex date arithmetic in SQL functions so the logic is defined once, tested once, and reused everywhere. At HydroAlpes, the date function library contains 12 functions (first_of_month, last_of_month, billing_period_start, fiscal_quarter, etc.) that the entire pipeline depends on.

Every SQL query in this course so far has been static: the table names, column names, and conditions are fixed at write time. But some pipeline operations require queries that are constructed at runtime: generating partition-specific DDL for each new month, building PIVOT column lists from dynamic data, or constructing WHERE clauses based on pipeline configuration. Dynamic SQL — generating and executing SQL strings at runtime — is the tool for these operations. It is also one of the most dangerous tools in SQL: a dynamically constructed query that includes unsanitized user input is vulnerable to SQL injection, and a dynamically constructed query that is syntactically wrong fails at runtime, not at development time.
Parameterized Queries

-- Parameterized query in PL/pgSQL: safe against SQL injection
-- The pipeline passes target_date as a parameter, not as string concatenation
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION hydroalpes.refresh_gold_billing(
    target_date DATE
) RETURNS VOID AS $$
BEGIN
  DELETE FROM gold.regional_consumption
  WHERE  month = DATE_TRUNC('month', target_date);

  INSERT INTO gold.regional_consumption
      (region, customer_type, month, total_kw, meter_count)
  SELECT mr.region,
         m.customer_type,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date) AS month,
         SUM(mr.daily_kw),
         COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id)
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
    INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  WHERE  mr.date >= target_date
    AND  mr.date <  target_date + INTERVAL '1 month'
  GROUP BY mr.region, m.customer_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

The function accepts target_date as a parameter and uses it in the WHERE clause. The parameter is bound by the database — it is never concatenated into the SQL string, so SQL injection is impossible. This is the safe approach to dynamic behavior: parameterize the values that change between runs, but keep the query structure static. At HydroAlpes, every pipeline query that varies by date, region, or batch ID uses parameterized functions rather than string-constructed SQL. The Airflow pipeline calls SELECT hydroalpes.refresh_gold_billing('2025-01-01') each day, passing the target date as a parameter.
Dynamic DDL for Partition Management

EXECUTE runs a dynamically constructed SQL string. The format function with %I (identifier, safely quoted) and %L (literal, safely quoted) prevents SQL injection even though the query is constructed at runtime. The function creates a new partition table named meter_readings_2025_02 for the February date range. This dynamic DDL is necessary because partition names include the month and year — values that change on each invocation. At HydroAlpes, this function is called by the Airflow DAG on the first of each month, creating the partition for the current month before the daily ingestion pipeline runs.
When Dynamic SQL Is Necessary

Dynamic SQL is necessary when table names, column names, or DDL structure vary at runtime — situations that parameterized queries cannot handle because SQL parameters only substitute values, not identifiers or keywords. At HydroAlpes, dynamic SQL appears in three contexts: partition management (creating monthly partitions with date-based names), dynamic pivoting (generating CASE WHEN columns from a list of values queried from a metadata table), and schema migration (generating ALTER TABLE statements from a configuration file). For everything else — date-filtered queries, region-filtered queries, batch-specific processing — parameterized static SQL is preferred because it is type-checked at function creation time and safe by construction.

blocks.title: Never concatenate unsanitized input into SQL strings
blocks.content: The SQL injection vulnerability is straightforward: if you construct a query like 'SELECT * FROM meters WHERE region = ''' || user_input || '''' and user_input contains '; DROP TABLE meters; --, the constructed string executes a destructive command. Always use format(%I, %L) in PL/pgSQL or parameterized queries in application code. At HydroAlpes, the CI linter flags any PL/pgSQL function that uses || for string concatenation inside EXECUTE — format() is the only permitted pattern for dynamic SQL construction.

This module covered the advanced SQL patterns that implement the data models from Module 4 in production pipelines: MERGE and INSERT ON CONFLICT for upsert operations, set operations for data combination and reconciliation, recursive CTEs for graph traversal and sequence generation, advanced CASE for conditional pipeline logic, NULL-safe comparison and cascading defaults, string functions for identifier parsing and validation, date patterns for period alignment and timezone handling, and dynamic SQL for partition management and schema evolution. This final lesson organizes these patterns into a catalog with selection guidance — a reference that HydroAlpes' team consults when choosing the right pattern for a new pipeline operation.
The Pattern Catalog

rows: Idempotent fact loading
INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE
MERGE (for multi-case logic)
ON CONFLICT for simple upserts; MERGE when different actions needed per match type
SCD Type 2 processing
MERGE + follow-up INSERT
Separate UPDATE + INSERT in transaction
MERGE for combined Type 1 + Type 2; separate statements when logic is complex
Cross-layer reconciliation
EXCEPT on key columns
LEFT JOIN + IS NULL (anti-join)
EXCEPT for simple existence check; anti-join when you need columns from both sides
Source combination
UNION ALL
INSERT from multiple sources
UNION ALL for in-query combination; separate INSERTs for staged loading
Hierarchy traversal
Recursive CTE with depth limit
Multiple self-joins (fixed depth only)
Recursive CTE for variable depth; self-joins for known fixed depth (simpler, faster)
NULL-safe comparison
IS DISTINCT FROM
COALESCE to a sentinel value, then compare
IS DISTINCT FROM for change detection; COALESCE for providing defaults
Identifier parsing
split_part (delimiter-based)
regexp_matches (pattern-based)
split_part for simple delimited IDs; regex for complex or variable formats
Period alignment
DATE_TRUNC + CASE for custom periods
Encapsulated function
Inline CASE for one-off queries; function for reusable period logic

blocks.content: The Architecture Phase Complete

Modules 4 and 5 together form the architecture phase. Module 4 taught what to build: star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, temporal tables, and denormalized wide tables. Module 5 taught how to build it: MERGE for upsert loading, EXCEPT for reconciliation, recursive CTEs for topology traversal, CASE and COALESCE for defensive pipeline logic, and parameterized functions for reusable pipeline operations. The data models from Module 4 and the SQL patterns from Module 5 combine to produce the complete Silver-to-Gold transformation pipeline that serves HydroAlpes' three teams with purpose-built analytical datasets.

The next phase — engineering discipline (Modules 6–7) — shifts from building pipelines to hardening them. Module 6 covers testing: how do you verify that the MERGE loaded the correct data, that the SCD processing created the right versions, and that the reconciliation EXCEPT returned zero rows? Module 7 covers deployment: how do you promote these pipeline changes through development, staging, and production environments safely, with migration scripts, version tracking, and rollback strategies? The architecture is designed. The patterns are implemented. Now the engineering practices that make them production-reliable are the focus.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 6, you will learn to test every pattern from this module systematically. The MERGE should produce the correct row counts — an assertion query verifies it. The SCD Type 2 pipeline should create exactly one new version per changed attribute — a regression test compares before-and-after snapshots. The EXCEPT reconciliation should return zero rows — but what if it returns rows? Module 6 teaches the testing framework that catches pipeline bugs before they reach production, turning ad-hoc verification into automated, repeatable, and comprehensive quality assurance.

modules.title: Testing SQL Pipelines
modules.description: Master systematic testing of SQL transformations with assertion queries, data contract tests, schema validation, statistical regression tests, and CI integration.

blocks.content: In April 2025, Markus refactored the billing CTE chain to improve readability. He renamed the intermediate CTE from filtered_readings to january_readings and adjusted the WHERE clause. The refactored query produced the same row count as the original. Code review approved the change. The billing pipeline ran for two weeks before the analytics team noticed that total consumption was 3% lower than the prior month's trend predicted. Investigation revealed that Markus had accidentally changed the date filter from mr.date < '2025-02-01' to mr.date <= '2025-01-31' — which excluded readings with a date of January 31 stored as a TIMESTAMP ('2025-01-31 00:00:01' is after midnight and does not match <= '2025-01-31'). The row count was the same because the timestamps all had time 00:00:00 in the test environment. Production data had sub-second timestamps from late-arriving corrections.
The Cost of Undetected SQL Bugs

The billing undercount affected two invoice cycles and required manual correction with 10 sub-regions worth of customers. The root cause — a boundary condition in a date filter — would have been caught by a simple assertion test: 'the sum of daily_kw for January must match the sum from the previous run within 0.1%.' The test would have failed on the first run after the refactor, flagging the discrepancy before the pipeline output reached any consumer. The two-week detection delay cost HydroAlpes approximately 40 hours of investigation and correction work. The assertion test would have taken 5 minutes to write and would have caught the bug in 5 seconds.
What SQL Testing Covers

SQL testing is not the same as application testing. There are no unit tests for individual functions (SQL queries are not decomposed into callable units in the same way as Python functions). Instead, SQL testing focuses on the contract between the query and its consumers: does the output have the expected schema, the expected row count, the expected value ranges, and the expected relationships with other tables? This module covers five categories of SQL tests. Assertion queries verify data properties (no negative values, no duplicates, no NULLs in required columns). Schema contract tests verify table structure (columns exist, types match, constraints are present). Statistical regression tests verify that aggregated metrics fall within expected ranges. Referential integrity tests verify cross-table relationships. And CTE-level tests verify that individual transformation steps produce correct intermediate results.

The testing approach is defensive: every test is a SELECT query that returns zero rows when the data is correct and returns violating rows when it is not. This design makes tests composable (combine with UNION ALL), automatable (run in CI, check exit code), and debuggable (the violating rows themselves tell you what went wrong). By the end of this module, you will have a complete testing framework that catches data quality issues, schema drift, and transformation regressions before they reach production consumers.

blocks.title: SQL tests are SQL queries
blocks.content: Every test in this module is a standard SELECT statement — the same SQL you have been writing since the junior track. There is no test framework to install, no special syntax to learn, and no external tooling required. A test is a query that returns zero rows on success. This simplicity is the reason SQL testing scales: any engineer who can write a WHERE clause can write a test, and any pipeline that can run a query can run a test.

An assertion query is a SELECT that returns zero rows when the data meets a condition and returns the violating rows when it does not. The pattern is: SELECT rows WHERE violation_condition. If the result is empty, the assertion passes. If the result has rows, each row is a specific violation that tells you exactly which record failed and why. Assertion queries are the simplest and most common form of SQL test, and they directly encode the business rules that the data must satisfy. At HydroAlpes, every Silver table has at least five assertion queries that run after every pipeline load, and the pipeline is blocked from promoting data to Gold if any assertion fails.
The Five Standard Assertions

-- Assertion 1: No NULL values in required columns
SELECT meter_id, date, 'NULL daily_kw' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw IS NULL
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 2: No negative consumption values
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Negative daily_kw' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw < 0
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 3: No duplicate primary keys
SELECT meter_id, date, COUNT(*) AS copies, 'Duplicate key' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
GROUP BY meter_id, date
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

-- Assertion 4: All regions are valid (referential integrity)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.region, 'Unknown region' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r ON mr.region = r.region_id
WHERE  r.region_id IS NULL
  AND  mr.date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

-- Assertion 5: Value range check
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Implausible value' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  daily_kw > 5000
  AND  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day';

Each assertion follows the same pattern: SELECT the identifying columns plus a violation description WHERE the violation condition is true. The violation column makes each assertion self-documenting — when the pipeline logs the results, the violation type is immediately visible without checking which assertion produced the row. The date filter restricts each assertion to yesterday's data (the most recent pipeline load), keeping the assertions fast even on large tables. At HydroAlpes, the five assertions above run in under 2 seconds total against 1.2 million rows — negligible cost compared to the hours of debugging they prevent.
Combining Assertions

-- Combine all assertions into one query for pipeline integration
-- Zero rows = all assertions pass; any rows = at least one assertion failed
SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw::TEXT AS value, violation
FROM (
  SELECT meter_id, date, NULL::DOUBLE PRECISION AS daily_kw, 'NULL daily_kw' AS violation
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw IS NULL AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Negative daily_kw'
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw < 0 AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
  UNION ALL
  SELECT meter_id, date, daily_kw, 'Implausible value'
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  daily_kw > 5000 AND date = CURRENT_DATE - 1
) AS violations
ORDER BY violation, meter_id;

UNION ALL combines multiple assertion queries into a single result set. The pipeline executes this one combined query and checks whether the result is empty. If empty, all assertions passed and the pipeline proceeds to Gold. If rows exist, the pipeline logs the violations, routes the failing data to the quarantine table, and alerts the team. This combined-assertion pattern is the foundation of HydroAlpes' automated data quality gate — a single query that encodes all business rules and produces a machine-readable violation report when any rule is broken.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Store assertion queries in version-controlled .sql files alongside the pipeline code. Each file contains one assertion, named descriptively: assert_no_null_daily_kw.sql, assert_no_duplicates.sql. The pipeline runner executes each file and checks the row count. This separation makes assertions reviewable, versionable, and independently modifiable — changing one assertion does not risk breaking others. At HydroAlpes, the assertion file directory is the single source of truth for what the pipeline guarantees about its output.

Assertion queries verify the data in a table. Schema contract tests verify the table itself: do the expected columns exist, are they the correct types, are the constraints in place, and does the table match the documented specification? Schema drift — where a table's actual structure diverges from its documented specification — is a subtle and common problem. An ALTER TABLE that adds a column during a hotfix, a migration that changes a column type for compatibility, or a dropped constraint that was 'temporary' but never restored — all produce schema drift that assertion queries do not detect because the data in the changed columns might still be valid. Schema contract tests catch the structural changes themselves.
Column Existence and Type Tests

-- Verify that silver.meter_readings has the expected columns and types
-- Returns rows for any column that is missing or has the wrong type
WITH expected AS (
  VALUES
    ('meter_id',      'character varying'),
    ('daily_kw',      'double precision'),
    ('date',          'date'),
    ('region',        'character varying'),
    ('customer_type', 'character varying'),
    ('substation_id', 'character varying'),
    ('loaded_at',     'timestamp with time zone')
),
actual AS (
  SELECT column_name, data_type
  FROM   information_schema.columns
  WHERE  table_schema = 'silver'
    AND  table_name   = 'meter_readings'
)
SELECT e.column1 AS expected_column,
       e.column2 AS expected_type,
       a.data_type AS actual_type,
       CASE
         WHEN a.column_name IS NULL THEN 'MISSING COLUMN'
         WHEN a.data_type <> e.column2 THEN 'WRONG TYPE'
       END AS violation
FROM   expected AS e
  LEFT JOIN actual AS a
    ON e.column1 = a.column_name
WHERE  a.column_name IS NULL
   OR  a.data_type <> e.column2;

The test defines the expected schema as a VALUES clause (column name, expected type), joins it with the actual schema from information_schema.columns, and reports any mismatches. A missing column produces 'MISSING COLUMN.' A column with the wrong type produces 'WRONG TYPE.' If the result is empty, the table matches the contract. This test catches the scenario where a migration renamed daily_kw to consumption_kw or changed its type from DOUBLE PRECISION to NUMERIC — changes that would break every downstream query that references daily_kw. At HydroAlpes, schema contract tests run before the pipeline starts — if the schema has drifted, the pipeline aborts before writing any data.
Constraint Verification

-- Verify that the primary key constraint exists on (meter_id, date)
SELECT 'Missing PRIMARY KEY' AS violation
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM   information_schema.table_constraints
  WHERE  table_schema    = 'silver'
    AND  table_name      = 'meter_readings'
    AND  constraint_type = 'PRIMARY KEY'
);

-- Verify that the NOT NULL constraint exists on daily_kw
SELECT 'daily_kw allows NULLs' AS violation
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM   information_schema.columns
  WHERE  table_schema = 'silver'
    AND  table_name   = 'meter_readings'
    AND  column_name  = 'daily_kw'
    AND  is_nullable   = 'YES'
);

These tests verify that structural constraints exist — not just that the data happens to satisfy them. A table without a PRIMARY KEY still accepts non-duplicate inserts today, but it provides no guarantee against duplicates tomorrow. A column without NOT NULL still has non-NULL values if the pipeline happens to always provide them, but it provides no protection against a source system change that starts sending NULLs. Constraint verification catches the gap between 'the data looks right' and 'the database guarantees it stays right.' At HydroAlpes, constraint verification runs after every migration to confirm that the migration did not accidentally drop or alter any constraint.

blocks.title: Schema tests must run before data tests
blocks.content: If the daily_kw column was renamed to consumption_kw, the assertion query SELECT ... WHERE daily_kw IS NULL fails with a column-not-found error — not a helpful diagnostic. The schema contract test catches the rename before the assertion runs, producing a clear 'MISSING COLUMN: daily_kw' message. At HydroAlpes, the test execution order is: schema contracts first, then assertion queries, then statistical tests. Each layer depends on the previous layer passing.

Assertion queries catch individual row violations. Schema tests catch structural drift. But some bugs produce data that passes both — every row is valid, the schema is correct, but the aggregate properties are wrong. The billing refactor from lesson 1 is a perfect example: every row had a valid daily_kw, no NULLs, no duplicates — but the total was 3% low because a date boundary condition excluded one day's readings. Statistical tests catch these aggregate-level bugs by comparing the pipeline output's statistical properties against expected ranges derived from historical baselines.
Row Count Tests

-- Row count test: yesterday's load should have between 1.1M and 1.3M readings
-- (1.2M meters, but some may not report and late arrivals may add extras)
SELECT COUNT(*)    AS actual_count,
       1100000     AS min_expected,
       1300000     AS max_expected,
       CASE
         WHEN COUNT(*) < 1100000 THEN 'TOO FEW ROWS'
         WHEN COUNT(*) > 1300000 THEN 'TOO MANY ROWS'
         ELSE 'PASS'
       END AS result
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
HAVING COUNT(*) < 1100000 OR COUNT(*) > 1300000;

The HAVING clause filters the single-row aggregate result: if the count is within the expected range (1.1M–1.3M), HAVING eliminates the row and the result is empty (pass). If the count is outside the range, the result row appears with a diagnostic message. The expected range is determined by historical observation: HydroAlpes' 1.2 million meters have a 92% daily reporting rate (minimum) and occasional late-arrival batches that inflate the count to 108% (maximum). These bounds are reviewed quarterly as the meter fleet grows. A sudden drop to 800,000 readings signals a source system outage. A spike to 2 million signals a duplicate ingestion batch.
Statistical Comparison Tests

-- Statistical test: yesterday's total consumption should be within 10% of
-- the same day last week (accounting for weekly seasonality)
WITH yesterday AS (
  SELECT SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
),
last_week AS (
  SELECT SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '8 days'
)
SELECT y.total_kw AS yesterday_kw,
       lw.total_kw AS last_week_kw,
       ROUND(ABS(y.total_kw - lw.total_kw) / lw.total_kw * 100, 1) AS pct_change,
       'Week-over-week deviation exceeds 10%' AS violation
FROM   yesterday y, last_week lw
WHERE  ABS(y.total_kw - lw.total_kw) / NULLIF(lw.total_kw, 0) > 0.10;

This test compares yesterday's total consumption against the same day of the previous week. If the difference exceeds 10%, the test returns a violation row with the actual values and the percentage change. The 10% threshold accounts for normal weather-driven fluctuation while catching pipeline errors that affect the total. The same-day-last-week comparison controls for weekly seasonality (weekday vs weekend patterns). At HydroAlpes, this week-over-week test is the one that would have caught the billing refactor bug: the 3% drop in total consumption after the date filter change would have triggered a violation on the first day, not two weeks later.
Distribution Tests

-- Distribution test: no single region should have zero readings
-- and no single customer type should exceed 90% of total readings
WITH region_counts AS (
  SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS cnt,
         COUNT(*)::NUMERIC / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER () AS pct
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
  GROUP BY region
)
SELECT region, cnt, ROUND(pct * 100, 1) AS pct,
       CASE
         WHEN cnt = 0   THEN 'Zero readings for region'
         WHEN pct > 0.9 THEN 'Region exceeds 90% of total'
       END AS violation
FROM   region_counts
WHERE  cnt = 0 OR pct > 0.9;

Distribution tests verify that the data's shape is plausible — not just that individual values are valid. A region with zero readings means its data source failed. A region with 90%+ of all readings means other regions' data was lost and one region's data was duplicated or misrouted. These structural anomalies produce data that passes every row-level assertion (no NULLs, no negatives, no duplicates) but is fundamentally wrong in aggregate. At HydroAlpes, distribution tests cover regions (10 regions, each should be 5–15% of total), customer types (3 types, none should exceed 85%), and date completeness (every date in the expected range should have readings).

blocks.title: Three layers of testing
blocks.content: Row-level assertions catch individual data quality violations (NULLs, negatives, duplicates). Schema tests catch structural drift (missing columns, changed types, dropped constraints). Statistical tests catch aggregate-level anomalies (wrong totals, missing regions, skewed distributions). Each layer catches a different category of bug, and a complete testing framework includes all three. At HydroAlpes, the billing refactor bug passed row-level assertions and schema tests — only a statistical test would have caught it.

In a fully constrained PostgreSQL database, foreign key constraints prevent orphan rows — a reading cannot reference a meter_id that does not exist in the meters table. But many production data platforms do not enforce foreign keys at the database level. Spark SQL and most cloud warehouses do not support foreign key constraints at all. Delta Lake tables on Databricks accept any data regardless of referential integrity. Even in PostgreSQL, some teams drop foreign keys for performance during bulk loads and re-add them afterward. In all these scenarios, referential integrity must be verified by tests rather than enforced by constraints. This lesson covers the patterns for testing cross-table relationships when the database does not enforce them.
Orphan Detection

-- Test: every reading should reference a meter that exists in the meters table
-- Anti-join pattern: find readings with no matching meter
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       COUNT(*) AS orphan_readings,
       'Reading references non-existent meter' AS violation
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
WHERE  m.meter_id IS NULL
  AND  mr.date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
GROUP BY mr.meter_id
ORDER BY orphan_readings DESC;

The anti-join pattern (LEFT JOIN plus IS NULL) from Module 6 of the junior track becomes a test. If the result is empty, every reading's meter_id exists in the meters table — referential integrity holds. If rows appear, those readings reference meters that do not exist and cannot be enriched with customer type, region, or substation information. The GROUP BY aggregation shows how many orphan readings each non-existent meter has, directing the investigation to the most impactful cases. At HydroAlpes, orphan detection tests cover every foreign key relationship in the Silver schema: readings → meters, meters → substations, meters → regions, substations → regions.
Dangling Reference Detection

-- Test: every meter should have at least one reading in the last 7 days
-- (Inactive meters should be flagged as is_active = FALSE)
SELECT m.meter_id,
       m.region,
       'Active meter with no recent readings' AS violation
FROM   silver.meters AS m
  LEFT JOIN silver.meter_readings AS mr
    ON m.meter_id = mr.meter_id
   AND mr.date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '7 days'
WHERE  m.is_active = TRUE
  AND  mr.meter_id IS NULL;

Dangling references are the reverse of orphans: dimension rows that have no corresponding fact rows. An active meter with no readings in 7 days is either offline (a hardware issue) or misclassified as active. The test surfaces these cases so the operations team can investigate — either fixing the meter or updating its is_active flag. This test catches a different class of problem than orphan detection: orphans indicate a pipeline issue (the meter was not loaded into the dimension), while dangling references indicate an operational issue (the meter is not reporting data).
Cross-Layer Reconciliation

-- Test: Silver reading count should match Gold fact count for yesterday
-- Uses EXCEPT to find keys in Silver but not in Gold
SELECT 'Readings in Silver but not Gold' AS violation,
       COUNT(*) AS missing_count
FROM (
  SELECT meter_id, date
  FROM   silver.meter_readings
  WHERE  date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
  EXCEPT
  SELECT d.meter_id, f.date_key
  FROM   gold.fact_meter_readings AS f
    INNER JOIN gold.dim_meters AS d ON f.meter_key = d.meter_key
  WHERE  f.date_key = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1 day'
) AS missing
HAVING COUNT(*) > 0;

The EXCEPT pattern from Module 5 (lesson 3) becomes a cross-layer reconciliation test. It finds readings that exist in Silver but were lost during the Silver-to-Gold transformation — typically because the meter had no dimension row (unregistered meter) and the INNER JOIN in the Gold pipeline excluded them. If the missing_count is zero, the Gold layer is complete. If it is non-zero, the pipeline investigation starts with the missing meter_ids: are they in the dim_meters table, and if not, why were they not loaded? At HydroAlpes, the cross-layer reconciliation test runs after every Gold refresh and blocks the Gold data from being served until the count is zero or below a configured tolerance threshold of 10 readings.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Referential integrity tests should cover both directions of every relationship: orphans (facts without dimensions) and dangling references (dimensions without facts). A one-directional test misses half the problems. At HydroAlpes, the test suite for each foreign key relationship contains two tests: one LEFT JOIN from fact to dimension (orphan detection) and one LEFT JOIN from dimension to fact (dangling detection). The pair takes under 5 seconds to execute and catches 100% of referential integrity violations.

Assertion queries verify that the output satisfies known rules. But what about rules you have not explicitly stated — properties of the output that are correct by construction but not verified by any explicit assertion? A query refactor might produce output that passes all assertions but differs from the previous version in subtle ways: different rounding, different NULL handling in edge cases, or different ordering that affects downstream LIMIT queries. Regression tests catch these unspecified changes by comparing the current output against a known-good baseline — a golden dataset — and flagging any difference.
The Golden Dataset Pattern

-- Step 1: Save the current output as the golden baseline
-- Run this once when the query is known to be correct
CREATE TABLE testing.gold_billing_baseline AS
SELECT region, customer_type, total_kw, active_meters
FROM   gold.regional_consumption
WHERE  month = '2025-01-01';

-- Step 2: After refactoring, compare the new output against the baseline
-- Any differences indicate a regression
SELECT 'In baseline but not in new output' AS direction,
       region, customer_type, total_kw, active_meters
FROM   testing.gold_billing_baseline
EXCEPT
SELECT 'In baseline but not in new output',
       region, customer_type, total_kw, active_meters
FROM   gold.regional_consumption
WHERE  month = '2025-01-01'

UNION ALL

SELECT 'In new output but not in baseline',
       region, customer_type, total_kw, active_meters
FROM   gold.regional_consumption
WHERE  month = '2025-01-01'
EXCEPT
SELECT 'In new output but not in baseline',
       region, customer_type, total_kw, active_meters
FROM   testing.gold_billing_baseline;

The bidirectional EXCEPT finds all differences between the baseline and the new output: rows in the baseline that are missing from the new output (deleted or changed rows) and rows in the new output that are not in the baseline (new or changed rows). If the result is empty, the refactored query produces identical output — the refactor is safe. If rows appear, the differences must be reviewed: are they intentional improvements or unintended regressions? At HydroAlpes, every query refactor includes a regression test against the golden baseline before the PR is approved. The baseline is refreshed after the refactor is validated and merged.
Statistical Regression Tests

-- Statistical regression: compare aggregate metrics within tolerance
-- Useful when exact row-level comparison is too strict (floating-point rounding)
WITH baseline AS (
  SELECT region, SUM(total_kw) AS baseline_total
  FROM   testing.gold_billing_baseline
  GROUP BY region
),
current_run AS (
  SELECT region, SUM(total_kw) AS current_total
  FROM   gold.regional_consumption
  WHERE  month = '2025-01-01'
  GROUP BY region
)
SELECT b.region,
       b.baseline_total,
       c.current_total,
       ABS(b.baseline_total - c.current_total) / b.baseline_total * 100 AS pct_diff,
       'Aggregate regression exceeds 0.01%' AS violation
FROM   baseline b
  INNER JOIN current_run c ON b.region = c.region
WHERE  ABS(b.baseline_total - c.current_total) / NULLIF(b.baseline_total, 0) > 0.0001;

Statistical regression tests compare aggregate metrics with a tolerance (0.01% in this case) rather than requiring exact row-level matches. This is appropriate when the transformation involves floating-point arithmetic (where rounding differences between implementation approaches are expected and harmless) or when the exact row-level order may differ between runs (parallel execution can produce different ordering). The tolerance threshold should be tight enough to catch real bugs (the 3% billing error from lesson 1) but loose enough to tolerate floating-point rounding (typically < 0.001%). At HydroAlpes, aggregate regression tests use a 0.01% tolerance for financial metrics and a 1% tolerance for non-financial metrics like meter counts.

blocks.title: Exact tests for correctness, statistical tests for robustness
blocks.content: Use exact golden-dataset comparison (bidirectional EXCEPT) when the output must be bit-for-bit identical — financial reports, regulatory filings, billing totals. Use statistical comparison (aggregate tolerance) when minor floating-point differences are acceptable — trend analysis, forecast inputs, operational dashboards. At HydroAlpes, the billing pipeline uses exact regression tests (zero tolerance on totals), and the capacity planning pipeline uses statistical tests (0.1% tolerance on moving averages).

The billing pipeline query chains three CTEs: filtered_readings (filter to the target date range), enriched_readings (join with meters dimension), and aggregated (GROUP BY region and customer type). When the final output is wrong, which CTE introduced the error? End-to-end testing tells you that the output is wrong but not where the bug is. CTE-level testing — running each CTE independently and verifying its output — localizes the bug to a specific transformation step. This is the SQL equivalent of unit testing individual functions: each CTE has a defined input (the CTE it reads from or the base table) and a defined output (its column set and expected properties), and each can be tested in isolation.
Extracting and Testing a CTE

-- The production query has three CTEs:
-- WITH filtered_readings AS (...), enriched_readings AS (...), aggregated AS (...)
-- SELECT * FROM aggregated;

-- Test the first CTE independently:
-- Copy the CTE definition and add assertions
WITH filtered_readings AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01'
    AND  mr.date <  '2025-02-01'
)
-- Assertion: row count should be between 35M and 40M for January
SELECT COUNT(*) AS actual,
       35000000 AS min_expected,
       40000000 AS max_expected,
       CASE
         WHEN COUNT(*) < 35000000 THEN 'filtered_readings: too few rows'
         WHEN COUNT(*) > 40000000 THEN 'filtered_readings: too many rows'
         ELSE 'PASS'
       END AS result
FROM   filtered_readings
HAVING COUNT(*) < 35000000 OR COUNT(*) > 40000000;

The CTE is extracted from the production query and tested independently. The assertion verifies that the filtered_readings CTE produces between 35 and 40 million rows for January — the expected range based on 1.2 million meters × 31 days with a 92–108% reporting tolerance. If this CTE produces the wrong row count, the bug is in the date filter — exactly where Markus's billing bug was. CTE-level testing would have caught the <= vs < boundary condition on the first test run, before the combined query produced a plausible-looking but incorrect total.
Testing the Join CTE

-- Test the second CTE: enriched_readings (join with meters)
-- Key assertion: the join should not change the row count (no fan-out)
WITH filtered_readings AS (
  SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date, mr.region
  FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
),
enriched_readings AS (
  SELECT fr.*, m.customer_type, m.substation_id
  FROM   filtered_readings AS fr
    INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON fr.meter_id = m.meter_id
)
SELECT (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM filtered_readings) AS before_join,
       (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM enriched_readings) AS after_join,
       CASE
         WHEN (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM filtered_readings) <>
              (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM enriched_readings)
           THEN 'Fan-out or row loss detected in enriched_readings'
         ELSE 'PASS'
       END AS result;

The join CTE test verifies the most critical property of dimension enrichment: the row count must not change. If after_join exceeds before_join, a fan-out occurred (duplicate dimension keys, per Module 2 lesson 2). If after_join is less than before_join, an INNER JOIN excluded unmatched readings (orphan meters). Both conditions are bugs. At HydroAlpes, every join CTE has a row-count preservation test. This single test catches both fan-out and row loss — the two most common join bugs — with a simple equality comparison.
When CTE-Level Testing Is Worth the Cost

CTE-level testing adds maintenance overhead: each CTE has its own test, and when the query changes, the tests must be updated. The overhead is justified for CTEs that perform transformations with non-obvious correctness properties — joins (fan-out risk), date filters (boundary conditions), aggregations (sum correctness). It is not justified for trivial CTEs like column renames or simple projections where the transformation is self-evidently correct. At HydroAlpes, the testing guideline is: test any CTE that contains a JOIN, a WHERE with date logic, or a GROUP BY. Skip CTEs that are pure SELECT column renames.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: When debugging a failing end-to-end test, convert the production query's CTEs into standalone test queries. Start at the first CTE and verify its output. If it passes, move to the second CTE (including the first as a dependency). Continue until you find the CTE that produces the wrong output. This progressive debugging approach localizes the bug to a single transformation step in minutes — far faster than debugging the full query by staring at the end-to-end output.

Every test in this module has run against production or staging data — the full 1.2-million-meter, 130-million-row Silver dataset. This works for integration tests (verifying the pipeline against realistic data), but it has two problems for development testing. First, it is slow: running assertions against 130 million rows takes seconds, but running CTE-level tests with multiple subqueries can take minutes. Second, it is non-deterministic: the data changes daily, so test results that pass today might fail tomorrow if the data composition shifts (a new region is added, a meter is decommissioned). Test fixtures — small, deterministic datasets created specifically for testing — solve both problems.
The Fixture Pattern

-- Create a test fixture: small, deterministic dataset for testing
-- 100 meters across 4 regions, 3 customer types, 7 days of readings
CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS testing;

CREATE TABLE testing.fixture_meters AS
SELECT 'TEST-' || region || '-' || LPAD(seq::TEXT, 5, '0') AS meter_id,
       region,
       customer_type,
       'SUB-' || region || '-01' AS substation_id,
       DATE '2020-01-01' AS installation_date,
       TRUE AS is_active
FROM   (VALUES ('BERN'), ('ZURICH'), ('INNSBRUCK'), ('SALZBURG')) AS r(region)
  CROSS JOIN (VALUES ('RESIDENTIAL'), ('COMMERCIAL'), ('INDUSTRIAL')) AS ct(customer_type)
  CROSS JOIN generate_series(1, 8) AS s(seq)  -- ~8 meters per region-type
WHERE  NOT (region = 'SALZBURG' AND customer_type = 'INDUSTRIAL' AND seq > 4);
-- 100 meters total (some regions have fewer industrial meters — realistic distribution)

CREATE TABLE testing.fixture_readings AS
SELECT fm.meter_id,
       d::DATE AS date,
       fm.region,
       fm.customer_type,
       ROUND((RANDOM() * 50 + 1)::NUMERIC, 2) AS daily_kw
FROM   testing.fixture_meters AS fm
  CROSS JOIN generate_series('2025-01-01'::DATE, '2025-01-07'::DATE, '1 day') AS d;

The fixture creates 100 meters and 700 readings (100 meters × 7 days) with known properties: 4 regions, 3 customer types, realistic but small scale. The daily_kw values are random but bounded (1–51 kW), providing deterministic-enough data for structural tests while maintaining realistic variation for statistical tests. The fixture takes milliseconds to create and milliseconds to query — enabling rapid iteration during development. The fixture data intentionally includes common edge cases: one region has fewer industrial meters than others (testing non-uniform distribution), and the date range is exactly 7 days (testing boundary conditions).
Running Tests Against Fixtures

-- Run the billing CTE chain against the fixture instead of production data
-- Substitute table names: silver.meter_readings → testing.fixture_readings
WITH filtered_readings AS (
  SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date, region
  FROM   testing.fixture_readings  -- fixture instead of production
  WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-01-08'
),
enriched_readings AS (
  SELECT fr.*, fm.customer_type
  FROM   filtered_readings AS fr
    INNER JOIN testing.fixture_meters AS fm ON fr.meter_id = fm.meter_id
)
-- Assertion: fixture has exactly 100 meters × 7 days = 700 readings
SELECT COUNT(*) AS actual, 700 AS expected,
       CASE WHEN COUNT(*) <> 700 THEN 'FAIL' ELSE 'PASS' END AS result
FROM   enriched_readings;

The test substitutes production table names with fixture table names, keeping the query logic identical. The expected row count is deterministic: 100 meters × 7 days = 700 readings. If the enrichment join changes this count, the test fails immediately with a clear expected-vs-actual comparison. This deterministic expectation is the key advantage of fixtures: you know exactly what the correct output should be, so any deviation is definitively a bug. In production data, 'expected' is a range; in fixture data, 'expected' is an exact number.
Fixtures vs Production Data

rows.left: Fast: milliseconds to query 100–1,000 rows
rows.right: Slow: seconds to minutes to query millions of rows

rows.left: Deterministic: exact expected results known in advance
rows.right: Non-deterministic: expected results are ranges, not exact numbers

rows.left: Artificial: may miss edge cases present only in real data
rows.right: Realistic: includes real-world edge cases and data quality issues

rows.left: Best for: development, CTE-level tests, refactor validation
rows.right: Best for: integration tests, statistical tests, reconciliation

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use both. Fixtures for fast development-time testing (run in seconds, deterministic expectations, CTE-level granularity). Production data for integration testing in CI and post-deployment validation (realistic scale, statistical tests, cross-layer reconciliation). At HydroAlpes, developers run fixture tests locally before pushing code. The CI pipeline runs fixture tests first (fast gate — fails in seconds if the query is broken), then integration tests against a staging copy of production data (slow gate — fails in minutes if the query is subtly wrong at scale).

Tests that run only when an engineer remembers to run them are not tests — they are suggestions. The billing refactor bug from lesson 1 was not caught because nobody ran the assertions after the change. The tests existed; they simply were not executed. CI (Continuous Integration) integration transforms SQL tests from manual suggestions into automated gates: every push to the repository triggers the test suite, and the PR cannot be merged until all tests pass. This lesson covers the technical implementation of CI-integrated SQL testing using GitHub Actions and PostgreSQL service containers.
The CI Pipeline Architecture

The CI pipeline for SQL testing has four stages. Stage 1 (Setup): spin up a PostgreSQL service container with the correct version and extensions. Stage 2 (Schema): run migration scripts to create the schema in the empty database. Stage 3 (Seed): load test fixtures into the database. Stage 4 (Test): execute every assertion query, schema contract test, and regression test; fail the pipeline if any test returns rows. The entire pipeline runs in under 60 seconds for a typical HydroAlpes module because it operates on fixture data (hundreds of rows, not millions). Failed tests produce the violating rows as pipeline output, making the failure cause immediately visible in the CI logs.

# .github/workflows/sql-tests.yml (simplified)
name: SQL Tests
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: hydroalpes_test
          POSTGRES_USER: test
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run migrations
        run: psql -f migrations/*.sql
        env:
          PGHOST: localhost
          PGUSER: test
          PGPASSWORD: test
          PGDATABASE: hydroalpes_test
      - name: Load fixtures
        run: psql -f tests/fixtures/*.sql
      - name: Run assertion tests
        run: |
          for f in tests/assertions/*.sql; do
            result=$(psql -t -f "$f" | head -1)
            if [ -n "$result" ]; then
              echo "FAIL: $f"
              psql -f "$f"
              exit 1
            fi
          done

The GitHub Actions workflow spins up a PostgreSQL 16 container, runs migrations to create the schema, loads fixtures, and executes every .sql file in the tests/assertions directory. The psql -t flag outputs only data rows (no headers or footers). If the output is non-empty (the assertion returned violating rows), the test fails and the full violation output is displayed. The exit 1 causes the CI pipeline to fail, blocking the PR from being merged. This shell-based approach is intentionally simple — no test framework, no special tooling, just psql and bash. At HydroAlpes, this simplicity means any engineer who can write a SQL assertion can add it to the test suite without learning a framework.
Test Execution Order

The CI pipeline executes tests in a specific order that respects dependencies between test categories. Schema contract tests run first — if the schema is wrong, data tests will produce confusing errors. Then assertion queries run against the fixture data — if individual data rules are violated, statistical tests may produce misleading results. Then referential integrity tests run — verifying cross-table relationships. Finally, regression tests run against a golden baseline stored in the test fixtures. If any stage fails, subsequent stages are skipped because their results would be unreliable. At HydroAlpes, the four-stage ordering is encoded in the CI workflow as sequential steps with fail-fast behavior.

blocks.title: Tests as PR gates
blocks.content: The CI test suite is the automated code reviewer. It catches the bugs that human reviewers miss: boundary conditions, fan-out from dimension duplicates, schema drift from migration errors, and aggregate discrepancies from filter changes. At HydroAlpes, the CI gate has caught 23 bugs in 6 months that passed human code review — including 4 that would have affected billing accuracy. The 60-second CI run is the highest-value investment in the pipeline's quality assurance.

This module taught five categories of SQL tests: assertion queries for data rule verification, schema contract tests for structural validation, statistical tests for aggregate-level anomaly detection, referential integrity tests for cross-table relationship verification, and regression tests for refactor safety. Each category catches a different class of bug, and a complete testing framework includes all five working together in a defined execution order. This final lesson assembles these categories into the complete testing framework that HydroAlpes operates in production — the directory structure, the execution order, the CI integration, and the team's testing standards that ensure every pipeline change is validated before it reaches consumers.
The Framework Structure

rows: Schema contracts
Before every pipeline run (CI + production)
Missing columns, wrong types, dropped constraints
tests/schema/*.sql
Assertion queries
After every pipeline load (CI + production)
NULLs, negatives, duplicates, invalid values
tests/assertions/*.sql
Referential integrity
After every pipeline load (production only)
Orphan rows, dangling references, cross-layer discrepancies
tests/integrity/*.sql
Statistical tests
After every pipeline load (production only)
Aggregate anomalies, distribution shifts, trend breaks
tests/statistics/*.sql
Regression tests
On every PR (CI only)
Unintended output changes from refactors
tests/regression/*.sql

blocks.content: Coverage Standards

HydroAlpes' testing standards specify minimum coverage for each table in the pipeline. Every Silver table must have: at least one assertion per NOT NULL column, a duplicate key check on the primary key, a row count range check, and referential integrity checks for every foreign key relationship. Every Gold table must additionally have: a cross-layer reconciliation check against Silver (using EXCEPT), and at least one statistical comparison against the prior run. The standards are documented in the team's engineering handbook and verified during quarterly test coverage audits. New tables added to the pipeline must include their tests in the same PR that creates the table — the CI gate rejects PRs that add tables without corresponding test files.

The testing framework is not a one-time investment — it is a living system that evolves alongside the pipeline. When a bug reaches production, the post-incident review asks: 'What test would have caught this?' The answer becomes a new assertion added to the test suite. After the billing refactor incident, the team added a week-over-week statistical test for total consumption. After the fan-out incident from Module 2, the team added a row-count preservation test for every join CTE. Each incident makes the test suite stronger, and the cumulative effect is a pipeline that becomes more reliable over time rather than accumulating technical debt.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 7, you will learn to deploy the pipeline and its tests safely across environments. Migration scripts, version tracking, environment promotion, and rollback strategies ensure that schema changes and query refactors move from development through staging to production without the ad-hoc ALTER TABLE commands that caused the billing incident. The testing framework from this module runs in every environment — catching bugs in development (CI), validating in staging (integration tests), and monitoring in production (daily assertions).

modules.title: Deployment and CI/CD
modules.description: Master SQL deployment with migration scripts, version tracking, environment promotion, schema change coordination across teams, and rollback strategies.

blocks.content: On a Tuesday afternoon, Markus ran ALTER TABLE silver.meters RENAME COLUMN customer_type TO segment directly in the production database. The change was requested by the analytics team, who wanted the column name to match their dashboards. The ALTER TABLE took 200 milliseconds. Within 3 minutes, the billing pipeline failed with 'column customer_type does not exist.' Within 5 minutes, 4 of the 8 Gold-layer views failed because they referenced customer_type. Within 10 minutes, the analytics team's dashboards — the ones that prompted the change — went blank because the views they queried were broken. Markus reversed the change with another ALTER TABLE, but the billing pipeline had already written a partial Gold dataset. The partial data was served to 3 dashboard consumers before it was detected and rolled back. The total impact: 45 minutes of dashboard downtime, one incorrect billing run, and a postmortem that produced HydroAlpes' first deployment rule: never modify production schema directly.
The Ad-Hoc Problem

Ad-hoc schema changes — ALTER TABLE, CREATE VIEW, DROP COLUMN executed directly in production — have three fundamental problems. First, they are not recorded: there is no history of what changed, when, or why. When a downstream query breaks a week later, tracing the cause requires detective work through pg_stat_activity logs and team Slack messages. Second, they are not tested: the change is applied without first running the test suite from Module 6, so broken views and failed assertions are discovered in production rather than in CI. Third, they are not coordinated: Markus's column rename affected 8 views and 3 pipelines, none of which were updated before the rename. Managed deployment — using versioned migration scripts, CI validation, and environment promotion — solves all three problems.
What Managed Deployment Provides

A managed deployment process treats SQL schema changes exactly like application code changes: they are written in files, version-controlled in Git, tested in CI, promoted through environments (development → staging → production), and rolled back if they fail. Every change has an author, a timestamp, a description, and a review trail. The test suite from Module 6 runs automatically before any migration reaches production. Downstream consumers are notified through a change coordination process that gives them time to update their queries before the change takes effect. This module teaches the tooling, the processes, and the team practices that make SQL deployment safe and predictable.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: HydroAlpes' deployment rule after the incident: every schema change is a versioned migration script. No direct ALTER TABLE, CREATE VIEW, or DROP COLUMN in production. The migration is written locally, reviewed in a PR, tested in CI, validated in staging, and applied to production by an automated deployment pipeline. The pipeline runs the Module 6 test suite after each migration. If any test fails, the migration is rolled back before the pipeline serves the new schema to consumers.

A migration script is a versioned SQL file that describes one atomic schema change. Each script has a version number (V001, V002), a description (create_silver_schema, add_loaded_at_column), and contains the SQL statements that implement the change. Migrations are sequential: V001 runs before V002, which runs before V003. They are idempotent when combined with a migration tool: running V001–V010 on a fresh database produces the same result as running V001–V005 on an existing database that already has V001–V005 applied. The migration tool tracks which versions have been applied and skips them on subsequent runs. This makes migrations safe to rerun after a failure — the tool picks up where it left off.
The Migration File Convention

-- File: migrations/V001__create_silver_schema.sql
-- Author: Lena Hartmann
-- Date: 2024-06-15
-- Description: Create the Silver schema and core tables

CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS silver;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.regions (
    region_id    VARCHAR(30)   PRIMARY KEY,
    name         VARCHAR(100)  NOT NULL,
    country      VARCHAR(5)    NOT NULL CHECK (country IN ('CH', 'AT'))
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS silver.meters (
    meter_id          VARCHAR(20)   PRIMARY KEY,
    region            VARCHAR(30)   NOT NULL REFERENCES silver.regions (region_id),
    customer_type     VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL,
    installation_date DATE          NOT NULL,
    is_active         BOOLEAN       DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- File: migrations/V007__add_loaded_at_to_meter_readings.sql
-- Author: Markus Weber
-- Date: 2025-01-10
-- Description: Add loaded_at column for temporal query support (Module 4, lesson 8)

ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;
