The naming convention V{number}__{description}.sql uses a version number (V001, V007) for ordering and a double-underscore separator before the description. The description is a human-readable summary of the change. The version number must be unique and sequential — the migration tool rejects duplicate version numbers and applies migrations in version order. The header comment block records the author, date, and rationale for the change. This header is not parsed by the migration tool but is invaluable during incident investigation: when V007 causes a problem, the header tells you who wrote it, when, and why.
The Up/Down Pattern

Each migration has a corresponding down migration that reverses the change. The up migration (V007) adds the column; the down migration (U007) removes it. If V007 causes a problem in production, applying U007 reverts the schema to its pre-V007 state. Not every change is reversible — a DROP COLUMN up migration cannot be reversed because the data is lost. For irreversible changes, the down migration either raises an error ('this migration cannot be reversed') or recreates the column with NULL values (accepting data loss). At HydroAlpes, every migration must have a down migration. Irreversible migrations require explicit approval from Lena, documented in the PR with a justification for why data loss is acceptable.

blocks.title: Migrations are append-only
blocks.content: Once a migration has been applied to any environment (including staging), it must never be modified. If V007 has a bug, do not edit V007 — write a new migration V008 that corrects the problem. Editing an applied migration causes the migration tool's checksum verification to fail, which blocks all subsequent migrations. The append-only principle ensures that every environment's migration history is consistent: the same sequence of files produces the same schema, regardless of when each file was applied.

Migration scripts define the changes. A migration tool applies them: it reads the migration files, determines which have already been applied (by checking a version tracking table), applies the pending migrations in order, and records each applied migration's version and checksum. The tool makes migrations idempotent (safe to rerun), sequential (applied in version order), and verifiable (checksums detect tampering). The major migration tools — Flyway, Liquibase, Alembic, and sqitch — differ in their configuration style and language support, but they all solve the same core problem: tracking which schema changes have been applied to each database.
Flyway

# Flyway configuration (flyway.conf)
flyway.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/hydroalpes
flyway.user=pipeline_user
flyway.password=${FLYWAY_PASSWORD}
flyway.locations=filesystem:./migrations
flyway.schemas=silver,gold,staging
flyway.baselineOnMigrate=true

# Apply pending migrations
$ flyway migrate
Flyway Community Edition 10.x
Schema "silver" is up to date. No migration necessary.
Successfully applied 2 migrations (V007, V008)

# Check migration status
$ flyway info
+---------+-------------------------------------------+----------+
| Version | Description                               | State    |
+---------+-------------------------------------------+----------+
| 001     | create silver schema                      | Success  |
| 002     | create meter readings table               | Success  |
| ...     | ...                                       | Success  |
| 007     | add loaded at to meter readings            | Success  |
| 008     | add quality flag column                    | Pending  |
+---------+-------------------------------------------+----------+

Flyway reads .sql files from the migrations directory, checks the flyway_schema_history table for already-applied versions, and applies pending migrations in order. The flyway_schema_history table records each migration's version, description, checksum, execution time, and success status. If a migration's file changes after it was applied (checksum mismatch), Flyway refuses to proceed — preventing the 'modified applied migration' problem. At HydroAlpes, Flyway was chosen for its simplicity: it is a single binary that reads SQL files and applies them. No XML configuration, no Java code, no framework dependencies. The pipeline calls flyway migrate as a single command in the deployment script.
The Version Tracking Table

-- Flyway's version tracking table (created automatically)
SELECT installed_rank, version, description, checksum, installed_on, success
FROM   flyway_schema_history
ORDER BY installed_rank;

The version tracking table is the migration tool's memory: it knows which migrations have been applied, when, and what their checksums were. This table must be included in database backups — losing it means the tool cannot determine the current schema state and refuses to run any migrations. At HydroAlpes, the flyway_schema_history table is treated as infrastructure: it is backed up with the database, never modified manually, and monitored for consistency across environments. If development has migrations V001–V010 and production only has V001–V007, the difference tells you exactly which migrations are pending for the next production deployment.

blocks.title: The tool enforces the process
blocks.content: Without a migration tool, deployment discipline depends on human compliance — and humans forget, skip steps, and make mistakes under time pressure. The migration tool enforces the process mechanically: it applies migrations in order, skips already-applied versions, verifies checksums, and refuses to proceed when something is wrong. At HydroAlpes, the deployment pipeline calls flyway migrate, and the tool ensures correctness. No human decision-making is required during the deployment itself — only during the migration authoring and review process.

A migration script changes the schema. But the schema is not consumed only by the pipeline — it is consumed by 8 Gold-layer views, 3 dashboard queries maintained by the analytics team, 2 ML feature pipelines maintained by the ML team, and the billing export maintained by the operations team. A column rename in Silver breaks every consumer that references the old name. A type change in Gold may silently alter aggregation behavior in downstream queries. Schema changes are not just DDL operations — they are coordination events that affect every consumer. This lesson covers the process that ensures consumers are ready before the change is applied.
The Impact Analysis

-- Find all objects that reference the column being changed
-- Step 1: Find views that reference the column
SELECT v.table_schema, v.table_name, v.view_definition
FROM   information_schema.views AS v
WHERE  v.view_definition LIKE '%customer_type%'
  AND  v.table_schema IN ('silver', 'gold');

-- Step 2: Find functions that reference the column
SELECT p.proname, p.prosrc
FROM   pg_proc AS p
  JOIN pg_namespace AS n ON p.pronamespace = n.oid
WHERE  p.prosrc LIKE '%customer_type%'
  AND  n.nspname IN ('silver', 'gold', 'hydroalpes');

Before writing the migration, the engineer runs an impact analysis: which views, functions, and queries reference the column being changed? The information_schema.views catalog contains the definition of every view, searchable with LIKE. The pg_proc catalog contains the source code of every function. At HydroAlpes, the impact analysis also checks the team's Git repository for SQL files that reference the column — including pipeline queries, test assertions, and dashboard queries maintained by other teams. The impact analysis produces a list of affected objects and their owning teams, which becomes the coordination checklist for the change.
The Deprecation Protocol

HydroAlpes uses a deprecation protocol for breaking changes — changes that rename, remove, or alter the type of an existing column. The protocol has four stages. Stage 1 (Announce): the engineer sends a change notice to all affected teams with the planned change, the impact analysis, and the proposed timeline. Stage 2 (Add): the new column is added alongside the old one (if renaming) or the new type is added as a parallel column (if changing types). Both old and new columns coexist. Stage 3 (Migrate): consumers update their queries to reference the new column. The pipeline populates both old and new columns during this transition period. Stage 4 (Remove): after all consumers have migrated, the old column is dropped. The minimum transition period is 2 weeks — enough for all three teams to update their queries and for the test suite to validate the changes.
The Add-Migrate-Remove Pattern

The add-migrate-remove pattern ensures zero downtime: at no point is any consumer broken because the column it references always exists. During the transition, the pipeline populates both customer_type and segment with the same value. Consumers migrate at their own pace within the 2-week window. The V012 migration that drops the old column is only written and deployed after all consumers have confirmed their migration is complete and the test suite passes without any reference to customer_type. This process is slower than a direct rename — but it produced zero incidents, compared to the 45-minute outage from Markus's direct ALTER TABLE.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Additive changes (new columns, new tables) can be deployed immediately because they break nothing. Breaking changes (renames, drops, type changes) must follow the deprecation protocol. At HydroAlpes, the PR template includes a checkbox: 'This migration contains a breaking change and requires a 2-week deprecation period.' If checked, the PR must include the change notice text, the impact analysis, and the planned timeline. The migration is not merged until all affected teams have acknowledged the notice.

HydroAlpes operates three database environments: development (local PostgreSQL on each engineer's machine), staging (a shared PostgreSQL instance with a copy of production data structure and a subset of production data), and production (the live system that serves dashboards, billing, and ML pipelines). Migrations flow through these environments in order: written and tested locally, applied to staging for integration testing, and finally applied to production. No migration reaches production without first passing through staging. This environment promotion path catches bugs at each stage before they affect the next — and the cost of catching a bug in staging (minutes of debugging) is orders of magnitude less than catching it in production (hours of incident response).
The Three Environments

rows: Development
Write and test migrations locally; run fixture-based tests
Fixtures only (100 meters, 700 readings) — fast, deterministic
Individual engineers
Staging
Integration testing with realistic data; validate cross-team impact
Subset of production data (100K meters, recent 3 months) — realistic, moderate size
Engineering team, CI pipeline
Production
Serve live data to consumers; run daily pipeline
Full dataset (1.2M meters, 3+ years history) — complete, authoritative
All teams and external consumers

blocks.content: The Promotion Pipeline

The promotion pipeline has five steps. Step 1 (Local): the engineer writes the migration and runs flyway migrate on their local development database. The Module 6 test suite runs against fixtures. Step 2 (PR): the migration is committed to a Git branch and a pull request is created. The CI pipeline spins up a fresh PostgreSQL container, runs all migrations from V001 to the new one, loads fixtures, and runs all tests. Step 3 (Review): a team member reviews the migration SQL, the EXPLAIN output for any new or modified queries, and the test results. Step 4 (Staging): after PR approval, the migration is merged to the main branch. A staging deployment pipeline applies the migration to the staging database and runs integration tests against the realistic staging data. Step 5 (Production): after staging passes, a production deployment pipeline applies the migration to production during the scheduled maintenance window (daily at 04:00 UTC, before the 05:30 pipeline run).
Configuration Differences Between Environments

The migration SQL is identical across all environments — the same V007 file runs in development, staging, and production. What differs is the configuration: connection strings, credentials, work_mem settings, and parallel query limits. These differences are managed through environment variables, not through different migration files. At HydroAlpes, the Flyway configuration uses ${FLYWAY_URL}, ${FLYWAY_USER}, and ${FLYWAY_PASSWORD} placeholders that resolve differently in each environment. The CI pipeline sets these to the ephemeral test database. The staging pipeline sets them to the staging instance. The production pipeline sets them to the production instance. The migration logic is environment-agnostic; only the connection target changes.

blocks.title: Never skip staging
blocks.content: The staging environment exists to catch bugs that fixture-based CI tests miss — bugs that only manifest with realistic data distributions, realistic data volumes, or realistic concurrent access patterns. Skipping staging (deploying directly from CI to production) saves 30 minutes of staging validation but risks the kind of production incident that costs hours or days. At HydroAlpes, the production deployment pipeline physically requires a 'staging validation passed' flag before it will execute. The flag is set automatically by the staging pipeline on success and cannot be set manually.

Despite testing, review, and staging validation, migrations sometimes fail in production. A migration that adds a NOT NULL column fails if existing rows have NULL values that the staging subset did not include. A migration that creates an index on a large table may time out during the maintenance window. A migration that drops a column may discover a previously unknown consumer that still references it. When a migration fails, the schema is in an intermediate state — some statements succeeded, others did not. The rollback strategy determines how to restore the schema to a known-good state. This lesson covers three rollback approaches, each appropriate for different failure scenarios.
Down Migration Rollback

The simplest rollback: apply the down migration (the U-prefixed file) that reverses the failed up migration. If V009 added a column, U009 drops it. If V009 created an index, U009 drops the index. Down migration rollback is fast, precise, and returns the schema to exactly its pre-migration state. The limitation: down migrations cannot reverse data-destructive operations. If V009 dropped a column, U009 cannot restore the lost data. If V009 updated 1.2 million rows, U009 cannot reconstruct the original values. At HydroAlpes, down migrations are the first-choice rollback for DDL changes (column adds, index creates, constraint modifications) where no data loss occurred.
Point-in-Time Recovery

When a migration causes data loss or data corruption that down migrations cannot reverse, point-in-time recovery (PITR) restores the entire database to a specific timestamp before the migration was applied. PostgreSQL's Write-Ahead Log (WAL) archiving enables PITR: the database continuously writes a log of every change, and the recovery process replays the log up to the specified timestamp. PITR is the nuclear option — it restores everything, not just the affected table — and it requires that WAL archiving is configured and that backups are current. At HydroAlpes, WAL archiving runs continuously, and the DBA performs a PITR test quarterly to verify that the recovery process works under time pressure.
Forward-Fix

-- Forward-fix: instead of rolling back V009, write V010 to correct the problem
-- This is appropriate when rolling back would cause more disruption than fixing

-- V009 (the problematic migration): renamed customer_type to segment
-- V010 (the forward-fix): add customer_type back as an alias view
-- File: migrations/V010__fix_customer_type_alias.sql
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW silver.meters_compat AS
SELECT *, segment AS customer_type
FROM   silver.meters;

A forward-fix writes a new migration that corrects the problem introduced by the previous migration, without reverting it. If V009 renamed customer_type to segment and downstream consumers are broken, V010 creates a compatibility view that exposes both names. The broken consumers use the view until they are updated to reference the new column name. Forward-fix is appropriate when the migration has been partially consumed by downstream systems (rolling back would break them too), when the migration contains irreversible changes (data modifications), or when the fix is simpler than the rollback. At HydroAlpes, the rollback decision tree is: try down migration first, forward-fix if down migration is impossible or more disruptive, and PITR only as a last resort for data corruption.
The Rollback Decision

rows: DDL change (add/drop column, create index) with no data modification
Down migration
Clean reversal, no data impact
Migration partially consumed by downstream systems
Forward-fix (new corrective migration)
Rolling back would break consumers that already adapted
DML change (UPDATE, DELETE) that corrupted data
Point-in-time recovery
Original data cannot be reconstructed from down migration
Migration timed out mid-execution
Down migration (if partial) or retry with longer timeout
Partial DDL changes are often auto-rolled back by transaction failure

blocks.title: The best rollback is the one you never need
blocks.content: Every rollback scenario in this lesson could have been prevented by better testing, review, or staging validation. The NOT NULL failure is caught by running the migration against realistic staging data. The timeout is caught by running EXPLAIN on large-table operations in staging. The unknown consumer is caught by the impact analysis from lesson 4. Rollback strategies are the safety net — but the goal is to never need them. At HydroAlpes, the rollback has been invoked twice in 14 months. Both times were forward-fixes for edge cases that staging data did not cover.

Tables are versioned by migration scripts: each CREATE TABLE or ALTER TABLE lives in a migration file with a version number. Views and functions need the same treatment, but they have a different lifecycle: a view's definition can be replaced in place with CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW, which means the 'migration' for a view is often a full replacement rather than an incremental change. This difference makes view versioning both simpler (no ALTER VIEW for most changes) and more complex (the entire definition must be included in the migration, not just the delta). This lesson covers the patterns for managing views and functions as versioned, deployable artifacts.
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW in Migrations

-- File: migrations/V011__update_enriched_readings_view.sql
-- Description: Add daily_mwh column to the enriched_readings view

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW silver.enriched_readings AS
SELECT mr.meter_id,
       mr.daily_kw,
       mr.daily_kw * 0.001 AS daily_mwh,   -- new column
       mr.date,
       m.customer_type,
       s.name          AS substation_name,
       s.capacity_mw,
       r.name          AS region_name,
       r.country
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  LEFT JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.substations AS s ON m.substation_id = s.substation_id
  LEFT JOIN silver.regions AS r ON m.region = r.region_id;

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW updates the view definition in place without dropping it. Existing queries that reference the view continue to work — they automatically see the new definition on their next execution. Adding a column (daily_mwh) is a non-breaking change because existing queries that do not reference the new column are unaffected. Removing or renaming a column is a breaking change that requires the deprecation protocol from lesson 4. At HydroAlpes, view updates are the most common migration type — more frequent than table changes — because views encapsulate join logic that evolves as the data model grows.
View Dependencies

When view A references view B, changing view B can break view A. If enriched_readings is modified to rename region_name to region_display_name, any view that selects region_name from enriched_readings will fail. The dependency chain can be deep: view C references view B, which references view A, which references the base table. A change at any level can cascade. At HydroAlpes, view dependencies are mapped using pg_depend: a query that traces the dependency chain from each view to its base tables, producing a dependency graph that the team reviews before modifying any view. Views at the bottom of the dependency chain (closest to base tables) are the most dangerous to modify because they affect the most downstream views.

-- Find all views that depend on a specific view
SELECT DISTINCT dependent_view.relname AS dependent_view_name
FROM   pg_depend AS d
  JOIN pg_rewrite AS r ON d.objid = r.oid
  JOIN pg_class AS dependent_view ON r.ev_class = dependent_view.oid
  JOIN pg_class AS source_view ON d.refobjid = source_view.oid
WHERE  source_view.relname = 'enriched_readings'
  AND  dependent_view.relname <> 'enriched_readings';

This query finds all views that reference enriched_readings. Before modifying that view, the engineer runs this query and includes the results in the PR description as part of the impact analysis. If the dependent view list is empty, the change is safe to proceed. If views appear, those views must be tested with the new definition and may need their own migration updates. At HydroAlpes, the dependency query is wrapped in a helper function: SELECT * FROM hydroalpes.view_dependencies('enriched_readings') — making it easy to run as part of the pre-migration checklist.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Keep view dependency chains shallow — no more than 3 levels deep. A chain of view A → view B → view C → base table means that changing the base table or view C requires verifying compatibility with views B and A. Each additional level adds review overhead and cascade risk. At HydroAlpes, the maximum allowed view depth is 3. Views that would create a 4th level are replaced with materialized views or CTEs in the consuming query.

Module 6 (lesson 9) covered CI integration for SQL tests — the automated execution of assertion queries and schema contracts on every pull request. This lesson extends that CI pipeline into a complete CI/CD pipeline that includes migration validation, multi-category test execution, staging deployment with integration testing, and production deployment with post-deploy validation. The CI side validates that the migration is syntactically correct, creates the expected schema, and passes all tests against fixture data. The CD side deploys the validated migration through staging and production in a controlled, gated sequence. Together, the CI/CD pipeline replaces manual deployment with an automated, auditable, and repeatable process.
The Complete Pipeline

The CI/CD pipeline has six stages. Stage 1 (Lint): validate SQL syntax using pg_format or sqlfluff. Stage 2 (Fresh build): spin up a clean PostgreSQL container, run all migrations from V001 to the latest, and verify the final schema. Stage 3 (Fixture tests): load test fixtures and run the Module 6 test suite — schema contracts, assertions, referential integrity, and regression tests. Stage 4 (Staging deploy): apply pending migrations to the staging database and run integration tests against realistic data. Stage 5 (Staging validation): run statistical tests and cross-layer reconciliation against staging data. Stage 6 (Production deploy): apply pending migrations to production during the maintenance window. Each stage gates the next — a failure at any stage prevents progression.

# Simplified CI/CD pipeline stages
# .github/workflows/sql-deploy.yml

# Stages 1-3: CI (on every push/PR)
- Lint SQL files (sqlfluff lint migrations/)
- Fresh-build test (flyway migrate on empty DB → verify schema)
- Fixture tests (load fixtures → run tests/assertions/*.sql)

# Stage 4-5: Staging (on merge to main)
- Staging deploy (flyway migrate on staging DB)
- Staging validation (run tests/integration/*.sql against staging)

# Stage 6: Production (manual approval + scheduled window)
- Production deploy (flyway migrate on production DB)
- Post-deploy validation (run tests/assertions/*.sql against production)
- Notify consumers (Slack message to #data-platform channel)

Stages 1–3 run automatically on every push and every pull request — they are the fast feedback loop that catches syntax errors, schema mismatches, and test failures within seconds. Stages 4–5 run automatically when a PR is merged to main — they are the integration validation that catches realistic-data issues within minutes. Stage 6 requires manual approval (a deployment engineer clicks 'Deploy to production' after verifying staging results) and runs during the scheduled maintenance window. The manual approval gate exists because production deployment is irreversible (data modifications) and high-impact (downstream consumers are affected). At HydroAlpes, the manual approver is always a senior engineer — Lena or the on-call lead.
Post-Deploy Validation

After the production deployment, the pipeline runs a subset of the test suite against production data. This is not the full fixture-based suite (which uses test data, not production data) but a targeted set of assertions: schema contract tests (verify the migration produced the expected schema), row count checks on affected tables (verify no data was lost or duplicated), and the daily assertion suite (verify that the pipeline output still satisfies all business rules). If any post-deploy test fails, the pipeline automatically invokes the rollback strategy: down migration for DDL changes, alerting for DML changes. At HydroAlpes, post-deploy validation adds approximately 90 seconds to the deployment and has caught two production issues in 6 months that staging validation missed.

blocks.title: Automation reduces risk, not oversight
blocks.content: The CI/CD pipeline automates the mechanical steps of deployment — running migrations, executing tests, promoting between environments. It does not automate the judgment calls: should this breaking change be deployed now or after the quarter-end billing cycle? Is the staging validation sufficient for this migration, or should we run additional manual checks? The pipeline provides the infrastructure for safe deployment. The team provides the judgment for when and how to use it.

Standard deployment applies migrations directly to the production database. During the migration window, the database is in a transitional state — some tables have the new schema, some have the old. Queries that run during this window may encounter errors if they reference columns that have been renamed or dropped. For most HydroAlpes migrations, the window is less than 1 second (a single ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN) and the risk is negligible. But for major schema changes — table reorganizations, partition migrations, index rebuilds on billion-row tables — the window can be minutes, and the risk of consumer disruption is real. Blue-green and canary deployment strategies eliminate or reduce this risk.
Blue-Green Schema Deployment

Blue-green deployment maintains two parallel schemas: blue (the current production schema) and green (the new schema with the migration applied). Consumers query a routing view or schema alias that points to blue. The deployment process applies the migration to green, runs tests against green, and if everything passes, switches the routing to point to green. The switch is instantaneous — a single ALTER VIEW or SET search_path command. If the switch fails or tests fail, green is discarded and blue remains untouched. The trade-off: maintaining two schemas requires double the storage for the affected tables, and the routing mechanism adds a layer of indirection that can complicate debugging.

-- Blue-green deployment using schema aliasing
-- Current state: consumers query via the 'live' schema alias
-- The 'live' schema is currently an alias for 'blue'

-- Step 1: Apply migration to green schema
SET search_path = green;
-- Run flyway migrate on green schema

-- Step 2: Validate green schema
-- Run all tests against green schema

-- Step 3: Switch live to green (instantaneous)
ALTER SCHEMA blue RENAME TO blue_old;
ALTER SCHEMA green RENAME TO blue;
-- Now 'blue' has the new schema, and consumers see it immediately

-- Step 4: If successful, drop old schema after grace period
-- DROP SCHEMA blue_old CASCADE;  -- after 24 hours of monitoring

Standard deployment (direct migration) is appropriate for the vast majority of changes: additive DDL such as new columns, new tables, and new views, as well as small DML corrections and constraint modifications. Blue-green is appropriate for major schema reorganizations that affect multiple tables simultaneously and require zero-downtime switching. Canary is appropriate for performance-sensitive changes like new indexes and partition restructuring where the impact on query performance needs real-world validation before full rollout. At HydroAlpes, approximately 90% of deployments are standard, 8% are canary for index changes on large tables, and 2% are blue-green for major reorganizations that occur once or twice per year.
Canary Deployment

Canary deployment applies the migration to a subset of the data or a single replica before applying it to the full production system. HydroAlpes' canary approach: apply the migration to a read replica, route 10% of analytical queries to the replica, monitor for errors and performance degradation for 4 hours, and if no issues are detected, apply the migration to the primary. This approach is most valuable for index changes and performance-sensitive migrations: the canary period reveals whether the new index actually improves query performance or whether it introduces unexpected lock contention. At HydroAlpes, canary deployment is reserved for migrations that affect tables with more than 100 million rows or indexes on high-traffic columns.
When to Use Each Strategy

Standard deployment (direct migration) is appropriate for the vast majority of changes: additive DDL (new columns, new tables, new views), small DML corrections, and constraint modifications. Blue-green is appropriate for major schema reorganizations that affect multiple tables simultaneously and require zero-downtime switching. Canary is appropriate for performance-sensitive changes (new indexes, partition restructuring) where the impact on query performance needs real-world validation. At HydroAlpes, approximately 90% of deployments are standard, 8% are canary (index changes on large tables), and 2% are blue-green (major schema reorganizations that occur once or twice per year).

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: The deployment strategy should be documented in the migration PR: 'This migration will be deployed using standard/canary/blue-green deployment.' The reviewer verifies that the strategy matches the migration's risk profile. A standard deployment for a 100-million-row index rebuild is a risk mismatch that the reviewer should flag. The strategy choice is not a deployment-time decision — it is a design-time decision made during migration authoring.

This module taught the complete deployment lifecycle for production SQL: versioned migration scripts as the unit of change, Flyway as the migration tool, schema change coordination with the deprecation protocol, environment promotion from development through staging to production, rollback strategies for when migrations fail, view and function versioning with dependency management, CI/CD pipelines that automate validation and deployment, and blue-green and canary strategies for high-risk changes. This final lesson codifies these practices into HydroAlpes' deployment standards — the rules that govern every schema change across all three teams.
The Deployment Standards

rows: No direct production changes
Every schema change is a versioned migration script
Production database user has no ALTER/DROP privileges
Every migration has a down script
Irreversible migrations require explicit approval
CI rejects migrations without a corresponding U-file
Breaking changes follow deprecation protocol
2-week transition period with parallel columns
PR template checkbox triggers coordination workflow
All migrations pass CI before merge
Fresh-build test + fixture tests must pass
GitHub branch protection requires CI pass
Staging validation before production
Integration tests against realistic data
Production deploy blocked until staging flag is set
Post-deploy validation in production
Schema contracts + row count checks after deployment
Automated in deployment pipeline; failure triggers rollback

blocks.content: The Deployment Calendar

HydroAlpes schedules production deployments on a weekly cadence: every Tuesday at 04:00 UTC. Non-breaking migrations (new columns, new views, new indexes) are batched and deployed together. Breaking migrations are deployed individually with dedicated monitoring during the deployment window. Deployments are frozen during the last week of each quarter (billing close period) and during regulatory filing windows. The deployment calendar is shared across all three teams, and any team can request a freeze extension if a critical pipeline is running. This cadence balances deployment frequency (engineers do not wait more than a week to see their changes in production) with stability (changes are batched and scheduled, not applied ad-hoc).
The Engineering Discipline Phase Complete

Modules 6 and 7 together form the engineering discipline phase. Module 6 taught testing: how to verify that the pipeline produces correct output through assertion queries, schema contracts, statistical tests, referential integrity checks, and regression tests. Module 7 taught deployment: how to move schema changes safely from development through staging to production using versioned migrations, automated CI/CD, and coordinated rollout. Together, these practices ensure that HydroAlpes' SQL pipelines are not only correct and performant (Modules 1–5) but also tested and safely deployable — the two engineering disciplines that separate ad-hoc SQL from production-grade data engineering.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 8, you will shift from engineering discipline to operational performance: indexing strategies for large tables, table partitioning for query acceleration and data retention, materialized views for pre-computed aggregation, statistics maintenance for optimizer accuracy, and the diagnostic workflow for resolving slow queries systematically. The operations phase (Modules 8–10) takes the tested, deployed pipelines from the discipline phase and ensures they run efficiently, reliably, and observably in production.

modules.title: Performance Tuning
modules.description: Master indexing strategies, table partitioning, materialized views, statistics maintenance, and the diagnostic workflow for systematically resolving slow queries.

blocks.content: After the billing pipeline's 12-minute degradation (Module 1, lesson 1), a well-intentioned engineer added six indexes to the meter_readings table: one on date, one on region, one on customer_type, one on meter_id, one on substation_id, and a composite on (date, region). The billing query dropped from 12 minutes to 9 seconds — a dramatic improvement. But the daily Silver pipeline, which inserts 1.2 million rows, slowed from 45 seconds to 3 minutes and 20 seconds. The weekly VACUUM, which maintains table health, went from 8 minutes to 22 minutes. The six indexes tripled the write overhead and consumed 12 GB of additional storage. The billing query needed only one of the six indexes — the composite on (date, region). The other five were unused by any production query, consuming resources for zero benefit.
Diagnose Before Optimizing

The incident illustrates the cardinal rule of performance tuning: diagnose before you optimize. The engineer skipped the diagnostic step (EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify the bottleneck) and jumped to the solution (add indexes). The correct workflow starts with measurement: run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the slow query, identify the specific operator that consumes the most time, determine why it is slow (missing index, stale statistics, hash spill, wrong join strategy), and apply a targeted fix. This diagnostic workflow — measure, identify, understand, fix — prevents the shotgun approach that creates new problems while solving the original one.
The Diagnostic Workflow

Every performance investigation at HydroAlpes follows a five-step workflow. Step 1: reproduce the slowness and measure it (note the current execution time). Step 2: run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) and read the plan bottom-to-top, identifying the operator with the highest actual time. Step 3: diagnose the root cause at that operator (sequential scan on a large table? hash spill? bad row estimate?). Step 4: apply a single targeted fix (create an index, increase work_mem, update statistics). Step 5: re-run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and measure the improvement. If the fix does not reduce execution time by at least 2x, the diagnosis was wrong — go back to step 3. This iterative, measurement-driven approach ensures that every optimization has a measurable impact and that no unnecessary changes accumulate.

-- Step 1-2: Diagnose the slow query
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT mr.region, SUM(mr.daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
WHERE  mr.date >= '2025-01-01' AND mr.date < '2025-02-01'
  AND  mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH'
GROUP BY mr.region;

-- Step 3: Read the plan — identify the bottleneck operator
-- Step 4: Apply ONE targeted fix based on the diagnosis
-- Step 5: Re-run EXPLAIN ANALYZE and compare

The plan reveals the bottleneck. If it is a Seq Scan with 90% of rows filtered, the fix is an index (lessons 2–4). If it is a Hash Join with temp spill, the fix is work_mem (Module 2, lesson 7). If it is a Sort consuming minutes, the fix is an index that provides pre-sorted output (lesson 2) or a materialized view that pre-computes the sort (lesson 6). If the row estimates are wildly wrong, the fix is ANALYZE (lesson 7). Each diagnosis has a specific fix, and each fix addresses one bottleneck. This module teaches the fixes — indexing, partitioning, materialized views, statistics, and query rewriting — organized by the diagnostic signal that indicates when each is needed.

blocks.title: Every optimization has a cost
blocks.content: Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume storage. Partitioning speeds up partition-pruned queries but adds DDL complexity and maintenance overhead. Materialized views speed up reads but require refresh schedules and consume storage. No optimization is free. The diagnostic workflow ensures you pay only the costs that produce measurable benefits — not the costs of unused indexes, unnecessary partitions, or stale materialized views.

Module 1 (lesson 4) introduced the four scan strategies and the role of indexes in enabling index scans. This lesson covers index design: which columns to index, what type of index to create, and how to verify that the optimizer uses the index you created. The B-tree index — PostgreSQL's default and most versatile index type — supports equality lookups (WHERE date = '2025-01-15'), range scans (WHERE date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'), and sorted output (ORDER BY date). It is the right choice for the vast majority of index needs at HydroAlpes. Other index types (GIN for full-text search, BRIN for large sequential tables) exist but are rarely needed in data engineering workloads.
Single-Column Index Design

-- Create a B-tree index on the most commonly filtered column
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_date
  ON silver.meter_readings (date);

-- Verify the index is used by the optimizer
EXPLAIN
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15';

-- Expected: Index Scan using idx_readings_date
-- If Seq Scan appears instead: check random_page_cost, statistics, or selectivity

A single-column index on date accelerates every query that filters on date — which at HydroAlpes is virtually every analytical query. The index reduces the scan from 130 million rows (full table) to approximately 1.2 million rows (one day's readings) for an equality filter, or 37.2 million rows (one month) for a range filter. The optimizer chooses the index when the filter selectivity is high enough that the index's random I/O cost is less than a full sequential scan's sequential I/O cost. On SSD storage with random_page_cost = 1.1, the threshold is roughly 30% — filters that select less than 30% of the table benefit from the index.
Which Columns to Index

Not every column needs an index. The decision is driven by query patterns: which columns appear in WHERE clauses, JOIN conditions, and ORDER BY clauses of production queries? At HydroAlpes, the index candidates are identified by reviewing the query library: date (appears in every query's WHERE clause), meter_id (appears in every JOIN condition), region (appears in filtered dashboard queries), and substation_id (appears in the substation-level reports). Columns that never appear in filters (daily_kw, loaded_at) do not need indexes because no query would use them. The rule: index columns that filter, join, or sort in production queries. Do not index columns that are only selected or aggregated.
Verifying Index Usage

-- Check which indexes exist and how often they are used
SELECT indexrelname AS index_name,
       idx_scan     AS times_used,
       idx_tup_read AS rows_read_from_index,
       pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(indexrelid)) AS index_size
FROM   pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
  AND  relname    = 'meter_readings'
ORDER BY idx_scan DESC;

index_name                   | times_used | rows_read_from_index | index_size
-----------------------------+------------+----------------------+-----------
idx_readings_date            |     12,481 |        1,842,000,000 | 2.8 GB
meter_readings_pkey          |      8,204 |          412,000,000 | 3.2 GB
idx_readings_region          |        342 |           48,000,000 | 1.4 GB
idx_readings_customer_type   |          0 |                    0 | 1.1 GB
idx_readings_substation_id   |          0 |                    0 | 1.6 GB

The pg_stat_user_indexes view shows how often each index has been used since the last statistics reset. idx_readings_date has been used 12,481 times — it is the workhorse index. The primary key index (meter_readings_pkey) has been used 8,204 times for join lookups. idx_readings_region is moderately used (342 times). But idx_readings_customer_type and idx_readings_substation_id have never been used — they consume 2.7 GB of storage and slow down every INSERT for zero benefit. These unused indexes should be dropped. At HydroAlpes, Lena reviews the pg_stat_user_indexes output quarterly and drops indexes with zero or near-zero usage.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: After creating an index, always verify it is used: run EXPLAIN on the queries it is intended to accelerate and check that the plan shows Index Scan or Bitmap Index Scan on the new index. If the plan still shows Seq Scan, the index is not being used — either the filter selectivity is too low, the statistics are stale (run ANALYZE), or the cost parameters do not favor index scans (check random_page_cost). An unused index is not harmless — it consumes storage and slows writes. Verify before declaring victory.

A single-column index on date helps queries that filter on date. A single-column index on region helps queries that filter on region. But the billing query filters on both: WHERE date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01' AND region = 'BERN-NORTH'. Two single-column indexes require the optimizer to either use one index and then filter the remaining condition, or combine both indexes with a bitmap AND operation. A composite index on (date, region) — or (region, date) — directly supports the combined filter, finding the matching rows in a single index traversal. The column order in a composite index dramatically affects which queries it accelerates, and choosing the wrong order can make the index useless for important queries.
The Leftmost Prefix Rule

A composite B-tree index on (A, B, C) supports queries that filter on A, on (A, B), and on (A, B, C) — but not on B alone or (B, C) alone. The index is organized as a tree sorted first by A, then by B within each A value, then by C within each (A, B) pair. A query that filters only on B cannot use the index because B values are scattered across different A subtrees. This is the leftmost prefix rule: the index supports any prefix of its column list, starting from the leftmost column. A query that filters on A and C but not B can use the index for A but must scan all B values within each matching A subtree to find matching C values — partial but not optimal use.
Column Order: Equality Before Range

-- Option 1: (region, date) — equality column first
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_region_date
  ON silver.meter_readings (region, date);

-- Option 2: (date, region) — range column first
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_date_region
  ON silver.meter_readings (date, region);

-- The billing query:
-- WHERE region = 'BERN-NORTH'       (equality)
--   AND date >= '2025-01-01'         (range)
--   AND date <  '2025-02-01'         (range)

The billing query uses an equality filter on region and a range filter on date. With (region, date), the index first narrows to all BERN-NORTH entries (one equality lookup), then scans the date range within BERN-NORTH — a tight, efficient traversal. With (date, region), the index first scans the date range across all regions (31 days × 10 regions = 310 subtree entries), then filters to BERN-NORTH within each date — a wider traversal that reads data for 9 irrelevant regions before discarding them. The principle: place equality columns before range columns in the composite index. Equality narrows to a single subtree; range scans within that subtree. This ordering minimizes the number of index pages read.
Designing for Multiple Query Patterns

A single composite index can serve multiple queries if they share a leftmost prefix. The index (region, date, customer_type) serves: queries that filter on region alone, queries that filter on region + date, and queries that filter on region + date + customer_type. It does not serve queries that filter on date alone or customer_type alone (no leftmost prefix match). At HydroAlpes, the query library analysis reveals three dominant filter patterns: (date) for pipeline loads, (region, date) for regional reports, and (region, date, customer_type) for detailed billing. The composite index (region, date, customer_type) serves all three patterns — the first through a separate single-column index on date, and the second and third through the composite index's leftmost prefixes.

blocks.title: Fewer indexes, broader coverage
blocks.content: A well-designed composite index replaces multiple single-column indexes. The composite (region, date) replaces separate indexes on region and on (region, date) — one index does the work of two. The goal is the minimum number of indexes that cover all production query patterns. At HydroAlpes, meter_readings has three indexes: the primary key (meter_id, date), a single-column index on date (for pipeline date-filtered loads), and a composite (region, date) for regional reports. Three indexes, not six, covering all production queries with lower write overhead and less storage.

Standard indexes cover every row in the table — the index on date contains an entry for each of the 130 million rows. But many queries only need a subset of rows: active meters only (95% of meters, filtering out the 5% that are inactive), recent readings only (the last 90 days, filtering out the 3+ years of history), or anomalous readings only (the 0.1% with daily_kw > 1000). A partial index — an index with a WHERE clause — covers only the rows that match the condition, making it smaller, faster to build, and faster to maintain. A covering index adds extra columns to the index so the database can satisfy the entire query from the index alone, without touching the table. Both are advanced indexing strategies that produce outsized performance gains for specific query patterns.
Partial Indexes

-- Partial index: only index recent readings (last 90 days)
-- The index is ~8% the size of a full index and faster to maintain
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_recent
  ON silver.meter_readings (date, region)
  WHERE date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days';

-- Partial index: only index anomalous readings
-- The data quality dashboard queries this subset frequently
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_anomalous
  ON silver.meter_readings (meter_id, date)
  WHERE daily_kw > 1000 OR daily_kw < 0;

-- Partial index: only index active meters
CREATE INDEX idx_meters_active
  ON silver.meters (region, customer_type)
  WHERE is_active = TRUE;

Each partial index is dramatically smaller than its full-table equivalent. The recent-readings index covers 90 days out of 1,300+ days of data — roughly 7% of the rows. The anomalous-readings index covers less than 0.1% of rows. The active-meters index covers 95% of meters (smaller savings, but the exclusion of inactive meters still reduces the index by 60,000 entries). The optimizer uses a partial index only when the query's WHERE clause matches or implies the index's condition. A query with WHERE date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' matches the 90-day partial index. A query without a date filter does not.
Covering Indexes (Index-Only Scans)

-- Covering index: include non-filter columns so the query can be satisfied
-- entirely from the index without touching the table (Index Only Scan)
CREATE INDEX idx_readings_date_covering
  ON silver.meter_readings (date, region)
  INCLUDE (daily_kw, meter_id);

-- This query can now be answered entirely from the index:
EXPLAIN
SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw, COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id) AS meters
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
GROUP BY region;
-- Plan: Index Only Scan using idx_readings_date_covering

The INCLUDE clause adds daily_kw and meter_id to the index's leaf pages without including them in the sort key. The index is sorted by (date, region) for filtering, and the included columns are stored alongside for retrieval. When a query needs only the indexed and included columns, PostgreSQL can perform an Index Only Scan — reading data entirely from the index without any table access. This eliminates the random I/O of fetching rows from the table, which is the single largest cost in an index scan. At HydroAlpes, the billing summary query dropped from 2.4 seconds (Index Scan with table fetch) to 0.3 seconds (Index Only Scan) after adding the covering index — an 8x improvement from avoiding table I/O.
The Trade-Off

Covering indexes are larger than non-covering indexes because they store additional column data in the leaf pages. The idx_readings_date_covering index is approximately 4.2 GB — larger than the 2.8 GB non-covering index on (date) — because it stores daily_kw and meter_id for every row. The storage cost is justified when the Index Only Scan eliminates table I/O for a frequently-run query. It is not justified when the included columns are rarely needed or when the query already runs fast enough with a regular index scan. At HydroAlpes, covering indexes are created only for the five core analytical queries from Module 3 (lesson 10) — the queries that run hundreds of times per day and whose performance directly affects dashboard responsiveness.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Partial indexes and covering indexes are precision tools, not defaults. Use a partial index when a query consistently filters to a small, well-defined subset of rows (recent data, active entities, anomalous values). Use a covering index when a high-frequency query selects only a few columns and the Index Only Scan eliminates significant table I/O. For general-purpose queries that access many columns or filter on varying conditions, a standard composite index is the right choice.

HydroAlpes' meter_readings table contains 130 million rows spanning 3+ years. The billing query filters to one month (37.2 million rows). The daily pipeline inserts 1.2 million rows. The retention policy requires deleting data older than 3 years. Without partitioning, the billing query scans the entire 130-million-row table and filters to 37.2 million. The daily insert appends to the same monolithic table. The retention delete runs DELETE WHERE date < '2022-01-01', which must scan for and individually remove millions of expired rows. Table partitioning — splitting the table into physically separate sub-tables by date range — addresses all three concerns: the billing query reads only the January partition (37.2 million rows, not 130 million), the daily insert writes to the current month's partition, and the retention policy drops the expired partition entirely (instantaneous, versus minutes of row-by-row deletion).
Declarative Partitioning

-- Create a partitioned table with monthly range partitions
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings (
    meter_id       VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    daily_kw       DOUBLE PRECISION   NOT NULL,
    date           DATE               NOT NULL,
    region         VARCHAR(30)        NOT NULL,
    customer_type  VARCHAR(20)        NOT NULL,
    substation_id  VARCHAR(20),
    loaded_at      TIMESTAMPTZ        DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
) PARTITION BY RANGE (date);

-- Create partitions for each month
CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings_2025_01
  PARTITION OF silver.meter_readings
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-01-01') TO ('2025-02-01');

CREATE TABLE silver.meter_readings_2025_02
  PARTITION OF silver.meter_readings
  FOR VALUES FROM ('2025-02-01') TO ('2025-03-01');

-- Automated partition creation uses the dynamic SQL function from Module 5

The PARTITION BY RANGE (date) clause tells PostgreSQL to route each inserted row to the partition whose range includes the row's date value. A row with date = '2025-01-15' goes to meter_readings_2025_01. A row with date = '2025-02-03' goes to meter_readings_2025_02. Queries against the parent table (silver.meter_readings) automatically query all partitions, but if the WHERE clause filters on date, the optimizer prunes partitions that cannot contain matching rows. WHERE date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01' scans only the January partition — the optimizer eliminates all other partitions without reading them.
Partition Pruning

-- Verify partition pruning in the plan
EXPLAIN
SELECT region, SUM(daily_kw) AS total_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01' AND date < '2025-02-01'
GROUP BY region;

The plan shows a scan on meter_readings_2025_01 only — no other partitions appear. The optimizer pruned all non-January partitions before execution began. The scan now reads 37.2 million rows (one month's partition) instead of 130 million rows (the full table). This is not an index — it is a structural optimization: the data is physically separated into files by month, and the optimizer skips entire files. Partition pruning and indexes are complementary: an index on (region) within the January partition further reduces the scan to 3.7 million rows (one sub-region within one month). At HydroAlpes, partitioned tables have both partition-level pruning (by date) and within-partition indexes (by region).
Data Retention with Partitioning

Dropping a partition is a metadata-only operation — it removes the entire sub-table and its data files in milliseconds, regardless of how many rows it contains. This is dramatically faster than DELETE, which must scan for matching rows, mark them as dead, and eventually VACUUM them. At HydroAlpes, the retention pipeline runs monthly: it drops the partition that is exactly 3 years old and creates the partition for the next month. Both operations complete in under 1 second. Without partitioning, the monthly retention DELETE on 1.2 million expired rows took approximately 4 minutes and generated significant WAL (write-ahead log) volume.

blocks.title: Partitioning is for large tables with natural time boundaries
blocks.content: Partition tables that are large enough to benefit (100+ million rows), have a natural partition key (date is the most common), and have query patterns that filter on the partition key. Do not partition small tables (the overhead exceeds the benefit) or tables without date-filtered access patterns (partitions that are never pruned add overhead without benefit). At HydroAlpes, only meter_readings and turbine_telemetry are partitioned — both have 100M+ rows and are always queried with a date filter. The meters table (1.2M rows) is not partitioned because it is small enough for a single-table scan to be fast.

Module 9 of the junior track introduced views as saved queries that execute fresh on every access. A materialized view is a saved query whose result is stored as a physical table: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW computes the query and stores the result, and subsequent SELECT statements read from the stored result instead of re-executing the query. The trade-off is freshness: a materialized view's data reflects the state of the source tables at the last refresh, not the current state. For HydroAlpes' billing summary — which is recomputed daily and consumed by dashboards throughout the day — the daily refresh latency is acceptable, and the query speedup from reading pre-computed results instead of re-aggregating 37.2 million rows is substantial.
Creating and Refreshing

-- Create the materialized view (computes and stores the result)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW gold.mv_regional_consumption AS
SELECT mr.region,
       mr.customer_type,
       DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)::DATE AS month,
       SUM(mr.daily_kw)              AS total_kw,
       COUNT(DISTINCT mr.meter_id)   AS active_meters,
       AVG(mr.daily_kw)             AS avg_daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings AS mr
  INNER JOIN silver.meters AS m ON mr.meter_id = m.meter_id
GROUP BY mr.region, mr.customer_type, DATE_TRUNC('month', mr.date)
WITH DATA;

-- Index the materialized view for fast lookups
CREATE INDEX idx_mv_regional_month ON gold.mv_regional_consumption (month, region);

-- Refresh: recompute the result from current source data
-- CONCURRENTLY allows reads during refresh (requires a unique index)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_mv_regional_unique
  ON gold.mv_regional_consumption (region, customer_type, month);

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY gold.mv_regional_consumption;

WITH DATA causes the materialized view to be populated immediately at creation time. The subsequent CREATE INDEX statements add indexes on the materialized view — just like a regular table — enabling fast filtered lookups. REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW recomputes the query and replaces the stored data with the new result. The CONCURRENTLY option allows other sessions to read the old data while the refresh is in progress, avoiding downtime. At HydroAlpes, the materialized view refresh runs daily at 06:00 as part of the Gold pipeline, after Silver processing completes. Dashboard queries against the materialized view return in under 100 milliseconds — versus 8 seconds for the live aggregation query.
Materialized Views vs CTAS Tables

rows.left: Stores the defining query — REFRESH recomputes from source
rows.right: No stored query — must DROP and recreate to refresh

rows.left: REFRESH CONCURRENTLY allows reads during refresh
rows.right: DROP + CTAS has a window where the table does not exist

rows.left: Cannot be INSERTed into directly — read-only
rows.right: Can be INSERTed, UPDATEd, DELETEd — full DML

rows.left: Indexes survive REFRESH and do not need to be recreated
rows.right: Indexes must be recreated after every DROP + CTAS cycle

rows.left: Best for: read-heavy precomputed aggregations with periodic refresh
rows.right: Best for: one-time snapshots or tables that need DML after creation

blocks.content: The key advantage of materialized views over CTAS is the refresh mechanism: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY recomputes the data, updates the stored result, and preserves indexes — all while other sessions continue to read the old data. CTAS requires DROP + CREATE, which has a window where the table does not exist (causing consumer errors), and indexes must be recreated (adding time and DDL complexity). At HydroAlpes, the billing summary was originally a CTAS table refreshed nightly. After three incidents where dashboard queries failed during the 4-second DROP-CREATE window, Lena migrated it to a materialized view with CONCURRENTLY refresh. Zero downtime since the migration.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use materialized views for precomputed aggregations that are queried frequently (dashboards, APIs) and refreshed periodically (daily, hourly). The refresh interval determines the maximum data staleness — choose an interval that balances freshness requirements with compute cost. At HydroAlpes, the billing summary refreshes daily (billing is a daily process), the capacity dashboard refreshes hourly (operations needs near-real-time visibility), and the regulatory report refreshes quarterly (regulatory filings are quarterly). Each materialized view's refresh is a scheduled pipeline step, not an ad-hoc operation.

Module 1 (lesson 7) introduced ANALYZE as the command that updates table statistics for the optimizer. This lesson covers statistics maintenance as an ongoing operational concern: how auto-analyze works and when it fails, how to tune statistics targets for better estimates, how to create extended statistics for correlated columns, and how to integrate ANALYZE into the pipeline schedule. Statistics maintenance is the least glamorous but most impactful operational practice — stale statistics cause more performance problems at HydroAlpes than missing indexes, wrong configurations, or suboptimal query structure combined.
Auto-Analyze Limitations

PostgreSQL's auto-analyze triggers when a configurable fraction of the table has been modified since the last analysis. The default threshold is 10% of the table plus 50 rows: for a 130-million-row table, auto-analyze triggers after 13 million modifications. HydroAlpes' daily load inserts 1.2 million rows — about 0.9% of the table. At this rate, auto-analyze triggers every 11 days. For 10 of those 11 days, the statistics reflect data that is up to 13 million rows stale. Queries that filter on recently-loaded dates have no statistics for those dates, causing the optimizer to rely on extrapolation — which may choose the wrong scan strategy or join algorithm.

-- Check auto-analyze settings and last run times
SELECT relname,
       n_live_tup,
       n_mod_since_analyze,
       last_analyze,
       last_autoanalyze
FROM   pg_stat_user_tables
WHERE  schemaname = 'silver'
ORDER BY n_mod_since_analyze DESC;

-- Tune auto-analyze to trigger more frequently for high-traffic tables
ALTER TABLE silver.meter_readings
  SET (autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.01);  -- 1% instead of 10%

The autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor parameter controls the modification threshold per-table. Reducing it from the default 0.10 (10%) to 0.01 (1%) makes auto-analyze trigger after 1.3 million modifications instead of 13 million — roughly daily at HydroAlpes' insertion rate. This is a targeted tuning: the parameter is set only on meter_readings (the high-traffic table), not globally. Small dimension tables that change rarely retain the default 10% threshold. At HydroAlpes, the auto-analyze scale factor is set as part of the table's migration script, ensuring the tuning persists across environments.
Pipeline-Integrated ANALYZE

Even with tuned auto-analyze, the safest approach is explicit ANALYZE after every bulk load. The pipeline sequence is: INSERT (load new data), ANALYZE (update statistics for the new data), then Gold queries (which use the fresh statistics). The ANALYZE takes 10–15 seconds on a 130-million-row table — negligible compared to the load time and the Gold query time. At HydroAlpes, ANALYZE is a mandatory pipeline step between Silver loading and Gold computation. The Airflow DAG includes an analyze_silver_tables task that runs ANALYZE on every Silver table that received data in the current pipeline run. This ensures that Gold queries always see fresh statistics, regardless of auto-analyze timing.
Statistics Target Tuning

The statistics target controls how many values PostgreSQL samples when computing column statistics: the most-common-values list and the histogram. The default of 100 is sufficient for uniformly distributed columns but may be insufficient for columns with skewed distributions or many distinct values. Increasing the target to 500 for region (10 distinct values with uneven distribution) and customer_type (3 values with highly uneven distribution — 80% residential) gives the optimizer more detailed distribution information, producing better row count estimates for filtered queries. The cost: ANALYZE takes slightly longer and the pg_statistic catalog uses slightly more storage. Both costs are negligible.

blocks.title: Statistics are the optimizer's view of the data
blocks.content: The optimizer does not see the actual data — it sees the statistics: row counts, distinct value counts, value distributions, and correlations. When the statistics are accurate, the optimizer makes good decisions. When they are stale, the optimizer makes decisions based on a picture of the data that no longer exists. Keeping statistics fresh is the single most impactful operational practice for query performance. At HydroAlpes, the pipeline-integrated ANALYZE prevents more performance problems than all indexing decisions combined.

Indexing, partitioning, and materialized views optimize the infrastructure around the query. Query rewriting optimizes the query itself — restructuring the SQL so the optimizer can produce a better plan. Some query patterns prevent the optimizer from using indexes, push down filters, or choose efficient algorithms. Rewriting these patterns into optimizer-friendly equivalents produces dramatic speedups without any infrastructure changes. This lesson covers the four most common rewriting patterns at HydroAlpes: replacing OR with UNION ALL, replacing NOT IN with NOT EXISTS, replacing functions on indexed columns with sargable equivalents, and replacing correlated subqueries with window functions.
OR to UNION ALL

-- SLOW: OR prevents the optimizer from using a single index scan
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15' OR region = 'BERN-NORTH';
-- Plan: Seq Scan (no single index covers both conditions)

-- FAST: UNION ALL allows each branch to use its own index
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date = '2025-01-15'
UNION ALL
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  region = 'BERN-NORTH'
  AND  date <> '2025-01-15';  -- exclude overlap to avoid duplicates
-- Plan: two Index Scans, one per branch

An OR condition on different columns prevents the optimizer from using a single index because no single B-tree index can efficiently satisfy both conditions simultaneously. The optimizer falls back to a Seq Scan or a Bitmap OR of two index scans. Rewriting as UNION ALL lets each branch use its own index independently. The second branch adds AND date <> '2025-01-15' to exclude rows that match both conditions (which appear in the first branch), preventing duplicates. At HydroAlpes, this rewrite reduced a reporting query from 14 seconds (Seq Scan) to 1.8 seconds (two Index Scans). The rewrite is not always beneficial — if both conditions are low-selectivity, two scans may read more data than one Seq Scan.
Functions on Indexed Columns (Sargability)

-- NON-SARGABLE: function on the indexed column prevents index use
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2025
  AND  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date) = 1;
-- Plan: Seq Scan (index on 'date' cannot be used because EXTRACT wraps it)

-- SARGABLE: rewrite as a range condition on the raw column
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-01'
  AND  date <  '2025-02-01';
-- Plan: Index Scan using idx_readings_date

A sargable (Search ARGument ABLE) condition uses the indexed column directly: date >= '2025-01-01'. A non-sargable condition wraps the column in a function: EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = 2025. The index is organized by date values, not by EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) values, so the optimizer cannot use the index for the function-wrapped condition. The fix is always the same: rewrite the condition to compare the raw column against computed constants. At HydroAlpes, the coding standard prohibits functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses. The code review checklist includes: 'Are all filter conditions on indexed columns sargable?'
Correlated Subquery to Window Function

-- SLOW: correlated subquery runs once per row (1.2 million executions)
SELECT mr.meter_id, mr.daily_kw, mr.date,
       (SELECT AVG(mr2.daily_kw)
        FROM silver.meter_readings mr2
        WHERE mr2.meter_id = mr.meter_id
          AND mr2.date >= mr.date - INTERVAL '7 days'
          AND mr2.date < mr.date) AS trailing_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings mr
WHERE  mr.date = '2025-01-15' AND mr.region = 'BERN-NORTH';

-- FAST: window function computes in a single pass
SELECT meter_id, daily_kw, date,
       AVG(daily_kw) OVER (
         PARTITION BY meter_id
         ORDER BY date
         RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '7 days' PRECEDING AND INTERVAL '1 day' PRECEDING
       ) AS trailing_avg
FROM   silver.meter_readings
WHERE  date >= '2025-01-08' AND date <= '2025-01-15'
  AND  region = 'BERN-NORTH';

The correlated subquery computes a trailing 7-day average for each meter by running a separate AVG query per row — 120,000 subquery executions for BERN-NORTH meters on January 15. The window function computes the same result in a single pass over the data, using the RANGE frame to define the trailing window. At HydroAlpes, the window function version runs in 1.2 seconds; the correlated subquery version runs in 47 seconds — a 39x improvement from the same logical computation expressed in an optimizer-friendly form. The broader date range in the WHERE clause (starting January 8) ensures the window function has data for the full 7-day lookback.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Before rewriting a query for performance, always EXPLAIN ANALYZE the original to confirm the bottleneck. Rewriting an OR to UNION ALL is wasted effort if the Seq Scan is caused by stale statistics, not by the OR. Rewriting a correlated subquery to a window function is wasted effort if the subquery is already executing as a hash semi-join. The diagnostic workflow from lesson 1 applies: measure, identify, understand, then rewrite.

Query-level performance optimization (indexes, partitioning, query rewriting) addresses individual query speed. Server-level resource management addresses the environment in which all queries run: how many concurrent connections the database handles, how much memory is available for each query, and how resources are shared across HydroAlpes' three teams. At mid-level scale, resource contention becomes a performance factor: the billing pipeline's large hash join competes with 15 analytics dashboards for work_mem, and 30 concurrent dashboard sessions compete for connection slots. This lesson covers the operational infrastructure that ensures all consumers get adequate resources.
Connection Pooling with PgBouncer

Each PostgreSQL connection consumes approximately 5–10 MB of memory for process overhead, independent of query activity. With 30 dashboard users, 3 pipeline connections, and 5 ML feature engineering sessions, HydroAlpes has 38 concurrent connections consuming 190–380 MB just for connection overhead. If each dashboard refreshes every 30 seconds, the 30 users generate 60 queries per minute — but each query runs for only 100–500 milliseconds, meaning the connection is idle for 29.5 of every 30 seconds. PgBouncer — a connection pooler — multiplexes many client connections over a smaller pool of database connections, reducing the overhead from 38 database connections to approximately 8 (enough for the concurrent active queries) while supporting all 38 clients.
Memory Configuration

rows: shared_buffers
8 GB (25% of 32 GB RAM)
PostgreSQL's shared memory cache for table and index pages
Standard recommendation: 25% of total RAM for dedicated database servers
work_mem
64 MB (global), 256 MB (pipeline sessions)
Per-operation memory for sorts and hash joins
Conservative global default; pipeline uses SET LOCAL for specific queries
effective_cache_size
24 GB (75% of 32 GB RAM)
Optimizer's estimate of available cache (OS + PostgreSQL)
Tells optimizer how much data is likely cached, affecting index scan decisions
maintenance_work_mem
2 GB
Memory for VACUUM, CREATE INDEX, and other maintenance operations
Higher than work_mem because maintenance runs infrequently with large data

blocks.content: The memory configuration balances three competing needs: shared_buffers for caching frequently accessed pages, work_mem for per-query operations (hash joins, sorts), and OS cache for everything else. The total must not exceed physical RAM — if shared_buffers + (max_connections × work_mem × operations_per_query) exceeds available memory, the server swaps to disk and performance collapses. At HydroAlpes, the worst-case memory demand is 8 GB (shared_buffers) + 38 connections × 256 MB × 3 operations = 37 GB — which exceeds the 32 GB server RAM. This is why work_mem is set conservatively at 64 MB globally and elevated only for pipeline sessions: the pipeline runs 3 connections with 256 MB work_mem (2.3 GB total), while dashboard users run 30 connections with 64 MB (5.8 GB total), and the combined peak is manageable.
Resource Isolation Between Teams

PostgreSQL does not natively support resource groups (like Snowflake's warehouses or Spark's dynamic resource allocation). Resource isolation at HydroAlpes is achieved through connection pooling configuration: the pipeline connects through a dedicated PgBouncer pool with higher work_mem and statement_timeout = 0 (no timeout for long-running pipeline queries). Dashboard users connect through a separate pool with lower work_mem and statement_timeout = 30s (queries that take longer than 30 seconds are killed to prevent resource hogging). The ML team connects through a third pool with medium work_mem and statement_timeout = 300s. This pool-based isolation ensures that a runaway dashboard query cannot consume the memory that the billing pipeline needs.

blocks.title: Resource management is about trade-offs, not maximums
blocks.content: Setting work_mem = 2 GB globally makes hash joins faster but risks out-of-memory crashes under concurrent load. Setting it to 4 MB prevents crashes but causes hash spill on moderate queries. The correct setting depends on the workload mix: how many concurrent queries, how large their hash tables, and how much memory is available. There is no universal 'best' value — only the right value for your specific workload, discovered through monitoring and iterative tuning.

This module taught the complete performance tuning toolkit: the diagnostic workflow for systematic investigation, B-tree and composite index design for accelerating filtered queries, partial and covering indexes for specialized access patterns, table partitioning for large time-series tables, materialized views for precomputed aggregations, statistics maintenance for optimizer accuracy, query rewriting for optimizer-friendly SQL, and connection pooling with resource management for concurrent workloads. This final lesson codifies these techniques into HydroAlpes' performance playbook — the standards, thresholds, and review practices that keep the platform fast as data grows.
The Performance Standards

rows: Every Silver table has a date index
CREATE INDEX on the date or timestamp column
Migration CI check: verify index exists after table creation
Every composite index follows equality-before-range
Equality columns left, range columns right
Code review checklist item
Tables over 100M rows are partitioned by month
PARTITION BY RANGE on date column
Architecture review for new tables; migration for existing tables
ANALYZE runs after every bulk load
Pipeline includes ANALYZE as a mandatory step
Airflow DAG includes analyze_tables task
Unused indexes are dropped quarterly
pg_stat_user_indexes.idx_scan = 0 for 90+ days
Quarterly index review by Lena
No functions on indexed columns in WHERE
All filter conditions are sargable
Code review checklist item; sqlfluff custom rule

blocks.content: The Quarterly Performance Review

Every quarter, HydroAlpes' engineering team conducts a performance review. The review examines: the 10 slowest queries from the performance_log table (ranked by average execution time), the index usage statistics from pg_stat_user_indexes (identifying unused indexes for removal and frequently-scanned tables for new indexes), the table size growth trajectory (projecting when tables will cross the 100M-row partitioning threshold), and the work_mem spill frequency from EXPLAIN ANALYZE samples (identifying queries that need higher work_mem or query rewriting). Each finding produces an action item: create an index, drop an index, partition a table, rewrite a query, or adjust a configuration parameter. The action items are tracked as migration scripts in the next deployment cycle.

The quarterly cadence balances thoroughness with effort. Monthly reviews would catch issues sooner but consume significant engineering time across all three teams. Annual reviews would miss gradual degradation until it becomes an acute production incident. Quarterly reviews catch slow-growing problems — table sizes approaching the partition threshold, index usage declining as query patterns shift, work_mem spill frequency creeping up as data volumes grow — before they become performance incidents, while respecting the team's capacity for operational overhead. The review output — a prioritized list of performance improvements with estimated impact — feeds directly into the next quarter's sprint planning.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 9, you will build the data quality framework that monitors the data flowing through the optimized pipeline. Quality dimension metrics (completeness, accuracy, timeliness), monitoring tables that track quality over time, anomaly detection queries, automated alerting, and quarantine patterns — these ensure that the pipeline produces not just fast results but correct results. Performance without quality is a fast pipeline that delivers wrong answers. Module 9 adds the quality layer that makes the performance meaningful.

modules.title: Data Quality Frameworks
modules.description: Build systematic data quality monitoring with quality dimension metrics, monitoring tables, anomaly detection, automated alerting, and quarantine patterns.

sections.title: From Ad-Hoc Checks to a Framework
